### [1] [SAC-NeRF: Adaptive Ray Sampling for Neural Radiance Fields via Soft Actor-Critic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15622)
*Chenyu Ge*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAC-NeRF uses reinforcement learning to adaptively sample rays in NeRF via SAC, achieving 35-48% fewer samples while preserving rendering quality within 0.3-0.8 dB PSNR of dense baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NeRF's heavy computation stems from dense ray sampling in volume rendering; enabling data-driven, adaptive sampling can reduce computation without sacrificing quality.

Method: Formulates sampling as a Markov Decision Process and trains a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) agent to allocate samples based on scene characteristics. Introduces (1) a Gaussian mixture color model for uncertainty estimates, (2) a multi-component reward balancing quality, efficiency, and consistency, and (3) a two-stage training strategy to mitigate environment non-stationarity.

Result: On Synthetic-NeRF and LLFF datasets, SAC-NeRF reduces sampling points by 35-48% while maintaining rendering quality within 0.3-0.8 dB PSNR of dense-sampling baselines.

Conclusion: The learned policy is scene-specific and adds RL complexity versus simple heuristics, but the work demonstrates that data-driven sampling patterns can capture effective sampling behaviors that are hard to hand-design.

Abstract: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) have achieved photorealistic novel view synthesis but suffer from computational inefficiency due to dense ray sampling during volume rendering. We propose SAC-NeRF, a reinforcement learning framework that learns adaptive sampling policies using Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). Our method formulates sampling as a Markov Decision Process where an RL agent learns to allocate samples based on scene characteristics. We introduce three technical components: (1) a Gaussian mixture distribution color model providing uncertainty estimates, (2) a multi-component reward function balancing quality, efficiency, and consistency, and (3) a two-stage training strategy addressing environment non-stationarity. Experiments on Synthetic-NeRF and LLFF datasets show that SAC-NeRF reduces sampling points by 35-48\% while maintaining rendering quality within 0.3-0.8 dB PSNR of dense sampling baselines. While the learned policy is scene-specific and the RL framework adds complexity compared to simpler heuristics, our work demonstrates that data-driven sampling strategies can discover effective patterns that would be difficult to hand-design.

</details>


### [2] [Exploring the Use of VLMs for Navigation Assistance for People with Blindness and Low Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15624)
*Yu Li,Yuchen Zheng,Giles Hamilton-Fletcher,Marco Mezzavilla,Yao Wang,Sundeep Rangan,Maurizio Porfiri,Zhou Yu,John-Ross Rizzo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vision-language models (VLMs) show promise for aiding navigation for people who are blind or have low vision, with GPT-4o leading performance. Closed-source models outperform open-source ones in spatial reasoning and scene understanding; open-source models struggle with nuanced reasoning and cluttered counting. Aligning VLMs with human feedback and improving spatial reasoning are key for effective assistive navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess the capabilities and limitations of current VLMs for assistive navigation tasks in pBLV users, to inform integration into practical technologies.

Method: Evaluate state-of-the-art closed-source (GPT-4V, GPT-4o, Gemini-1.5-Pro, Claude-3.5-Sonnet) and open-source (Llava-v1.6-mistral, Llava-onevision-qwen) VLMs on foundational visual tasks (counting ambient obstacles, relative spatial reasoning, commonsense wayfinding scene understanding) and on navigation scenarios using pBLV-specific prompts designed to mimic real-world assistance tasks.

Result: GPT-4o consistently outperforms others across tasks, especially in spatial reasoning and scene understanding. Open-source models show weaker performance and struggle with nuanced reasoning and adapting to complex environments. Common challenges include miscounting in clutter, spatial biases, and prioritizing object details over spatial feedback.

Conclusion: VLMs hold promise for wayfinding assistance but require better alignment with human feedback and improved spatial reasoning to be reliably usable in pBLV navigation; findings guide developers in integrating VLMs into assistive tech while addressing key limitations.

Abstract: This paper investigates the potential of vision-language models (VLMs) to assist people with blindness and low vision (pBLV) in navigation tasks. We evaluate state-of-the-art closed-source models, including GPT-4V, GPT-4o, Gemini-1.5-Pro, and Claude-3.5-Sonnet, alongside open-source models, such as Llava-v1.6-mistral and Llava-onevision-qwen, to analyze their capabilities in foundational visual skills: counting ambient obstacles, relative spatial reasoning, and common-sense wayfinding-pertinent scene understanding. We further assess their performance in navigation scenarios, using pBLV-specific prompts designed to simulate real-world assistance tasks. Our findings reveal notable performance disparities between these models: GPT-4o consistently outperforms others across all tasks, particularly in spatial reasoning and scene understanding. In contrast, open-source models struggle with nuanced reasoning and adaptability in complex environments. Common challenges include difficulties in accurately counting objects in cluttered settings, biases in spatial reasoning, and a tendency to prioritize object details over spatial feedback, limiting their usability for pBLV in navigation tasks. Despite these limitations, VLMs show promise for wayfinding assistance when better aligned with human feedback and equipped with improved spatial reasoning. This research provides actionable insights into the strengths and limitations of current VLMs, guiding developers on effectively integrating VLMs into assistive technologies while addressing key limitations for enhanced usability.

</details>


### [3] [Improving Generative Adversarial Network Generalization for Facial Expression Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15648)
*Arbish Akram,Nazar Khan,Arif Mahmood*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RegGAN introduces a regression-based intermediate representation with a ridge loss and a subsequent refinement GAN to improve generalization in facial expression synthesis. It shows strong quantitative gains on ECS, FID, QualiCLIP and competitive FSS, with substantial human-evaluated improvements, particularly on out-of-distribution data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the generalization gap of conditional GANs for facial expression synthesis when test data deviates from the training distribution, by learning an intermediate representation that captures expression details.

Method: A two-part framework: (1) a regression layer with local receptive fields that learns expression details via ridge regression loss to produce an intermediate representation; (2) a refinement network trained adversarially to enhance realism of the final image. Trained on CFEE; evaluated on CFEE and out-of-distribution images (celebrities, portraits, statues, avatars). Evaluation uses ECS, FSS, QualiCLIP, and FID.

Result: RegGAN outperforms six state-of-the-art models in ECS, FID, and QualiCLIP, ranking second in FSS. Human evaluation shows RegGAN surpassing the best competitor by 25% in expression quality, 26% in identity preservation, and 30% in realism.

Conclusion: The intermediate regression-based representation effectively enhances generalization for facial expression synthesis, enabling better expression fidelity and realism across diverse, out-of-distribution inputs while maintaining competitive identity preservation.

Abstract: Facial expression synthesis aims to generate realistic facial expressions while preserving identity. Existing conditional generative adversarial networks (GANs) achieve excellent image-to-image translation results, but their performance often degrades when test images differ from the training dataset. We present Regression GAN (RegGAN), a model that learns an intermediate representation to improve generalization beyond the training distribution. RegGAN consists of two components: a regression layer with local receptive fields that learns expression details by minimizing the reconstruction error through a ridge regression loss, and a refinement network trained adversarially to enhance the realism of generated images. We train RegGAN on the CFEE dataset and evaluate its generalization performance both on CFEE and challenging out-of-distribution images, including celebrity photos, portraits, statues, and avatar renderings. For evaluation, we employ four widely used metrics: Expression Classification Score (ECS) for expression quality, Face Similarity Score (FSS) for identity preservation, QualiCLIP for perceptual realism, and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) for assessing both expression quality and realism. RegGAN outperforms six state-of-the-art models in ECS, FID, and QualiCLIP, while ranking second in FSS. Human evaluations indicate that RegGAN surpasses the best competing model by 25% in expression quality, 26% in identity preservation, and 30% in realism.

</details>


### [4] [OrthoAI v2: From Single-Agent Segmentation to Dual-Agent Treatment Planning for Clear Aligners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15663)
*Lansiaux Edouard,Leman Margaux*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OrthoAI v2 advances AI-assisted orthodontic planning with a second agent for direct landmark detection, a six-category biomechanical scoring, and a multi-frame 4D treatment simulator; on synthetic data it improves planning quality by ~21% over v1 and remains CPU-friendly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address v1 limitations: (i) segmentation-based tooth landmarks only; (ii) landmark-level precision missing; (iii) binary pass/fail quality score and no staging simulation.

Method: Three contributions: (i) CHARM-based second agent for direct landmark detection, fused with Agent 1 via a confidence-weighted orchestrator in parallel/sequential/single-agent modes; (ii) composite biomechanical scoring model with weighted components; (iii) multi-frame simulator generating 6-DoF tooth trajectories using SLERP and staging rules for ClinCheck 4D visualization.

Result: Synthetic benchmark of 200 crowding scenarios shows parallel OrthoAI v2 achieving planning quality 92.8 ± 4.1 vs 76.4 ± 8.3 for v1 (+21% relative); CPU time 4.2 ± 0.8 s; full CPU deployability.

Conclusion: v2 meaningfully enhances landmark precision, staging simulation, and interpretability while preserving CPU efficiency; external validation on real-world data and robustness checks remain for future work.

Abstract: We present OrthoAI v2, the second iteration of our open-source pipeline for AI-assisted orthodontic treatment planning with clear aligners, substantially extending the single-agent framework previously introduced. The first version established a proof-of-concept based on Dynamic Graph Convolutional Neural Networks (\dgcnn{}) for tooth segmentation but was limited to per-tooth centroid extraction, lacked landmark-level precision, and produced a scalar quality score without staging simulation. \vtwo{} addresses all three limitations through three principal contributions: (i)~a second agent adopting the Conditioned Heatmap Regression Methodology (\charm{})~\cite{rodriguez2025charm} for direct, segmentation-free dental landmark detection, fused with Agent~1 via a confidence-weighted orchestrator in three modes (parallel, sequential, single-agent); (ii)~a composite six-category biomechanical scoring model (biomechanics $\times$ 0.30 + staging $\times$ 0.20 + attachments $\times$ 0.15 + IPR $\times$ 0.10 + occlusion $\times$ 0.10 + predictability $\times$ 0.15) replacing the binary pass/fail check of v1; (iii)~a multi-frame treatment simulator generating $F = A \times r$ temporally coherent 6-DoF tooth trajectories via SLERP interpolation and evidence-based staging rules, enabling ClinCheck 4D visualisation. On a synthetic benchmark of 200 crowding scenarios, the parallel ensemble of OrthoAI v2 reaches a planning quality score of $92.8 \pm 4.1$ vs.\ $76.4 \pm 8.3$ for OrthoAI v1, a $+21\%$ relative gain, while maintaining full CPU deployability ($4.2 \pm 0.8$~s).

</details>


### [5] [CLRNet: Targetless Extrinsic Calibration for Camera, Lidar and 4D Radar Using Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15767)
*Marcell Kegl,Andras Palffy,Csaba Benedek,Dariu M. Gavrila*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CLRNet is a multi-modal end-to-end DL framework for joint and pairwise extrinsic calibration of camera, LiDAR, and 4D radar, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy (≥50% reduction in median translational and rotational errors) on View-of-Delft and Dual-Radar datasets, with domain transfer analysis and public code release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Radar extrinsic calibration is particularly challenging due to sparse radar data; there is a need for robust joint calibration across camera, LiDAR, and radar modalities to enable accurate sensor fusion.

Method: An end-to-end network (CLRNet) that supports joint camera-LiDAR-radar and pairwise calibrations. Key components include equirectangular projection, camera-based depth image prediction, additional radar channels, shared feature space with LiDAR, and a loop closure loss to improve consistency across calibrations.

Result: Experiments on View-of-D Delft and Dual-Radar show superior calibration accuracy compared with state-of-the-art methods, with at least 50% reductions in median translational and rotational errors. The study also analyzes domain transfer across datasets.

Conclusion: CLRNet provides accurate, versatile calibration across multiple sensing modalities with potential for practical deployment; code will be publicly released upon acceptance.

Abstract: In this paper, we address extrinsic calibration for camera, lidar, and 4D radar sensors. Accurate extrinsic calibration of radar remains a challenge due to the sparsity of its data. We propose CLRNet, a novel, multi-modal end-to-end deep learning (DL) calibration network capable of addressing joint camera-lidar-radar calibration, or pairwise calibration between any two of these sensors. We incorporate equirectangular projection, camera-based depth image prediction, additional radar channels, and leverage lidar with a shared feature space and loop closure loss. In extensive experiments using the View-of-Delft and Dual-Radar datasets, we demonstrate superior calibration accuracy compared to existing state-of-the-art methods, reducing both median translational and rotational calibration errors by at least 50%. Finally, we examine the domain transfer capabilities of the proposed network and baselines, when evaluating across datasets. The code will be made publicly available upon acceptance at: https://github.com/tudelft-iv.

</details>


### [6] [Domain Adaptation Without the Compute Burden for Efficient Whole Slide Image Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15774)
*Umar Marikkar,Muhammad Awais,Sara Atito*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EfficientWSI (eWSI) integrates Parameter-Efficient-Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) to enable near end-to-end training on whole slide images (WSIs). It achieves competitive performance using ImageNet features and can surpass MILs with in-domain features, reducing the need for costly domain-specific pre-training; when paired with in-domain features, it yields further gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: WSIs are extremely high-resolution, making end-to-end training computationally prohibitive. Conventional pipelines rely on fixed feature extractors and MIL, with domain-specific pre-training often being expensive and potentially suboptimal for task-specific cues. A lightweight, task-targeted fine-tuning approach could capture task-specific information without heavy pre-training.

Method: Utilize Parameter-Efficient-Fine-Tuning (PEFT) within an MIL framework to adapt pre-trained feature extractors for WSI tasks. Evaluate eWSI end-to-end on seven WSI-level tasks across Camelyon16, TCGA, and BRACS datasets, comparing ImageNet-based features and in-domain extractors.

Result: eWSI with ImageNet-based features matches or surpasses MILs that use in-domain extractors; when combined with in-domain feature extractors, eWSI often yields further improvements. The approach reduces dependence on extensive domain-specific pre-training while enabling task-targeted adaptation and maintaining strong classification performance across multiple datasets.

Conclusion: eWSI offers a computationally efficient, task-targeted path for WSI learning, enabling end-to-end-like training and improved performance with domain-specific adaptation when beneficial, suggesting a promising direction for task-specific learning in computational pathology.

Abstract: Computational methods on analyzing Whole Slide Images (WSIs) enable early diagnosis and treatments by supporting pathologists in detection and classification of tumors. However, the extremely high resolution of WSIs makes end-to-end training impractical compared to typical image analysis tasks. To address this, most approaches use pre-trained feature extractors to obtain fixed representations of whole slides, which are then combined with Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) for downstream tasks. These feature extractors are typically pre-trained on natural image datasets such as ImageNet, which fail to capture domain-specific characteristics. Although domain-specific pre-training on histopathology data yields more relevant feature representations, it remains computationally expensive and fail to capture task-specific characteristics within the domain. To address the computational cost and lack of task-specificity in domain-specific pre-training, we propose EfficientWSI (eWSI), a careful integration of Parameter-Efficient-Fine-Tuning (PEFT) and Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) that enables end-to-end training on WSI tasks. We evaluate eWSI on seven WSI-level tasks over Camelyon16, TCGA and BRACS datasets. Our results show that eWSI when applied with ImageNet feature extractors yields strong classification performance, matching or outperforming MILs with in-domain feature extractors, alleviating the need for extensive in-domain pre-training. Furthermore, when eWSI is applied with in-domain feature extractors, it further improves classification performance in most cases, demonstrating its ability to capture task-specific information where beneficial. Our findings suggest that eWSI provides a task-targeted, computationally efficient path for WSI tasks, offering a promising direction for task-specific learning in computational pathology.

</details>


### [7] [Parallelised Differentiable Straightest Geodesics for 3D Meshes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15780)
*Hippolyte Verninas,Caner Korkmaz,Stefanos Zafeiriou,Tolga Birdal,Simone Foti*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A differentiable exponential map on mesh-based Riemannian surfaces achieved via straightest geodesics, with parallel GPU implementation and two differentiation schemes; enables new mesh-based geometric ML components (geodesic conv layers, flow matching) and optimizers; code/library available (digeo).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of differentiable, scalable Riemannian operators on meshes due to non-differentiable discretizations and limited parallelism; provide a differentiable exponential map and associated tools to advance learning on non-Euclidean surfaces.

Method: Compute the exponential map through straightest geodesics on mesh discretizations; implement parallel GPU algorithm; derive two differentiable schemes: an extrinsic proxy function and a geodesic finite-differences-based method; validate parallel performance and accuracy; showcase via a geodesic convolutional layer, a flow matching method, and a second-order optimizer for centroidal Voronoi tessellation; release as digeo.

Result: Demonstrates efficient parallelization with accurate exponential-map computations on meshes; integrates differentiable maps into learning pipelines, yielding new mesh-based layers/methods and optimizers; code and models publicly available.

Conclusion: A practical, differentiable exponential map for mesh-based Riemannian learning enabling broader, more accurate ML on general geometries, backed by implementations and demonstrations.

Abstract: Machine learning has been progressively generalised to operate within non-Euclidean domains, but geometrically accurate methods for learning on surfaces are still falling behind. The lack of closed-form Riemannian operators, the non-differentiability of their discrete counterparts, and poor parallelisation capabilities have been the main obstacles to the development of the field on meshes. A principled framework to compute the exponential map on Riemannian surfaces discretised as meshes is straightest geodesics, which also allows to trace geodesics and parallel-transport vectors as a by-product. We provide a parallel GPU implementation and derive two different methods for differentiating through the straightest geodesics, one leveraging an extrinsic proxy function and one based upon a geodesic finite differences scheme. After proving our parallelisation performance and accuracy, we demonstrate how our differentiable exponential map can improve learning and optimisation pipelines on general geometries. In particular, to showcase the versatility of our method, we propose a new geodesic convolutional layer, a new flow matching method for learning on meshes, and a second-order optimiser that we apply to centroidal Voronoi tessellation. Our code, models, and pip-installable library (digeo) are available at: circle-group.github.io/research/DSG.

</details>


### [8] [Evolving Contextual Safety in Multi-Modal Large Language Models via Inference-Time Self-Reflective Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15800)
*Ce Zhang,Jinxi He,Junyi He,Katia Sycara,Yaqi Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes MM-SafetyBench++ for contextual safety evaluation and EchoSafe, a training-free, memory-based framework enabling context-aware safety during inference; reports strong empirical gains and open-source data/code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety risk in multimodal LLMs extends beyond explicit jailbreaks; models must infer safety intent from context, which is often subtle and scenario-dependent.

Method: Construct MM-SafetyBench++ by creating unsafe image-text pairs and generating corresponding safe counterparts via minimal modifications that flip user intent while preserving context; develop EchoSafe with a self-reflective memory bank that stores and retrieves past safety insights to influence current prompts.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multi-modal safety benchmarks demonstrates EchoSafe improves context-aware safety and sets a strong baseline; benchmarks and code released.

Conclusion: Contextual safety in MLLMs can be advanced via memory-augmented prompting strategies and carefully designed benchmarks, enabling adaptive safety behaviors without training.

Abstract: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable performance across a wide range of visual reasoning tasks, yet their vulnerability to safety risks remains a pressing concern. While prior research primarily focuses on jailbreak defenses that detect and refuse explicitly unsafe inputs, such approaches often overlook contextual safety, which requires models to distinguish subtle contextual differences between scenarios that may appear similar but diverge significantly in safety intent. In this work, we present MM-SafetyBench++, a carefully curated benchmark designed for contextual safety evaluation. Specifically, for each unsafe image-text pair, we construct a corresponding safe counterpart through minimal modifications that flip the user intent while preserving the underlying contextual meaning, enabling controlled evaluation of whether models can adapt their safety behaviors based on contextual understanding. Further, we introduce EchoSafe, a training-free framework that maintains a self-reflective memory bank to accumulate and retrieve safety insights from prior interactions. By integrating relevant past experiences into current prompts, EchoSafe enables context-aware reasoning and continual evolution of safety behavior during inference. Extensive experiments on various multi-modal safety benchmarks demonstrate that EchoSafe consistently achieves superior performance, establishing a strong baseline for advancing contextual safety in MLLMs. All benchmark data and code are available at https://echosafe-mllm.github.io.

</details>


### [9] [Feed-forward Gaussian Registration for Head Avatar Creation and Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15811)
*Malte Prinzler,Paulo Gotardo,Siyu Tang,Timo Bolkart*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MATCH is a fast, end-to-end transformer-based method for multi-view head avatar creation that predicts Gaussian splat textures in a fixed UV layout, enabling 0.5s per frame and a 10x speedup over baselines; it introduces a registration-guided attention block and learns intra- and inter-subject correspondences for editing, tracking, and synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the time and complexity of head-avatar pipelines that currently require time-consuming tracking and expensive optimization, enabling fast, data-efficient creation and editing from calibrated multi-view images.

Method: End-to-end transformer model that predicts Gaussian splat textures directly in a template mesh's UV layout; introduces a registration-guided attention block where each UV-map token attends only to image tokens from the corresponding mesh region; leverages multi-view Gaussian registration and learned intra-subject correspondences across frames.

Result: MATCH outperforms existing methods in novel-view synthesis, geometry registration, and head avatar generation; achieves ~0.5 seconds per frame; around 10x faster than the closest competing baseline; enables expression transfer, optimization-free tracking, semantic editing, and identity interpolation.

Conclusion: MATCH enables fast, high-quality head avatar creation and editing from calibrated multi-view data by learning explicit intra- and inter-subject correspondences and employing an efficient, region-restricted attention mechanism.

Abstract: We present MATCH (Multi-view Avatars from Topologically Corresponding Heads), a multi-view Gaussian registration method for high-quality head avatar creation and editing. State-of-the-art multi-view head avatar methods require time-consuming head tracking followed by expensive avatar optimization, often resulting in a total creation time of more than one day. MATCH, in contrast, directly predicts Gaussian splat textures in correspondence from calibrated multi-view images in just 0.5 seconds per frame, without requiring data preprocessing. The learned intra-subject correspondence across frames enables fast creation of personalized head avatars, while correspondence across subjects supports applications such as expression transfer, optimization-free tracking, semantic editing, and identity interpolation. We establish these correspondences end-to-end using a transformer-based model that predicts Gaussian splat textures in the fixed UV layout of a template mesh. To achieve this, we introduce a novel registration-guided attention block, where each UV-map token attends exclusively to image tokens depicting its corresponding mesh region. This design improves efficiency and performance compared to dense cross-view attention. MATCH outperforms existing methods in novel-view synthesis, geometry registration, and head avatar generation, while making avatar creation 10 times faster than the closest competing baseline. The code and model weights are available on the project website.

</details>


### [10] [ModTrack: Sensor-Agnostic Multi-View Tracking via Identity-Informed PHD Filtering with Covariance Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15812)
*Aditya Iyer,Jack Roberts,Nora Ayanian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ModTrack is a modular MV-MOT system that decouples detection/feature extraction from fusion/association/tracking to provide principled uncertainty handling and cross-modal generalization, while retaining end-to-end performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of principled uncertainty, sensitivity to training configuration, and poor generalization across sensor layouts, modalities, or datasets in end-to-end MV-MOT methods.

Method: Constrain learning to detection and feature extraction; reduce each sensor output to calibrated position-covariance pairs (z, R); perform cross-view clustering and precision-weighted fusion to obtain unified estimates ( ẑ, R̂ ); apply a feedback-coupled GM-PHD filter with HMM motion modes using fused estimates for identity maintenance under missed detections/occlusion; reuse a fixed tracker core across domains with perception-module replacement.

Result: ModTrack achieves 95.5 IDF1 and 91.4 MOTA on WildTrack, surpassing prior modular methods by >21 points and rivaling state-of-the-art end-to-end methods; demonstrates cross-domain generalization to MultiviewX and RadarScenes with unchanged tracker core and only perception-module adaptation.

Conclusion: A modular MV-MOT framework can match end-to-end performance while providing cross-modal generalization and traceable uncertainty through calibrated detections, principled fusion, and an uncertainty-aware GM-PHD tracker.

Abstract: Multi-View Multi-Object Tracking (MV-MOT) aims to localize and maintain consistent identities of objects observed by multiple sensors. This task is challenging, as viewpoint changes and occlusion disrupt identity consistency across views and time. Recent end-to-end approaches address this by jointly learning 2D Bird's Eye View (BEV) representations and identity associations, achieving high tracking accuracy. However, these methods offer no principled uncertainty accounting and remain tightly coupled to their training configuration, limiting generalization across sensor layouts, modalities, or datasets without retraining. We propose ModTrack, a modular MV-MOT system that matches end-to-end performance while providing cross-modal, sensor-agnostic generalization and traceable uncertainty. ModTrack confines learning methods to just the \textit{Detection and Feature Extraction} stage of the MV-MOT pipeline, performing all fusion, association, and tracking with closed-form analytical methods. Our design reduces each sensor's output to calibrated position-covariance pairs $(\mathbf{z}, R)$; cross-view clustering and precision-weighted fusion then yield unified estimates $(\hat{\mathbf{z}}, \hat{R})$ for identity assignment and temporal tracking. A feedback-coupled, identity-informed Gaussian Mixture Probability Hypothesis Density (GM-PHD) filter with HMM motion modes uses these fused estimates to maintain identities under missed detections and heavy occlusion. ModTrack achieves 95.5 IDF1 and 91.4 MOTA on \textit{WildTrack}, surpassing all prior modular methods by over 21 points and rivaling the state-of-the-art end-to-end methods while providing deployment flexibility they cannot. Specifically, the same tracker core transfers unchanged to \textit{MultiviewX} and \textit{RadarScenes}, with only perception-module replacement required to extend to new domains and sensor modalities.

</details>


### [11] [Conflict-Aware Multimodal Fusion for Ambivalence and Hesitancy Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15818)
*Salah Eddine Bekhouche,Hichem Telli,Azeddine Benlamoudi,Salah Eddine Herrouz,Abdelmalik Taleb-Ahmed,Abdenour Hadid*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ConflictAwareAH is a multimodal framework that uses pairwise cross-modal differences to detect ambivalence/hesitancy (A/H) and achieves strong macro F1 on the ABAW10 BAH task, using efficient single-GPU training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: A/H signals manifest as disagreements among spoken content, voice, and facial cues; text-dominant methods over-detect A/H and struggle to confirm its absence, necessitating robust multimodal integration.

Method: Three pre-trained encoders extract video, audio, and text representations. Pairwise element-wise absolute differences between modality embeddings form conflict features that serve as bidirectional cues for A/H presence/absence. A text-guided late fusion augments the full multimodal model with a text-only auxiliary head at inference.

Result: On BAH (ABAW10): Macro F1 = 0.694 on the labelled test split and 0.715 on the private leaderboard, outperforming published multimodal baselines by over 10 points. The approach improves F1-NoAH by 4.6 points over text alone and the text-guided fusion adds 4.1 Macro F1.

Conclusion: Conflict-aware, multimodal cues effectively capture disagreements among modalities, improving detection of A/H and reducing reliance on text alone, with strong performance and efficient training.

Abstract: Ambivalence and hesitancy (A/H) are subtle affective states where a person shows conflicting signals through different channels -- saying one thing while their face or voice tells another story. Recognising these states automatically is valuable in clinical settings, but it is hard for machines because the key evidence lives in the \emph{disagreements} between what is said, how it sounds, and what the face shows.
  We present \textbf{ConflictAwareAH}, a multimodal framework built for this problem. Three pre-trained encoders extract video, audio, and text representations. Pairwise conflict features -- element-wise absolute differences between modality embeddings -- serve as \emph{bidirectional} cues: large cross-modal differences flag A/H, while small differences confirm behavioural consistency and anchor the negative class. This conflict-aware design addresses a key limitation of text-dominant approaches, which tend to over-detect A/H (high F1-AH) while struggling to confirm its absence: our multimodal model improves F1-NoAH by +4.6 points over text alone and halves the class-performance gap. A complementary \emph{text-guided late fusion} strategy blends a text-only auxiliary head with the full model at inference, adding +4.1 Macro F1.
  On the BAH dataset from the ABAW10 Ambivalence/Hesitancy Challenge, our method reaches \textbf{0.694 Macro F1} on the labelled test split and \textbf{0.715} on the private leaderboard, outperforming published multimodal baselines by over 10 points -- all on a single GPU in under 25 minutes of training.

</details>


### [12] [Beyond the Embedding Bottleneck: Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented 3D CT Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15822)
*Renjie Liang,Yiling Ma,Yang Xing,Zhengkang Fan,Jinqian Pan,Chengkun Sun,Li Li,Kuang Gong,Jie Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AdaRAG-CT addresses a representational bottleneck in 3D CT-to-text report generation, where contrastive embeddings for CT volumes are highly concentrated in few dimensions (~2 of 512). Language-model scaling does not help, indicating a bottleneck in the visual representation. The authors propose AdaRAG-CT, an adaptive retrieval-based augmentation that injects supplementary textual information during generation. On CT-RATE, it achieves state-of-the-art clinical efficacy (Clinical F1 from 0.420 to 0.480), with ablations showing both retrieval and generation contribute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated radiology report generation from 3D CT volumes often misses pathology due to a bottleneck in visual representations. The paper aims to identify and bypass this bottleneck to improve clinical efficacy.

Method: Introduce AdaRAG-CT, an adaptive augmentation framework that uses controlled retrieval of supplementary textual information and selective integration during generation to mitigate the visual bottleneck. Demonstrates that naive retrieval can hurt and that 3D CT embeddings are highly concentrated in a few dimensions (e.g., ~2/512).

Result: AdaRAG-CT yields state-of-the-art clinical efficacy on CT-RATE, with Clinical F1 improving from 0.420 (CT-Agent) to 0.480 (+6 points). Ablation studies indicate both retrieval and generation components are essential for the improvement.

Conclusion: The visual representation bottleneck constrains both generation and retrieval; adaptive text augmentation via controlled retrieval can compensate for this bottleneck and enhance clinical report quality.

Abstract: Automated radiology report generation from 3D CT volumes often suffers from incomplete pathology coverage. We provide empirical evidence that this limitation stems from a representational bottleneck: contrastive 3D CT embeddings encode discriminative pathology signals, yet exhibit severe dimensional concentration, with as few as 2 effective dimensions out of 512. Corroborating this, scaling the language model yields no measurable improvement, suggesting that the bottleneck lies in the visual representation rather than the generator. This bottleneck limits both generation and retrieval; naive static retrieval fails to improve clinical efficacy and can even degrade performance. We propose \textbf{AdaRAG-CT}, an adaptive augmentation framework that compensates for this visual bottleneck by introducing supplementary textual information through controlled retrieval and selectively integrating it during generation. On the CT-RATE benchmark, AdaRAG-CT achieves state-of-the-art clinical efficacy, improving Clinical F1 from 0.420 (CT-Agent) to 0.480 (+6 points); ablation studies confirm that both the retrieval and generation components contribute to the improvement. Code is available at https://github.com/renjie-liang/Adaptive-RAG-for-3DCT-Report-Generation.

</details>


### [13] [FEEL (Force-Enhanced Egocentric Learning): A Dataset for Physical Action Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15847)
*Eadom Dessalene,Botao He,Michael Maynord,Yonatan Tussa,Pavan Mantripragada,Yianni Karabati,Nirupam Roy,Yiannis Aloimonos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FEEL is a large-scale dataset pairing force measurements from piezoresistive gloves with egocentric video, enabling scalable data collection (~3M force-synced frames, 45% with hand-object contact) for physical action understanding; achieves state-of-the-art temporal contact segmentation and competitive pixel-level segmentation; improves transfer in action understanding without manual labels on multiple benchmarks through force-based self-supervised pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To treat force as a fundamental primitive for physical action understanding and to address the lack of large-scale, force-measured data that can be collected in natural environments.

Method: Developed custom piezoresistive gloves to capture force synchronized with egocentric video. Collected ~3 million frames in kitchen settings, with 45% frames showing contact. Introduced two tasks: (1) temporal contact segmentation with joint pixel-level contacted object segmentation; (2) action representation learning by predicting force as a self-supervised objective to pretrain video backbones.

Result: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on temporal contact segmentation; competitive pixel-level segmentation without manual contacted object annotations. Demonstrated improved transfer on action understanding tasks without manual labels across EPIC-Kitchens, SomethingSomething-V2, EgoExo4D, and Meccano.

Conclusion: Force is a fundamental driver of physical interaction and a productive supervisory signal. FEEL demonstrates scalable collection of force-labeled egocentric data and its utility for both fine-grained contact understanding and self-supervised action representations, with strong cross-dataset transfer without manual annotations.

Abstract: We introduce FEEL (Force-Enhanced Egocentric Learning), the first large-scale dataset pairing force measurements gathered from custom piezoresistive gloves with egocentric video. Our gloves enable scalable data collection, and FEEL contains approximately 3 million force-synchronized frames of natural unscripted manipulation in kitchen environments, with 45% of frames involving hand-object contact. Because force is the underlying cause that drives physical interaction, it is a critical primitive for physical action understanding. We demonstrate the utility of force for physical action understanding through application of FEEL to two families of tasks: (1) contact understanding, where we jointly perform temporal contact segmentation and pixel-level contacted object segmentation; and, (2) action representation learning, where force prediction serves as a self-supervised pretraining objective for video backbones. We achieve state-of-the-art temporal contact segmentation results and competitive pixel-level segmentation results without any need for manual contacted object segmentation annotations. Furthermore we demonstrate that action representation learning with FEEL improves transfer performance on action understanding tasks without any manual labels over EPIC-Kitchens, SomethingSomething-V2, EgoExo4D and Meccano.

</details>


### [14] [Self-supervised Disentanglement of Disease Effects from Aging in 3D Medical Shapes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15862)
*Jakaria Rabbi,Nilanjan Ray,Dana Cobzas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage framework using unsupervised pseudo-label discovery from implicit SDF shape embeddings, followed by multi-objective disentanglement with age and pseudo-disease labels, achieving near-supervised performance in 3D medical shapes (hippocampus, distal femur) with interpretable synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for interpretable biomarkers and patient stratification when disease and aging have overlapping shape changes and labels are limited; propose unsupervised discovery plus disentanglement.

Method: Stage 1: train an implicit neural model with signed distance functions to learn stable shape embeddings; cluster latent space to derive pseudo disease labels without ground-truth diagnoses. Stage 2: disentangle factors in a compact variational space using pseudo disease labels and ground-truth age labels; enforce separation via multi-objective loss combining covariance and supervised contrastive loss.

Result: Near-supervised performance; improved disentanglement and reconstruction relative to state-of-the-art unsupervised baselines; enables high-fidelity reconstruction, controllable synthesis, and factor-based explainability on ADNI hippocampus and OAI distal femur.

Conclusion: The two-stage framework effectively separates disease- and aging-related shape changes under limited labels, enabling interpretable biomarkers; code available.

Abstract: Disentangling pathological changes from physiological aging in 3D medical shapes is crucial for developing interpretable biomarkers and patient stratification. However, this separation is challenging when diagnosis labels are limited or unavailable, since disease and aging often produce overlapping effects on shape changes, obscuring clinically relevant shape patterns. To address this challenge, we propose a two-stage framework combining unsupervised disease discovery with self-supervised disentanglement of implicit shape representations. In the first stage, we train an implicit neural model with signed distance functions to learn stable shape embeddings. We then apply clustering on the shape latent space, which yields pseudo disease labels without using ground-truth diagnosis during discovery. In the second stage, we disentangle factors in a compact variational space using pseudo disease labels discovered in the first stage and the ground truth age labels available for all subjects. We enforce separation and controllability with a multi-objective disentanglement loss combining covariance and a supervised contrastive loss. On ADNI hippocampus and OAI distal femur shapes, we achieve near-supervised performance, improving disentanglement and reconstruction over state-of-the-art unsupervised baselines, while enabling high-fidelity reconstruction, controllable synthesis, and factor-based explainability. Code and checkpoints are available at https://github.com/anonymous-submission01/medical-shape-disentanglement

</details>


### [15] [EvoIQA - Explaining Image Distortions with Evolved White-Box Logic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15887)
*Ruchika Gupta,Illya Bakurov,Nathan Haut,Wolfgang Banzhaf*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EvoIQA evolves interpretable IQA formulas via genetic programming, combining insights from VSI, VIF, FSIM, HaarPSI to rival deep learning while remaining human-readable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between rigid hand-crafted IQA metrics and opaque deep learning models; deliver interpretable, human-readable equations that reflect structural, chromatic, and information-theoretic degradations.

Method: Symbolic regression using genetic programming with a terminal set derived from VSI, VIF, FSIM, HaarPSI; evolve explicit mathematical formulas mapping image degradations to quality scores; train/evaluate against human preferences.

Result: GP-evolved models align with human visual preferences, outperform traditional metrics, and achieve parity with state-of-the-art deep learning models like DB-CNN.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that interpretability and strong predictive performance are not mutually exclusive; EvoIQA offers transparent, competitive IQA metrics.

Abstract: Traditional Image Quality Assessment (IQA) metrics typically fall into one of two extremes: rigid, hand-crafted mathematical models or "black-box" deep learning architectures that completely lack interpretability. To bridge this gap, we propose EvoIQA, a fully explainable symbolic regression framework based on Genetic Programming that Evolves explicit, human-readable mathematical formulas for image quality assessment (IQA). Utilizing a rich terminal set from the VSI, VIF, FSIM, and HaarPSI metrics, our framework inherently maps structural, chromatic, and information-theoretic degradations into observable mathematical equations. Our results demonstrate that the evolved GP models consistently achieve strong alignment between the predictions and human visual preferences. Furthermore, they not only outperform traditional hand-crafted metrics but also achieve performance parity with complex, state-of-the-art deep learning models like DB-CNN, proving that we no longer have to sacrifice interpretability for state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [16] [Sparse but not Simpler: A Multi-Level Interpretability Analysis of Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15919)
*Siyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sparse networks show a structural sparsity effect but do not reliably improve interpretability; a multi-level IMPACT framework reveals no systematic gains in neuron-level selectivity, SAE interpretability, or attribution faithfulness, despite fewer edges and redistributed computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test the common claim that weight sparsity yields more interpretable models, specifically in Vision Transformers, and to quantify interpretability across multiple levels beyond circuit size.

Method: Evaluate DeiT-III B/16 models pruned with Wanda using IMPACT, a four-level interpretability framework: (1) neurons, (2) layer representations via BatchTopK sparse autoencoders, (3) task circuits via learnable node masking, (4) model-level attribution via transformer attribution with insertion/deletion metrics.

Result: Structural sparsity effects observed: pruned models have ~2.5× fewer edges, but the fraction of active nodes is similar or higher. Pruning redistributes computation rather than creating simpler modules. No systematic improvements in neuron-level selectivity, SAE feature interpretability, or attribution faithfulness.

Conclusion: Structural sparsity alone is insufficient to enhance interpretability in vision models; evaluation frameworks like IMPACT are needed to assess interpretability beyond circuit compactness.

Abstract: Sparse neural networks are often hypothesized to be more interpretable than dense models, motivated by findings that weight sparsity can produce compact circuits in language models. However, it remains unclear whether structural sparsity itself leads to improved semantic interpretability. In this work, we systematically evaluate the relationship between weight sparsity and interpretability in Vision Transformers using DeiT-III B/16 models pruned with Wanda. To assess interpretability comprehensively, we introduce \textbf{IMPACT}, a multi-level framework that evaluates interpretability across four complementary levels: neurons, layer representations, task circuits, and model-level attribution. Layer representations are analyzed using BatchTopK sparse autoencoders, circuits are extracted via learnable node masking, and explanations are evaluated with transformer attribution using insertion and deletion metrics. Our results reveal a clear structural effect but limited interpretability gains. Sparse models produce circuits with approximately $2.5\times$ fewer edges than dense models, yet the fraction of active nodes remains similar or higher, indicating that pruning redistributes computation rather than isolating simpler functional modules. Consistent with this observation, sparse models show no systematic improvements in neuron-level selectivity, SAE feature interpretability, or attribution faithfulness. These findings suggest that structural sparsity alone does not reliably yield more interpretable vision models, highlighting the importance of evaluation frameworks that assess interpretability beyond circuit compactness.

</details>


### [17] [Nodule-Aligned Latent Space Learning with LLM-Driven Multimodal Diffusion for Lung Nodule Progression Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15932)
*James Song,Yifan Wang,Chuan Zhou,Liyue Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NAMD is a diffusion-based framework that generates 1-year follow-up lung nodule CT images conditioned on baseline scans and EHR to predict malignancy; achieves AUROC 0.805 and AUPRC 0.346, approaching performance of real follow-ups (AUROC 0.819, AUPRC 0.393).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early diagnosis of lung cancer is challenging due to biological uncertainty and limited understanding of mechanisms driving nodule progression; a multimodal, generative approach may forecast progression and aid diagnosis.

Method: Nodule-Aligned Multimodal (Latent) Diffusion (NAMD) introduces a nodule-aligned latent space where latent distances map to changes in nodule attributes, and uses an LLM-driven control mechanism to condition the diffusion backbone on patient data. It generates 1-year follow-up CT images from baseline scans and the patient's and nodule's EHR.

Result: Evaluated on the NLST dataset, the method synthesizes follow-up nodule images achieving AUROC 0.805 and AUPRC 0.346 for malignancy prediction, outperforming baseline scans and state-of-the-art synthesis methods, and approaching the performance of real follow-up scans (AUROC 0.819, AUPRC 0.393).

Conclusion: NAMD captures clinically relevant features of lung nodule progression, enabling earlier and more accurate diagnosis.

Abstract: Early diagnosis of lung cancer is challenging due to biological uncertainty and the limited understanding of the biological mechanisms driving nodule progression. To address this, we propose Nodule-Aligned Multimodal (Latent) Diffusion (NAMD), a novel framework that predicts lung nodule progression by generating 1-year follow-up nodule computed tomography images with baseline scans and the patient's and nodule's Electronic Health Record (EHR). NAMD introduces a nodule-aligned latent space, where distances between latents directly correspond to changes in nodule attributes, and utilizes an LLM-driven control mechanism to condition the diffusion backbone on patient data. On the National Lung Screening Trial (NLST) dataset, our method synthesizes follow-up nodule images that achieve an AUROC of 0.805 and an AUPRC of 0.346 for lung nodule malignancy prediction, significantly outperforming both baseline scans and state-of-the-art synthesis methods, while closely approaching the performance of real follow-up scans (AUROC: 0.819, AUPRC: 0.393). These results demonstrate that NAMD captures clinically relevant features of lung nodule progression, facilitating earlier and more accurate diagnosis.

</details>


### [18] [Towards Fair and Robust Volumetric CT Classification via KL-Regularised Group Distributionally Robust Optimisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15941)
*Samuel Johnny,Blessed Guda,Frank Ebeledike,Goodness Obasi,Moise Busogi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A study addressing distribution shift and demographic fairness in two chest CT tasks using Group DRO with KL penalty; Task 1: binary COVID-19 classification from multi-site CT volumes; Task 2: four-class pathology with gender-based fairness constraints. Achieves state-of-the-art results and improved fairness metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical deployment of AI for chest CTs suffers from site distribution shifts and subgroup performance gaps; need robust and fair models across sites and demographics.

Method: MobileViT-XXS slice encoder + two-layer SliceTransformer aggregator for volumetric reasoning; train with KL-regularised Group DRO that adaptively upweights underperforming groups; KL term prevents weight collapse.

Result: Task 1 F1 = 0.835 ( > best challenge by +5.9 ). Task 2 mean per-gender macro F1 = 0.815 ( > best challenge by +11.1 pp; Female Squamous F1 improved by +17.4 over baseline).

Conclusion: The approach offers improved robustness to site shifts and demographic fairness, with stable optimization and competitive challenge performance; applicable to radiology AI tasks with multiple groups.

Abstract: Automated diagnosis from chest computed tomography (CT) scans faces two persistent challenges in clinical deployment: distribution shift across acquisition sites and performance disparity across demographic subgroups. We address both simultaneously across two complementary tasks: binary COVID-19 classification from multi-site CT volumes (Task 1) and four-class lung pathology recognition with gender-based fairness constraints (Task 2). Our framework combines a lightweight MobileViT-XXS slice encoder with a two-layer SliceTransformer aggregator for volumetric reasoning, and trains with a KL-regularised Group Distributionally Robust Optimisation (Group DRO) objective that adaptively upweights underperforming acquisition centres and demographic subgroups. Unlike standard Group DRO, the KL penalty prevents group weight collapse, providing a stable balance between worst-case protection and average performance. For Task 2, we define groups at the granularity of gender class, directly targeting severely underrepresented combinations such as female Squamous cell carcinoma. On Task 1, our best configuration achieves a challenge F1 of 0.835, surpassing the best published challenge entry by +5.9. On Task 2, Group DRO with α = 0.5 achieves a mean per-gender macro F1 of 0.815, outperforming the best challenge entry by +11.1 pp and improving Female Squamous F1 by +17.4 over the Fo- cal Loss baseline.

</details>


### [19] [UMO: Unified In-Context Learning Unlocks Motion Foundation Model Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15975)
*Xiaoyan Cong,Zekun Li,Zhiyang Dou,Hongyu Li,Omid Taheri,Chuan Guo,Abhay Mittal,Sizhe An,Taku Komura,Wojciech Matusik,Michael J. Black,Srinath Sridhar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UMO offers a unified framework to adapt text-to-motion foundation models to diverse downstream tasks via per-frame meta-operations and lightweight temporal fusion, achieving better performance with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unlock the broad potential of single-purpose motion LFMs for a wide range of downstream, in-context motion generation tasks within a single framework, addressing the limitation of task-specific, ad-hoc adaptations.

Method: Introduce three learnable frame-level meta-operation embeddings to encode per-frame intent, plus lightweight temporal fusion to incorporate in-context cues into a pretrained DiT-based motion LFM; fine-tune the model to support diverse tasks treated as compositions of atomic per-frame operations (temporal inpainting, editing, geometric constraints, multi-identity generation).

Result: Empirical evaluation shows UMO consistently outperforms task-specific and training-free baselines across multiple benchmarks, with negligible runtime overhead relative to the base model; code and model will be released.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a simple, general unified formulation for leveraging motion LFMs across diverse tasks, enabling broad in-context adaptation while maintaining efficiency.

Abstract: Large-scale foundation models (LFMs) have recently made impressive progress in text-to-motion generation by learning strong generative priors from massive 3D human motion datasets and paired text descriptions. However, how to effectively and efficiently leverage such single-purpose motion LFMs, i.e., text-to-motion synthesis, in more diverse cross-modal and in-context motion generation downstream tasks remains largely unclear. Prior work typically adapts pretrained generative priors to individual downstream tasks in a task-specific manner. In contrast, our goal is to unlock such priors to support a broad spectrum of downstream motion generation tasks within a single unified framework. To bridge this gap, we present UMO, a simple yet general unified formulation that casts diverse downstream tasks into compositions of atomic per-frame operations, enabling in-context adaptation to unlock the generative priors of pretrained DiT-based motion LFMs. Specifically, UMO introduces three learnable frame-level meta-operation embeddings to specify per-frame intent and employs lightweight temporal fusion to inject in-context cues into the pretrained backbone, with negligible runtime overhead compared to the base model. With this design, UMO finetunes the pretrained model, originally limited to text-to-motion generation, to support diverse previously unsupported tasks, including temporal inpainting, text-guided motion editing, text-serialized geometric constraints, and multi-identity reaction generation. Experiments demonstrate that UMO consistently outperforms task-specific and training-free baselines across a wide range of benchmarks, despite using a single unified model. Code and model will be publicly available. Project Page: https://oliver-cong02.github.io/UMO.github.io/

</details>


### [20] [Mostly Text, Smart Visuals: Asymmetric Text-Visual Pruning for Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16001)
*Sijie Li,Biao Qian,Jungong Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes ATV-Pruning, an asymmetric text–visual weight pruning method for LVLMs that decouples token sensitivities across modalities. Text tokens drive calibration due to higher sensitivity; visual tokens are highly redundant, allowing up to 50% sparsity. The method constructs an adaptive calibration pool (all text tokens + a subset of visual tokens) and uses a layer-adaptive strategy to select important visual tokens, achieving superior pruning performance on multimodal benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LVLM pruning methods apply a unified calibration across modalities, neglecting modality-specific token sensitivities. This leads to suboptimal pruning, particularly underpruning textual pathways and over-pruning visual pathways. The work aims to uncover and leverage the distinct sensitivities of textual and visual tokens to improve pruning efficiency and preserve accuracy.

Method: Introduce Asymmetric Text-Visual Weight Pruning (ATV-Pruning) for LVLMs. Decouple weights of text and visual pathways and construct an adaptive calibration pool comprising all textual tokens and a subset of visual tokens. Develop a layer-adaptive visual token selection mechanism to identify the most informative visual tokens for pruning. The overall importance metric combines information from both modalities to guide weight pruning, leveraging the observed textual sensitivity and visual redundancy.

Result: Extensive experiments on standard multimodal benchmarks show ATV-Pruning outperforms state-of-the-art pruning methods, validating the effectiveness of modality-specific pruning and the proposed calibration strategy.

Conclusion: Textual pathways require careful calibration due to higher sensitivity, while visual pathways exhibit higher redundancy and can tolerate substantial sparsity. ATV-Pruning provides an effective asymmetric pruning framework for LVLMs, improving pruning efficiency and preserving performance across benchmarks.

Abstract: Network pruning is an effective technique for enabling lightweight Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), which primarily incorporates both weights and activations into the importance metric. However, existing efforts typically process calibration data from different modalities in a unified manner, overlooking modality-specific behaviors. This raises a critical challenge: how to address the divergent behaviors of textual and visual tokens for accurate pruning of LVLMs. To this end, we systematically investigate the sensitivity of visual and textual tokens to the pruning operation by decoupling their corresponding weights, revealing that: (i) the textual pathway should be calibrated via text tokens, since it exhibits higher sensitivity than the visual pathway; (ii) the visual pathway exhibits high redundancy, permitting even 50% sparsity. Motivated by these insights, we propose a simple yet effective Asymmetric Text-Visual Weight Pruning method for LVLMs, dubbed ATV-Pruning, which establishes the importance metric for accurate weight pruning by selecting the informative tokens from both textual and visual pathways. Specifically, ATV-Pruning integrates two primary innovations: first, a calibration pool is adaptively constructed by drawing on all textual tokens and a subset of visual tokens; second, we devise a layer-adaptive selection strategy to yield important visual tokens. Finally, extensive experiments across standard multimodal benchmarks verify the superiority of our ATV-Pruning over state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [21] [FlatLands: Generative Floormap Completion From a Single Egocentric View](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16016)
*Subhransu S. Bhattacharjee,Dylan Campbell,Rahul Shome*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlatLands provides a large-scale real indoor dataset and benchmark for single-view BEV floor completion, enabling uncertainty-aware, end-to-end RGB-to-floor-map pipelines for indoor navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between egocentric observations and a complete metric traversability map; support uncertainty-aware indoor mapping; evaluate across out-of-distribution scenarios.

Method: Dataset curation: 270,575 observations, 17,656 scenes from six datasets; aligned observation, visibility, validity, and ground-truth BEV maps. Benchmark design with in-/out-of-distribution protocols. Systematic comparison across training-free, deterministic, ensembles, and stochastic generative models. End-to-end monocular RGB-to-floormap pipeline.

Result: Establishes a rigorous testbed and baseline analyses for uncertainty-aware BEV completion; demonstrates the feasibility and diversity of approaches, though no specific numeric results reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: FlatLands enables robust evaluation and development of single-view BEV floor completion methods, advancing embodied navigation through uncertainty-aware indoor mapping and generative completion.

Abstract: A single egocentric image typically captures only a small portion of the floor, yet a complete metric traversability map of the surroundings would better serve applications such as indoor navigation. We introduce FlatLands, a dataset and benchmark for single-view bird's-eye view (BEV) floor completion. The dataset contains 270,575 observations from 17,656 real metric indoor scenes drawn from six existing datasets, with aligned observation, visibility, validity, and ground-truth BEV maps, and the benchmark includes both in- and out-of-distribution evaluation protocols. We compare training-free approaches, deterministic models, ensembles, and stochastic generative models. Finally, we instantiate the task as an end-to-end monocular RGB-to-floormaps pipeline. FlatLands provides a rigorous testbed for uncertainty-aware indoor mapping and generative completion for embodied navigation.

</details>


### [22] [Speak, Segment, Track, Navigate: An Interactive System for Video-Guided Skull-Base Surgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16024)
*Jecia Z. Y. Mao,Francis X. Creighton,Russell H. Taylor,Manish Sahu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A speech-guided embodied agent framework for video-guided skull base surgery that operates on intraoperative video, enabling natural-language queries and tracking a segmented instrument as a spatial anchor to support downstream perception, registration, and overlays without external trackers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on external optical tracking hardware and enable seamless, real-time computational assistance directly on live surgical video to improve workflow and deployment speed.

Method: A speech-guided embodied agent framework that performs interactive instrument segmentation/labeling on live video, autonomously tracks the segmented instrument as a spatial anchor, and supports downstream tasks including anatomical segmentation, interactive registration of preoperative 3D models, monocular tool pose estimation, and real-time anatomical overlays, all purely from intraoperative video.

Result: Evaluated in video-guided skull base surgery scenarios and benchmarked against a commercial optical tracking system; achieved competitive spatial accuracy while enhancing workflow integration and enabling rapid deployment of video-guided systems.

Conclusion: Speech-guided embodied agents can deliver competitive spatial accuracy and improved workflow integration for video-guided surgical systems without external trackers.

Abstract: We introduce a speech-guided embodied agent framework for video-guided skull base surgery that dynamically executes perception and image-guidance tasks in response to surgeon queries. The proposed system integrates natural language interaction with real-time visual perception directly on live intraoperative video streams, thereby enabling surgeons to request computational assistance without disengaging from operative tasks. Unlike conventional image-guided navigation systems that rely on external optical trackers and additional hardware setup, the framework operates purely on intraoperative video. The system begins with interactive segmentation and labeling of the surgical instrument. The segmented instrument is then used as a spatial anchor that is autonomously tracked in the video stream to support downstream workflows, including anatomical segmentation, interactive registration of preoperative 3D models, monocular video-based estimation of the surgical tool pose, and support image guidance through real-time anatomical overlays.We evaluate the proposed system in video-guided skull base surgery scenarios and benchmark its tracking performance against a commercially available optical tracking system. Results demonstrate that speech-guided embodied agents can achieve competitive spatial accuracy while improving workflow integration and enabling rapid deployment of video-guided surgical systems.

</details>


### [23] [ViT-AdaLA: Adapting Vision Transformers with Linear Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16063)
*Yifan Li,Seunghyun Yoon,Viet Dac Lai,Franck Dernoncourt,Jason Kuen,Yu Kong,Trung Bui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViT-AdaLA proposes a three-stage framework to transfer and adapt prior knowledge from high-performance softmax-based vision foundation models (VFMs) to linear-attention ViTs, reducing quadratic self-attention complexity while preserving accuracy for classification and segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quadratic complexity of self-attention in Vision Transformers limits scalability to longer sequences and higher-resolution inputs. Existing linear-attention methods are typically trained from scratch and do not transfer well to ViTs; linearization-based methods developed for LLM decoders do not translate effectively to ViTs. There is a need to efficiently leverage pretrained VFMs to enable fast, scalable linear-attention ViTs with strong performance.

Method: ViT-AdaLA consists of three stages: (1) attention alignment, where vanilla linear attention is aligned with the original softmax-based attention in each block to approximate softmax behavior; (2) feature alignment, addressing residual approximation errors accumulated across layers by fine-tuning the linearized ViT so its final-layer features align with a frozen softmax VFM teacher; (3) supervised fine-tuning to transfer the adapted prior knowledge to downstream tasks.

Result: Extensive experiments on classification and segmentation demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of ViT-AdaLA when compared to various state-of-the-art linear attention baselines.

Conclusion: ViT-AdaLA enables effective adaptation and transfer of knowledge from high-quality VFMs to linear-attention ViTs, achieving improved performance and generality across tasks while reducing attention complexity.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) based vision foundation models (VFMs) have achieved remarkable performance across diverse vision tasks, but suffer from quadratic complexity that limits scalability to long sequences. Existing linear attention approaches for ViTs are typically trained from scratch, requiring substantial computational resources, while linearization-based methods developed for large language model decoders do not transfer well to ViTs. To address these challenges, we propose ViT-AdaLA, a novel framework for effectively adapting and transferring prior knowledge from VFMs to linear attention ViTs. ViT-AdaLA consists of three stages: attention alignment, feature alignment, and supervised fine-tuning. In the attention alignment stage, we align vanilla linear attention with the original softmax-based attention in each block to approximate the behavior of softmax attention. However, residual approximation errors inevitably accumulate across layers. We mitigate this by fine-tuning the linearized ViT to align its final-layer features with a frozen softmax VFM teacher. Finally, the adapted prior knowledge is transferred to downstream tasks through supervised fine-tuning. Extensive experiments on classification and segmentation tasks demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of ViT-AdaLA over various state-of-the-art linear attention counterpart.

</details>


### [24] [Attribution Upsampling should Redistribute, Not Interpolate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16067)
*Vincenzo Buono,Peyman Sheikholharam Mashhadi,Mahmoud Rahat,Prayag Tiwari,Stefan Byttner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: USU introduces a mass-preserving, semantically aware upsampling for attribution maps, reframing upsampling as ratio-form mass redistribution to preserve attribution mass and relative importance, guided by four axioms; proven to violate by standard interpolation and validated across ImageNet, CIFAR-10, and CUB-200.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current attribution upsampling uses interpolation designed for natural images, causing aliasing, ringing, boundary bleeding and misrepresentation of model reasoning. A principled, model-semantic aligned upsampling is needed to faithfully propagate attribution signals.

Method: Formalize four desiderata (axioms) for faithful upsampling. Prove interpolation violates three axioms. Show that any redistribution operator must be ratio-form; identify a unique solution within that family and instantiate Universal Semantic-Aware Upsampling (USU). Implement USU and validate with controlled experiments and cross-dataset evaluation.

Result: USU provably preserves attribution mass and relative importance ordering. Demonstrates faithfulness improvements and semantically coherent explanations. Outperforms standard interpolation baselines on ImageNet, CIFAR-10, and CUB-200.

Conclusion: Axiomatically grounded upsampling method; USU mitigates misalignment between attribution and model reasoning by mass redistribution, yielding faithful, coherent explanations across multiple datasets and architectures; represents a unique solution within the ratio-form family.

Abstract: Attribution methods in explainable AI rely on upsampling techniques that were designed for natural images, not saliency maps. Standard bilinear and bicubic interpolation systematically corrupts attribution signals through aliasing, ringing, and boundary bleeding, producing spurious high-importance regions that misrepresent model reasoning. We identify that the core issue is treating attribution upsampling as an interpolation problem that operates in isolation from the model's reasoning, rather than a mass redistribution problem where model-derived semantic boundaries must govern how importance flows. We present Universal Semantic-Aware Upsampling (USU), a principled method that reformulates upsampling through ratio-form mass redistribution operators, provably preserving attribution mass and relative importance ordering. Extending the axiomatic tradition of feature attribution to upsampling, we formalize four desiderata for faithful upsampling and prove that interpolation structurally violates three of them. These same three force any redistribution operator into a ratio form; the fourth selects the unique potential within this family, yielding USU. Controlled experiments on models with known attribution priors verify USU's formal guarantees; evaluation across ImageNet, CIFAR-10, and CUB-200 confirms consistent faithfulness improvements and qualitatively superior, semantically coherent explanations.

</details>


### [25] [Volumetrically Consistent Implicit Atlas Learning via Neural Diffeomorphic Flow for Placenta MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16078)
*Athena Taymourtash,S. Mazdak Abulnaga,Esra Abaci Turk,P. Ellen Grant,Polina Golland*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A volumetric, penalized implicit registration framework aligns multiple placentas by jointly reconstructing SDFs and a diffeomorphic flow to a shared canonical template, enabling voxel-wise analysis in a topologically coherent space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current implicit registration methods emphasize surface correspondences via near-zero level-set supervision, leaving interior deformations ill-posed. A volumetric approach is needed for accurate group-level analyses and interior mapping.

Method: The method couples unified SDF reconstruction with a neural diffeomorphic flow to learn a population-derived implicit placenta template. Volumetric regularization (Jacobian-determinant penalties and biharmonic penalties) discourages folding and promotes globally coherent deformations, yielding a single canonical space for individual placentas which can be mapped voxel-wise.

Result: On in-vivo placenta MRI, the approach improves geometric fidelity and volumetric alignment compared with surface-based implicit baselines, producing anatomically interpretable and topologically consistent flattenings suitable for group analysis.

Conclusion: Volumetric implicit registration with SDF reconstruction and diffeomorphic flow enables dense, topology-preserving alignment to a canonical template, facilitating voxel-wise analysis and interpretable flattening in medical imaging.

Abstract: Establishing dense volumetric correspondences across anatomical shapes is essential for group-level analysis but remains challenging for implicit neural representations. Most existing implicit registration methods rely on supervision near the zero-level set and thus capture only surface correspondences, leaving interior deformations under-constrained. We introduce a volumetrically consistent implicit model that couples reconstruction of signed distance functions (SDFs) with neural diffeomorphic flow to learn a shared canonical template of the placenta. Volumetric regularization, including Jacobian-determinant and biharmonic penalties, suppresses local folding and promotes globally coherent deformations. In the motivating application to placenta MRI, our formulation jointly reconstructs individual placentas, aligns them to a population-derived implicit template, and enables voxel-wise intensity mapping in a unified canonical space. Experiments on in-vivo placenta MRI scans demonstrate improved geometric fidelity and volumetric alignment over surface-based implicit baseline methods, yielding anatomically interpretable and topologically consistent flattening suitable for group analysis.

</details>


### [26] [Structured prototype regularization for synthetic-to-real driving scene parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16083)
*Jiahe Fan,Xiao Ma,Sergey Vityazev,George Giakos,Shaolong Shu,Rui Fan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel unsupervised domain adaptation framework for driving scene parsing that preserves semantic structure via class-specific prototypes, entropy-based pseudo-label filtering, and pixel-level attention, achieving state-of-the-art results on synthetic-to-real benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthetic data enables cheap, scalable annotation but suffers from a domain gap when deployed on real scenes. Existing unsupervised domain adaptation methods mostly align global features and overlook semantic relationships, limiting generalization. Preserving semantic structure in the feature space can enhance cross-domain robustness.

Method: Introduce semantic-structure regularization using class-specific prototypes to enforce inter-class separation and intra-class compactness. Use entropy-based pseudo-label filtering to improve label reliability and incorporate a pixel-level attention mechanism to refine alignment.

Result: Extensive experiments on representative benchmarks show consistent improvements over recent state-of-the-art methods, validating the benefit of preserving semantic structure for synthetic-to-real driving scene parsing.

Conclusion: Maintaining semantic structure in the feature space is beneficial for robust synthetic-to-real adaptation in driving scene parsing, as the proposed framework yields more discriminative and coherent semantic feature clusters and better cross-domain generalization.

Abstract: Driving scene parsing is critical for autonomous vehicles to operate reliably in complex real-world traffic environments. To reduce the reliance on costly pixel-level annotations, synthetic datasets with automatically generated labels have become a popular alternative.
