Building on SemTok, we construct a masked autoregressive generation framework, which yields notable improvements in downstream image generation tasks. Experiments confirm the effectiveness of our semantic 1D tokenization. Our code will be open-sourced.

</details>


### [78] [Unpaired Cross-Domain Calibration of DMSP to VIIRS Nighttime Light Data Based on CUT Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16385)
*Zhan Tong,ChenXu Zhou,Fei Tang,Yiming Tu,Tianyu Qin,Kaihao Fang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A CUT-based cross-sensor translation calibrates DMSP-OLS to VIIRS-like data to extend nighttime light time series, achieving high agreement with VIIRS and socio-economic indicators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable reliable long-term analysis of urbanization using nighttime light data by reconciling differences between DMSP-OLS and VIIRS sensors and correcting DMSP defects.

Method: Train a Contrastive Unpaired Translation (CUT) network with multilayer patch-wise contrastive learning on 2012-2013 overlapping data to map DMSP-OLS to VIIRS-like representations; apply to 1992-2013 DMSP imagery to produce enhanced VIIRS-style rasters.

Result: Generated VIIRS-like data exhibit strong consistency with actual VIIRS observations (R^2 > 0.87) and with socioeconomic indicators, enabling improved cross-sensor data fusion and extended NTL time series.

Conclusion: Cross-sensor calibration via CUT effectively reconciles DMSP and VIIRS data and mitigates DMSP defects, facilitating long-term urbanization analyses.

Abstract: Defense Meteorological Satellite Program (DMSP-OLS) and Suomi National Polar-orbiting Partnership (SNPP-VIIRS) nighttime light (NTL) data are vital for monitoring urbanization, yet sensor incompatibilities hinder long-term analysis. This study proposes a cross-sensor calibration method using Contrastive Unpaired Translation (CUT) network to transform DMSP data into VIIRS-like format, correcting DMSP defects. The method employs multilayer patch-wise contrastive learning to maximize mutual information between corresponding patches, preserving content consistency while learning cross-domain similarity. Utilizing 2012-2013 overlapping data for training, the network processes 1992-2013 DMSP imagery to generate enhanced VIIRS-style raster data. Validation results demonstrate that generated VIIRS-like data exhibits high consistency with actual VIIRS observations (R-squared greater than 0.87) and socioeconomic indicators. This approach effectively resolves cross-sensor data fusion issues and calibrates DMSP defects, providing reliable attempt for extended NTL time-series.

</details>


### [79] [DermaFlux: Synthetic Skin Lesion Generation with Rectified Flows for Enhanced Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16392)
*Stathis Galanakis,Alexandros Koliousis,Stefanos Zafeiriou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A rectified flow-based text-to-image approach, DermaFlux, uses LoRA fine-tuning on Flux.1 to generate clinically grounded skin lesion images from dermatology-inspired captions and augments small datasets, boosting skin lesion classifier performance versus diffusion-based synthetic images; achieves strong results with limited real data (78.04% accuracy, AUC 0.859).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limited, imbalanced, and poorly annotated dermatology datasets that hinder generalization; improve data augmentation with clinically meaningful, diverse images generated from text.

Method: Rectified flow-based text-to-image generation (DermaFlux) built on Flux.1; parameter-efficient LoRA fine-tuning; synthetic captions from Llama 3.2 based on dermatological criteria (asymmetry, border irregularity, color variation); dataset assembled from public clinical image collections; ViT classifier trained with 2,500 real images + 4,375 synthetic images; comparison to diffusion-based synthesis.

Result: DermaFlux yields diverse, clinically meaningful images; improves binary classification performance by up to 6% when augmenting small real-world datasets; up to 9% improvement when using DermaFlux-generated images over diffusion-based ones; with limited data, an ImageNet-pretrained ViT achieves 78.04% accuracy and AUC 0.859.

Conclusion: DermaFlux demonstrates effective, data-efficient dermatology data augmentation via rectified flow-based generation guided by dermatology-informed captions, outperforming diffusion-based synthesis and enabling strong performance with limited real data.

Abstract: Despite recent advances in deep generative modeling, skin lesion classification systems remain constrained by the limited availability of large, diverse, and well-annotated clinical datasets, resulting in class imbalance between benign and malignant lesions and consequently reduced generalization performance. We introduce DermaFlux, a rectified flow-based text-to-image generative framework that synthesizes clinically grounded skin lesion images from natural language descriptions of dermatological attributes. Built upon Flux.1, DermaFlux is fine-tuned using parameter-efficient Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on a large curated collection of publicly available clinical image datasets. We construct image-text pairs using synthetic textual captions generated by Llama 3.2, following established dermatological criteria including lesion asymmetry, border irregularity, and color variation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DermaFlux generates diverse and clinically meaningful dermatology images that improve binary classification performance by up to 6% when augmenting small real-world datasets, and by up to 9% when classifiers are trained on DermaFlux-generated synthetic images rather than diffusion-based synthetic images. Our ImageNet-pretrained ViT fine-tuned with only 2,500 real images and 4,375 DermaFlux-generated samples achieves 78.04% binary classification accuracy and an AUC of 0.859, surpassing the next best dermatology model by 8%.

</details>


### [80] [Near-light Photometric Stereo with Symmetric Lights](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16404)
*Lilika Makabe,Heng Guo,Hiroaki Santo,Fumio Okura,Yasuyuki Matsushita*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Linear, closed-form near-light photometric stereo using symmetric light source pairs for initialization-free surface normal and depth estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Eliminate non-convex optimization pitfalls and calibration requirements in near-light PS by exploiting symmetry in light arrangement; aim for robust, initialization-free depth/normal recovery.

Method: Arrange multiple sets of symmetric nearby light source pairs; derive a closed-form solution for surface normals and depth from photometric data; works without initialization and requires only symmetry about an arbitrary point, with uncalibrated spatial offset.

Result: Experiments show the method achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art calibrated near-light photometric stereo, while reducing the need for depth initialization and precise light calibration.

Conclusion: Symmetric light-source design enables a simple, robust, calibration-free pipeline for joint normal and depth estimation in near-light PS, with competitive performance to calibrated methods.

Abstract: This paper describes a linear solution method for near-light photometric stereo by exploiting symmetric light source arrangements. Unlike conventional non-convex optimization approaches, by arranging multiple sets of symmetric nearby light source pairs, our method derives a closed-form solution for surface normal and depth without requiring initialization. In addition, our method works as long as the light sources are symmetrically distributed about an arbitrary point even when the entire spatial offset is uncalibrated. Experiments showcase the accuracy of shape recovery accuracy of our method, achieving comparable results to the state-of-the-art calibrated near-light photometric stereo method while significantly reducing requirements of careful depth initialization and light calibration.

</details>


### [81] [HGP-Mamba: Integrating Histology and Generated Protein Features for Mamba-based Multimodal Survival Risk Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16421)
*Jing Dai,Chen Wu,Ming Wu,Qibin Zhang,Zexi Wu,Jingdong Zhang,Hongming Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes HGP-Mamba, a multimodal framework that mixes histology with generated protein features from WSIs for survival risk prediction; introduces PFE, LiAM, and GiEM to capture local and global cross-modal interactions; achieves state-of-the-art results with efficient computation on four cancer datasets and is open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scarcity and cost of protein expression profiling by deriving protein-level embeddings directly from histology images, enabling data-efficient multimodal fusion for survival risk prediction.

Method: A Mamba-based architecture comprising: (1) Protein Feature Extractor (PFE) that uses pretrained foundation models to generate protein embeddings from Whole Slide Images; (2) LiAM for fine-grained local interaction modeling between modalities; (3) GiEM for global slide-level fusion to capture holistic cross-modal dependencies.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four public cancer datasets shows state-of-the-art survival risk prediction performance with higher computational efficiency than existing methods.

Conclusion: HGP-Mamba demonstrates effective integration of generated molecular features with histology for accurate and efficient cancer survival prediction, and provides an accessible open-source implementation.

Abstract: Recent advances in multimodal learning have significantly improved cancer survival risk prediction. However, the joint prognostic potential of protein markers and histopathology images remains underexplored, largely due to the high cost and limited availability of protein expression profiling. To address this challenge, we propose HGP-Mamba, a Mamba-based multimodal framework that efficiently integrates histological with generated protein features for survival risk prediction. Specifically, we introduce a protein feature extractor (PFE) that leverages pretrained foundation models to derive high-throughput protein embeddings directly from Whole Slide Images (WSIs), enabling data-efficient incorporation of molecular information. Together with histology embeddings that capture morphological patterns, we further introduce the Local Interaction-aware Mamba (LiAM) for fine-grained feature interaction and the Global Interaction-enhanced Mamba (GiEM) to promote holistic modality fusion at the slide level, thus capture complex cross-modal dependencies. Experiments on four public cancer datasets demonstrate that HGP-Mamba achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining superior computational efficiency compared with existing methods. Our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Daijing-ai/HGP-Mamba.git.

</details>


### [82] [SF-Mamba: Rethinking State Space Model for Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16423)
*Masakazu Yoshimura,Teruaki Hayashi,Yuki Hoshino,Wei-Yao Wang,Takeshi Ohashi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SF-Mamba introduces auxiliary patch swapping and batch folding with periodic state reset to enable bidirectional information flow in a unidirectional scan, boosting throughput and accuracy for vision tasks compared to ViTs and prior Mamba variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mamba offers computational efficiency but limits non-causal interactions between image patches due to unidirectional scanning. Prior multi-scan strategies add complexity and data rearrangement and can be slow at short token lengths. A truly efficient, scalable vision encoder is sought.

Method: Two key proposals: (1) auxiliary patch swapping to encode bidirectional information flow under an unidirectional scan, (2) batch folding with periodic state reset to enable advanced GPU parallelism and higher throughput.

Result: Extensive experiments on image classification, object detection, and both instance and semantic segmentation show SF-Mamba significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines and improves throughput across model sizes.

Conclusion: SF-Mamba provides a more efficient vision encoder by addressing non-causal interaction and data rearrangement inefficiencies, delivering improved accuracy and throughput; source code will be released after publication.

Abstract: The realm of Mamba for vision has been advanced in recent years to strike for the alternatives of Vision Transformers (ViTs) that suffer from the quadratic complexity. While the recurrent scanning mechanism of Mamba offers computational efficiency, it inherently limits non-causal interactions between image patches. Prior works have attempted to address this limitation through various multi-scan strategies; however, these approaches suffer from inefficiencies due to suboptimal scan designs and frequent data rearrangement. Moreover, Mamba exhibits relatively slow computational speed under short token lengths, commonly used in visual tasks. In pursuit of a truly efficient vision encoder, we rethink the scan operation for vision and the computational efficiency of Mamba. To this end, we propose SF-Mamba, a novel visual Mamba with two key proposals: auxiliary patch swapping for encoding bidirectional information flow under an unidirectional scan and batch folding with periodic state reset for advanced GPU parallelism. Extensive experiments on image classification, object detection, and instance and semantic segmentation consistently demonstrate that our proposed SF-Mamba significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines while improving throughput across different model sizes. We will release the source code after publication.

</details>


### [83] [3D Fourier-based Global Feature Extraction for Hyperspectral Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16426)
*Muhammad Ahmad*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hybrid GFNet (HGFNet) fuses localized 3D convolutions with frequency-domain global filtering to enable efficient, robust hyperspectral image classification; it introduces three frequency transforms (Spectral FFT, Spatial FFT, and Spatial-Spatial FFT) and Adaptive Focal Loss to handle class imbalance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer-based HSIC models suffer from quadratic self-attention complexity and scalability; 2D FFT-based approaches ignore crucial inter-band spectral dependencies. There is a need for scalable, joint spatial-spectral modeling that slows noise and handles imbalanced data.

Method: 3D convolutional layers capture local spatial-spectral features; GFNet-style blocks perform global filtering in frequency domains; three tailored frequency transforms model spectral, spatial, and joint spectral-spatial information; Adaptive Focal Loss dynamically adjusts class-wise weight and focusing to mitigate imbalance.

Result: The abstract asserts improved spatial-spectral representation learning, robustness to noise, and computational efficiency, aided by the three frequency transforms and GFNet-style global filtering, as well as better handling of class imbalance via Adaptive Focal Loss; no explicit quantitative results are provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: HGFNet presents a scalable, hybrid approach that combines localized 3D spatial-spectral feature extraction with frequency-domain global filtering and an adaptive loss to advance HSIC, addressing both representation quality and class imbalance challenges.

Abstract: Hyperspectral image classification (HSIC) has been significantly advanced by deep learning methods that exploit rich spatial-spectral correlations. However, existing approaches still face fundamental limitations: transformer-based models suffer from poor scalability due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention, while recent Fourier transform-based methods typically rely on 2D spatial FFTs and largely ignore critical inter-band spectral dependencies inherent to hyperspectral data. To address these challenges, we propose Hybrid GFNet (HGFNet), a novel architecture that integrates localized 3D convolutional feature extraction with frequency-domain global filtering via GFNet-style blocks for efficient and robust spatial-spectral representation learning. HGFNet introduces three complementary frequency transforms tailored to hyperspectral imagery: Spectral Fourier Transform (a 1D FFT along the spectral axis), Spatial Fourier Transform (a 2D FFT over spatial dimensions), and Spatial-Spatial Fourier Transform (a 3D FFT jointly over spectral and spatial dimensions), enabling comprehensive and high-dimensional frequency modeling. The 3D convolutional layers capture fine-grained local spatial-spectral structures, while the Fourier-based global filtering modules efficiently model long-range dependencies and suppress noise. To further mitigate the severe class imbalance commonly observed in HSIC, HGFNet incorporates an Adaptive Focal Loss (AFL) that dynamically adjusts class-wise focusing and weighting, improving discrimination for underrepresented classes.

</details>


### [84] [Cross-modal learning for plankton recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16427)
*Joona Kareinen,Veikka Immonen,Tuomas Eerola,Lumi Haraguchi,Lasse Lensu,Kaisa Kraft,Sanna Suikkanen,Heikki Kälviäinen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Self-supervised cross-modal learning for multimodal plankton recognition using image and optical profile data with binary pairing labels, achieving high accuracy with few labeled examples via a k-NN classifier on a small gallery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce labeling burden in plankton recognition while leveraging abundant unlabeled multimodal data (images and optical measurements like scatter/fluorescence) to improve performance beyond image-only methods.

Method: Train encoders for image and profile modalities with a binary supervisory signal indicating whether a paired image and profile originate from the same particle, following a contrastive framework akin to CLIP. Use a small labeled gallery of known species and a k-NN classifier for final recognition, yielding a multimodal model.

Result: High recognition accuracy with a minimal amount of labeled images; outperforms a purely image-based self-supervised baseline; demonstrates effective utilization of both image and profile data.

Conclusion: Proposes an effective multimodal self-supervised approach for plankton recognition that exploits abundant unlabeled data and limited labeled specimens, enabling cross-modal information fusion without extensive labeling; code released.

Abstract: This paper considers self-supervised cross-modal coordination as a strategy enabling utilization of multiple modalities and large volumes of unlabeled plankton data to build models for plankton recognition. Automated imaging instruments facilitate the continuous collection of plankton image data on a large scale. Current methods for automatic plankton image recognition rely primarily on supervised approaches, which require labeled training sets that are labor-intensive to collect. On the other hand, some modern plankton imaging instruments complement image information with optical measurement data, such as scatter and fluorescence profiles, which currently are not widely utilized in plankton recognition. In this work, we explore the possibility of using such measurement data to guide the learning process without requiring manual labeling. Inspired by the concepts behind Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training, we train encoders for both modalities using only binary supervisory information indicating whether a given image and profile originate from the same particle or from different particles. For plankton recognition, we employ a small labeled gallery of known plankton species combined with a $k$-NN classifier. This approach yields a recognition model that is inherently multimodal, i.e., capable of utilizing information extracted from both image and profile data. We demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high recognition accuracy while requiring only a minimal number of labeled images. Furthermore, we show that the approach outperforms an image-only self-supervised baseline. Code available at https://github.com/Jookare/cross-modal-plankton.

</details>


### [85] [IRIS: A Real-World Benchmark for Inverse Recovery and Identification of Physical Dynamic Systems from Monocular Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16432)
*Rasul Khanbayov,Mohamed Rayan Barhdadi,Erchin Serpedin,Hasan Kurban*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IRIS: a high-fidelity real-world benchmark for unsupervised physical parameter estimation and governing-equation identification in video.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No standard benchmark exists for unsupervised physical parameter estimation from video; current datasets are synthetic, non-overlapping, or limited to single-body systems, hindering principled evaluation and equation discovery.

Method: IRIS provides 220 real-world 4K60 videos of single- and multi-body dynamics with independently measured ground-truth parameters and uncertainties; controlled lab conditions; paired with governing equations; standardized evaluation protocol across parameter accuracy, identifiability, extrapolation, robustness, and equation selection; multiple baselines including multi-step physics loss and four equation-identification strategies (VLM temporal reasoning, describe-then-classify prompting, CNN-based classification, path-based labeling); dataset, annotations, toolkit, and baselines released.

Result: Establishes reference performance across IRIS scenarios, reveals systematic failure modes, and enables principled evaluation of unsupervised parameter estimation and governing-equation discovery; provides publicly available data and tools.

Conclusion: IRIS enables rigorous benchmarking for unsupervised physical parameter estimation and governing-equation identification from video and is expected to catalyze future research by exposing limitations and providing a standardized evaluation framework.

Abstract: Unsupervised physical parameter estimation from video lacks a common benchmark: existing methods evaluate on non-overlapping synthetic data, the sole real-world dataset is restricted to single-body systems, and no established protocol addresses governing-equation identification. This work introduces IRIS, a high-fidelity benchmark comprising 220 real-world videos captured at 4K resolution and 60\,fps, spanning both single- and multi-body dynamics with independently measured ground-truth parameters and uncertainty estimates. Each dynamical system is recorded under controlled laboratory conditions and paired with its governing equations, enabling principled evaluation. A standardized evaluation protocol is defined encompassing parameter accuracy, identifiability, extrapolation, robustness, and governing-equation selection. Multiple baselines are evaluated, including a multi-step physics loss formulation and four complementary equation-identification strategies (VLM temporal reasoning, describe-then-classify prompting, CNN-based classification, and path-based labelling), establishing reference performance across all IRIS scenarios and exposing systematic failure modes that motivate future research. The dataset, annotations, evaluation toolkit, and all baseline implementations are publicly released.

</details>


### [86] [CD-FKD: Cross-Domain Feature Knowledge Distillation for Robust Single-Domain Generalization in Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16439)
*Junseok Lee,Sungho Shin,Seongju Lee,Kyoobin Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes CD-FKD to improve single-domain generalization for object detection by cross-domain feature distillation; the student learns from diversified/corrupted inputs while mimicking a teacher trained on clean source data, using both global and instance-wise distillation to enhance robustness, achieving state-of-the-art results on unseen domains while preserving source performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shifts (weather, lighting, scene conditions) degrade object detection generalization. A single-source trained model often fails on unseen domains. The method aims to transfer robust, object-centric representations from a source-domain teacher to a diversified student.

Method: Cross-Domain Feature Knowledge Distillation (CD-FKD): teacher is trained on original source data; student is trained with downscaled and corrupted inputs. Distillation occurs at both global features and instance-wise features, guiding the student to mimic the teacher's representations and improve object-centric feature extraction under corruption.

Result: Extensive experiments on challenging scenes show CD-FKD outperforms state-of-the-art methods in target-domain generalization and maintains strong source-domain performance.

Conclusion: CD-FKD offers a robust framework for single-domain generalization in object detection, enhancing resilience to domain shifts and with practical applicability to automotive and surveillance scenarios.

Abstract: Single-domain generalization is essential for object detection, particularly when training models on a single source domain and evaluating them on unseen target domains. Domain shifts, such as changes in weather, lighting, or scene conditions, pose significant challenges to the generalization ability of existing models. To address this, we propose Cross-Domain Feature Knowledge Distillation (CD-FKD), which enhances the generalization capability of the student network by leveraging both global and instance-wise feature distillation. The proposed method uses diversified data through downscaling and corruption to train the student network, whereas the teacher network receives the original source domain data. The student network mimics the features of the teacher through both global and instance-wise distillation, enabling it to extract object-centric features effectively, even for objects that are difficult to detect owing to corruption. Extensive experiments on challenging scenes demonstrate that CD-FKD outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both target domain generalization and source domain performance, validating its effectiveness in improving object detection robustness to domain shifts. This approach is valuable in real-world applications, like autonomous driving and surveillance, where robust object detection in diverse environments is crucial.

</details>


### [87] [Fast-HaMeR: Boosting Hand Mesh Reconstruction using Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16444)
*Hunain Ahmed Jillani,Ahmed Tawfik Aboukhadra,Ahmed Elhayek,Jameel Malik,Nadia Robertini,Didier Stricker*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lightweight backbones combined with knowledge distillation accelerate HaMeR 3D hand reconstruction (~1.5x faster) with minimal accuracy loss (~0.4 mm) by using backbones at ~35% size; output-level distillation helps most, feature-level benefits higher-capacity students; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable real-time 3D hand reconstruction on resource-constrained devices (VR/AR, HCI, robotics, healthcare) by reducing model size and computation while maintaining accuracy.

Method: Replace HaMeR's ViT-H backbone with lightweight backbones (MobileNet, MobileViT, ConvNeXt, ResNet) and apply three knowledge-distillation strategies: output-level, feature-level, and a hybrid of both; evaluate impact on speed and reconstruction accuracy.

Result: Lightweight backbones (about 35% of original size) yield ~1.5x faster inference with only ~0.4 mm accuracy loss. Output-level distillation notably improves student performance; feature-level distillation is more effective for higher-capacity students; a practical path for deploying on low-power devices.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of combining lightweight architectures with knowledge distillation to achieve real-time, accurate 3D hand reconstruction on resource-constrained hardware; provides a practical pipeline and public code for broader application.

Abstract: Fast and accurate 3D hand reconstruction is essential for real-time applications in VR/AR, human-computer interaction, robotics, and healthcare. Most state-of-the-art methods rely on heavy models, limiting their use on resource-constrained devices like headsets, smartphones, and embedded systems. In this paper, we investigate how the use of lightweight neural networks, combined with Knowledge Distillation, can accelerate complex 3D hand reconstruction models by making them faster and lighter, while maintaining comparable reconstruction accuracy. While our approach is suited for various hand reconstruction frameworks, we focus primarily on boosting the HaMeR model, currently the leading method in terms of reconstruction accuracy. We replace its original ViT-H backbone with lighter alternatives, including MobileNet, MobileViT, ConvNeXt, and ResNet, and evaluate three knowledge distillation strategies: output-level, feature-level, and a hybrid of both. Our experiments show that using lightweight backbones that are only 35% the size of the original achieves 1.5x faster inference speed while preserving similar performance quality with only a minimal accuracy difference of 0.4mm. More specifically, we show how output-level distillation notably improves student performance, while feature-level distillation proves more effective for higher-capacity students. Overall, the findings pave the way for efficient real-world applications on low-power devices. The code and models are publicly available under https://github.com/hunainahmedj/Fast-HaMeR.

</details>


### [88] [Unified Removal of Raindrops and Reflections: A New Benchmark and A Novel Pipeline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16446)
*Xingyu Liu,Zewei He,Yu Chen,Chunyu Zhu,Zixuan Chen,Xing Luo,Zhe-Ming Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Defines UR^3 (unified removal of raindrops and reflections), introduces RainDrop and ReFlection (RDRF) real-shot dataset, and presents DiffUR^3, a diffusion-based framework that removes both degradations. Claims state-of-the-art results on RDRF and challenging real-world images; code and data to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practical problem: simultaneous raindrop and reflection degradation on glass or windshields reduces visibility. Existing methods address raindrops or reflections separately and fail under composite degradation; need a unified solution and a real-world benchmark.

Method: Introduce UR^3 task and the RDRF dataset. Propose DiffUR^3, a diffusion-based framework leveraging generative priors to remove both raindrops and reflections. Likely includes task-specific design choices and training on real-shot data.

Result: DiffUR^3 achieves state-of-the-art performance on the RDRF benchmark and on challenging in-the-wild images according to the abstract.

Conclusion: UR^3 provides a unified framework and dataset for single-image restoration under composite degradation; DiffUR^3 demonstrates effectiveness and generalization; dataset and code to be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: When capturing images through glass surfaces or windshields on rainy days, raindrops and reflections frequently co-occur to significantly reduce the visibility of captured images. This practical problem lacks attention and needs to be resolved urgently. Prior de-raindrop, de-reflection, and all-in-one models have failed to address this composite degradation. To this end, we first formally define the unified removal of raindrops and reflections (UR$^3$) task for the first time and construct a real-shot dataset, namely RainDrop and ReFlection (RDRF), which provides a new benchmark with substantial, high-quality, diverse image pairs. Then, we propose a novel diffusion-based framework (i.e., DiffUR$^3$) with several target designs to address this challenging task. By leveraging the powerful generative prior, DiffUR$^3$ successfully removes both types of degradations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on our benchmark and on challenging in-the-wild images. The RDRF dataset and the codes will be made public upon acceptance.

</details>


### [89] [ProgressiveAvatars: Progressive Animatable 3D Gaussian Avatars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16447)
*Kaiwen Song,Jinkai Cui,Juyong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ProgressiveAvatars: a progressive avatar representation using a hierarchically grown 3D Gaussian field with adaptive subdivision on a template mesh for progressive loading/rendering under variable bandwidth and compute resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time XR/telepresence face challenges from fluctuating network and compute resources; need scalable, animatable, progressive fidelity supporting varying expressions and head motion.

Method: Construct a hierarchy of 3D Gaussians defined in face-local coordinates. Grow the hierarchy via adaptive implicit subdivision on a template mesh. Use screen-space signals to trigger detail expansion. Apply importance ranking to guide loading and rendering order. Enable incremental loading so new Gaussians arrive without discarding existing content, enabling smooth quality improvements across bandwidths.

Result: Conceptual framework and prototype approach demonstrating progressive delivery and rendering under fluctuating bandwidth and resources; supports incremental updates while preserving prior content.

Conclusion: ProgressiveAvatars enable robust progressive delivery and rendering for XR/telepresence under variable network bandwidth and compute/memory resources, with animatable, region-focused detail suitable for real-time applications.

Abstract: In practical real-time XR and telepresence applications, network and computing resources fluctuate frequently. Therefore, a progressive 3D representation is needed. To this end, we propose ProgressiveAvatars, a progressive avatar representation built on a hierarchy of 3D Gaussians grown by adaptive implicit subdivision on a template mesh. 3D Gaussians are defined in face-local coordinates to remain animatable under varying expressions and head motion across multiple detail levels. The hierarchy expands when screen-space signals indicate a lack of detail, allocating resources to important areas. Leveraging importance ranking, ProgressiveAvatars supports incremental loading and rendering, adding new Gaussians as they arrive while preserving previous content, thus achieving smooth quality improvements across varying bandwidths. ProgressiveAvatars enables progressive delivery and progressive rendering under fluctuating network bandwidth and varying compute and memory resources.

</details>


### [90] [TinyGLASS: Real-Time Self-Supervised In-Sensor Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16451)
*Pietro Bonazzi,Rafael Sutter,Luigi Capogrosso,Mischa Buob,Michele Magno*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TinyGLASS is a lightweight edge-adapted version of GLASS for in-sensor anomaly detection on the Sony IMX500, achieving real-time performance with substantial parameter compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial anomaly detection often lacks labeled fault samples and standard GLASS-like self-supervised models are too compute-heavy for edge deployment; there is a need for efficient, in-sensor inference.

Method: Replace WideResNet-50 with a compact ResNet-18, introduce static graph tracing and INT8 quantization via Sony Model Compression Toolkit, evaluate on MVTec-AD and a new MMS dataset for cross-device robustness.

Result: 8.7x parameter compression; 94.2% image-level AUROC on MVTec-AD; 20 FPS on IMX500 within 8 MB; 4.0 mJ per inference; 470 GMAC/J; robust under moderate training data contamination.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a practical, energy-efficient, real-time anomaly detector suitable for edge sensing with competitive accuracy and robustness, enabling deployment on resource-constrained devices.

Abstract: Anomaly detection plays a key role in industrial quality control, where defects must be identified despite the scarcity of labeled faulty samples. Recent self-supervised approaches, such as GLASS, learn normal visual patterns using only defect-free data and have shown strong performance on industrial benchmarks. However, their computational requirements limit deployment on resource-constrained edge platforms.
  This work introduces TinyGLASS, a lightweight adaptation of the GLASS framework designed for real-time in-sensor anomaly detection on the Sony IMX500 intelligent vision sensor. The proposed architecture replaces the original WideResNet-50 backbone with a compact ResNet-18 and introduces deployment-oriented modifications that enable static graph tracing and INT8 quantization using Sony's Model Compression Toolkit.
  In addition to evaluating performance on the MVTec-AD benchmark, we investigate robustness to contaminated training data and introduce a custom industrial dataset, named MMS Dataset, for cross-device evaluation. Experimental results show that TinyGLASS achieves 8.7x parameter compression while maintaining competitive detection performance, reaching 94.2% image-level AUROC on MVTec-AD and operating at 20 FPS within the 8 MB memory constraints of the IMX500 platform.
  System profiling demonstrates low power consumption (4.0 mJ per inference), real-time end-to-end latency (20 FPS), and high energy efficiency (470 GMAC/J). Furthermore, the model maintains stable performance under moderate levels of training data contamination.

</details>


### [91] [Evo-Retriever: LLM-Guided Curriculum Evolution with Viewpoint-Pathway Collaboration for Multimodal Document Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16455)
*Weiqing Li,Jinyue Guo,Yaqi Wang,Haiyang Xiao,Yuewei Zhang,Guohua Liu,Hao Henry Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Evo-Retriever introduces an LLM-guided curriculum evolution for cross-modal retrieval, combining multi-view image alignment, bidirectional contrastive learning, and a model-state aware meta-controller to adapt training dynamically; achieves state-of-the-art on ViDoRe V2 and MMEB (VisDoc).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world documents are heterogeneous and unstructured, causing instability in cross-modal embeddings. Static training strategies and limited data impede adaptation to a model's evolving dynamics, leading to retrieval confusion.

Method: 1) Multi-view image alignment with multi-scale and multi-directional perspectives to improve fine-grained matching. 2) Bidirectional contrastive learning to generate hard queries and create complementary learning paths for visual and textual disambiguation. 3) An LLM-based meta-controller that uses a model-state summary to adaptively adjust the training curriculum, enabling progressive evolution through expert knowledge.

Result: On ViDoRe V2 and MMEB (VisDoc), Evo-Retriever achieves state-of-the-art performance with nDCG@5 scores of 65.2% and 77.1%.

Conclusion: The proposed Viewpoint-Pathway collaboration and LLM-guided curriculum evolution effectively enhance cross-modal alignment and adaptation, yielding robust retrieval performance in heterogeneous, unstructured document settings.

Abstract: Visual-language models (VLMs) excel at data mappings, but real-world document heterogeneity and unstructuredness disrupt the consistency of cross-modal embeddings. Recent late-interaction methods enhance image-text alignment through multi-vector representations, yet traditional training with limited samples and static strategies cannot adapt to the model's dynamic evolution, causing cross-modal retrieval confusion. To overcome this, we introduce Evo-Retriever, a retrieval framework featuring an LLM-guided curriculum evolution built upon a novel Viewpoint-Pathway collaboration. First, we employ multi-view image alignment to enhance fine-grained matching via multi-scale and multi-directional perspectives. Then, a bidirectional contrastive learning strategy generates "hard queries" and establishes complementary learning paths for visual and textual disambiguation to rebalance supervision. Finally, the model-state summary from the above collaboration is fed into an LLM meta-controller, which adaptively adjusts the training curriculum using expert knowledge to promote the model's evolution. On ViDoRe V2 and MMEB (VisDoc), Evo-Retriever achieves state-of-the-art performance, with nDCG@5 scores of 65.2% and 77.1%.

</details>


### [92] [GAP-MLLM: Geometry-Aligned Pre-training for Activating 3D Spatial Perception in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16461)
*Jiaxin Zhang,Junjun Jiang,Haijie Li,Youyu Chen,Kui Jiang,Dave Zhenyu Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GAP-MLLM introduces Geometry-Aligned Pre-training for Multimodal LLMs, using a visual-prompted task to predict sparse pointmaps and semantic labels and a multi-level progressive fusion with token-level gating, to align geometric priors with semantic reasoning and improve 3D perception tasks (grounding, dense captioning, video object detection).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs excel at semantic reasoning but struggle with 3D spatial perception when limited to RGB inputs. The gap is not solely due to insufficient geometric priors but due to a misaligned training paradigm where text-dominant fine-tuning fails to activate geometric representations. Existing fusion approaches are naive and task-specific without geometry-focused supervision.

Method: (1) A visual-prompted joint task that forces MLLMs to predict sparse pointmaps together with semantic labels to induce geometric awareness. (2) A multi-level progressive fusion module with a token-level gating mechanism to adaptively fuse geometric priors without suppressing semantic reasoning.

Result: Extensive experiments show GAP-MLLM significantly improves geometric feature fusion and yields consistent performance gains across 3D visual grounding, 3D dense captioning, and 3D video object detection tasks.

Conclusion: Geometry-aligned pre-training that activates structural perception before downstream adaptation can close the gap between RGB-based methods and explicit 3D data, outperforming naive fusion strategies across multiple 3D vision-language tasks.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) demonstrate exceptional semantic reasoning but struggle with 3D spatial perception when restricted to pure RGB inputs. Despite leveraging implicit geometric priors from 3D reconstruction models, image-based methods still exhibit a notable performance gap compared to methods using explicit 3D data. We argue that this gap does not arise from insufficient geometric priors, but from a misalignment in the training paradigm: text-dominated fine-tuning fails to activate geometric representations within MLLMs. Existing approaches typically resort to naive feature concatenation and optimize directly for downstream tasks without geometry-specific supervision, leading to suboptimal structural utilization. To address this limitation, we propose GAP-MLLM, a Geometry-Aligned Pre-training paradigm that explicitly activates structural perception before downstream adaptation. Specifically, we introduce a visual-prompted joint task that compels the MLLMs to predict sparse pointmaps alongside semantic labels, thereby enforcing geometric awareness. Furthermore, we design a multi-level progressive fusion module with a token-level gating mechanism, enabling adaptive integration of geometric priors without suppressing semantic reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GAP-MLLM significantly enhances geometric feature fusion and consistently enhances performance across 3D visual grounding, 3D dense captioning, and 3D video object detection tasks.

</details>


### [93] [DST-Net: A Dual-Stream Transformer with Illumination-Independent Feature Guidance and Multi-Scale Spatial Convolution for Low-Light Image Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16482)
*Yicui Shi,Yuhan Chen,Xiangfei Huang,Zhenguo Wang,Wenxuan Yu,Ying Fang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dual-stream transformer network (DST-Net) for low-light image enhancement using illumination-agnostic priors and multi-scale spatial fusion to preserve texture, with state-of-the-art results on LOL and cross-dataset generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing low-light methods often erase intrinsic signal priors and struggle to preserve fine textures; there is a need to leverage priors and multi-scale features to improve both visual quality and generalization.

Method: Propose DST-Net with (1) a feature extraction module combining Difference of Gaussians, LAB color space, and VGG-16 to obtain illumination-agnostic priors; (2) a dual-stream interaction architecture using cross-modal attention to iteratively rectify degraded signal representations via differentiable curve estimation; (3) a Multi-Scale Spatial Fusion Block (MSFB) with pseudo-3D and 3D gradient convolutions to recover high-frequency edges and capture inter-channel correlations.

Result: Extensive evaluations and ablations show improved subjective and objective performance; achieves PSNR 25.64 dB on the LOL dataset and demonstrates robust cross-scene generalization on the LSRW dataset.

Conclusion: DST-Net effectively leverages priors and multi-scale spatial features to enhance low-light images, achieving superior visual quality, strong quantitative performance, and cross-dataset robustness.

Abstract: Low-light image enhancement aims to restore the visibility of images captured by visual sensors in dim environments by addressing their inherent signal degradations, such as luminance attenuation and structural corruption. Although numerous algorithms attempt to improve image quality, existing methods often cause a severe loss of intrinsic signal priors. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Dual-Stream Transformer Network (DST-Net) based on illumination-agnostic signal prior guidance and multi-scale spatial convolutions. First, to address the loss of critical signal features under low-light conditions, we design a feature extraction module. This module integrates Difference of Gaussians (DoG), LAB color space transformations, and VGG-16 for texture extraction, utilizing decoupled illumination-agnostic features as signal priors to continuously guide the enhancement process. Second, we construct a dual-stream interaction architecture. By employing a cross-modal attention mechanism, the network leverages the extracted priors to dynamically rectify the deteriorated signal representation of the enhanced image, ultimately achieving iterative enhancement through differentiable curve estimation. Furthermore, to overcome the inability of existing methods to preserve fine structures and textures, we propose a Multi-Scale Spatial Fusion Block (MSFB) featuring pseudo-3D and 3D gradient operator convolutions. This module integrates explicit gradient operators to recover high-frequency edges while capturing inter-channel spatial correlations via multi-scale spatial convolutions. Extensive evaluations and ablation studies demonstrate that DST-Net achieves superior performance in subjective visual quality and objective metrics. Specifically, our method achieves a PSNR of 25.64 dB on the LOL dataset. Subsequent validation on the LSRW dataset further confirms its robust cross-scene generalization.

</details>


### [94] [Unlearning for One-Step Generative Models via Unbalanced Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16489)
*Hyundo Choi,Junhyeong An,Jinseong Park,Jaewoong Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UOT-Unlearn achieves class unlearning for one-step generative models by formulating unlearning as Unbalanced Optimal Transport (UOT) between the target class and remaining classes, balancing forget cost with an f-divergence constraint to preserve fidelity; it redistributes probability mass of the forgotten class, outperforming baselines on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-256 for unlearning and retention metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: One-step flow-map generative models enable direct noise-to-data synthesis but lack safe unlearning mechanisms. Existing diffusion-based unlearning is incompatible due to multi-step denoising. There is a need for a principled, plug-and-play unlearning framework tailored to one-step models to ensure safety while maintaining quality.

Method: Formulate unlearning as an optimization based on Unbalanced Optimal Transport (UOT) that trades off a forget cost against an f-divergence penalty with relaxed marginal constraints. The forgotten class's probability mass is smoothly redistributed to remaining classes, rather than collapsing into low-quality or noisy samples. The approach is plug-and-play for one-step models.

Result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-256 shows superior unlearning success (PUL) and retention quality (u-FID) compared to baselines, indicating effective forgetting with preserved generation fidelity.

Conclusion: UOT-Unlearn provides a principled, practical unlearning framework for one-step generative models, enabling safe removal of a target class by mass redistribution across remaining classes. The results suggest broad applicability and improved safety/quality trade-offs over existing methods.

Abstract: Recent advances in one-step generative frameworks, such as flow map models, have significantly improved the efficiency of image generation by learning direct noise-to-data mappings in a single forward pass. However, machine unlearning for ensuring the safety of these powerful generators remains entirely unexplored. Existing diffusion unlearning methods are inherently incompatible with these one-step models, as they rely on a multi-step iterative denoising process. In this work, we propose UOT-Unlearn, a novel plug-and-play class unlearning framework for one-step generative models based on the Unbalanced Optimal Transport (UOT). Our method formulates unlearning as a principled trade-off between a forget cost, which suppresses the target class, and an $f$-divergence penalty, which preserves overall generation fidelity via relaxed marginal constraints. By leveraging UOT, our method enables the probability mass of the forgotten class to be smoothly redistributed to the remaining classes, rather than collapsing into low-quality or noise-like samples. Experimental results on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-256 demonstrate that our framework achieves superior unlearning success (PUL) and retention quality (u-FID), significantly outperforming baselines.

</details>


### [95] [VIEW2SPACE: Studying Multi-View Visual Reasoning from Sparse Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16506)
*Fucai Ke,Zhixi Cai,Boying Li,Long Chen,Beibei Lin,Weiqing Wang,Pari Delir Haghighi,Gholamreza Haffari,Hamid Rezatofighi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VIEW2SPACE provides a scalable, disjoint-split benchmark for sparse multi-view reasoning using high-fidelity simulated 3D scenes, revealing that current vision-language and spatial models struggle with multi-view reasoning; a Grounded Chain-of-Thought with Visual Evidence method improves moderate-difficulty tasks and generalizes across datasets, though deep compositional reasoning across sparse views remains a key challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in sparse multi-view reasoning where real-world data are scarce and annotations are hard to obtain, by generating diverse, high-fidelity 3D scenes with precise per-view metadata to enable scalable, transferable benchmarking and analysis of reasoning under limited views.

Method: 1) Create a physically grounded simulation engine to generate large-scale, multimodal data with per-view metadata; 2) Construct VIEW2SPACE, a multi-dimensional benchmark for sparse multi-view reasoning with a disjoint training split covering millions of grounded QA pairs; 3) Evaluate state-of-the-art vision-language and spatial models on the benchmark; 4) Propose Grounded Chain-of-Thought with Visual Evidence to improve reasoning with visible evidence; 5) Perform difficulty-aware scaling analyses across model size, data scale, reasoning depth, and visibility constraints; 6) Assess cross-dataset generalization to real-world data.

Result: State-of-the-art models perform only marginally above random on sparse multi-view reasoning tasks. Grounded Chain-of-Thought with Visual Evidence substantially improves performance at moderate difficulty and generalizes to real-world data, outperforming existing approaches in cross-dataset evaluation. Scaling analyses suggest advantages for geometric perception with sufficient visibility, while deep compositional reasoning across sparse views remains challenging.

Conclusion: Sparse multi-view reasoning is largely unsolved by current models. The VIEW2SPACE benchmark exposes the gap, and while guided reasoning frameworks like Grounded CoT with Visual Evidence offer gains, fundamental challenges in compositional reasoning across sparse views persist, indicating the need for further advances in perception, reasoning, and data efficiency.

Abstract: Multi-view visual reasoning is essential for intelligent systems that must understand complex environments from sparse and discrete viewpoints, yet existing research has largely focused on single-image or temporally dense video settings. In real-world scenarios, reasoning across views requires integrating partial observations without explicit guidance, while collecting large-scale multi-view data with accurate geometric and semantic annotations remains challenging. To address this gap, we leverage physically grounded simulation to construct diverse, high-fidelity 3D scenes with precise per-view metadata, enabling scalable data generation that remains transferable to real-world settings. Based on this engine, we introduce VIEW2SPACE, a multi-dimensional benchmark for sparse multi-view reasoning, together with a scalable, disjoint training split supporting millions of grounded question-answer pairs. Using this benchmark, a comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art vision-language and spatial models reveals that multi-view reasoning remains largely unsolved, with most models performing only marginally above random guessing. We further investigate whether training can bridge this gap. Our proposed Grounded Chain-of-Thought with Visual Evidence substantially improves performance under moderate difficulty, and generalizes to real-world data, outperforming existing approaches in cross-dataset evaluation. We further conduct difficulty-aware scaling analyses across model size, data scale, reasoning depth, and visibility constraints, indicating that while geometric perception can benefit from scaling under sufficient visibility, deep compositional reasoning across sparse views remains a fundamental challenge.

</details>


### [96] [An approximate graph elicits detonation lattice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16524)
*Vansh Sharma,Venkat Raman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Graph-theoretic, training-free segmentation for 3D detonation lattices; achieves 2% error on synthetic data and 17% cell-axis deviation on 3D simulations; robust across geometries but struggles with highly complex patterns; valuable groundwork for triple-point collision studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detonation cell patterns are difficult to extract with manual methods or 2D edge detection; there is a need for automated, scalable 3D segmentation to quantify cellular morphology and dynamics in detonation research.

Method: A segmentation model using a graph-based, training-free workflow to extract detonation lattices from 3D pressure traces; validated on generated data and 3D simulations; computes cellular metrics via graph constructs and analyzes statistics and joint probability densities.

Result: Prediction error of 2% on generated data; 3D simulations show oblong cells aligned with the propagation axis with 17% deviation; larger dispersion in cell volumes consistent with cubic amplification of linear variability; overall robustness and geometric generalizability of the framework.

Conclusion: The graph-based formulation provides a practical, generalizable tool for detonation analysis and a solid basis for future extensions, including triple-point collision studies, though segmentation remains challenging for highly complex cellular patterns.

Abstract: This study presents a novel algorithm based on graph theory for the precise segmentation and measurement of detonation cells from 3D pressure traces, termed detonation lattices, addressing the limitations of manual and primitive 2D edge detection methods prevalent in the field. Using a segmentation model, the proposed training-free algorithm is designed to accurately extract cellular patterns, a longstanding challenge in detonations research. First, the efficacy of segmentation on generated data is shown with a prediction error 2%. Next, 3D simulation data is used to establish performance of the graph-based workflow. The results of statistics and joint probability densities show oblong cells aligned with the wave propagation axis with 17% deviation, whereas larger dispersion in volume reflects cubic amplification of linear variability. Although the framework is robust, it remains challenging to reliably segment and quantify highly complex cellular patterns. However, the graph-based formulation generalizes across diverse cellular geometries, positioning it as a practical tool for detonation analysis and a strong foundation for future extensions in triple-point collision studies.

</details>


### [97] [Rethinking Pose Refinement in 3D Gaussian Splatting under Pose Prior and Geometric Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16538)
*Mangyu Kong,Jaewon Lee,Seongwon Lee,Euntai Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Monte Carlo pose sampling combined with Fisher Information-based PnP to account for pose prior and geometric uncertainty in 3D Gaussian Splatted relocalization, improving robustness without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting-based pose refinement is highly sensitive to initial pose and geometry; unreliability comes from deterministic pose priors and imperfect 3DGS reconstructions that propagate into PnP, causing reprojection distortions and unstable optimization.

Method: Introduce a relocalization framework that samples multiple pose hypotheses via Monte Carlo methods and performs PnP optimization guided by Fisher Information to account for pose and geometric uncertainty, without retraining or extra supervision. Uses 3DGS rendering as the scene representation.

Result: Across indoor and outdoor benchmarks, the approach consistently improves localization accuracy and substantially enhances stability under pose and depth noise compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Accounting for both pose and geometric uncertainty within 3DGS-based relocalization yields more robust pose estimates without additional supervision, broadening the applicability of 3DGS for visual localization.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has recently emerged as a powerful scene representation and is increasingly used for visual localization and pose refinement. However, despite its high-quality differentiable rendering, the robustness of 3DGS-based pose refinement remains highly sensitive to both the initial camera pose and the reconstructed geometry. In this work, we take a closer look at these limitations and identify two major sources of uncertainty: (i) pose prior uncertainty, which often arises from regression or retrieval models that output a single deterministic estimate, and (ii) geometric uncertainty, caused by imperfections in the 3DGS reconstruction that propagate errors into PnP solvers. Such uncertainties can distort reprojection geometry and destabilize optimization, even when the rendered appearance still looks plausible. To address these uncertainties, we introduce a relocalization framework that combines Monte Carlo pose sampling with Fisher Information-based PnP optimization. Our method explicitly accounts for both pose and geometric uncertainty and requires no retraining or additional supervision. Across diverse indoor and outdoor benchmarks, our approach consistently improves localization accuracy and significantly increases stability under pose and depth noise.

</details>


### [98] [Bridging the Simulation-to-Reality Gap in Electron Microscope Calibration via VAE-EM Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16549)
*Jilles S. van Hulst,W. P. M. H.,Heemels,Duarte J. Antunes*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A VAE-EM pipeline calibrates STEM by learning latent representations from simulated images and jointly estimating calibration parameters with a mapping to latent space, achieving faster, more accurate calibration while addressing the sim-to-real gap and identifiability through optical symmetry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibrating Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopes is challenging due to high-dimensional, noisy images and the need to tune many parameters; existing methods extract scalar metrics and struggle with sim-to-real gap.

Method: Train variational autoencoders on simulated STEM images to compress information into low-dimensional latent representations; jointly estimate the calibration-to-latent mapping and the calibration parameters via an expectation–maximization framework; exploit optical symmetry to ensure global identifiability; validate on real STEM data, showing speed and accuracy gains.

Result: The approach yields faster and more consistent calibration than existing methods, with about a 2× reduction in estimation error and requiring fewer observations.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the potential of VAEs for information compression in imaging-based inverse problems; the VAE–EM framework mitigates simulation-to-reality gaps and enables unique solutions even when mappings are non-injective, with applicability beyond microscopy.

Abstract: Electron microscopy has enabled many scientific breakthroughs across multiple fields. A key challenge is the tuning of microscope parameters based on images to overcome optical aberrations that deteriorate image quality. This calibration problem is challenging due to the high-dimensional and noisy nature of the diagnostic images, and the fact that optimal parameters cannot be identified from a single image. We tackle the calibration problem for Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopes (STEM) by employing variational autoencoders (VAEs), trained on simulated data, to learn low-dimensional representations of images, whereas most existing methods extract only scalar values. We then simultaneously estimate the model that maps calibration parameters to encoded representations and the optimal calibration parameters using an expectation maximization (EM) approach. This joint estimation explicitly addresses the simulation-to-reality gap inherent in data-driven methods that train on simulated data from a digital twin. We leverage the known symmetry property of the optical system to establish global identifiability of the joint estimation problem, ensuring that a unique optimum exists. We demonstrate that our approach is substantially faster and more consistent than existing methods on a real STEM, achieving a 2x reduction in estimation error while requiring fewer observations. This represents a notable advance in automated STEM calibration and demonstrates the potential of VAEs for information compression in images. Beyond microscopy, the VAE-EM framework applies to inverse problems where simulated training data introduces a reality gap and where non-injective mappings would otherwise prevent unique solutions.

</details>


### [99] [CompDiff: Hierarchical Compositional Diffusion for Fair and Zero-Shot Intersectional Medical Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16551)
*Mahmoud Ibrahim,Bart Elen,Chang Sun,Gokhan Ertaylan,Michel Dumontier*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CompDiff introduces a hierarchical compositional diffusion framework to address the imbalanced generator problem in medical image synthesis by decomposing demographic conditioning with a dedicated Hierarchical Conditioned Network, enabling cross-group parameter sharing and zero-shot intersectional generalization; it achieves improved image quality (FID), subgroup equity (ES-FID), and downstream AUROC with reduced bias on chest X-ray (MIMIC-CXR) and fundus (FairGenMed) datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the imbalanced generator problem where generative models trained on skewed demographic data produce lower-quality images for rare subgroups and unseen intersections, potentially propagating bias in synthetic data and downstream models.

Method: Introduce Hierarchical Conditioner Network (HCN) that decomposes demographic conditioning into a demographic token plus CLIP embeddings, integrated via cross-attention in a diffusion model. This representation-level factorization enables parameter sharing across subgroups and compositional generalization to rare/unseen intersections. Compare CompDiff to standard fine-tuning and FairDiffusion on chest X-ray (MIMIC-CXR) and fundus (FairGenMed), using FID, ES-FID, and zero-shot intersectional generalization (intersections not seen during training). Assess downstream classifiers trained on synthetic data for AUROC and bias.

Result: CompDiff achieves improved image quality and fairness metrics: FID 64.3 vs 75.1 for baseline; better ES-FID and up to 21% FID improvement on held-out intersections. Downstream classifiers trained on CompDiff-generated data show higher AUROC and reduced demographic bias, indicating practical benefits beyond image synthesis quality.

Conclusion: Architectural design of demographic conditioning at the representation level is crucial for fair medical image generation. The HCN-based hierarchical conditioning supports compositional generalization to rare or unseen demographic intersections and improves downstream task performance; code is available.

Abstract: Generative models are increasingly used to augment medical imaging datasets for fairer AI. Yet a key assumption often goes unexamined: that generators themselves produce equally high-quality images across demographic groups. Models trained on imbalanced data can inherit these imbalances, yielding degraded synthesis quality for rare subgroups and struggling with demographic intersections absent from training. We refer to this as the imbalanced generator problem. Existing remedies such as loss reweighting operate at the optimization level and provide limited benefit when training signal is scarce or absent for certain combinations. We propose CompDiff, a hierarchical compositional diffusion framework that addresses this problem at the representation level. A dedicated Hierarchical Conditioner Network (HCN) decomposes demographic conditioning, producing a demographic token concatenated with CLIP embeddings as cross-attention context. This structured factorization encourages parameter sharing across subgroups and supports compositional generalization to rare or unseen demographic intersections. Experiments on chest X-rays (MIMIC-CXR) and fundus images (FairGenMed) show that CompDiff compares favorably against both standard fine-tuning and FairDiffusion across image quality (FID: 64.3 vs. 75.1), subgroup equity (ES-FID), and zero-shot intersectional generalization (up to 21% FID improvement on held-out intersections). Downstream classifiers trained on CompDiff-generated data also show improved AUROC and reduced demographic bias, suggesting that architectural design of demographic conditioning is an important and underexplored factor in fair medical image generation. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/CompDiff-6FE6.

</details>


### [100] [Segmentation-Based Attention Entropy: Detecting and Mitigating Object Hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16558)
*Jiale Song,Jiaxin Luo,Xue-song Tang,Kuangrong Hao,Mingbo Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAE uses segmentation-guided attention entropy to quantify visual attention uncertainty at the object level, enabling an inference-time attention adjustment and reliability scoring to reduce object hallucinations in LVLMs without extra training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Object hallucinations in LVLMs are not solely caused by language priors; visual attention patterns can mislead the model, so quantifying visual attention uncertainty at the object level can improve reliability.

Method: Compute attention entropy in a semantic segmentation space; derive an SAE-based reliability score; propose SAE-guided attention adjustment at inference to mitigate hallucinations; test on benchmarks and real robot scenarios; claim zero additional training cost.

Result: SAE substantially reduces object hallucinations; improves trustworthy perception and decision-making; works without extra training; validated on public benchmarks and embodied scenarios.

Conclusion: Segmentation-based attention entropy is effective for detecting and mitigating object hallucinations in LVLMs and can be integrated at inference to enhance reliability in multimodal perception tasks.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) achieve strong performance on many multimodal tasks, but object hallucinations severely undermine their reliability. Most existing studies focus on the text modality, attributing hallucinations to overly strong language priors and insufficient visual grounding. In contrast, we observe that abnormal attention patterns within the visual modality can also give rise to hallucinated objects. Building on this observation, we propose Segmentation-based Attention Entropy (SAE), which leverages semantic segmentation to quantify visual attention uncertainty in an object-level semantic space. Based on SAE, we further design a reliability score for hallucination detection and an SAE-guided attention adjustment method that modifies visual attention at inference time to mitigate hallucinations. We evaluate our approach on public benchmarks and in real embodied multimodal scenarios with quadruped robots. Experimental results show that SAE substantially reduces object hallucinations without any additional training cost, thereby enabling more trustworthy LVLM-driven perception and decision-making.

</details>


### [101] [Understanding Cell Fate Decisions with Temporal Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16562)
*Florian Bürger,Martim Dias Gomes,Adrián E. Granada,Noémie Moreau,Katarzyna Bozek*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Transformer-based deep learning model predicts cancer cell fate from raw long-term live-cell video sequences under chemotherapy with high accuracy, plus a comprehensive explainability framework revealing temporal and morphological cues; predictions possible up to 10 hours before outcomes; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand non-genetic determinants of cell fate in cancer, where genetically identical cells diverge under treatment; leveraging raw imaging data to avoid predefined features and to gain interpretable insights into decision-making processes.

Method: Train a Transformer model on raw video sequences (no predefined morphological/molecular features) to classify cell fate. Develop an explainability framework using attention and masking to interpret temporal and morphological cues. Evaluate on live-cell recordings under chemotherapeutic treatment; perform temporal analysis of predictive information and assess role of morphology and p53 signaling.

Result: Achieves balanced accuracy 0.94 and F1-score 0.93 on video-based predictions. Predictions remain reliable up to 10 hours before the cellular event. Temporal analysis shows distinct predictive information distributions for mitotic vs apoptotic sequences. Morphology and p53 signaling contribute to outcomes. Attention/MASKing experiments indicate the predictive signal is not solely in the final frames. Code available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: Attention-based temporal models enable accurate cell fate prediction from raw video data and provide biologically interpretable insights into non-genetic determinants, including temporal dynamics and signaling influences like p53. The work supports using end-to-end video models for understanding cellular decision-making and offers a reproducible framework.

Abstract: Understanding non-genetic determinants of cell fate is critical for developing and improving cancer therapies, as genetically identical cells can exhibit divergent outcomes under the same treatment conditions. In this work, we present a deep learning approach for cell fate prediction from raw long-term live-cell recordings of cancer cell populations under chemotherapeutic treatment. Our Transformer model is trained to predict cell fate directly from raw image sequences, without relying on predefined morphological or molecular features. Beyond classification, we introduce a comprehensive explainability framework for interpreting the temporal and morphological cues guiding the model's predictions. We demonstrate that prediction of cell outcomes is possible based on the video only, our model achieves balanced accuracy of 0.94 and an F1-score of 0.93. Attention and masking experiments further indicate that the signal predictive of the cell fate is not uniquely located in the final frames of a cell trajectory, as reliable predictions are possible up to 10 h before the event. Our analysis reveals distinct temporal distribution of predictive information in the mitotic and apoptotic sequences, as well as the role of cell morphology and p53 signaling in determining cell outcomes. Together, these findings demonstrate that attention-based temporal models enable accurate cell fate prediction while providing biologically interpretable insights into non-genetic determinants of cellular decision-making. The code is available at https://github.com/bozeklab/Cell-Fate-Prediction.

</details>


### [102] [VideoMatGen: PBR Materials through Joint Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16566)
*Jon Hasselgren,Zheng Zeng,Milos Hasan,Jacob Munkberg*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A geometry- and text-conditioned video diffusion transformer for unified, multi-modal material generation for 3D shapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To streamline creation of physically plausible materials by integrating geometry and natural-language prompts, jointly modeling multiple material properties, and enabling compact latent representations for efficient, token-efficient generation.

Method: A video diffusion transformer conditioned on input geometry and text, jointly modeling base color, roughness, metallicity, and height map. Introduces a custom variational auto-encoder that encodes multiple material modalities into a compact latent space to enable joint generation of modalities without increasing token count.

Result: Pipeline yields high-quality materials for 3D shapes from text prompts, compatible with common content creation tools.

Conclusion: Shows feasibility of unified, multi-property material generation for 3D shapes, with an efficient latent representation and tool compatibility.

Abstract: We present a method for generating physically-based materials for 3D shapes based on a video diffusion transformer architecture. Our method is conditioned on input geometry and a text description, and jointly models multiple material properties (base color, roughness, metallicity, height map) to form physically plausible materials. We further introduce a custom variational auto-encoder which encodes multiple material modalities into a compact latent space, which enables joint generation of multiple modalities without increasing the number of tokens. Our pipeline generates high-quality materials for 3D shapes given a text prompt, compatible with common content creation tools.

</details>


### [103] [Face2Scene: Using Facial Degradation as an Oracle for Diffusion-Based Scene Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16570)
*Amirhossein Kazerouni,Maitreya Suin,Tristan Aumentado-Armstrong,Sina Honari,Amanpreet Walia,Iqbal Mohomed,Konstantinos G. Derpanis,Babak Taati,Alex Levinshtein*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Face2Scene uses a face-based degradation cue derived from a Ref-FR output to guide a diffusion model for full-scene restoration, addressing limitations of face-only and full-scene approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current face restoration methods neglect non-facial regions, while full-scene restorers ignore degradation cues, leading to inconsistent results. A unified approach leveraging reliable facial restoration as an oracle could improve full-image restoration.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) apply reference-based face restoration (Ref-FR) to recover facial details; (2) extract a face-derived degradation code from the restored-face pair, transform it into multi-scale degradation-aware tokens, and condition a diffusion model to restore the entire scene in one step (body and background).

Result: Experiments indicate that Face2Scene achieves superior effectiveness compared with state-of-the-art methods, across extensive evaluations.

Conclusion: A face-guided degradation-aware diffusion framework can produce coherent, high-quality full-scene restorations by using facial restoration as a perceptual oracle for degradation estimation.

Abstract: Recent advances in image restoration have enabled high-fidelity recovery of faces from degraded inputs using reference-based face restoration models (Ref-FR). However, such methods focus solely on facial regions, neglecting degradation across the full scene, including body and background, which limits practical usability. Meanwhile, full-scene restorers often ignore degradation cues entirely, leading to underdetermined predictions and visual artifacts. In this work, we propose Face2Scene, a two-stage restoration framework that leverages the face as a perceptual oracle to estimate degradation and guide the restoration of the entire image. Given a degraded image and one or more identity references, we first apply a Ref-FR model to reconstruct high-quality facial details. From the restored-degraded face pair, we extract a face-derived degradation code that captures degradation attributes (e.g., noise, blur, compression), which is then transformed into multi-scale degradation-aware tokens. These tokens condition a diffusion model to restore the full scene in a single step, including the body and background. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior effectiveness of the proposed method compared to state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [104] [REFORGE: Multi-modal Attacks Reveal Vulnerable Concept Unlearning in Image Generation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16576)
*Yong Zou,Haoran Li,Fanxiao Li,Shenyang Wei,Yunyun Dong,Li Tang,Wei Zhou,Renyang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: REFORGE is a black-box red-teaming framework that tests Image Generation Model Unlearning (IGMU) robustness against adversarial image prompts. It uses stroke-based initializations and a cross-attention-guided masking strategy to perturb concept-relevant regions, achieving higher attack success, better semantic alignment, and greater efficiency than baselines; code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
