Motivation: Image Generation Models pose risks by enabling content creation, and IGMU aims to unlearn harmful concepts without full retraining. However, robustness to black-box, multi-modal adversarial attacks on IGMU remains underexplored, motivating a dedicated red-teaming framework.

Method: REFORGE initializes stroke-based images and learns perturbations via a cross-attention-guided masking strategy that concentrates noise in regions relevant to target concepts. It operates in a black-box setting to evaluate IGMU robustness across representative unlearning tasks and defenses, comparing against baselines.

Result: Empirical results show REFORGE substantially increases attack success rates, yields better semantic alignment with target concepts, and improves efficiency compared with competing methods, thereby exposing persistent vulnerabilities in current IGMU approaches.

Conclusion: Current IGMU methods remain vulnerable to multi-modal, black-box adversarial prompts. Robustness-aware unlearning is needed, and REFORGE provides a practical evaluation framework and baseline for strengthening IGMU against such threats; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Recent progress in image generation models (IGMs) enables high-fidelity content creation but also amplifies risks, including the reproduction of copyrighted content and the generation of offensive content. Image Generation Model Unlearning (IGMU) mitigates these risks by removing harmful concepts without full retraining. Despite growing attention, the robustness under adversarial inputs, particularly image-side threats in black-box settings, remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we present REFORGE, a black-box red-teaming framework that evaluates IGMU robustness via adversarial image prompts. REFORGE initializes stroke-based images and optimizes perturbations with a cross-attention-guided masking strategy that allocates noise to concept-relevant regions, balancing attack efficacy and visual fidelity. Extensive experiments across representative unlearning tasks and defenses demonstrate that REFORGE significantly improves attack success rate while achieving stronger semantic alignment and higher efficiency than involved baselines. These results expose persistent vulnerabilities in current IGMU methods and highlight the need for robustness-aware unlearning against multi-modal adversarial attacks. Our code is at: https://github.com/Imfatnoily/REFORGE.

</details>


### [105] [On the Transfer of Collinearity to Computer Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16592)
*Frederik Beuth,Danny Kowerko*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes and evaluates a collinearity-inspired principle for computer vision, showing selective gains in defect detection and recognition across industrial and nanotech tasks, with limited benefit on ImageNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether leveraging human visual collinearity can improve CV tasks and to identify domains where it is beneficial, particularly industrial or structured scenes.

Method: Developed a prototype model implementing collinearity, tested across four use cases combining with deep learning, saliency models, and as a feature detector; experiments include wafer fault detection, nanotech defect recognition, occlusions, and ImageNet evaluation.

Result: Notable gains: wafer faults 1.24x improvement (error 6.5%→5.26%); nanotech defects 3.2x improvement (error 21.65%→6.64%); occlusions favorable; ImageNet not benefited.

Conclusion: Collinearity appears useful for CV in industrial/man-made structured contexts, offering a new tool with potential domain-specific benefits while showing limited generalization to broad natural-image benchmarks.

Abstract: Collinearity is a visual perception phenomenon in the human brain that amplifies spatially aligned edges arranged along a straight line. However, it is vague for which purpose humans might have this principle in the real-world, and its utilization in computer vision and engineering applications even is a largely unexplored field. In this work, our goal is to transfer the collinearity principle to computer vision, and we explore the potential usages of this novel principle for computer vision applications. We developed a prototype model to exemplify the principle, then tested it systematically, and benchmarked it in the context of four use cases. Our cases are selected to spawn a broad range of potential applications and scenarios: sketching the combination of collinearity with deep learning (case I and II), using collinearity with saliency models (case II), and as a feature detector (case I). In the first use case, we found that collinearity is able to improve the fault detection of wafers and obtain a performance increase by a factor 1.24 via collinearity (decrease of the error rate from 6.5% to 5.26%). In the second use case, we test the defect recognition in nanotechnology materials and achieve a performance increase by 3.2x via collinearity (deep learning, error from 21.65% to 6.64%), and also explore saliency models. As third experiment, we cover occlusions; while as fourth experiment, we test ImageNet and observe that it might not be very beneficial for ImageNet. Therefore, we can assemble a list of scenarios for which collinearity is beneficial (wafers, nanotechnology, occlusions), and for what is not beneficial (ImageNet). Hence, we infer collinearity might be suitable for industry applications as it helps if the image structures of interest are man-made because they often consist of lines. Our work provides another tool for CV, hope to capture the power of human processing.

</details>


### [106] [Rationale Matters: Learning Transferable Rubrics via Proxy-Guided Critique for VLM Reward Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16600)
*Weijie Qiu,Dai Guan,Junxin Wang,Zhihang Li,Yongbo Gai,Mengyu Zhou,Erchao Zhao,Xiaoxi Jiang,Guanjun Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proxy-GRM introduces proxy-guided rubric verification to optimize rubrics in generative reward models for vision-language models. It trains lightweight proxies (Proxy-SFT and Proxy-RL) to predict preference ordering using only the rubric, using the proxy accuracy as a rubric-quality reward. With ~50k data, it achieves state-of-the-art on VL-Reward Bench, Multimodal Reward Bench, and MM-RLHF-Reward Bench, and the learned rubrics transfer to unseen evaluators, without extra training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rubrics in three-stage GRMs are often not optimized; relying on expensive LLM-as-judge checks provides no differentiable signal and limited training guidance. There is a need for differentiable, data-efficient rubric optimization that yields rubrics which are internally consistent and transferable across evaluators.

Method: Train lightweight proxy agents (Proxy-SFT and Proxy-RL) that take a candidate rubric, the original query, and a preference pair, and predict the preference ordering using only the rubric as evidence. The proxy's prediction accuracy serves as a rubric-quality reward. Integrate this rubric-quality signal into Reinforcement Learning to guide rubric generation and alignment of the VLM. Ablations show Proxy-SFT outperforms Proxy-RL as a verifier; implicit reward aggregation performs best. The approach uses ~50k data samples and demonstrates transferability of learned rubrics to unseen evaluators.

Result: Proxy-GRM achieves state-of-the-art results on VL-Reward Bench, Multimodal Reward Bench, and MM-RLHF-Reward Bench, outperforming methods trained on roughly four times more data. Proxy-SFT is a stronger verifier than Proxy-RL; implicit reward aggregation yields best performance. Learned rubrics transfer to unseen evaluators, improving reward accuracy at test time without additional training.

Conclusion: Proxy-GRM provides a data-efficient, transferable rubric optimization method for GRMs in vision-language settings. By using proxy-based rubric verification within RL, it yields high-quality, transferable rubrics and improved reward modeling with modest data requirements; code is available.

Abstract: Generative reward models (GRMs) for vision-language models (VLMs) often evaluate outputs via a three-stage pipeline: rubric generation, criterion-based scoring, and a final verdict. However, the intermediate rubric is rarely optimized directly. Prior work typically either treats rubrics as incidental or relies on expensive LLM-as-judge checks that provide no differentiable signal and limited training-time guidance. We propose Proxy-GRM, which introduces proxy-guided rubric verification into Reinforcement Learning (RL) to explicitly enhance rubric quality. Concretely, we train lightweight proxy agents (Proxy-SFT and Proxy-RL) that take a candidate rubric together with the original query and preference pair, and then predict the preference ordering using only the rubric as evidence. The proxy's prediction accuracy serves as a rubric-quality reward, incentivizing the model to produce rubrics that are internally consistent and transferable. With ~50k data samples, Proxy-GRM reaches state-of-the-art results on the VL-Reward Bench, Multimodal Reward Bench, and MM-RLHF-Reward Bench, outperforming the methods trained on four times the data. Ablations show Proxy-SFT is a stronger verifier than Proxy-RL, and implicit reward aggregation performs best. Crucially, the learned rubrics transfer to unseen evaluators, improving reward accuracy at test time without additional training. Our code is available at https://github.com/Qwen-Applications/Proxy-GRM.

</details>


### [107] [ACPV-Net: All-Class Polygonal Vectorization for Seamless Vector Map Generation from Aerial Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16616)
*Weiqin Jiao,Hao Cheng,George Vosselman,Claudio Persello*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified All-Class Polygonal Vectorization (ACPV) framework (ACPV-Net) generates complete multi-class polygon maps in a single pass, enforcing shared edges and no gaps/overlaps. It introduces Deventer-512 benchmark with metrics for semantic/geometry/topology, and Semantically Supervised Conditioning (SSC) plus a topology-aware reconstruction to achieve state-of-the-art results on both multi-class and single-class tasks; data/code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing polygonization methods are typically class-specific; extending to multiple classes via per-class runs creates topological inconsistencies (duplicated edges, gaps, overlaps). A unified, topology-conscious approach is needed.

Method: ACPV-Net with Semantically Supervised Conditioning (SSC) that couples semantic perception with geometric primitive generation, plus a topological reconstruction enforcing shared-edge consistency; single-pass all-class vectorization; applicable to single-class too.

Result: Outperforms class-specific baselines on Deventer-512 for polygon quality across classes; achieves best-reported results on WHU-Building for single-class; releases benchmark, data, code, and models.

Conclusion: Formalizes ACPV task and provides first public benchmark. Demonstrates that topology-aware single-pass multi-class polygonization can achieve high semantic and geometric fidelity with consistent topology; broad applicability and reproducibility.

Abstract: We tackle the problem of generating a complete vector map representation from aerial imagery in a single run: producing polygons for all land-cover classes with shared boundaries and without gaps or overlaps. Existing polygonization methods are typically class-specific; extending them to multiple classes via per-class runs commonly leads to topological inconsistencies, such as duplicated edges, gaps, and overlaps. We formalize this new task as All-Class Polygonal Vectorization (ACPV) and release the first public benchmark, Deventer-512, with standardized metrics jointly evaluating semantic fidelity, geometric accuracy, vertex efficiency, per-class topological fidelity and global topological consistency. To realize ACPV, we propose ACPV-Net, a unified framework introducing a novel Semantically Supervised Conditioning (SSC) mechanism coupling semantic perception with geometric primitive generation, along with a topological reconstruction that enforces shared-edge consistency by design. While enforcing such strict topological constraints, ACPV-Net surpasses all class-specific baselines in polygon quality across classes on Deventer-512. It also applies to single-class polygonal vectorization without any architectural modification, achieving the best-reported results on WHU-Building. Data, code, and models will be released at: https://github.com/HeinzJiao/ACPV-Net.

</details>


### [108] [TCATSeg: A Tooth Center-Wise Attention Network for 3D Dental Model Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16620)
*Qiang He,Wentian Qu,Jiajia Dai,Changsong Lei,Shaofeng Wang,Feifei Zuo,Yajie Wang,Yaqian Liang,Xiaoming Deng,Cuixia Ma,Yong-Jin Liu,Hongan Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TCATSeg: a 3D dental model segmentation framework that fuses local geometry with global context via sparse yet meaningful superpoints; introduces a 400-model dental dataset; reports state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate segmentation of 3D dental models is hindered by complex tooth arrangements and intra-class shape similarity; current methods emphasize local geometry and neglect global context, reducing segmentation accuracy.

Method: Proposes TCATSeg, which combines local geometric features with global semantic context. Introduces sparse, physically meaningful superpoints to encode global relationships and improve segmentation. Evaluates on a new dataset of 400 dental models including pre-orthodontic samples.

Result: Extensive experiments show TCATSeg outperforms state-of-the-art approaches on 3D dental model segmentation tasks.

Conclusion: Integrating global semantic context via sparse superpoints enhances segmentation accuracy; the new dataset enables evaluation and generalization analysis for digital dentistry applications.

Abstract: Accurate semantic segmentation of 3D dental models is essential for digital dentistry applications such as orthodontics and dental implants. However, due to complex tooth arrangements and similarities in shape among adjacent teeth, existing methods struggle with accurate segmentation, because they often focus on local geometry while neglecting global contextual information. To address this, we propose TCATSeg, a novel framework that combines local geometric features with global semantic context. We introduce a set of sparse yet physically meaningful superpoints to capture global semantic relationships and enhance segmentation accuracy. Additionally, we present a new dataset of 400 dental models, including pre-orthodontic samples, to evaluate the generalization of our method. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TCATSeg outperforms state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [109] [MLLM-based Textual Explanations for Face Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16629)
*Redwan Sony,Anil K Jain,Ross Arun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MLLM explanations for unconstrained face verification are unreliable: correct decisions often accompany unverifiable/hallucinated attributes; incorporating recognition scores helps verification but not faithfulness; a likelihood-ratio framework is proposed to quantify evidential strength of explanations; code provided for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether multimodal LLMs provide trustworthy, faithful explanations for face recognition decisions on challenging, unconstrained images, where extreme pose and surveillance settings amplify potential misalignment between decision and rationale.

Method: Systematically analyze MLLM-generated explanations on the IJB-S dataset focusing on extreme pose and surveillance imagery; examine the faithfulness of explanations to visual evidence even when decisions are correct; augment input with traditional face recognition scores/decisions to assess impact on explanations; introduce a likelihood-ratio-based framework to quantify the evidential strength of textual explanations; release code implementing the evaluation.

Result: Findings indicate that explanations often rely on non-verifiable or hallucinated facial attributes despite correct verification; combining recognition scores with input improves categorical verification but does not consistently yield faithful explanations; the proposed likelihood-ratio framework provides a quantitative measure of evidential strength for explanations; emphasizes limitations of current MLLMs and the need for principled evaluation of explainability in biometric tasks.

Conclusion: Current MLLMs exhibit fundamental shortcomings in producing reliable, trustworthy explanations for face recognition under unconstrained conditions; there is a need for principled, quantitative evaluation frameworks for explanation faithfulness in biometric applications; the work provides a starting point and release of code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently been proposed as a means to generate natural-language explanations for face recognition decisions. While such explanations facilitate human interpretability, their reliability on unconstrained face images remains underexplored. In this work, we systematically analyze MLLM-generated explanations for the unconstrained face verification task on the challenging IJB-S dataset, with a particular focus on extreme pose variation and surveillance imagery. Our results show that even when MLLMs produce correct verification decisions, the accompanying explanations frequently rely on non-verifiable or hallucinated facial attributes that are not supported by visual evidence. We further study the effect of incorporating information from traditional face recognition systems, viz., scores and decisions, alongside the input images. Although such information improves categorical verification performance, it does not consistently lead to faithful explanations. To evaluate the explanations beyond decision accuracy, we introduce a likelihood-ratio-based framework that measures the evidential strength of textual explanations. Our findings highlight fundamental limitations of current MLLMs for explainable face recognition and underscore the need for a principled evaluation of reliable and trustworthy explanations in biometric applications. Code is available at https://github.com/redwankarimsony/LR-MLLMFR-Explainability.

</details>


### [110] [FlowComposer: Composable Flows for Compositional Zero-Shot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16641)
*Zhenqi He,Lin Li,Long Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes FlowComposer, a model-agnostic framework for CZSL that uses flow matching to explicitly compose attribute and object representations via two primitive flows and a learnable composer, with leakage-guided augmentation to handle residual entanglement; shows consistent improvements across baselines on three CZSL benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address implicit composition construction and entanglement limitations in existing PEFT-based CZSL strategies that rely on token-level prompts; aims to explicitly manipulate embedding space via visual feature flows to improve generalization.

Method: Introduce FlowComposer with two primitive flows to transport visual features to attribute and object text embeddings; learn a composition flow by fusing their velocity fields through a Composer module; employ leakage-guided augmentation reusing leaked features as auxiliary signals; plug-and-play with various baselines; evaluate on three CZSL benchmarks.

Result: Significant improvements over baselines on three public CZSL benchmarks; FlowComposer improves generalization by explicit composition flow and flow-based feature disentanglement; robust to residual entanglement via augmentation.

Conclusion: FlowComposer is a model-agnostic, plug-and-play enhancement for CZSL that improves composition construction in embedding space and yields better generalization; encourages explicit flow-based manipulation of visual-text alignment, with potential broader applicability.

Abstract: Compositional zero-shot learning (CZSL) aims to recognize unseen attribute-object compositions by recombining primitives learned from seen pairs. Recent CZSL methods built on vision-language models (VLMs) typically adopt parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT). They apply visual disentanglers for decomposition and manipulate token-level prompts or prefixes to encode compositions. However, such PEFT-based designs suffer from two fundamental limitations: (1) Implicit Composition Construction, where composition is realized only via token concatenation or branch-wise prompt tuning rather than an explicit operation in the embedding space; (2) Remained Feature Entanglement, where imperfect disentanglement leaves attribute, object, and composition features mutually contaminated. Together, these issues limit the generalization ability of current CZSL models. In this paper, we are the first to systematically study flow matching for CZSL and introduce FlowComposer, a model-agnostic framework that learns two primitive flows to transport visual features toward attribute and object text embeddings, and a learnable Composer that explicitly fuses their velocity fields into a composition flow. To exploit the inevitable residual entanglement, we further devise a leakage-guided augmentation scheme that reuses leaked features as auxiliary signals. We thoroughly evaluate FlowComposer on three public CZSL benchmarks by integrating it as a plug-and-play component into various baselines, consistently achieving significant improvements.

</details>


### [111] [BUSSARD: Normalizing Flows for Bijective Universal Scene-Specific Anomalous Relationship Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16645)
*Melissa Schween,Mathis Kruse,Bodo Rosenhahn*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A normalizing-flow-based model (BUSSARD) detects anomalous relations in scene graphs by learning bijective mappings of object-relation-object triplets embedded with language model tokens to a Gaussian base distribution, enabling likelihood-based anomaly detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address robust, scalable detection of anomalous relationships in image-derived scene graphs by leveraging semantic knowledge and multimodal embedding to improve robustness to synonyms and real-world semantics.

Method: Embed scene-graph triplets via language-model-based tokens for objects and relations; train a normalizing flow to learn bijective transformations to a simple base distribution (Gaussian); anomaly detection via likelihood; multimodal fusion and ablations to assess robustness to synonyms.

Result: Evaluated on the SARD dataset (office and dining room scenes); AUROC ~10% higher than state-of-the-art; ~5x faster inference; ablation studies show enhanced robustness/universality with reduced performance deviation under synonyms (baseline shows 17.5% deviation).

Conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of learning-based relationship anomaly detection in scene graphs; provides code at GitHub for BUSSARD, indicating practical feasibility and potential for real-world deployment.

Abstract: We propose Bijective Universal Scene-Specific Anomalous Relationship Detection (BUSSARD), a normalizing flow-based model for detecting anomalous relations in scene graphs, generated from images. Our work follows a multimodal approach, embedding object and relationship tokens from scene graphs with a language model to leverage semantic knowledge from the real world. A normalizing flow model is used to learn bijective transformations that map object-relation-object triplets from scene graphs to a simple base distribution (typically Gaussian), allowing anomaly detection through likelihood estimation. We evaluate our approach on the SARD dataset containing office and dining room scenes. Our method achieves around 10% better AUROC results compared to the current state-of-the-art model, while simultaneously being five times faster. Through ablation studies, we demonstrate superior robustness and universality, particularly regarding the use of synonyms, with our model maintaining stable performance while the baseline shows 17.5% deviation. This work demonstrates the strong potential of learning-based methods for relationship anomaly detection in scene graphs. Our code is available at https://github.com/mschween/BUSSARD .

</details>


### [112] [Mixture of Style Experts for Diverse Image Stylization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16649)
*Shihao Zhu,Ziheng Ouyang,Yijia Kang,Qilong Wang,Mi Zhou,Bo Li,Ming-Ming Cheng,Qibin Hou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StyleExpert introduces a semantic-aware diffusion stylization framework using a Mixture of Experts (MoE) to route styles to specialized experts, improving semantic and material detail preservation and generalization to unseen styles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion-based stylization is largely color-driven and struggles to preserve semantic content and material details across diverse styles; a semantic-aware, extensible model is needed.

Method: A unified style encoder is trained on a large-scale dataset of content-style-stylized triplets to embed styles into a latent space. A similarity-aware gating mechanism conditions MoE routing, sending styles to specialized experts. The MoE handles diverse styles across semantic levels from textures to deeper semantics.

Result: StyleExpert outperforms existing methods in preserving semantics and material details and generalizes to unseen styles, demonstrated through extensive experiments.

Conclusion: A semantic-aware MoE-based framework can effectively unify and route diverse styles in diffusion stylization, achieving robust semantic and material preservation and broad generalization; code and images are released.

Abstract: Diffusion-based stylization has advanced significantly, yet existing methods are limited to color-driven transformations, neglecting complex semantics and material details.We introduce StyleExpert, a semantic-aware framework based on the Mixture of Experts (MoE). Our framework employs a unified style encoder, trained on our large-scale dataset of content-style-stylized triplets, to embed diverse styles into a consistent latent space. This embedding is then used to condition a similarity-aware gating mechanism, which dynamically routes styles to specialized experts within the MoE architecture. Leveraging this MoE architecture, our method adeptly handles diverse styles spanning multiple semantic levels, from shallow textures to deep semantics. Extensive experiments show that StyleExpert outperforms existing approaches in preserving semantics and material details, while generalizing to unseen styles. Our code and collected images are available at the project page: https://hh-lg.github.io/StyleExpert-Page/.

</details>


### [113] [Efficient Brood Cell Detection in Layer Trap Nests for Bees and Wasps: Balancing Labeling Effort and Species Coverage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16652)
*Chenchang Liu,Felix Fornoff,Annika Grasreiner,Patrick Maeder,Henri Greil,Marco Seeland*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A deep learning approach with Constrained False Positive Loss (CFPL) for brood cell detection in Layer Trap Nests to handle labeling constraints and data incompleteness, tested on 712 images over 28 classes with up to 300 labels per class; shows improved performance under labeling constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monitoring cavity-nesting wild bees and wasps via LTNs is labor-intensive. Dense brood cell packing and long-tail class distribution create labeling burden and data incompleteness, reducing model performance. A method to reduce labeling effort and mitigate unlabeled data impact is needed.

Method: Proposes CFPL that dynamically masks predictions from unlabeled data during training to prevent them from contributing to the classification loss. Uses a deep learning detection/classification framework on LTNs; dataset of 712 images; 28 fine-grained classes; training restricted to at most 300 labels per class.

Result: CFPL improves performance, reduces sensitivity to unlabeled data, and helps balance accuracy with labeling effort; mitigates class imbalance in experiments.

Conclusion: CFPL is effective for learning from partially labeled, densely packed LTNs and can be extended to other settings with incomplete labels and class imbalance; future work could explore active labeling and semi-supervised strategies.

Abstract: Monitoring cavity-nesting wild bees and wasps is vital for biodiversity research and conservation. Layer trap nests (LTNs) are emerging as a valuable tool to study the abundance and species richness of these insects, offering insights into their nesting activities and ecological needs. However, manually evaluating LTNs to detect and classify brood cells is labor-intensive and time-consuming. To address this, we propose a deep learning based approach for efficient brood cell detection and classification in LTNs. LTNs present additional challenges due to densely packed brood cells, leading to a high labeling effort per image. Moreover, we observe a significant imbalance in class distribution, with common species having notably more occurrences than rare species. Comprehensive labeling of common species is time-consuming and exacerbates data imbalance, while partial labeling introduces data incompleteness which degrades model performance. To reduce labeling effort and mitigate the impact of unlabeled data, we introduce a novel Constrained False Positive Loss (CFPL) strategy. CFPL dynamically masks predictions from unlabeled data, preventing them from interfering with the classification loss during training. We evaluate our approach on a dataset of 712 LTN images collected over one season, covering 28 fine-grained classes describing the taxonomy and status of brood cells. To minimize labeling effort, we limit the training set to a maximum of 300 labels per class. Experimental results demonstrate that deep learning can be effectively used to detect brood cells in LTNs. Our CFPL method further improves performance and balances model accuracy and labeling effort while also mitigating class imbalance.

</details>


### [114] [HeBA: Heterogeneous Bottleneck Adapters for Robust Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16653)
*Md Jahidul Islam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: We propose HeBA, a heterogeneous bottleneck adapter for Vision-Language Models that uses modality-specific processing (2D depthwise-separable convs for images, dense projections for text), a D→D/4 bottleneck for regularization, and Kaiming initialization to improve gradient flow. This yields superior stability and state-of-the-art few-shot performance across 11 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard one-size-fits-all adapters ignore the distinct structural properties of images (spatial locality) and text (semantic density). Introducing modality-aware inductive biases can better tailor adaptation of large-scale VLMs to downstream tasks.

Method: Three innovations: (1) Heterogeneity: visual tokens are processed with 2D depthwise-separable convolutions to preserve spatial structure, while text tokens are processed with dense linear projections to capture semantic relationships; (2) Bottleneck Regularization: a compression bottleneck D→D/4 to enforce compact, robust features and act as a structural regularizer; (3) Active Gradient Initialization: use Kaiming initialization to ensure sufficient gradient flow with a frozen backbone.

Result: Extensive experiments show improved stability and accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art performance on 11 few-shot benchmarks; code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Modality-aware architectural design with specialized inductive biases (HeBA) improves adaptation of VLMs to downstream tasks and establishes a new standard for few-shot performance.

Abstract: Adapting large-scale Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like CLIP to downstream tasks often suffers from a "one-size-fits-all" architectural approach, where visual and textual tokens are processed uniformly by wide, generic adapters. We argue that this homogeneity ignores the distinct structural nature of the modalities -- spatial locality in images versus semantic density in text. To address this, we propose HeBA (Heterogeneous Bottleneck Adapter), a unified architectural framework that introduces modality-specific structural inductive biases. HeBA departs from conventional designs through three key architectural innovations: (1) Heterogeneity: It processes visual tokens via 2D depthwise-separable convolutions to preserve spatial correlations, while distinctively processing text tokens via dense linear projections to capture semantic relationships; (2) Bottleneck Regularization: Unlike standard expanding adapters, HeBA employs a compression bottleneck (D -> D/4) that explicitly forces the model to learn compact, robust features and acts as a structural regularizer; and (3) Active Gradient Initialization: We challenge the restrictive zero-initialization paradigm, utilizing a Kaiming initialization strategy that ensures sufficient initial gradient flow to accelerate convergence without compromising the frozen backbone's pre-trained knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HeBA's architecturally specialized design achieves superior stability and accuracy, establishing a new state-of-the-art on 11 few-shot benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/Jahid12012021/VLM-HeBA.

</details>


### [115] [Spectral Property-Driven Data Augmentation for Hyperspectral Single-Source Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16662)
*Taiqin Chen,Yifeng Wang,Xiaochen Feng,Zhilin Zhu,Hao Sha,Yingjian Li,Yongbing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SPDDA is a spectral property-driven augmentation for hyperspectral SDG that resamples spectral channels and adaptively mixes channels, with spatial-spectral co-optimization to maintain realism and diversity, achieving state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HSI domain shifts are influenced by device-dependent spectral channel variation and inter-channel coupling. Single-source domain generalization with naive augmentation risks misalignment or over-smoothing; a principled augmentation that respects hyperspectral properties can improve cross-domain robustness.

Method: SPDDA consists of a spectral diversity module that resamples data along the spectral dimension to create samples with varying spectral channels, a channel-wise adaptive spectral mixer that models inter-channel similarity to avoid fixed augmentation patterns, and a spatial-spectral co-optimization mechanism that jointly enforces a spatial fidelity constraint and a spectral continuity self-constraint. The spectral self-constraint weight is adaptively adjusted based on the spatial counterpart to prevent over-smoothing.

Result: Extensive experiments on three remote sensing benchmarks show SPDDA outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: SPDDA effectively balances realism and diversity in augmentation for hyperspectral SDG by accounting for device-dependent spectral channels and channel mixing, leading to better cross-domain generalization.

Abstract: While hyperspectral images (HSI) benefit from numerous spectral channels that provide rich information for classification, the increased dimensionality and sensor variability make them more sensitive to distributional discrepancies across domains, which in turn can affect classification performance. To tackle this issue, hyperspectral single-source domain generalization (SDG) typically employs data augmentation to simulate potential domain shifts and enhance model robustness under the condition of single-source domain training data availability. However, blind augmentation may produce samples misaligned with real-world scenarios, while excessive emphasis on realism can suppress diversity, highlighting a tradeoff between realism and diversity that limits generalization to target domains. To address this challenge, we propose a spectral property-driven data augmentation (SPDDA) that explicitly accounts for the inherent properties of HSI, namely the device-dependent variation in the number of spectral channels and the mixing of adjacent channels. Specifically, SPDDA employs a spectral diversity module that resamples data from the source domain along the spectral dimension to generate samples with varying spectral channels, and constructs a channel-wise adaptive spectral mixer by modeling inter-channel similarity, thereby avoiding fixed augmentation patterns. To further enhance the realism of the augmented samples, we propose a spatial-spectral co-optimization mechanism, which jointly optimizes a spatial fidelity constraint and a spectral continuity self-constraint. Moreover, the weight of the spectral self-constraint is adaptively adjusted based on the spatial counterpart, thus preventing over-smoothing in the spectral dimension and preserving spatial structure. Extensive experiments conducted on three remote sensing benchmarks demonstrate that SPDDA outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [116] [Kestrel: Grounding Self-Refinement for LVLM Hallucination Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16664)
*Jiawei Mao,Hardy Chen,Haoqin Tu,Yuhan Wang,Letian Zhang,Zeyu Zheng,Huaxiu Yao,Zirui Wang,Cihang Xie,Yuyin Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Kestrel is a training-free framework that combines an explicit visual grounding agent with an evidence-verified self-refinement loop to mitigate large vision-language model hallucinations, yielding improved hallucination metrics and transparent evidence traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations severely limit the deployment of LVLMs; training-time mitigation is expensive for large models; a training-free, interpretable approach offers a practical alternative.

Method: 1) An explicit visual grounding agent collects visual evidence. 2) Tool outputs are converted into reusable, structured textual evidence. 3) An LVLM judge verifies the evidence. 4) The system iteratively self-refines answers based on verified evidence to reduce over-correction.

Result: Kestrel improves performance on hallucination benchmarks (average +3.31% on POPE; +28.34 on MME-Hallucination with Qwen3-VL). The grounding agent and self-refinement contribute an average +2.0% gain on POPE. It also provides transparent verification traces for diagnosis and analysis.

Conclusion: Kestrel offers a scalable, training-free approach to LVLM hallucination mitigation that combines explicit grounding, evidence verification, and self-refinement, delivering improved accuracy with interpretable traces.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have become increasingly strong but remain prone to hallucinations in multimodal tasks, which significantly narrows their deployment. As training these LVLMs to avoid hallucinations becomes prohibitively expensive for larger models, training-free methods offer a cheap and flexible solution to this problem, yet existing approaches based on decoding or tool use often bring limited gains and/or weak interpretability. We propose Kestrel, a training-free framework for LVLM hallucination mitigation that combines an explicit visual-grounding agent with evidence-verified self-refinement mechanism. In detail, Kestrel first collects explicit visual evidence and converts tool outputs into reusable and structured textual evidence. Second, to take full advantage of these evidence, Kestrel verifies them via an LVLM judge for evidence checking, then iteratively self-refine answers based on verified evidence to reduce the risk of over-correction. Extensive experiments show that Kestrel improves performance over strong baselines across hallucination benchmarks (e.g., average +3.31% on POPE and +28.34 on MME-Hallucination with Qwen3-VL), while providing transparent verification traces for hallucination diagnosis and analysis -- e.g., both the integrated self-refinement module and grounding agent contributing an average +2.0% gain on POPE.

</details>


### [117] [Fast-WAM: Do World Action Models Need Test-time Future Imagination?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16666)
*Tianyuan Yuan,Zibin Dong,Yicheng Liu,Hang Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Fast-WAM shows that test-time future imagination may be unnecessary for strong WAM performance. By keeping video co-training during training but skipping future prediction at inference, it achieves real-time control with competitive results, and video modeling at training time is more critical than test-time generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine whether the benefits of video modeling in World Action Models come from training-time representations or test-time future generation, and to reduce test-time latency.

Method: Propose Fast-WAM: retain video co-training during training but omit explicit future prediction at test time. Instantiate several variants to isolate the effects of training-time video modeling vs. test-time imagination. Evaluate on LIBERO, RoboTwin, and real-world tasks; compare against imagine-then-execute WAMs; report latency (~190ms) and lack of need for embodied pretraining.

Result: Fast-WAM variants remain competitive with imagine-then-execute baselines; removing video co-training causes larger performance drops than removing test-time imagination. Achieves real-time latency (~190ms) and is over 4x faster than existing WAMs while performing competitively on simulation benchmarks and real-world tasks.

Conclusion: The primary value of video prediction in WAMs lies in improving world representations during training rather than generating future observations at test time; training-time video co-training is essential for strong performance, enabling real-time control without embodied pretraining.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for embodied control because they explicitly model how visual observations may evolve under action. Most existing WAMs follow an imagine-then-execute paradigm, incurring substantial test-time latency from iterative video denoising, yet it remains unclear whether explicit future imagination is actually necessary for strong action performance. In this paper, we ask whether WAMs need explicit future imagination at test time, or whether their benefit comes primarily from video modeling during training. We disentangle the role of video modeling during training from explicit future generation during inference by proposing \textbf{Fast-WAM}, a WAM architecture that retains video co-training during training but skips future prediction at test time. We further instantiate several Fast-WAM variants to enable a controlled comparison of these two factors. Across these variants, we find that Fast-WAM remains competitive with imagine-then-execute variants, while removing video co-training causes a much larger performance drop. Empirically, Fast-WAM achieves competitive results with state-of-the-art methods both on simulation benchmarks (LIBERO and RoboTwin) and real-world tasks, without embodied pretraining. It runs in real time with 190ms latency, over 4$\times$ faster than existing imagine-then-execute WAMs. These results suggest that the main value of video prediction in WAMs may lie in improving world representations during training rather than generating future observations at test time. Project page: https://yuantianyuan01.github.io/FastWAM/

</details>


### [118] [$x^2$-Fusion: Cross-Modality and Cross-Dimension Flow Estimation in Event Edge Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16671)
*Ruishan Guo,Ciyu Ruan,Haoyang Wang,Zihang Gong,Jingao Xu,Xinlei Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces x^2-Fusion, a unified edge-centric latent space (Event Edge Space) for multimodal fusion of event, image, and LiDAR data to jointly estimate dense 2D optical flow and 3D scene flow, with reliability-aware adaptive fusion and cross-dimension contrast learning; achieves state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal fusion methods rely on modality-specific pipelines and separate feature spaces, leading to cross-sensor misalignment. Event cameras offer a natural spatiotemporal edge signal; leveraging this as a shared anchor can unify representations.

Method: Define Event Edge Space as a shared latent domain; align image and LiDAR features to this space guided by event-derived spatiotemporal edges; perform reliability-aware adaptive fusion to estimate modality reliability and emphasize stable cues under degradation; apply cross-dimension contrast learning to tightly couple 2D optical flow with 3D scene flow; fuse data from images, LiDAR, and events to predict dense motion.

Result: Comprehensive experiments on synthetic and real benchmarks show state-of-the-art accuracy under standard conditions and notable improvements in challenging scenarios.

Conclusion: A unified edge-centric latent representation via Event Edge Space enables simpler, more robust multimodal fusion for joint 2D/3D motion estimation; cross-dimension learning strengthens 2D-3D coupling and robustness to degradation.

Abstract: Estimating dense 2D optical flow and 3D scene flow is essential for dynamic scene understanding. Recent work combines images, LiDAR, and event data to jointly predict 2D and 3D motion, yet most approaches operate in separate heterogeneous feature spaces. Without a shared latent space that all modalities can align to, these systems rely on multiple modality-specific blocks, leaving cross-sensor mismatches unresolved and making fusion unnecessarily complex.Event cameras naturally provide a spatiotemporal edge signal, which we can treat as an intrinsic edge field to anchor a unified latent representation, termed the Event Edge Space. Building on this idea, we introduce $x^2$-Fusion, which reframes multimodal fusion as representation unification: event-derived spatiotemporal edges define an edge-centric homogeneous space, and image and LiDAR features are explicitly aligned in this shared representation.Within this space, we perform reliability-aware adaptive fusion to estimate modality reliability and emphasize stable cues under degradation. We further employ cross-dimension contrast learning to tightly couple 2D optical flow with 3D scene flow. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real benchmarks show that $x^2$-Fusion achieves state-of-the-art accuracy under standard conditions and delivers substantial improvements in challenging scenarios.

</details>


### [119] [HMAR: Hierarchical Modality-Aware Expert and Dynamic Routing Medical Image Retrieval Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16679)
*Aojie Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HMAR is a Hierarchical MoE-based medical image retrieval system that combines global and local feature Experts, two-stage contrastive learning, and hash-based retrieval with Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) layers, achieving state-of-the-art mAP on RadioImageNet-CT while reducing annotation needs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current medical image retrieval (MIR) systems rely on uniform, global feature encodings and coarse similarity labels, which ignore varying clinical importance of anatomical regions and hinder fine-grained, region-specific retrieval. Bounding-box annotations are expensive, and there is a demand for efficient, region-focused matching with scalable search.

Method: Mixture-of-Experts framework with Expert0 for global feature extraction and expert1 for local, position-invariant region representations. A two-stage contrastive learning strategy eliminates the need for bounding-box annotations. Inference uses a sliding-window mechanism for dense local matching. Hash codes are produced via Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) layers to enable fast Hamming-distance search.

Result: On RadioImageNet-CT (16 patterns, 29,903 images) HMAR yields mAP of 0.711 (64-bit) and 0.724 (128-bit), surpassing ACIR by 0.7% and 1.1% respectively.

Conclusion: HMAR demonstrates effective hierarchical, modality-aware retrieval with global-local matching and efficient hashing, addressing annotation-free dense local retrieval and delivering improved accuracy and efficiency for clinical MIR tasks.

Abstract: Medical image retrieval (MIR) is a critical component of computer-aided diagnosis, yet existing systems suffer from three persistent limitations: uniform feature encoding that fails to account for the varying clinical importance of anatomical structures, ambiguous similarity metrics based on coarse classification labels, and an exclusive focus on global image similarity that cannot meet the clinical demand for fine-grained region-specific retrieval. We propose HMAR (Hierarchical Modality-Aware Expert and Dynamic Routing), an adaptive retrieval framework built on a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture. HMAR employs a dual-expert mechanism: Expert0 extracts global features for holistic similarity matching, while Expert1 learns position-invariant local representations for precise lesion-region retrieval. A two-stage contrastive learning strategy eliminates the need for expensive bounding-box annotations, and a sliding-window matching algorithm enables dense local comparison at inference time. Hash codes are generated via Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) layers for efficient Hamming-distance search. Experiments on the RadioImageNet-CT dataset (16 clinical patterns, 29,903 images) show that HMAR achieves mean Average Precision (mAP) of 0.711 and 0.724 for 64-bit and 128-bit hash codes, improving over the state-of-the-art ACIR method by 0.7% and 1.1%, respectively.

</details>


### [120] [Search2Motion: Training-Free Object-Level Motion Control via Attention-Consensus Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16711)
*Sainan Liu,Tz-Ying Wu,Hector A Valdez,Subarna Tripathi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Search2Motion is a training-free framework for object-level motion editing in image-to-video generation, enabling object relocation using first-last-frame priors without fine-tuning, with robust target-frame construction and interpretable early-step cues, plus a lightweight search strategy and new benchmarks; it outperforms baselines on object-focused metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable high-fidelity motion editing without trajectory/annotation requirements and without fine-tuning, while providing interpretability and reliable evaluation of object vs camera motion.

Method: Target-frame-based control using first/last frame priors; semantic-guided object insertion; robust background inpainting; analysis of early-step self-attention maps; ACE-Seed search strategy; new benchmarks S2M-DAVIS/S2M-OMB; FLF2V-obj metric.

Result: Consistent improvement over baselines on FLF2V-obj and VBench; provides stable object relocation with scene stability; interpretable cues via early-step attention; benchmarks isolate object motion.

Conclusion: Training-free object-level motion editing is feasible and effective, and the introduced benchmarks and metrics enable disentangling object vs camera motion and evaluating motion fidelity.

Abstract: We present Search2Motion, a training-free framework for object-level motion editing in image-to-video generation. Unlike prior methods requiring trajectories, bounding boxes, masks, or motion fields, Search2Motion adopts target-frame-based control, leveraging first-last-frame motion priors to realize object relocation while preserving scene stability without fine-tuning. Reliable target-frame construction is achieved through semantic-guided object insertion and robust background inpainting. We further show that early-step self-attention maps predict object and camera dynamics, offering interpretable user feedback and motivating ACE-Seed (Attention Consensus for Early-step Seed selection), a lightweight search strategy that improves motion fidelity without look-ahead sampling or external evaluators. Noting that existing benchmarks conflate object and camera motion, we introduce S2M-DAVIS and S2M-OMB for stable-camera, object-only evaluation, alongside FLF2V-obj metrics that isolate object artifacts without requiring ground-truth trajectories. Search2Motion consistently outperforms baselines on FLF2V-obj and VBench.

</details>


### [121] [Emotion-Aware Classroom Quality Assessment Leveraging IoT-Based Real-Time Student Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16719)
*Hai Nguyen,Hieu Dao,Hung Nguyen,Nam Vu,Cong Tran*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An IoT-enabled, real-time multi-agent affective computing framework for classroom engagement monitoring, introducing the Classroom Emotion Dataset; achieves 50-face detection at 25 FPS and 88% engagement classification accuracy across three schools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses large class sizes and reduced teacher-student interaction by providing scalable, data-driven, real-time emotion/engagement monitoring in class.

Method: Developed a high-throughput IoT-based framework with multi-agent affective computing for real-time processing; built/used the Classroom Emotion Dataset (1,500 labeled images, 300 classroom videos); field-tested across a primary, secondary, and high school; optimized for load balancing and low latency; reports up to 50 faces at 25 FPS and 88% accuracy in engagement state classification.

Result: System demonstrated robust performance with favorable engagement outcomes; positive feedback from students, teachers, and parents; improved classroom interaction and teaching adaptation.

Conclusion: Proposes a practical IoT-enabled emotion-aware learning framework and introduces the Classroom Emotion Dataset to aid validation and further research, supporting scalable emotion-aware education.

Abstract: This study presents high-throughput, real-time multi-agent affective computing framework designed to enhance classroom learning through emotional state monitoring. As large classroom sizes and limited teacher student interaction increasingly challenge educators, there is a growing need for scalable, data-driven tools capable of capturing students' emotional and engagement patterns in real time. The system was evaluated using the Classroom Emotion Dataset, consisting of 1,500 labeled images and 300 classroom detection videos. Tailored for IoT devices, the system addresses load balancing and latency challenges through efficient real-time processing. Field testing was conducted across three educational institutions in a large metropolitan area: a primary school (hereafter school A), a secondary school (school B), and a high school (school C). The system demonstrated robust performance, detecting up to 50 faces at 25 FPS and achieving 88% overall accuracy in classifying classroom engagement states. Implementation results showed positive outcomes, with favorable feedback from students, teachers, and parents regarding improved classroom interaction and teaching adaptation. Key contributions of this research include establishing a practical, IoT-based framework for emotion-aware learning environments and introducing the 'Classroom Emotion Dataset' to facilitate further validation and research.

</details>


### [122] [World Reconstruction From Inconsistent Views](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16736)
*Lukas Höllein,Matthias Nießner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Non-rigidly align per-frame pixel-wise 3D point clouds derived from video diffusion outputs into a globally consistent frame, using an iterative frame-to-model ICP followed by global optimization, then use the refined point cloud for initialization of 3D reconstruction with an inverse deformation rendering loss to produce high-quality, explorable 3D scenes from inconsistent video views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video diffusion models yield high-quality frames but suffer from 3D inconsistency across frames, hindering coherent 3D reconstruction. A global, consistent 3D frame is needed to turn sequences into explorable environments.

Method: 1) Lift each frame into a pixel-wise 3D point cloud via a geometric foundation model. 2) Apply a tailored non-rigid iterative frame-to-model ICP to obtain an initial alignment across all frames. 3) Perform a global optimization to sharpen the point cloud. 4) Use the refined point cloud as initialization for 3D reconstruction. 5) Introduce an inverse deformation rendering loss to synthesize high-quality, explorable 3D environments from inconsistent views.

Result: The proposed pipeline yields higher-quality 3D scenes than baselines, effectively turning video diffusion outputs into 3D-consistent world generators.

Conclusion: The method demonstrates that non-rigid, globally-consistent point-cloud alignment combined with deformation-aware rendering losses can convert inconsistent video frames into sharp, explorable 3D environments, advancing video-to-3D reconstruction.

Abstract: Video diffusion models generate high-quality and diverse worlds; however, individual frames often lack 3D consistency across the output sequence, which makes the reconstruction of 3D worlds difficult. To this end, we propose a new method that handles these inconsistencies by non-rigidly aligning the video frames into a globally-consistent coordinate frame that produces sharp and detailed pointcloud reconstructions. First, a geometric foundation model lifts each frame into a pixel-wise 3D pointcloud, which contains unaligned surfaces due to these inconsistencies. We then propose a tailored non-rigid iterative frame-to-model ICP to obtain an initial alignment across all frames, followed by a global optimization that further sharpens the pointcloud. Finally, we leverage this pointcloud as initialization for 3D reconstruction and propose a novel inverse deformation rendering loss to create high quality and explorable 3D environments from inconsistent views. We demonstrate that our 3D scenes achieve higher quality than baselines, effectively turning video models into 3D-consistent world generators.

</details>


### [123] [Retrieving Counterfactuals Improves Visual In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16737)
*Guangzhi Xiong,Sanchit Sinha,Zhenghao He,Aidong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A counterfactual, attribute-guided demonstration retrieval framework (CIRCLES) improves in-context learning for vision-language models by selecting causal, diverse exemplars, yielding robustness gains—especially for small models under data scarcity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate reliance on spurious correlations from passive similarity-based retrieval in ICL for VLMs and to enable causal reasoning about attribute-outcome relations by using counterfactual-style demonstrations.

Method: Introduce CIRCLES: actively retrieve counterfactual-style, attribute-guided composed images to form demonstration sets for ICL. This creates diverse, causally informative exemplars that encourage models to infer causal relationships between attributes and outcomes.

Result: CIRCLES consistently outperforms existing retrieval-based baselines across four datasets and multiple architectures, with pronounced gains for small-scale models and under information scarcity. It also yields more diverse and causally informative demonstrations, with qualitative evidence of improved causal reasoning.

Conclusion: CIRCLES enhances causal reasoning in vision-language models by replacing passive, similarity-based demonstration retrieval with active counterfactual-style exemplar selection, improving robustness and generalization; code available.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved impressive performance across a wide range of multimodal reasoning tasks, but they often struggle to disentangle fine-grained visual attributes and reason about underlying causal relationships. In-context learning (ICL) offers a promising avenue for VLMs to adapt to new tasks, but its effectiveness critically depends on the selection of demonstration examples. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches typically rely on passive similarity-based retrieval, which tends to select correlated but non-causal examples, amplifying spurious associations and limiting model robustness. We introduce CIRCLES (Composed Image Retrieval for Causal Learning Example Selection), a novel framework that actively constructs demonstration sets by retrieving counterfactual-style examples through targeted, attribute-guided composed image retrieval. By incorporating counterfactual-style examples, CIRCLES enables VLMs to implicitly reason about the causal relations between attributes and outcomes, moving beyond superficial correlations and fostering more robust and grounded reasoning. Comprehensive experiments on four diverse datasets demonstrate that CIRCLES consistently outperforms existing methods across multiple architectures, especially on small-scale models, with pronounced gains under information scarcity. Furthermore, CIRCLES retrieves more diverse and causally informative examples, providing qualitative insights into how models leverage in-context demonstrations for improved reasoning. Our code is available at https://github.com/gzxiong/CIRCLES.

</details>


### [124] [When the City Teaches the Car: Label-Free 3D Perception from Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16742)
*Zhen Xu,Jinsu Yoo,Cristian Bautista,Zanming Huang,Tai-Yu Pan,Zhenzhen Liu,Katie Z Luo,Mark Campbell,Bharath Hariharan,Wei-Lun Chao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Infrastructure-taught, label-free 3D perception uses roadside units (RSUs) as unsupervised teachers to generate pseudo-labels from their fixed viewpoints, enabling ego-vehicle detectors to be trained without labeling data. A three-stage, fully label-free pipeline shows feasibility in CARLA with CenterPoint, achieving 82.3% AP (vehicles) vs 94.4% supervised upper bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce data collection and annotation burden for 3D autonomous driving by leveraging existing city infrastructure. RSUs offer stable viewpoints and repeated observations, enabling self-supervised, cross-view supervision for ego detectors.

Method: Three-stage pipeline where RSUs first learn local 3D detectors from unlabeled data, broadcast predictions to vehicles, which are aggregated into pseudo-labels to train an ego detector. Evaluation in CARLA with CenterPoint; analysis of scalability and complementarity with ego-centric label-free methods; emphasis on end-to-end, test-time no-infrastructure requirement.

Result: Demonstrates feasibility of infrastructure-taught learning: ego detector trained with pseudo-labels achieves 82.3% AP on vehicle detection, compared to a supervised upper bound of 94.4%. Shows complementarity and scalability potential; controlled, concept-level feasibility study in a simulated environment.

Conclusion: City infrastructure can provide scalable supervisory signals for autonomous vehicles, offering an orthogonal approach to reduce annotation costs in 3D perception; further work needed on performance gaps, real-world deployment, and integration with existing ego-centric methods.

Abstract: Building robust 3D perception for self-driving still relies heavily on large-scale data collection and manual annotation, yet this paradigm becomes impractical as deployment expands across diverse cities and regions. Meanwhile, modern cities are increasingly instrumented with roadside units (RSUs), static sensors deployed along roads and at intersections to monitor traffic. This raises a natural question: can the city itself help train the vehicle? We propose infrastructure-taught, label-free 3D perception, a paradigm in which RSUs act as stationary, unsupervised teachers for ego vehicles. Leveraging their fixed viewpoints and repeated observations, RSUs learn local 3D detectors from unlabeled data and broadcast predictions to passing vehicles, which are aggregated as pseudo-label supervision for training a standalone ego detector. The resulting model requires no infrastructure or communication at test time. We instantiate this idea as a fully label-free three-stage pipeline and conduct a concept-and-feasibility study in a CARLA-based multi-agent environment. With CenterPoint, our pipeline achieves 82.3% AP for detecting vehicles, compared to a fully supervised ego upper bound of 94.4%. We further systematically analyze each stage, evaluate its scalability, and demonstrate complementarity with existing ego-centric label-free methods. Together, these results suggest that city infrastructure itself can potentially provide a scalable supervisory signal for autonomous vehicles, positioning infrastructure-taught learning as a promising orthogonal paradigm for reducing annotation cost in 3D perception.

</details>


### [125] [Semi-supervised Latent Disentangled Diffusion Model for Textile Pattern Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16747)
*Chenggong Hu,Yi Wang,Mengqi Xue,Haofei Zhang,Jie Song,Li Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage SLDDM-TPG framework for faithful textile pattern generation: first, a latent disentangled network (LDN) to separate clothing features; second, a semi-supervised latent diffusion model (S-LDM) guided by LDN with a fine-grained alignment strategy, yielding higher fidelity and better generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Textile pattern generation (TPG) faces unfaithful results when using standard image-to-image models due to feature confusion between complex textile patterns and non-rigid texture distortions in clothing images. A method that preserves fine-grained details and textures is needed.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) Latent disentangled network (LDN) to resolve feature confusion and create a multi-dimensional, independent clothing feature space; (2) Semi-supervised latent diffusion model (S-LDM) guided by LDN, trained with semi-supervised diffusion and a designed fine-grained alignment strategy to produce faithful results.

Result: On the CTP-HD dataset, SLDDM-TPG reduces FID by 4.1 and increases SSIM by up to 0.116; it also demonstrates good generalization on the VITON-HD dataset.

Conclusion: SLDDM-TPG achieves faithful, high-fidelity textile pattern generation with improved quantitative metrics and robust cross-dataset generalization.

Abstract: Textile pattern generation (TPG) aims to synthesize fine-grained textile pattern images based on given clothing images. Although previous studies have not explicitly investigated TPG, existing image-to-image models appear to be natural candidates for this task. However, when applied directly, these methods often produce unfaithful results, failing to preserve fine-grained details due to feature confusion between complex textile patterns and the inherent non-rigid texture distortions in clothing images. In this paper, we propose a novel method, SLDDM-TPG, for faithful and high-fidelity TPG. Our method consists of two stages: (1) a latent disentangled network (LDN) that resolves feature confusion in clothing representations and constructs a multi-dimensional, independent clothing feature space; and (2) a semi-supervised latent diffusion model (S-LDM), which receives guidance signals from LDN and generates faithful results through semi-supervised diffusion training, combined with our designed fine-grained alignment strategy. Extensive evaluations show that SLDDM-TPG reduces FID by 4.1 and improves SSIM by up to 0.116 on our CTP-HD dataset, and also demonstrate good generalization on the VITON-HD dataset.

</details>


### [126] [SuCor: Susceptibility Distortion Correction via Parameter-Free and Self-Regularized Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16758)
*Sreekar Chigurupati,Eleftherios Garyfallidis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SuCor is a fast optimal-transport–based correction for susceptibility-induced EPI distortions, with automatic regularization via Morozov discrepancy, yielding higher mutual information with T1 than TOPUP on HCP data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Susceptibility-induced geometric distortions in EPI degrade alignment with structural scans. Existing methods often rely on manual tuning and can be computationally slower; an automatic, data-driven correction is desirable.

Method: Use reversed phase-encoding EPI pairs. Model each distortion-field column as a Wasserstein-2 barycentric displacement between opposing-polarity intensity profiles. Apply spectral-domain regularization with a bending-energy penalty. Automatically select regularization strength via the Morozov discrepancy principle. Evaluate on HCP left-right/right-left b0 pairs with co-registered T1, comparing to FSL TOPUP.

Result: On HCP data, SuCor achieves mean volumetric mutual information with T1 of 0.341 versus 0.317 for TOPUP, and runs in ~12 seconds on a single CPU core.

Conclusion: SuCor provides a fast, auto-tuned OT-based correction that improves alignment with structural MRI over TOPUP in the tested dataset.

Abstract: We present SuCor, a method for correcting susceptibility induced geometric distortions in echo planar imaging (EPI) using optimal transport (OT) along the phase encoding direction. Given a pair of reversed phase encoding EPI volumes, we model each column of the distortion field as a Wasserstein-2 barycentric displacement between the opposing-polarity intensity profiles. Regularization is performed in the spectral domain using a bending-energy penalty whose strength is selected automatically via the Morozov discrepancy principle, requiring no manual tuning. On a human connectome project (HCP) dataset with left-right/right-left b0 EPI pairs and a co-registered T1 structural reference, SuCor achieves a mean volumetric mutual information of 0.341 with the T1 image, compared to 0.317 for FSL TOPUP, while running in approximately 12 seconds on a single CPU core.

</details>


### [127] [Dual Stream Independence Decoupling for True Emotion Recognition under Masked Expressions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16760)
*Jinsheng Wei,Xiguang Zhang,Zheng Shi,Guanming Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Apexframe-based paradigm to classify true emotions from masked expressions using a stable apexframe, coupled with a dual-stream independence decoupling framework that separates true vs disguised emotion features via a decoupling loss group and a Hilbert-Schmidt Independence (HSIC) loss, improving recognition performance over onset-frame methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recognizing true emotions under face masking is hard; onset-frame cues may leak true emotion information, failing to reflect a stable disguise state. A stable apexframe can better represent the disguised state and facilitate learning of true vs disguised signals.

Method: Introduce an apexframe-based paradigm and a dual-stream independence decoupling framework. Use a decoupling loss group (two classification losses for true emotion and disguised expression) plus an HSIC loss to enforce independence between the two feature streams.

Result: Experiments show that the apexframe-based paradigm is challenging (a difficult task), and the proposed decoupling framework improves recognition performance for true emotions by reducing interference from disguised expressions.

Conclusion: Apexframe-based approach is a viable but challenging pathway for true emotion recognition under masking; the independence decoupling framework with dedicated losses effectively disentangles true and disguised emotion features, boosting classification accuracy.

Abstract: Recongnizing true emotions from masked expressions is extremely challenging due to deliberate concealment. Existing paradigms recognize true emotions from masked-expression clips that contain onsetframes just starting to disguise. However, this paradigm may not reflect the actual disguised state, as the onsetframe leaks the true emotional information without reaching a stable disguise state. Thus, this paper introduces a novel apexframe-based paradigm that classifies true emotions from the apexframe with a stable disguised state. Furthermore, this paper proposes a novel dual stream independence decoupling framework that decouples true and disguised emotion features, avoiding the interference of disguised emotions on true emotions. For efficient decoupling, we design a decoupling loss group, comprising two classification losses that learn true emotion and disguised expression features, respectively, and a Hilbert-Schmidt Independence loss that enhances the independence of two features. Experiments demonstrate that the apexframe-based paradigm is challenging, and the proposed decouple framework improves recogntion performances.

</details>


### [128] [GDPO-SR: Group Direct Preference Optimization for One-Step Generative Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16769)
*Qiaosi Yi,Shuai Li,Rongyuan Wu,Lingchen Sun,Zhengqiang Zhang,Lei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GDPO combines group-relative policy optimization with Direct Preference Optimization for one-step generative image super-resolution, using a noise-aware diffusion model with an unequal-timestep strategy and an attribute-aware reward to optimize online samples, showing improved performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current one-step RL methods for ISR struggle with limited stochasticity (DPO offline pair generation) and neglect local details (GRPO's whole-image likelihood). A method that increases output diversity, decouples noise timing, and evaluates local sample attributes could enhance one-step ISR.

Method: Introduce GDPO: (1) a noise-aware one-step diffusion model to generate diverse ISR outputs; (2) an unequal-timestep strategy to decouple the timestep of noise addition from diffusion; (3) integrate GRPO principles into DPO to compute group-relative advantage for online samples; (4) design an attribute-aware reward that evaluates samples based on smooth and texture area statistics.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that GDPO enhances the performance of one-step generative ISR models; code available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: GDPO effectively fuses GRPO and DPO for one-step ISR, enabling diverse outputs through controlled noise and improving training via group-relative advantages and attribute-aware rewards.

Abstract: Recently, reinforcement learning (RL) has been employed for improving generative image super-resolution (ISR) performance. However, the current efforts are focused on multi-step generative ISR, while one-step generative ISR remains underexplored due to its limited stochasticity. In addition, RL methods such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) require the generation of positive and negative sample pairs offline, leading to a limited number of samples, while Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) only calculates the likelihood of the entire image, ignoring local details that are crucial for ISR. In this paper, we propose Group Direct Preference Optimization (GDPO), a novel approach to integrate RL into one-step generative ISR model training. First, we introduce a noise-aware one-step diffusion model that can generate diverse ISR outputs. To prevent performance degradation caused by noise injection, we introduce an unequal-timestep strategy to decouple the timestep of noise addition from that of diffusion. We then present the GDPO strategy, which integrates the principle of GRPO into DPO, to calculate the group-relative advantage of each online generated sample for model optimization. Meanwhile, an attribute-aware reward function is designed to dynamically evaluate the score of each sample based on its statistics of smooth and texture areas. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of GDPO in enhancing the performance of one-step generative ISR models. Code: https://github.com/Joyies/GDPO.

</details>


### [129] [IOSVLM: A 3D Vision-Language Model for Unified Dental Diagnosis from Intraoral Scans](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16781)
*Huimin Xiong,Zijie Meng,Tianxiang Hu,Chenyi Zhou,Yang Feng,Zuozhu Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An end-to-end 3D vision-language model (IOSVLM) for intraoral scans is proposed, using point-cloud 3D data and a 3D encoder-projector-LLM to enable unified diagnosis and VQA, along with IOSVQA—a large multi-source dataset for 23 oral diseases. A geometry-to-chromatic proxy and two-stage curriculum training address color-geometry gaps and robustness, yielding substantial improvements over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage native 3D geometry of intraoral scans for unified dental diagnosis and report generation, addressing heterogeneous scan types, disease co-occurrence with class imbalance, and scarcity of paired 3D IOS-text data.

Method: Propose IOSVLM: an end-to-end 3D VLM that represents scans as point clouds with a 3D encoder-projector-LLM architecture for unified diagnosis and VQA. Introduce IOSVQA dataset (19,002 cases, 249,055 VQA pairs across 23 diseases and diverse scan types). Employ a geometry-to-chromatic proxy to bridge color-free 3D data with color-dependent pre-training, and apply a two-stage curriculum training strategy to improve robustness.

Result:  IOSVLM/outperforms strong baselines with macro metric gains: at least +9.58% macro accuracy and +1.46% macro F1.

Conclusion: Direct 3D geometry modeling enhances IOS-based diagnosis; the geometry-aware 3D VLM and the IOSVQA dataset enable unified diagnosis and generative VQA on dental IOS data.

Abstract: 3D intraoral scans (IOS) are increasingly adopted in routine dentistry due to abundant geometric evidence, and unified multi-disease diagnosis is desirable for clinical documentation and communication. While recent works introduce dental vision-language models (VLMs) to enable unified diagnosis and report generation on 2D images or multi-view images rendered from IOS, they do not fully leverage native 3D geometry. Such work is necessary and also challenging, due to: (i) heterogeneous scan forms and the complex IOS topology, (ii) multi-disease co-occurrence with class imbalance and fine-grained morphological ambiguity, (iii) limited paired 3D IOS-text data. Thus, we present IOSVLM, an end-to-end 3D VLM that represents scans as point clouds and follows a 3D encoder-projector-LLM design for unified diagnosis and generative visual question-answering (VQA), together with IOSVQA, a large-scale multi-source IOS diagnosis VQA dataset comprising 19,002 cases and 249,055 VQA pairs over 23 oral diseases and heterogeneous scan types. To address the distribution gap between color-free IOS data and color-dependent 3D pre-training, we propose a geometry-to-chromatic proxy that stabilizes fine-grained geometric perception and cross-modal alignment. A two-stage curriculum training strategy further enhances robustness. IOSVLM consistently outperforms strong baselines, achieving gains of at least +9.58% macro accuracy and +1.46% macro F1, indicating the effectiveness of direct 3D geometry modeling for IOS-based diagnosis.

</details>


### [130] [V-Co: A Closer Look at Visual Representation Alignment via Co-Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16792)
*Han Lin,Xichen Pan,Zun Wang,Yue Zhang,Chu Wang,Jaemin Cho,Mohit Bansal*
