Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A systematic investigation (V-Co) of visual co-denoising in pixel-space diffusion, identifying four essential ingredients to enable effective representation-aligned generation and demonstrating improved ImageNet-256 results with fewer training epochs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify which design choices in visual co-denoising are genuinely beneficial and provide guidance for representation-aligned diffusion; address the weak semantic supervision in pixel-space diffusion and the need to disentangle design factors.

Method: JiT-based unified framework with a fully dual-stream architecture preserving feature-specific computation while enabling cross-stream interaction; a structurally defined unconditional predictor for classifier-free guidance (CFG); a perceptual-drifting hybrid loss for stronger semantic supervision; RMS-based feature rescaling for cross-stream calibration; comprehensive ablations on ImageNet-256.

Result: V-Co outperforms the underlying pixel-space diffusion baseline and strong prior pixel-diffusion methods at comparable model sizes, and does so with fewer training epochs.

Conclusion: Four ingredients constitute a simple, effective recipe for visual co-denoising, providing practical guidance for future representation-aligned generative models and a clearer understanding of what drives performance in co-denoising.

Abstract: Pixel-space diffusion has recently re-emerged as a strong alternative to latent diffusion, enabling high-quality generation without pretrained autoencoders. However, standard pixel-space diffusion models receive relatively weak semantic supervision and are not explicitly designed to capture high-level visual structure. Recent representation-alignment methods (e.g., REPA) suggest that pretrained visual features can substantially improve diffusion training, and visual co-denoising has emerged as a promising direction for incorporating such features into the generative process. However, existing co-denoising approaches often entangle multiple design choices, making it unclear which design choices are truly essential. Therefore, we present V-Co, a systematic study of visual co-denoising in a unified JiT-based framework. This controlled setting allows us to isolate the ingredients that make visual co-denoising effective. Our study reveals four key ingredients for effective visual co-denoising. First, preserving feature-specific computation while enabling flexible cross-stream interaction motivates a fully dual-stream architecture. Second, effective classifier-free guidance (CFG) requires a structurally defined unconditional prediction. Third, stronger semantic supervision is best provided by a perceptual-drifting hybrid loss. Fourth, stable co-denoising further requires proper cross-stream calibration, which we realize through RMS-based feature rescaling. Together, these findings yield a simple recipe for visual co-denoising. Experiments on ImageNet-256 show that, at comparable model sizes, V-Co outperforms the underlying pixel-space diffusion baseline and strong prior pixel-diffusion methods while using fewer training epochs, offering practical guidance for future representation-aligned generative models.

</details>


### [131] [WildDepth: A Multimodal Dataset for 3D Wildlife Perception and Depth Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16816)
*Muhammad Aamir,Naoya Muramatsu,Sangyun Shin,Matthew Wijers,Jiaxing Jhong,Xinyu Hou,Amir Patel,Andrew Markham*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WildDepth introduces a synchronized RGB-LiDAR dataset and benchmarks for depth estimation, behavior detection, and 3D reconstruction across diverse animal species, showing gains from multimodal data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing animal depth models are trained on metric-scale data or image-only datasets, limiting reliability and cross-domain generalization; there is a need for a multimodal, scalable benchmark spanning domestic to wild animals.

Method: Dataset collection with synchronized RGB and LiDAR across varied animal categories; development of benchmarks for depth estimation and 3D reconstruction; comparative experiments with single-modality vs multimodal fusion; evaluation metrics include RMSE for depth and Chamfer distance for 3D reconstruction.

Result: Incorporating multimodal data improves depth reliability by up to 10% RMSE; RGB-LiDAR fusion improves 3D reconstruction fidelity by 12% Chamfer distance; demonstrates advantage of multimodal perception.

Conclusion: Public release of WildDepth and benchmarks aims to foster robust multimodal perception systems that generalize across domains, enabling more accurate depth estimation and 3D reconstruction in animal scenes.

Abstract: Depth estimation and 3D reconstruction have been extensively studied as core topics in computer vision. Starting from rigid objects with relatively simple geometric shapes, such as vehicles, the research has expanded to address general objects, including challenging deformable objects, such as humans and animals. However, for the animal, in particular, the majority of existing models are trained based on datasets without metric scale, which can help validate image-only models. To address this limitation, we present WildDepth, a multimodal dataset and benchmark suite for depth estimation, behavior detection, and 3D reconstruction from diverse categories of animals ranging from domestic to wild environments with synchronized RGB and LiDAR. Experimental results show that the use of multi-modal data improves depth reliability by up to 10% RMSE, while RGB-LiDAR fusion enhances 3D reconstruction fidelity by 12% in Chamfer distance. By releasing WildDepth and its benchmarks, we aim to foster robust multimodal perception systems that generalize across domains.

</details>


### [132] [Deep Reinforcement Learning-driven Edge Offloading for Latency-constrained XR pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16823)
*Sourya Saha,Saptarshi Debroy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A battery-aware online DRL framework for edge-assisted XR that jointly optimizes execution placement, workload quality, latency, and battery dynamics to extend device lifetime while preserving real-time responsiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Immersive XR workloads are highly latency-sensitive and energy-constrained. Existing offloading methods optimize average metrics and miss the sustained interaction between latency requirements and battery life in closed-loop XR scenarios.

Method: Proposes an online decision mechanism using a lightweight deep reinforcement learning policy that continuously adapts execution placement under dynamic network conditions, balancing latency compliance (motion-to-photon) with battery health.

Result: The approach extends projected device battery lifetime by up to 163% compared to latency-optimal local execution, while maintaining over 90% motion-to-photon latency compliance under stable network. Latency compliance remains above 80% even with substantially limited network bandwidth.

Conclusion: Explicitly managing latency-energy trade-offs via an adaptive DRL-based execution framework improves energy efficiency and robustness of edge-assisted XR without sacrificing real-time performance.

Abstract: Immersive extended reality (XR) applications introduce latency-critical workloads that must satisfy stringent real-time responsiveness while operating on energy- and battery-constrained devices, making execution placement between end devices and nearby edge servers a fundamental systems challenge. Existing approaches to adaptive execution and computation offloading typically optimize average performance metrics and do not fully capture the sustained interaction between real-time latency requirements and device battery lifetime in closed-loop XR workloads. In this paper, we present a battery-aware execution management framework for edge-assisted XR systems that jointly considers execution placement, workload quality, latency requirements, and battery dynamics. We design an online decision mechanism based on a lightweight deep reinforcement learning policy that continuously adapts execution decisions under dynamic network conditions while maintaining high motion-to-photon latency compliance. Experimental results show that the proposed approach extends the projected device battery lifetime by up to 163% compared to latency-optimal local execution while maintaining over 90% motion-to-photon latency compliance under stable network conditions. Such compliance does not fall below 80% even under significantly limited network bandwidth availability, thereby demonstrating the effectiveness of explicitly managing latency-energy trade-offs in immersive XR systems.

</details>


### [133] [An assessment of data-centric methods for label noise identification in remote sensing data sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16835)
*Felix Kröber,Genc Hoxha,Ribana Roscher*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Examines three data-centric methods to handle label noise in remote sensing, injecting 10–70% synthetic label noise on two datasets, and analyzes noise identification and task performance to determine best method per setting and highlight transfer gaps to remote sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Label noise degrades generalization, and remote sensing has limited exploration of data-centric approaches that identify and isolate noisy labels; a systematic analysis is needed to guide method selection and practical deployment in remote sensing.

Method: Inject controlled label noise (10–70%) into two benchmark remote sensing datasets. Evaluate three data-centric methods designed to identify and filter noisy labels. Assess their performance in noise detection and the resulting impact on the primary task (e.g., classification). Compare methods across noise regimes and provide guidance on when to use which method; discuss transferability to remote sensing.

Result: The data-centric methods effectively identify and filter noisy labels and improve task performance. Performance gains depend on the setting; no single method dominates across all scenarios. The study demonstrates the value of such methods in remote sensing and clarifies when each method is preferable. It also identifies areas where transfer to remote sensing remains challenging and calls for further research.

Conclusion: Data-centric label-noise methods are valuable for remote sensing, but more work is needed to transfer them to real-world datasets and settings. The work moves toward bridging methodological development with practical remote sensing usage.

Abstract: Label noise in the sense of incorrect labels is present in many real-world data sets and is known to severely limit the generalizability of deep learning models. In the field of remote sensing, however, automated treatment of label noise in data sets has received little attention to date. In particular, there is a lack of systematic analysis of the performance of data-centric methods that not only cope with label noise but also explicitly identify and isolate noisy labels. In this paper, we examine three such methods and evaluate their behavior under different label noise assumptions. To do this, we inject different types of label noise with noise levels ranging from 10 to 70% into two benchmark data sets, followed by an analysis of how well the selected methods filter the label noise and how this affects task performances. With our analyses, we clearly prove the value of data-centric methods for both parts - label noise identification and task performance improvements. Our analyses provide insights into which method is the best choice depending on the setting and objective. Finally, we show in which areas there is still a need for research in the transfer of data-centric label noise methods to remote sensing data. As such, our work is a step forward in bridging the methodological establishment of data-centric label noise methods and their usage in practical settings in the remote sensing domain.

</details>


### [134] [What DINO saw: ALiBi positional encoding reduces positional bias in Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16840)
*Moritz Pawlowsky,Antonis Vamvakeros,Alexander Weiss,Anja Bielefeld,Samuel J. Cooper,Ronan Docherty*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViTs exhibit positional biases across encodings; linear probing reveals bias; finetuning with ALiBi reduces bias while preserving semantics, enabling effective segmentation of isotropic microscopic images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In fields like material science, images are isotropic and lack preferred directions. Positional encodings in ViTs can introduce artefacts that hinder zero-shot adaptation and domain transfer.

Method: Evaluate positional bias with linear probing across multiple objectives and positional encodings in ViTs (e.g., DINOv2). Finetune models to use ALiBi relative positional encoding and assess whether semantic content is preserved. Apply the unbiased representations to trainable segmentation of complex microscopy images.

Result: Positional bias is detectable across a range of objectives and encodings. Finetuning with ALiBi reduces bias while retaining general semantic features. The unbiased representations enable effective trainable segmentation of complex microscopy images.

Conclusion: ALiBi-based finetuning mitigates positional bias in Vision Transformers without harming downstream performance, improving zero-shot adaptability for isotropic domains such as material microstructures.

Abstract: Vision transformers (ViTs) - especially feature foundation models like DINOv2 - learn rich representations useful for many downstream tasks. However, architectural choices (such as positional encoding) can lead to these models displaying positional biases and artefacts independent of semantic content. This makes zero-shot adaption difficult in fields like material science, where images are often cross-sections of homogeneous microstructure (i.e. having no preferred direction). In this work, we investigate the positional bias in ViTs via linear probing, finding it present across a range of objectives and positional encodings, and subsequently reduce it by finetuning models to use ALiBi relative positional encoding. We demonstrate that these models retain desirable general semantics and their unbiased features can be used successfully in trainable segmentation of complex microscopy images.

</details>


### [135] [M^3: Dense Matching Meets Multi-View Foundation Models for Monocular Gaussian Splatting SLAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16844)
*Kerui Ren,Guanghao Li,Changjian Jiang,Yingxiang Xu,Tao Lu,Linning Xu,Junting Dong,Jiangmiao Pang,Mulin Yu,Bo Dai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: M^3 bridges multi-view foundation models with geometry-based monocular SLAM by adding a dedicated Matching head for dense correspondences and stability mechanisms, achieving state-of-the-art pose and reconstruction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the precision gap of feed-forward multi-view foundation models in dynamic, online monocular SLAM, where pixel-level correspondences are insufficient for rigorous geometric optimization.

Method: Augment the MV foundation model with a specialized Matching head to produce fine-grained dense correspondences, integrate into Monocular Gaussian Splatting SLAM, and enhance stability via dynamic area suppression and cross-inference intrinsic alignment.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art pose estimation and scene reconstruction; reduces ATE RMSE by 64.3% versus VGGT-SLAM 2.0; improves PSNR by 2.11 dB on ScanNet++ beyond ARTDECO.

Conclusion: M^3 effectively combines foundation-model-based Matching with geometry-driven SLAM techniques to deliver robust, high-precision monocular SLAM in diverse environments.

Abstract: Streaming reconstruction from uncalibrated monocular video remains challenging, as it requires both high-precision pose estimation and computationally efficient online refinement in dynamic environments. While coupling 3D foundation models with SLAM frameworks is a promising paradigm, a critical bottleneck persists: most multi-view foundation models estimate poses in a feed-forward manner, yielding pixel-level correspondences that lack the requisite precision for rigorous geometric optimization. To address this, we present M^3, which augments the Multi-view foundation model with a dedicated Matching head to facilitate fine-grained dense correspondences and integrates it into a robust Monocular Gaussian Splatting SLAM. M^3 further enhances tracking stability by incorporating dynamic area suppression and cross-inference intrinsic alignment. Extensive experiments on diverse indoor and outdoor benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy in both pose estimation and scene reconstruction. Notably, M^3 reduces ATE RMSE by 64.3% compared to VGGT-SLAM 2.0 and outperforms ARTDECO by 2.11 dB in PSNR on the ScanNet++ dataset.

</details>


### [136] [SparkVSR: Interactive Video Super-Resolution via Sparse Keyframe Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16864)
*Jiongze Yu,Xiangbo Gao,Pooja Verlani,Akshay Gadde,Yilin Wang,Balu Adsumilli,Zhengzhong Tu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SparkVSR proposes an interactive video super-resolution framework that uses sparse keyframes as control signals to guide cross-space propagation from LR video latents to HR keyframe latents via a keyframe-conditioned latent-pixel two-stage training pipeline. It supports flexible keyframe selection and reference-free guidance to balance fidelity to keyframes with blind restoration, improving temporal consistency and restoration quality on benchmarks and generalizing to tasks like old-film restoration and style transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of user controllability in VSR, where outputs are largely determined by the model (black-box inference), by introducing sparse keyframe priors that can steer the restoration while preserving original LR motion.

Method: A keyframe-conditioned latent-pixel two-stage training pipeline that fuses LR video latents with sparsely encoded HR keyframe latents to enable robust cross-space propagation and detail refinement; inference-time mechanisms include flexible keyframe selection (manual, I-frame extraction, or random) and reference-free guidance balancing keyframe adherence and blind restoration.

Result: Empirical results show improved temporal consistency and restoration quality; reported gains on CLIP-IQA, DOVER, and MUSIQ metrics up to 24.6%, 21.8%, and 5.6% over baselines; demonstrates robustness to imperfect or absent keyframes and applicability to other tasks.

Conclusion: SparkVSR offers a generic, interactive, keyframe-conditioned framework for VSR that enhances controllability and restoration quality, and can be applied to unseen tasks such as old-film restoration and video style transfer.

Abstract: Video Super-Resolution (VSR) aims to restore high-quality video frames from low-resolution (LR) estimates, yet most existing VSR approaches behave like black boxes at inference time: users cannot reliably correct unexpected artifacts, but instead can only accept whatever the model produces. In this paper, we propose a novel interactive VSR framework dubbed SparkVSR that makes sparse keyframes a simple and expressive control signal. Specifically, users can first super-resolve or optionally a small set of keyframes using any off-the-shelf image super-resolution (ISR) model, then SparkVSR propagates the keyframe priors to the entire video sequence while remaining grounded by the original LR video motion. Concretely, we introduce a keyframe-conditioned latent-pixel two-stage training pipeline that fuses LR video latents with sparsely encoded HR keyframe latents to learn robust cross-space propagation and refine perceptual details. At inference time, SparkVSR supports flexible keyframe selection (manual specification, codec I-frame extraction, or random sampling) and a reference-free guidance mechanism that continuously balances keyframe adherence and blind restoration, ensuring robust performance even when reference keyframes are absent or imperfect. Experiments on multiple VSR benchmarks demonstrate improved temporal consistency and strong restoration quality, surpassing baselines by up to 24.6%, 21.8%, and 5.6% on CLIP-IQA, DOVER, and MUSIQ, respectively, enabling controllable, keyframe-driven video super-resolution. Moreover, we demonstrate that SparkVSR is a generic interactive, keyframe-conditioned video processing framework as it can be applied out of the box to unseen tasks such as old-film restoration and video style transfer. Our project page is available at: https://sparkvsr.github.io/

</details>


### [137] [SegviGen: Repurposing 3D Generative Model for Part Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16869)
*Lin Li,Haoran Feng,Zehuan Huang,Haohua Chen,Wenbo Nie,Shaohua Hou,Keqing Fan,Pan Hu,Sheng Wang,Buyu Li,Lu Sheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SegviGen uses pretrained 3D generative priors to perform 3D part segmentation via colorization, enabling interactive and full segmentation with minimal supervision and outperforming prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches either rely on 2D priors transferred to 3D (prone to cross-view inconsistency and blurred boundaries) or require large-scale annotated 3D data for native 3D segmentation. There is a need to leverage generative priors to enable efficient 3D part segmentation with limited supervision.

Method: Encode a 3D asset and predict part-indicative colors on active voxels of a geometry-aligned reconstruction. SegviGen supports interactive part segmentation, full segmentation, and full segmentation with 2D guidance within a single framework, leveraging structured priors from pretrained 3D generative models to induce segmentation via colorization.

Result: SegviGen improves over prior state of the art by 40% on interactive part segmentation and 15% on full segmentation, while using only 0.32% of the labeled training data.

Conclusion: Pretrained 3D generative priors transfer effectively to 3D part segmentation, enabling strong performance with limited supervision and a unified framework for multiple segmentation modes.

Abstract: We introduce SegviGen, a framework that repurposes native 3D generative models for 3D part segmentation. Existing pipelines either lift strong 2D priors into 3D via distillation or multi-view mask aggregation, often suffering from cross-view inconsistency and blurred boundaries, or explore native 3D discriminative segmentation, which typically requires large-scale annotated 3D data and substantial training resources. In contrast, SegviGen leverages the structured priors encoded in pretrained 3D generative model to induce segmentation through distinctive part colorization, establishing a novel and efficient framework for part segmentation. Specifically, SegviGen encodes a 3D asset and predicts part-indicative colors on active voxels of a geometry-aligned reconstruction. It supports interactive part segmentation, full segmentation, and full segmentation with 2D guidance in a unified framework. Extensive experiments show that SegviGen improves over the prior state of the art by 40% on interactive part segmentation and by 15% on full segmentation, while using only 0.32% of the labeled training data. It demonstrates that pretrained 3D generative priors transfer effectively to 3D part segmentation, enabling strong performance with limited supervision. See our project page at https://fenghora.github.io/SegviGen-Page/.

</details>


### [138] [Demystifing Video Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16870)
*Ruisi Wang,Zhongang Cai,Fanyi Pu,Junxiang Xu,Wanqi Yin,Maijunxian Wang,Ran Ji,Chenyang Gu,Bo Li,Ziqi Huang,Hokin Deng,Dahua Lin,Ziwei Liu,Lei Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based video models reason via diffusion denoising steps (Chain-of-Steps) rather than across frames (Chain-of-Frames), with emergent behaviors and layer-wise specialization; a training-free latent-trajectory ensembling strategy further suggests how to improve reasoning. Provides groundwork to harness diffusion dynamics for video intelligence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the Chain-of-Frames assumption and elucidate where reasoning arises in diffusion video models; identify emergent behaviors and mechanisms that govern performance.

Method: Qualitative analysis and targeted probing; analysis of denoising steps; investigation of Diffusion Transformers; latent trajectory ensembling as a proof-of-concept.

Result: Reasoning emerges along denoising steps (CoS); early steps explore multiple candidates, later steps converge; emergent mechanisms: working memory, self-correction/enhancement, perception before action; diffusion-transformer layers specialize (early: perceptual structure, middle: reasoning, late: consolidation); ensembling across seeds improves reasoning.

Conclusion: We provide a systematic understanding of reasoning dynamics in video diffusion models; the findings offer a foundation for future research to exploit these dynamics and propose a simple training-free ensembling strategy as proof-of-concept.

Abstract: Recent advances in video generation have revealed an unexpected phenomenon: diffusion-based video models exhibit non-trivial reasoning capabilities. Prior work attributes this to a Chain-of-Frames (CoF) mechanism, where reasoning is assumed to unfold sequentially across video frames. In this work, we challenge this assumption and uncover a fundamentally different mechanism. We show that reasoning in video models instead primarily emerges along the diffusion denoising steps. Through qualitative analysis and targeted probing experiments, we find that models explore multiple candidate solutions in early denoising steps and progressively converge to a final answer, a process we term Chain-of-Steps (CoS). Beyond this core mechanism, we identify several emergent reasoning behaviors critical to model performance: (1) working memory, enabling persistent reference; (2) self-correction and enhancement, allowing recovery from incorrect intermediate solutions; and (3) perception before action, where early steps establish semantic grounding and later steps perform structured manipulation. During a diffusion step, we further uncover self-evolved functional specialization within Diffusion Transformers, where early layers encode dense perceptual structure, middle layers execute reasoning, and later layers consolidate latent representations. Motivated by these insights, we present a simple training-free strategy as a proof-of-concept, demonstrating how reasoning can be improved by ensembling latent trajectories from identical models with different random seeds. Overall, our work provides a systematic understanding of how reasoning emerges in video generation models, offering a foundation to guide future research in better exploiting the inherent reasoning dynamics of video models as a new substrate for intelligence.

</details>


### [139] [WorldCam: Interactive Autoregressive 3D Gaming Worlds with Camera Pose as a Unifying Geometric Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16871)
*Jisu Nam,Yicong Hong,Chun-Hao Paul Huang,Feng Liu,JoungBin Lee,Jiyoung Kim,Siyoon Jin,Yunsung Lee,Jaeyoon Jung,Suhwan Choi,Seungryong Kim,Yang Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Grounds action control and long-horizon 3D consistency in video diffusion transformers by using camera pose as a unifying geometric representation; introduces a Lie-algebra-based continuous action space, a camera embedder for action alignment, and global pose indexing for revisiting past observations; includes a 3000-minute dataset; reports state-of-the-art improvements in controllability, visual quality, and 3D consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing interactive world models struggle with precise action control and long-horizon 3D consistency because actions are treated as abstract conditioning signals. A geometric coupling—where actions induce camera motion that aggregates into a global pose—offers a principled basis for grounding control and maintaining 3D coherence.

Method: Define a physics-based continuous action space and represent user inputs in the Lie algebra to compute precise 6-DoF camera poses. Inject poses into the generative model via a camera embedder to align actions. Use global camera poses as spatial indices to retrieve relevant past observations, enabling geometrically consistent revisiting during long-horizon navigation. Build a large-scale dataset (3,000 minutes) with camera trajectories and textual descriptions.

Result: The proposed approach substantially outperforms state-of-the-art interactive gaming world models in action controllability, long-horizon visual quality, and 3D spatial consistency.

Conclusion: Camera pose serves as a unifying geometric representation that grounds immediate action control and long-term 3D consistency, with a dataset supporting future research and improved interactive gaming world modeling.

Abstract: Recent advances in video diffusion transformers have enabled interactive gaming world models that allow users to explore generated environments over extended horizons. However, existing approaches struggle with precise action control and long-horizon 3D consistency. Most prior works treat user actions as abstract conditioning signals, overlooking the fundamental geometric coupling between actions and the 3D world, whereby actions induce relative camera motions that accumulate into a global camera pose within a 3D world. In this paper, we establish camera pose as a unifying geometric representation to jointly ground immediate action control and long-term 3D consistency. First, we define a physics-based continuous action space and represent user inputs in the Lie algebra to derive precise 6-DoF camera poses, which are injected into the generative model via a camera embedder to ensure accurate action alignment. Second, we use global camera poses as spatial indices to retrieve relevant past observations, enabling geometrically consistent revisiting of locations during long-horizon navigation. To support this research, we introduce a large-scale dataset comprising 3,000 minutes of authentic human gameplay annotated with camera trajectories and textual descriptions. Extensive experiments show that our approach substantially outperforms state-of-the-art interactive gaming world models in action controllability, long-horizon visual quality, and 3D spatial consistency.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [140] [You've Got a Golden Ticket: Improving Generative Robot Policies With A Single Noise Vector](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15757)
*Omkar Patil,Ondrej Biza,Thomas Weng,Karl Schmeckpeper,Wil Thomason,Xiaohan Zhang,Robin Walters,Nakul Gopalan,Sebastian Castro,Eric Rosen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Fixing the initial noise input to pretrained diffusion/flow policies (golden tickets) boosts downstream rewards without retraining. A Monte Carlo search identifies constant initial noise inputs that improve performance across tasks (38/43), enabling Pareto frontiers in multi-task settings; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable performance improvement of frozen pretrained generative policies by exploiting sensitivity to the initial input noise, without training or altering models.

Method: Monte-Carlo policy evaluation on fixed initial-noise injections. Keep the pretrained policy frozen; no new networks are trained. Search for a constant initial noise vector (the golden ticket) that, when fed into diffusion/flow policies, yields higher task rewards. Applicable to diffusion and flow-matching policies and thus many VLAs. Use sparse episodic rewards to guide selection; 50-episode search windows reported.

Result: Policy improvements observed in 38 of 43 tasks (simulated and real-world robot manipulation). Relative improvements up to 58% in simulated tasks; up to 60% improvement within 50 search episodes for real-world tasks. Golden tickets also create Pareto fronts for multi-objectives (e.g., speed vs success) and can transfer across related tasks.

Conclusion: Golden tickets provide a simple, deployable mechanism to boost pretrained generative policies without retraining or extra infrastructure. They are widely applicable across diffusion/flow policies, enable multi-task diversity, and can transfer between related tasks. A codebase with pretrained policies and tickets is released.

Abstract: What happens when a pretrained generative robot policy is provided a constant initial noise as input, rather than repeatedly sampling it from a Gaussian? We demonstrate that the performance of a pretrained, frozen diffusion or flow matching policy can be improved with respect to a downstream reward by swapping the sampling of initial noise from the prior distribution (typically isotropic Gaussian) with a well-chosen, constant initial noise input -- a golden ticket. We propose a search method to find golden tickets using Monte-Carlo policy evaluation that keeps the pretrained policy frozen, does not train any new networks, and is applicable to all diffusion/flow matching policies (and therefore many VLAs). Our approach to policy improvement makes no assumptions beyond being able to inject initial noise into the policy and calculate (sparse) task rewards of episode rollouts, making it deployable with no additional infrastructure or models. Our method improves the performance of policies in 38 out of 43 tasks across simulated and real-world robot manipulation benchmarks, with relative improvements in success rate by up to 58% for some simulated tasks, and 60% within 50 search episodes for real-world tasks. We also show unique benefits of golden tickets for multi-task settings: the diversity of behaviors from different tickets naturally defines a Pareto frontier for balancing different objectives (e.g., speed, success rates); in VLAs, we find that a golden ticket optimized for one task can also boost performance in other related tasks. We release a codebase with pretrained policies and golden tickets for simulation benchmarks using VLAs, diffusion policies, and flow matching policies.

</details>


### [141] [Simulation Distillation: Pretraining World Models in Simulation for Rapid Real-World Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15759)
*Jacob Levy,Tyler Westenbroek,Kevin Huang,Fernando Palafox,Patrick Yin,Shayegan Omidshafiei,Dong-Ki Kim,Abhishek Gupta,David Fridovich-Keil*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SimDist distills simulator priors into a latent world model to enable rapid, data-efficient sim-to-real adaptation via online planning and supervised dynamics finetuning, avoiding long-horizon credit assignment and providing dense planning signals from perception; it outperforms prior methods in manipulation and locomotion tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotics faces sim-to-real mismatch, where model-based or RL policies trained in simulation fail on real hardware. In low-data real-world settings, exploration and long-horizon credit assignment are particularly challenging, hindering reliable adaptation.

Method: SimDist distills structural priors from a simulator into a latent world model and enables rapid real-world adaptation through online planning and supervised dynamics finetuning. It transfers reward and value models from simulation to provide dense planning signals from raw perception without requiring value learning during deployment, reducing real-world adaptation to short-horizon system identification.

Result: Empirically, SimDist yields substantial improvements in data efficiency, stability, and final performance across precise manipulation and quadruped locomotion tasks compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: SimDist enables fast, stable sim-to-real adaptation by importing simulator priors into a latent model and leveraging online planning with supervised dynamics finetuning; adaptation becomes short-horizon system identification, avoiding long-horizon credit assignment and facilitating rapid performance gains.

Abstract: Simulation-to-real transfer remains a central challenge in robotics, as mismatches between simulated and real-world dynamics often lead to failures. While reinforcement learning offers a principled mechanism for adaptation, existing sim-to-real finetuning methods struggle with exploration and long-horizon credit assignment in the low-data regimes typical of real-world robotics. We introduce Simulation Distillation (SimDist), a sim-to-real framework that distills structural priors from a simulator into a latent world model and enables rapid real-world adaptation via online planning and supervised dynamics finetuning. By transferring reward and value models directly from simulation, SimDist provides dense planning signals from raw perception without requiring value learning during deployment. As a result, real-world adaptation reduces to short-horizon system identification, avoiding long-horizon credit assignment and enabling fast, stable improvement. Across precise manipulation and quadruped locomotion tasks, SimDist substantially outperforms prior methods in data efficiency, stability, and final performance. Project website and code: https://sim-dist.github.io/

</details>


### [142] [CorrectionPlanner: Self-Correction Planner with Reinforcement Learning in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15771)
*Yihong Guo,Dongqiangzi Ye,Sijia Chen,Anqi Liu,Xianming Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Autonomous-planning with a self-correcting, autoregressive planner that generates motion tokens in a propose-evaluate-correct loop, using a collision critic to reject unsafe tokens and maintain a self-correction trace; trained with imitation learning and model-based RL using a world model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of explicit self-correction in learning-based planners for autonomous driving, which can lead to unsafe actions despite planning. The goal is to introduce a self-correcting mechanism that can iteratively adjust proposed actions to ensure safety.

Method: Introduce CorrectionPlanner, an autoregressive planner that generates motion tokens in a propose-evaluate-correct loop. At each step, propose a motion token, predict collision risk with a learned critic; if unsafe, retain the history of unsafe tokens as a self-correction trace, condition the next token on it, and repeat until a safe token is produced or safety is satisfied. Train via imitation learning followed by model-based RL using rollouts from a pretrained world model that captures agent reactivity. Evaluate in closed-loop scenarios, comparing collision rates and planning performance.

Result: Collision rate reduced by over 20% on Waymax; achieved state-of-the-art planning scores on nuPlan, indicating improved safety and planning performance in standard benchmarks.

Conclusion: Self-correction via an explicit planning trace in motion-token space enhances safety and planning quality in learning-based planners, with interpretability through the self-correction trace and strong performance on established benchmarks.

Abstract: Autonomous driving requires safe planning, but most learning-based planners lack explicit self-correction ability: once an unsafe action is proposed, there is no mechanism to correct it. Thus, we propose CorrectionPlanner, an autoregressive planner with self-correction that models planning as motion-token generation within a propose, evaluate, and correct loop. At each planning step, the policy proposes an action, namely a motion token, and a learned collision critic predicts whether it will induce a collision within a short horizon. If the critic predicts a collision, we retain the sequence of historical unsafe motion tokens as a self-correction trace, generate the next motion token conditioned on it, and repeat this process until a safe motion token is proposed or the safety criterion is met. This self-correction trace, consisting of all unsafe motion tokens, represents the planner's correction process in motion-token space, analogous to a reasoning trace in language models. We train the planner with imitation learning followed by model-based reinforcement learning using rollouts from a pretrained world model that realistically models agents' reactive behaviors. Closed-loop evaluations show that CorrectionPlanner reduces collision rate by over 20% on Waymax and achieves state-of-the-art planning scores on nuPlan.

</details>


### [143] [Emergent Dexterity via Diverse Resets and Large-Scale Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15789)
*Patrick Yin,Tyler Westenbroek,Zhengyu Zhang,Joshua Tran,Ignacio Dagnino,Eeshani Shilamkar,Numfor Mbiziwo-Tiapo,Simran Bagaria,Xinlei Liu,Galen Mullins,Andrey Kolobov,Abhishek Gupta*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OMNIreset enables on-policy RL to master long-horizon dexterous manipulation via automated simulator resets, using a single reward and fixed hyperparameters, avoiding demonstrations or curricula, and scaling with compute to improve transfer to the real world.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current sim-to-real RL methods rely on task-specific rewards, curricula, and demonstrations; they are brittle, struggle with long-horizon, contact-rich tasks, and do not scale well with compute. A simple, generalizable framework is needed to reduce manual engineering and improve robustness across tasks.

Method: Programmatically generate resets in simulation to expose the diverse robot-object interactions underlying dexterous manipulation. Use on-policy RL with a single reward, fixed hyperparameters, no curricula, and no human demonstrations. Convert additional compute into broader behavioral coverage via resets, enabling scalable learning and robust performance, then distill policies into visuomotor controllers for real-world transfer.

Result: The approach scales to long-horizon dexterous manipulation tasks beyond the capabilities of existing methods, learning robust policies over wide initial-condition ranges, and achieving higher robustness in sim-to-real transfer. It demonstrates that systematic resets can dramatically broaden exploration and performance.

Conclusion: OMNIreset is a simple, scalable framework that reduces manual engineering for dexterous manipulation, enabling on-policy RL to solve diverse tasks with a single reward and fixed hyperparameters, and supports zero-shot transfer to the real world through visuomotor policy distillation.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning in massively parallel physics simulations has driven major progress in sim-to-real robot learning. However, current approaches remain brittle and task-specific, relying on extensive per-task engineering to design rewards, curricula, and demonstrations. Even with this engineering, they often fail on long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation tasks and do not meaningfully scale with compute, as performance quickly saturates when training revisits the same narrow regions of state space. We introduce \Method, a simple and scalable framework that enables on-policy reinforcement learning to robustly solve a broad class of dexterous manipulation tasks using a single reward function, fixed algorithm hyperparameters, no curricula, and no human demonstrations. Our key insight is that long-horizon exploration can be dramatically simplified by using simulator resets to systematically expose the RL algorithm to the diverse set of robot-object interactions which underlie dexterous manipulation. \Method\ programmatically generates such resets with minimal human input, converting additional compute directly into broader behavioral coverage and continued performance gains. We show that \Method\ gracefully scales to long-horizon dexterous manipulation tasks beyond the capabilities of existing approaches and is able to learn robust policies over significantly wider ranges of initial conditions than baselines. Finally, we distill \Method \ into visuomotor policies which display robust retrying behavior and substantially higher success rates than baselines when transferred to the real world zero-shot. Project webpage: https://omnireset.github.io

</details>


### [144] [Robust Dynamic Object Detection in Cluttered Indoor Scenes via Learned Spatiotemporal Cues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15826)
*Juan Rached,Yixuan Jia,Kota Kondo,Jonathan P. How*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A LiDAR-only framework fuses temporal occupancy-grid motion segmentation with a learned BEV dynamic prior to robustly detect dynamic objects in clutter, achieving higher recall and F1 than the state-of-the-art while maintaining precision and pose accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of purely geometric LiDAR pipelines (missed dynamics near static structures) and vision-based methods (closed-set detectors, limited FOV) by developing a LiDAR-only system that leverages temporal motion cues and a learned dynamic prior to improve detection robustness in cluttered environments.

Method: Combine temporal occupancy-grid-based motion segmentation with a learned bird's-eye-view (BEV) dynamic prior. A fusion module prioritizes 3D detections when available and uses the dynamic prior to recover detections that would be missed due to proximity to static structures, improving recall in clutter.

Result: On motion-capture ground truth, the approach achieves about 28.67% higher recall and 18.50% higher F1 score than the state-of-the-art in substantially cluttered environments, while maintaining comparable precision and position error.

Conclusion: The proposed LiDAR-only framework effectively leverages both temporal motion information and a learned BEV prior to boost dynamic object detection in clutter, improving recall and F1 without sacrificing precision or localization accuracy; the fusion strategy helps recover near-static or near-occluded objects that would be missed by purely geometric or camera-based methods.

Abstract: Reliable dynamic object detection in cluttered environments remains a critical challenge for autonomous navigation. Purely geometric LiDAR pipelines that rely on clustering and heuristic filtering can miss dynamic obstacles when they move in close proximity to static structure or are only partially observed. Vision-augmented approaches can provide additional semantic cues, but are often limited by closed-set detectors and camera field-of-view constraints, reducing robustness to novel obstacles and out-of-frustum events. In this work, we present a LiDAR-only framework that fuses temporal occupancy-grid-based motion segmentation with a learned bird's-eye-view (BEV) dynamic prior. A fusion module prioritizes 3D detections when available, while using the learned dynamic grid to recover detections that would otherwise be lost due to proximity-induced false negatives. Experiments with motion-capture ground truth show our method achieves 28.67% higher recall and 18.50% higher F1 score than the state-of-the-art in substantially cluttered environments while maintaining comparable precision and position error.

</details>


### [145] [Gaze-Aware Task Progression Detection Framework for Human-Robot Interaction Using RGB Cameras](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15951)
*Linlin Cheng,Koen Hindriks,Artem V. Belopolsky*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Calibration-free RGB-only gaze-based HRI framework that uses a monocular camera to detect task progression via three AOIs, achieving 77.6% accuracy and offering more natural interaction with gains in comfort and social presence despite a slight latency increase.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gaze is a valuable cue for interpreting user attention and intent in HRI, but most gaze methods require specialized hardware. RGB-only, calibration-free approaches aim to enable practical, everyday deployment and natural communication via gaze in HRI.

Method: A calibration-free framework using the robot's built-in 640x480 monocular RGB camera and state-of-the-art gaze estimation to monitor attention across three AOIs (tablet, robot face, elsewhere). Systematic parameter optimization balances detection accuracy and interaction latency. Evaluation in a 'First Day at Work' scenario comparing gaze-based control to button-based interaction.

Result: Task completion detection accuracy of 77.6%. Compared to button-based interaction, the gaze-based system shows slightly higher response latency but preserves information retention and significantly improves comfort, social presence, and perceived naturalness. Most participants did not consciously use eye movements, highlighting gaze as an intuitive communicative cue.

Conclusion: RGB-only, calibration-free gaze-based HRI is feasible for natural, low-cost interaction and can enhance user experience in everyday tasks without specialized eye-tracking hardware.

Abstract: In human-robot interaction (HRI), detecting a human's gaze helps robots interpret user attention and intent. However, most gaze detection approaches rely on specialized eye-tracking hardware, limiting deployment in everyday settings. Appearance-based gaze estimation methods remove this dependency by using standard RGB cameras, but their practicality in HRI remains underexplored. We present a calibration-free framework for detecting task progression when information is conveyed via integrated display interfaces. The framework uses only the robot's built-in monocular RGB camera (640x480 resolution) and state-of-the-art gaze estimation to monitor attention patterns. It leverages natural behavior, where users shift focus from task interfaces to the robot's face to signal task completion, formalized through three Areas of Interest (AOI): tablet, robot face, and elsewhere. Systematic parameter optimization identifies configurations that balance detection accuracy and interaction latency. We validate our framework in a "First Day at Work" scenario, comparing it to button-based interaction. Results show a task completion detection accuracy of 77.6%. Compared to button-based interaction, the proposed system exhibits slightly higher response latency but preserves information retention and significantly improves comfort, social presence, and perceived naturalness. Notably, most participants reported that they did not consciously use eye movements to guide the interaction, underscoring the intuitive role of gaze as a communicative cue. This work demonstrates the feasibility of intuitive, low-cost, RGB-only gaze-based HRI for natural and engaging interactions.

</details>


### [146] [ExpertGen: Scalable Sim-to-Real Expert Policy Learning from Imperfect Behavior Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15956)
*Zifan Xu,Ran Gong,Maria Vittoria Minniti,Ahmet Salih Gundogdu,Eric Rosen,Kausik Sivakumar,Riedana Yan,Zixing Wang,Di Deng,Peter Stone,Xiaohan Zhang,Karl Schmeckpeper*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ExpertGen automates expert policy learning in simulation to enable scalable sim-to-real transfer for robotics. It freezes a diffusion-based policy trained on imperfect demonstrations and refines it with reinforcement learning by optimizing the initial noise, enabling safe, human-like exploration and high-task-success policies without reward engineering. The policies transfer to real hardware via DAgger, achieving high success on industrial and long-horizon tasks and demonstrating robust generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality robotics data for behavior cloning is scarce and expensive to collect in the real world. A scalable, sim-to-real approach is needed to generate expert-like policies without extensive human data collection or reward engineering.

Method: 1) Train a diffusion-based policy (the behavior prior) on imperfect demonstrations (potentially synthesized by LLMs or humans). 2) Freeze the diffusion policy. 3) Use reinforcement learning to optimize the diffusion model’s initial noise to maximize task success, keeping the policy frozen to regularize exploration within human-like manifolds. 4) Distill learned state-based policies to visuomotor controllers via DAgger for deployment on real robots.

Result: On industrial assembly tasks, 90.5% overall success; on long-horizon manipulation tasks, 85% overall success. Outperforms baselines and shows dexterous, robust behavior across diverse initial states and failure modes. Demonstrates effective sim-to-real transfer, with policies distilled to visuomotor controllers and deployed on real hardware.

Conclusion: ExpertGen demonstrates scalable, safe, and effective expert-policy learning for robotics by leveraging a frozen diffusion-based prior guided through sparse-reward RL, enabling high-quality sim-to-real transfer without reward engineering and with strong real-world performance.

Abstract: Learning generalizable and robust behavior cloning policies requires large volumes of high-quality robotics data. While human demonstrations (e.g., through teleoperation) serve as the standard source for expert behaviors, acquiring such data at scale in the real world is prohibitively expensive. This paper introduces ExpertGen, a framework that automates expert policy learning in simulation to enable scalable sim-to-real transfer. ExpertGen first initializes a behavior prior using a diffusion policy trained on imperfect demonstrations, which may be synthesized by large language models or provided by humans. Reinforcement learning is then used to steer this prior toward high task success by optimizing the diffusion model's initial noise while keep original policy frozen. By keeping the pretrained diffusion policy frozen, ExpertGen regularizes exploration to remain within safe, human-like behavior manifolds, while also enabling effective learning with only sparse rewards. Empirical evaluations on challenging manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that ExpertGen reliably produces high-quality expert policies with no reward engineering. On industrial assembly tasks, ExpertGen achieves a 90.5% overall success rate, while on long-horizon manipulation tasks it attains 85% overall success, outperforming all baseline methods. The resulting policies exhibit dexterous control and remain robust across diverse initial configurations and failure states. To validate sim-to-real transfer, the learned state-based expert policies are further distilled into visuomotor policies via DAgger and successfully deployed on real robotic hardware.

</details>


### [147] [Safety Case Patterns for VLA-based driving systems: Insights from SimLingo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16013)
*Gerhard Yu,Fuyuki Ishikawa,Oluwafemi Odu,Alvine Boaye Belle*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces RAISE, a safety-case design approach for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) driven autonomous systems, extending Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA) with VLA-specific patterns, safe scenarios, and a design method; validated with a SimLingo case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA-based driving systems enable instruction-responsive behavior but introduce new hazards via natural language inputs in the multimodal control loop; a rigorous safety-case framework is needed to ensure safety and trust.

Method: Proposes RAISE: (1) safety-case design patterns tailored to instruction-based driving; (2) an extension of HARA detailing safe scenarios and outcomes; (3) a design technique to build the safety cases; (4) a case study using SimLingo to demonstrate construction of evidence-based safety claims.

Result: A structured safety-case approach for VLA-based driving with an evidence-based method and an illustrative SimLingo application.

Conclusion: RAISE enables rigorous safety claims for instruction-based driving systems and supports trust in VLA-based autonomous driving; demonstrates feasibility of extending traditional safety-case practices to multilingual, multimodal control contexts.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA)-based driving systems represent a significant paradigm shift in autonomous driving since, by combining traffic scene understanding, linguistic interpretation, and action generation, these systems enable more flexible, adaptive, and instruction-responsive driving behaviors. However, despite their growing adoption and potential to support socially responsible autonomous driving while understanding high-level human instructions, VLA-based driving systems may exhibit new types of hazardous behaviors. Such as the addition of natural language inputs (e.g., user or navigation instructions) into the multimodal control loop, which may lead to unpredictable and unsafe behaviors that could endanger vehicle occupants and pedestrians. Hence, assuring the safety of these systems is crucial to help build trust in their operations. To support this, we propose a novel safety case design approach called RAISE. Our approach introduces novel patterns tailored to instruction-based driving systems such as VLA-based driving systems, an extension of Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA) detailing safe scenarios and their outcomes, and a design technique to create the safety cases of VLA-based driving systems. A case study on SimLingo illustrates how our approach can be used to construct rigorous, evidence-based safety claims for this emerging class of autonomous driving systems.

</details>


### [148] [Geometry-Aligned LLM Fine-Tuning for Sequential Narrow-Opening Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16028)
*Al Jaber Mahmud,Xuan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Geometry-aligned LLM framework for long-horizon rigid-body planning across sequential narrow openings, using failure-driven LoRA SFT and Group Relative Policy Optimization with geometric verification to produce fixed-length waypoint sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sequential narrow openings impose long-horizon constraints: the choice of an early traversal configuration constrains later reachability. A geometry-aware, coordinated planner is needed to reason over multiple openings.

Method: Bi-level training: (1) failure-driven LoRA SFT on human demonstrations with structured failure feedback and enforced output format; (2) refine LoRA adapters using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with geometric verification, densifying waypoint sequences via a model-based planner and scoring with a deterministic geometry-derived reward to ensure continuous-motion feasibility.

Result: In simulations, the method achieves the highest success rate in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution environments and demonstrates long-horizon geometric reasoning by selecting exit poses that facilitate subsequent openings.

Conclusion: The framework effectively enables geometry-consistent, coordinated long-horizon motion planning for sequential openings by fusing failure-informed SFT with geometry-aware GRPO and verification.

Abstract: We study rigid-body motion planning through multiple sequential narrow openings, which requires long-horizon geometric reasoning because the configuration used to traverse an early opening constrains the set of reachable configurations for subsequent ones. To achieve this, we propose a geometry-aligned large language model (LLM) fine-tuning framework that generates fixed-length, machine-readable waypoint sequences that are both geometrically feasible and coordinated across openings. Our approach uses a bi-level training pipeline. First, we perform failure-driven LoRA supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on human demonstrations, which incorporates structured failure feedback to teach the model common failure modes and enforce the output format. Second, we refine the same LoRA adapters using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with geometric verification: each sampled waypoint sequence is densified by a model-based planner and scored with a deterministic geometry-derived reward to achieve continuous-motion feasibility. To validate the effectiveness of our proposed method, we provide both quantitative and qualitative results from simulations. Our method achieves the highest success rate in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution environments and qualitatively exhibits long-horizon geometric reasoning by selecting exit poses that facilitate entry into subsequent openings.

</details>


### [149] [Compact Optical Single-axis Joint Torque Sensor Using Redundant Photo-Reflectors and Quadratic-Programming Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16040)
*Hyun-Bin Kim,Byeong-Il Ham,Kyung-Soo Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A non-contact optical torque sensor uses a redundant photo-reflector array to measure micro-deformation in an elastic element for precise joint torque control; augmented by a quadratic-programming calibration to boost resolution and reduce noise beyond LS methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome poor low-torque accuracy and nonlinearity in current-sensor torque estimation due to gearbox stiction/friction, enabling safer, more precise interaction in collaborative robots.

Method: Optical sensing of elastic micro-deformation with a redundant four-direction photo-reflector array in a compact 96 mm diameter, 12 mm thick package; quadratic-programming calibration leveraging redundancy to suppress noise and improve resolution; temperature compensation via rational fitting; motor-level validation.

Result: Maximum error 0.083% FS; RMS error 0.0266 Nm for z-axis; 3-sigma resolution 0.0224 Nm at 1 kHz without filtering; 2.14x improvement over least-squares baseline; temperature chamber characterization mitigates zero drift; motor-level validation shows improved low-torque tracking and disturbance robustness vs current-sensor-based control.

Conclusion: The approach provides high-precision, low-drift joint torque sensing suitable for safe, robust joint-level torque control, outperforming conventional current-sensor-based methods and enabling tighter low-torque performance with temperature compensation.

Abstract: This study proposes a non-contact photo-reflector-based joint torque sensor for precise joint-level torque control and safe physical interaction. Current-sensor-based torque estimation in many collaborative robots suffers from poor low-torque accuracy due to gearbox stiction/friction and current-torque nonlinearity, especially near static conditions. The proposed sensor optically measures micro-deformation of an elastic structure and employs a redundant array of photo-reflectors arranged in four directions to improve sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio. We further present a quadratic-programming-based calibration method that exploits redundancy to suppress noise and enhance resolution compared to least-squares calibration. The sensor is implemented in a compact form factor (96 mm diameter, 12 mm thickness). Experiments demonstrate a maximum error of 0.083%FS and an RMS error of 0.0266 Nm for z-axis torque measurement. Calibration tests show that the proposed calibration achieves a 3 sigma resolution of 0.0224 Nm at 1 kHz without filtering, corresponding to a 2.14 times improvement over the least-squares baseline. Temperature chamber characterization and rational fitting based compensation mitigate zero drift induced by MCU self heating and motor heat. Motor-level validation via torque control and admittance control confirms improved low torque tracking and disturbance robustness relative to current-sensor-based control.

</details>


### [150] [The Era of End-to-End Autonomy: Transitioning from Rule-Based Driving to Large Driving Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16050)
*Eduardo Nebot,Julie Stephany Berrio Perez*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: End-to-end driving models (LDMs) and supervised E2E (FSD Supervised/L2+) are driving a shift from modular rule-based pipelines to integrated driving systems. The abstract argues these models can handle most Dynamic Driving Task (DDT) with human supervision, with early evidence suggesting reliance on long-tail real-world scenarios; forecasts broader industry adoption and cross-domain applicability to embodied AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the architectural evolution, deployment strategies, safety considerations, and industry implications as autonomous driving transitions from modular pipelines to end-to-end and supervised end-to-end approaches, and to assess potential extensions to other embodied AI systems.

Method: Analytical synthesis of recent developments and deployments (e.g., Tesla FSD V12/V14, Rivian Unified Intelligence, NVIDIA Cosmos, robotaxi deployments); evaluation of architectural designs, deployment strategies, safety considerations; discussion of long-tail driving handling and cross-domain implications for embodied AI.

Result: The abstract indicates that end-to-end learning can address the long-tail distribution of real-world driving more effectively and is becoming a dominant commercial strategy. Supervised E2E (FSD Supervised/L2+) is expected to be deployed from 2026 onwards, enabling most of the Dynamic Driving Task under human supervision. Early operational evidence suggests E2E systems can perform in complex environments with oversight, and these architectural advances may extend beyond AVs to humanoid robotics.

Conclusion: E2E/LDM-based approaches are reshaping autonomous driving and potentially other embodied AI systems by enabling high task coverage with supervisor oversight. Safety, deployment strategies, and cross-domain adaptability are critical factors for future adoption and governance.

Abstract: Autonomous driving is undergoing a shift from modular rule based pipelines toward end to end (E2E) learning systems. This paper examines this transition by tracing the evolution from classical sense perceive plan control architectures to large driving models (LDMs) capable of mapping raw sensor input directly to driving actions. We analyze recent developments including Tesla's Full Self Driving (FSD) V12 V14, Rivian's Unified Intelligence platform, NVIDIA Cosmos, and emerging commercial robotaxi deployments, focusing on architectural design, deployment strategies, safety considerations and industry implications. A key emerging product category is supervised E2E driving, often referred to as FSD (Supervised) or L2 plus plus, which several manufacturers plan to deploy from 2026 onwards. These systems can perform most of the Dynamic Driving Task (DDT) in complex environments while requiring human supervision, shifting the driver's role to safety oversight. Early operational evidence suggests E2E learning handles the long tail distribution of real world driving scenarios and is becoming a dominant commercial strategy. We also discuss how similar architectural advances may extend beyond autonomous vehicles (AV) to other embodied AI systems, including humanoid robotics.

</details>


### [151] [Ultrafast Sampling-based Kinodynamic Planning via Differential Flatness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16059)
*Thai Duong,Clayton W. Ramsey,Zachary Kingston,Wil Thomason,Lydia E. Kavraki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Fast parallelized kinodynamic planning via differential flatness (AkinoPDF) enabling microsecond–millisecond planning for diverse high-DOF robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address computational bottlenecks in enforcing dynamics within sampling-based kinodynamic planners (solving BVPs or simulating dynamics) to enable real-time planning; extend microseconds-scale geometric planners to dynamic feasibility across a broad class of robots.

Method: Leverage differential flatness to map the motion planning problem to a flat output space, derive an analytical time-parameterized solution for the BVP and dynamics integration, then recover a closed-form dynamically feasible trajectory in the original state space; perform trajectory validation and planning in parallel using SIMD/MIMD; compatible with any sampling-based planner.

Result: Validated in simulations and real-world experiments in cluttered and dynamic environments; achieved planning times ranging from microseconds to milliseconds; demonstrated applicability to manipulators, ground and aerial vehicles, and more.

Conclusion: AkinoPDF provides fast, exact, and scalable kinodynamic motion planning by combining differential flatness with parallel computation, producing dynamically feasible trajectories efficiently and being broadly compatible with existing sampling-based planners.

Abstract: Motion planning under dynamics constraints, i.e., kinodynamic planning, enables safe robot operation by generating dynamically feasible trajectories that the robot can accurately track. For high-\dof robots such as manipulators, sampling-based motion planners are commonly used, especially for complex tasks in cluttered environments. However, enforcing constraints on robot dynamics in such planners requires solving either challenging two-point boundary value problems (BVPs) or propagating robot dynamics over time, both of which are computational bottlenecks that drastically increase planning times. Meanwhile, recent efforts have shown that sampling-based motion planners can generate plans in microseconds using parallelization, but are limited to geometric paths. This paper develops AkinoPDF, a fast parallelized sampling-based kinodynamic motion planning technique for a broad class of differentially flat robot systems, including manipulators, ground and aerial vehicles, and more. Differential flatness allows us to transform the motion planning problem from the original state space to a flat output space, where an analytical time-parameterized solution of the BVP and dynamics integration can be obtained. A trajectory in the flat output space is then converted back to a closed-form dynamically feasible trajectory in the original state space, enabling fast validation via ``single instruction, multiple data" parallelism. Our method is fast, exact, and compatible with any sampling-based motion planner. We extensively verify the effectiveness of our approach in both simulated benchmarks and real experiments with cluttered and dynamic environments, requiring mere microseconds to milliseconds of planning time.

</details>


### [152] [Large Reward Models: Generalizable Online Robot Reward Generation with Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16065)
*Yanru Wu,Weiduo Yuan,Ang Qi,Vitor Guizilini,Jiageng Mao,Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A framework that uses a foundation vision-language model as an online reward generator to refine robot manipulation policies, achieving sample-efficient improvement from an IL base policy in 30 RL iterations without manual reward engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation is hampered by designing generalizable reward functions; online, zero-shot reward signals from VLMs can guide refinement.

Method: Train/finetune a state-of-the-art VLM on diverse real/simulated data to provide process, completion, and temporal contrastive rewards from current observations; initialize with IL policy; perform closed-loop RL using VLM rewards; zero-shot application in test environments.

Result: Significant improvement in success rate of the IL policy within 30 RL iterations; demonstrates sample efficiency and robustness; reward signals resolve execution errors without manual reward engineering.

Conclusion: VLM-generated rewards can reliably guide online policy refinement for long-horizon manipulation tasks, enabling efficient, generalizable learning and reducing reliance on hand-crafted reward design.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has shown great potential in refining robotic manipulation policies, yet its efficacy remains strongly bottlenecked by the difficulty of designing generalizable reward functions. In this paper, we propose a framework for online policy refinement by adapting foundation VLMs into online reward generators. We develop a robust, scalable reward model based on a state-of-the-art VLM, trained on a large-scale, multi-source dataset encompassing real-world robot trajectories, human-object interactions, and diverse simulated environments. Unlike prior approaches that evaluate entire trajectories post-hoc, our method leverages the VLM to formulate a multifaceted reward signal comprising process, completion, and temporal contrastive rewards based on current visual observations. Initializing with a base policy trained via Imitation Learning (IL), we employ these VLM rewards to guide the model to correct sub-optimal behaviors in a closed-loop manner. We evaluate our framework on challenging long-horizon manipulation benchmarks requiring sequential execution and precise control. Crucially, our reward model operates in a purely zero-shot manner within these test environments. Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly improves the success rate of the initial IL policy within just 30 RL iterations, demonstrating remarkable sample efficiency. This empirical evidence highlights that VLM-generated signals can provide reliable feedback to resolve execution errors, effectively eliminating the need for manual reward engineering and facilitating efficient online refinement for robot learning.

</details>


### [153] [Towards the Vision-Sound-Language-Action Paradigm: The HEAR Framework for Sound-Centric Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16086)
*Chang Nie,Tianchen Deng,Guangming Wang,Zhe Liu,Hesheng Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A continuous, sound-centered VSLA paradigm (HEAR) for embodied agents, featuring a Historizer, Envisioner, Advancer, and Realizer; supported by OpenX-Sound pretraining and HEAR-Bench assessment, emphasizing causal persistence and temporal learning to bridge acoustic observation gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models treat audio as static prompts or focus on human speech, missing real-time environmental sounds. Action chunking with open-loop execution causes Blind Execution Intervals where important acoustic events are not observed due to low update rates and latency, hindering sound-centric state verification.

Method: Propose Vision-Sound-Language-Action (VSLA) as a continuous control paradigm conditioned on vision, streaming audio, language, and proprioception under delayed decision loops. HEAR instantiates four components: (i) Historizer to maintain a compact, causal audio context across execution gaps; (ii) Envisioner adapted from omni foundation models to reason over multi-sensory inputs; (iii) Advancer, an audio world model that predicts near-future audio codes to capture temporal dynamics; (iv) Realizer, a flow-matching policy to generate smooth action chunks. Address data scarcity with OpenX-Sound pretraining and introduce HEAR-Bench, a sound-centric manipulation benchmark with strict causal timing rules.

Result: Results suggest that robust sound-centric manipulation requires causal persistence and explicit temporal learning; the framework demonstrates the importance of maintaining continuous auditory context and temporal prediction for effective VSLA.

Conclusion: The HEAR framework advances multi-sensory foundation models for embodied agents, enabling robots to perceive and interact with dynamic environments. The work provides OpenX-Sound and HEAR-Bench for pretraining and evaluation; code and videos are publicly available.

Abstract: While recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have begun to incorporate audio, they typically treat sound as static pre-execution prompts or focus exclusively on human speech. This leaves a significant gap in real-time, sound-centric manipulation where fleeting environmental acoustics provide critical state verification during task execution. Consequently, key sounds are easily missed due to low-frequency updates or system latency. This problem is exacerbated by action chunking with open-loop execution, which creates a Blind Execution Interval where acoustic events are lost between discrete audio observation windows. Recognizing the necessity of continuous auditory awareness, we formalize Vision-Sound-Language-Action (VSLA) as a continuous control paradigm conditioned on vision, streaming audio, language, and proprioception under delayed decision loops. As an instantiation, we introduce HEAR, a VSLA framework integrating four components: (i) a streaming Historizer to maintain a compact, causal audio context across execution gaps; (ii) an Envisioner adapted from omni foundation models to reason over multi-sensory inputs; (iii) an Advancer, formulated as an audio world model, to learn temporal dynamics by predicting near-future audio codes; and (iv) a flow-matching Realizer policy to generate smooth action chunks. To address the scarcity of pretraining data and evaluations for VSLA, we construct OpenX-Sound for pretraining, alongside HEAR-Bench, the first sound-centric manipulation benchmark with strict causal timing rules. Our results suggest that robust sound-centric manipulation necessitates causal persistence and explicit temporal learning. This framework provides a practical step toward multi-sensory foundation models for embodied agents, enabling robots to perceive and interact with dynamic environments. Code and videos are available at https://hear.irmv.top.

</details>


### [154] [SE(3)-LIO: Smooth IMU Propagation With Jointly Distributed Poses on SE(3) Manifold for Accurate and Robust LiDAR-Inertial Odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16118)
*Gunhee Shin,Seungjae Lee,Jei Kong,Youngwoo Seo,Hyun Myung*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SE(3)-LIO propagates poses on the SE(3) manifold and uses correlation-aware uncertainty in motion compensation to improve LiDAR-inertial odometry, addressing rotation-translation coupling and prediction uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing IMU propagation often treats rotation and translation separately, causing rotation to be poorly reflected in translation and introducing uncertainty in motion compensation; there is a need for a unified SE(3) approach with uncertainty modeling.

Method: Represent and propagate pose directly on SE(3) to capture rotational effects on translation. Precisely model the relative-transformation uncertainty by considering correlations between predicted poses and integrate this into measurement noise during motion compensation.

Result: SE(3)-LIO is validated on diverse datasets, showing improved odometry performance with the proposed propagation and uncertainty-aware compensation. Source code and materials are available at the provided URL.

Conclusion: Unifying pose propagation on SE(3) and incorporating correlation-aware uncertainty into motion compensation yields more accurate and robust LiDAR-inertial odometry (SE(3)-LIO); implementation is publicly available.

Abstract: In estimating odometry accurately, an inertial measurement unit (IMU) is widely used owing to its high-rate measurements, which can be utilized to obtain motion information through IMU propagation. In this paper, we address the limitations of existing IMU propagation methods in terms of motion prediction and motion compensation.
