In motion prediction, the existing methods typically represent a 6-DoF pose by separating rotation and translation and propagate them on their respective manifold, so that the rotational variation is not effectively incorporated into translation propagation. During motion compensation, the relative transformation between predicted poses is used to compensate motion-induced distortion in other measurements, while inherent errors in the predicted poses introduce uncertainty in the relative transformation. To tackle these challenges, we represent and propagate the pose on SE(3) manifold, where propagated translation properly accounts for rotational variation. Furthermore, we precisely characterize the relative transformation uncertainty by considering the correlation between predicted poses, and incorporate this uncertainty into the measurement noise during motion compensation. To this end, we propose a LiDAR-inertial odometry (LIO), referred to as SE(3)-LIO, that integrates the proposed IMU propagation and uncertainty-aware motion compensation (UAMC). We validate the effectiveness of SE(3)-LIO on diverse datasets. Our source code and additional material are available at: https://se3-lio.github.io/.

</details>


### [155] [SignNav: Leveraging Signage for Semantic Visual Navigation in Large-Scale Indoor Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16166)
*Jian Sun,Yuming Huang,He Li,Shuqi Xiao,Shenyan Guo,Maani Ghaffari,Qingbiao Li,Chengzhong Xu,Hui Kong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces SignNav, a new embodied navigation task that uses semantic signage hints in large-scale indoor environments; presents LSI-Dataset and START model with spatial-temporal grounding; achieves state-of-the-art results (80% SR, 0.74 NDTW) with DAgger, and validates in real-world deployments without pre-built maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the underexplored problem of interpreting semantic signage within embodied navigation in novel LSI environments. Motivates the need to leverage dynamic semantic cues from signage to guide actions, and to develop benchmarks and models capable of end-to-end decision-making in cluttered, sign-sparse, and evolving environments.

Method: Task formulation (SignNav) requiring interpretation of signage and action within current observation. Dataset construction (LSI-Dataset) for training/evaluating SignNav agents. Model (START) with a spatial-aware module to ground sign semantics in physical space and a temporal-aware module to capture long-range dynamics. Two-stage training using Dataset Aggregation (DAgger).

Result: State-of-the-art performance on val-unseen: 80% success rate (SR) and 0.74 Normalized Distance to Waypoint (NDTW). Real-world deployment demonstrates practicality in physical environments without pre-built maps.

Conclusion: The START model effectively grounds signage semantics and leverages temporal context to navigate sign-rich, large-scale indoor spaces. The SignNav framework, together with LSI-Dataset, provides a benchmark and methodology for robust, real-world embodied navigation guided by signage.

Abstract: Humans routinely leverage semantic hints provided by signage to navigate to destinations within novel Large-Scale Indoor (LSI) environments, such as hospitals and airport terminals. However, this capability remains underexplored within the field of embodied navigation. This paper introduces a novel embodied navigation task, SignNav, which requires the agent to interpret semantic hint from signage and reason about the subsequent action based on current observation. To facilitate research in this domain, we construct the LSI-Dataset for the training and evaluation of various SignNav agents. Dynamically changing semantic hints and sparse placement of signage in LSI environments present significant challenges to the SignNav task. To address these challenges, we propose the Spatial-Temporal Aware Transformer (START) model for end-to-end decision-making. The spatial-aware module grounds the semantic hint of signage into physical world, while the temporal-aware module captures long-range dependencies between historical states and current observation. Leveraging a two-stage training strategy with Dataset Aggregation (DAgger), our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, recording an 80% Success Rate (SR) and 0.74 NDTW on val-unseen split. Real-world deployment further demonstrates the practicality of our method in physical environment without pre-built map.

</details>


### [156] [Enforcing Task-Specified Compliance Bounds for Humanoids via Anisotropic Lipschitz-Constrained Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16180)
*Zewen He,Yoshihiko Nakamura*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes anisotropic Lipschitz-constrained policy (ALCP) for RL-based humanoid locomotion by mapping a task-space stiffness bound to a state-dependent, directional Lipschitz constraint on the policy Jacobian, enforced with a hinge-squared spectral-norm penalty; improves stability, impact robustness, and reduces oscillations and energy use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable physically interpretable, verifiable compliance in model-free RL for humanoid robots and connect policy sensitivity to a user-specified stiffness target, addressing the gap where conventional LCP uses a single scalar budget and lacks task-specific compliance semantics.

Method: Introduce ALCP framework that imposes an anisotropic, state-dependent Lipschitz-style constraint on the policy Jacobian by mapping a task-space stiffness upper bound to directional penalties. Enforce the constraint via a hinge-squared spectral-norm penalty during RL training, enabling direction-dependent compliance.

Result: Experimental results on humanoid robots show enhanced locomotion stability and impact robustness, with reduced oscillations and lower energy consumption compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: Anisotropic, physically interpretable Lipschitz constraints preserve RL training stability while providing direction-dependent compliance, offering a principled pathway to integrate task-specific stiffness into model-free control of humanoid locomotion.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has demonstrated substantial potential for humanoid bipedal locomotion and the control of complex motions. To cope with oscillations and impacts induced by environmental interactions, compliant control is widely regarded as an effective remedy. However, the model-free nature of RL makes it difficult to impose task-specified and quantitatively verifiable compliance objectives, and classical model-based stiffness designs are not directly applicable. Lipschitz-Constrained Policies (LCP), which regularize the local sensitivity of a policy via gradient penalties, have recently been used to smooth humanoid motions. Nevertheless, existing LCP-based methods typically employ a single scalar Lipschitz budget and lack an explicit connection to physically meaningful compliance specifications in real-world systems. In this study, we propose an anisotropic Lipschitz-constrained policy (ALCP) that maps a task-space stiffness upper bound to a state-dependent Lipschitz-style constraint on the policy Jacobian. The resulting constraint is enforced during RL training via a hinge-squared spectral-norm penalty, preserving physical interpretability while enabling direction-dependent compliance. Experiments on humanoid robots show that ALCP improves locomotion stability and impact robustness, while reducing oscillations and energy usage.

</details>


### [157] [PanguMotion: Continuous Driving Motion Forecasting with Pangu Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16196)
*Quanhao Ren,Yicheng Li,Nan Song*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces PanguMotion, a continuous-scene motion forecasting framework that augments autonomous driving models with Transformer blocks from the Pangu-1B LLM, evaluated on Argoverse 2 with RealMotion to simulate continuous sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing motion forecasting methods typically treat driving scenes as independent units, overlooking temporal continuity and historical context across scenes. Incorporating Transformer-based features from a large language model aims to capture long-range temporal dependencies and richer context for improved predictions.

Method: Proposes PanguMotion, a motion forecasting framework that integrates Transformer blocks from Pangu-1B as feature-enhancement modules into standard autonomous-driving prediction architectures. Uses RealMotion data reorganization to transform Argoverse 2 scenes into continuous sequences, mimicking real-world driving scenarios.

Result: The paper reports experiments on RealMotion-processed Argoverse 2 data to evaluate PanguMotion, but the abstract does not provide specific quantitative results or performance metrics.

Conclusion: PanguMotion offers a path to continuous, context-aware motion forecasting by fusing LLM-based temporal features with driving-scene data, highlighting the potential of leveraging large-scale transformers to improve autonomous driving predictions.

Abstract: Motion forecasting is a core task in autonomous driving systems, aiming to accurately predict the future trajectories of surrounding agents to ensure driving safety. Existing methods typically process discrete driving scenes independently, neglecting the temporal continuity and historical context correlations inherent in real-world driving environments. This paper proposes PanguMotion, a motion forecasting framework for continuous driving scenarios that integrates Transformer blocks from the Pangu-1B large language model as feature enhancement modules into autonomous driving motion prediction architectures. We conduct experiments on the Argoverse 2 datasets processed by the RealMotion data reorganization strategy, transforming each independent scene into a continuous sequence to mimic real-world driving scenarios.

</details>


### [158] [Enabling Dynamic Tracking in Vision-Language-Action Models via Time-Discrete and Time-Continuous Velocity Feedforward](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16218)
*Johannes Hechtl,Philipp Schmitt,Georg von Wichert,Wolfram Burgard*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Integrates velocity feedforward into vision-language-action policies for robot manipulation; compares finite-difference velocity targets vs continuous cubic B-spline action space; finds finite differences boost speed, B-spline yields smooth high-rate control and high success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rigid industrial robots face a trade-off between compliance and responsiveness when using vision-language-action (VLA) policies. Standard behavior cloning predicts low-frequency, discrete poses, omitting velocity/acceleration feedforward terms used by compliant controllers, causing reliance on high stiffness and unsafe contact dynamics. Integrating velocity feedforward aims to improve tracking, safety, and performance.

Method: Two model-agnostic approaches to extract velocity targets from VLA outputs. (1) Time-discrete finite-difference approximation as a bridge for existing models. (2) Continuous Cubic B-Spline action space yielding C^2 continuous trajectories. Both are compatible with standard action-chunking architectures and require only modifications to teleoperation, data processing, and the low-level controller. The π_0.5 model is fine-tuned; evaluation on a demanding, contact-rich cube-in-hole task.

Result: Finite-difference velocity targets significantly improve task execution speed. The continuous B-Spline approach maintains high overall success rates and provides smoother higher-order derivatives without compromising compliance.

Conclusion: Incorporating velocity feedforward terms via the proposed finite-difference and B-Spline approaches mitigates the compliance–responsiveness trade-off in VLA-based manipulation. Finite-difference velocity targets boost speed, while B-Spline action space preserves high success and enables smoother derivatives; both are model-agnostic and compatible with common architectures.

Abstract: While vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown great promise for robot manipulation, their deployment on rigid industrial robots remains challenging due to the inherent trade-off between compliance and responsiveness. Standard Behavior Cloning (BC) approaches predict discrete poses at low frequencies, omitting the velocity and acceleration feedforward terms typically used by low-level compliant controllers. This requires to rely on high stiffness for accurate tracking, thereby sacrificing safe contact dynamics. In this paper, we demonstrate the importance of integrating velocity feedforward terms into VLA policies to resolve this trade-off. We propose two methods for extracting velocity targets from VLAs: a time-discrete finite-difference approximation that serves as a highly effective bridge for existing models, and a continuous Cubic B-Spline action space that natively yields $C^2$ continuous trajectories for high-frequency control. Crucially, both approaches are strictly model-agnostic and compatible with any standard action-chunking architecture, requiring modifications only to teleoperation, data processing, and the low-level controller. We fine-tune the $π_{0.5}$ model and evaluate both of our approaches on a demanding, contact-rich cube-in-hole task. Our results indicate that incorporating the velocity feedforward term via finite differences significantly improves task execution speed, while the continuous B-Spline approach maintains high overall success rates and provides a foundation for smoother higher-order derivatives without compromising compliance.

</details>


### [159] [PA-LVIO: Real-Time LiDAR-Visual-Inertial Odometry and Mapping with Pose-Only Bundle Adjustment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16228)
*Hailiang Tang,Tisheng Zhang,Liqiang Wang,Xin Ding,Man Yuan,Xiaoji Niu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Presents PA-LVIO, a real-time pose-only bundle adjustment framework for LiDAR-visual-inertial odometry that uses a marginalization-free F2M LiDAR model and IMU-centric online calibration to achieve accurate odometry and RGB point-cloud maps, running in real-time on desktop and onboard computers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time, accurate navigation and mapping in intelligent transportation systems requires reliable multi-sensor fusion (LiDAR, camera, IMU) while mitigating drift and handling extrinsic calibration online. Existing LVIO pipelines struggle with real-time frame-to-map consistency and dynamic extrinsics. A pose-only BA can improve efficiency while maintaining accuracy.

Method: A PA-LVIO pipeline performing pose-only bundle adjustment for LiDAR+visual+IMU data. Key components include (1) a marginalization-free, frame-to-map (F2M) LiDAR measurement model to constrain odometry without losing past information, (2) an IMU-centric online spatial-temporal calibration for pixel-level LiDAR-camera alignment, and (3) integration of optimized odometry and extrinsics to build a high-quality, RGB-rendered point-cloud map. The method emphasizes real-time performance and reliable cross-sensor constraints across multiple frames.

Result: Extensive experiments on 28 sequences with over 50 km trajectories across wheeled robots, UAVs, and handheld devices show PA-LVIO achieves superior or comparable odometry accuracy and mapping quality versus state-of-the-art LVIO methods, while running in real time on both desktop PCs and ARM onboard computers.

Conclusion: PA-LVIO delivers accurate, real-time pose estimation and high-quality RGB maps by combining a marginalization-free F2M LiDAR model with online spatial-temporal calibration, making it suitable for robust navigation and mapping across diverse platforms.

Abstract: Real-time LiDAR-visual-inertial odometry and mapping is crucial for navigation and planning tasks in intelligent transportation systems. This study presents a pose-only bundle adjustment (PA) LiDAR-visual-inertial odometry (LVIO), named PA-LVIO, to meet the urgent need for real-time navigation and mapping. The proposed PA framework for LiDAR and visual measurements is highly accurate and efficient, and it can derive reliable frame-to-frame constraints within multiple frames. A marginalization-free and frame-to-map (F2M) LiDAR measurement model is integrated into the state estimator to eliminate odometry drifts. Meanwhile, an IMU-centric online spatial-temporal calibration is employed to obtain a pixel-wise LiDAR-camera alignment. With accurate estimated odometry and extrinsics, a high-quality and RGB-rendered point-cloud map can be built. Comprehensive experiments are conducted on both public and private datasets collected by wheeled robot, unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV), and handheld devices with 28 sequences and more than 50 km trajectories. Sufficient results demonstrate that the proposed PA-LVIO yields superior or comparable performance to state-of-the-art LVIO methods, in terms of the odometry accuracy and mapping quality. Besides, PA-LVIO can run in real-time on both the desktop PC and the onboard ARM computer.

</details>


### [160] [Industrial cuVSLAM Benchmark & Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16240)
*Charbel Abi Hana,Kameel Amareen,Mohamad Mostafa,Dmitry Slepichev,Hesam Rabeti,Zheng Wang,Mihir Acharya,Anthony Rizk*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Benchmark of VO/VSLAM in real-world logistics; hybrid cuVSLAM front-end with custom back-end yields best mapping accuracy; validated on NVIDIA Jetson.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess VO/VSLAM performance in realistic logistics environments and determine a strong, deployable stack for mobile robotics.

Method: Evaluate multiple VO approaches on controlled trajectories (translational, rotational, mixed) and a large-scale facility dataset (~1.7 km). Ground truth from Vicon and a LiDAR-based SLAM reference. Compare against a hybrid stack (cuVSLAM front-end + custom back-end) and validate on NVIDIA Jetson.

Result: Hybrid cuVSLAM front-end with a custom SLAM back-end provides the strongest mapping accuracy; cuVSLAM-based VO is recommended as core component in the robotics stack; Jetson deployment confirms real-time viability.

Conclusion: cuVSLAM-based VO, integrated with a tailored back-end, offers robust, accurate mapping suitable for real-world logistics robotics; integration into the production stack and edge hardware is feasible.

Abstract: This work presents a comprehensive benchmark evaluation of visual odometry (VO) and visual SLAM (VSLAM) systems for mobile robot navigation in real-world logistical environments. We compare multiple visual odometry approaches across controlled trajectories covering translational, rotational, and mixed motion patterns, as well as a large-scale production facility dataset spanning approximately 1.7 km. Performance is evaluated using Absolute Pose Error (APE) against ground truth from a Vicon motion capture system and a LiDAR-based SLAM reference. Our results show that a hybrid stack combining the cuVSLAM front-end with a custom SLAM back-end achieves the strongest mapping accuracy, motivating a deeper integration of cuVSLAM as the core VO component in our robotics stack. We further validate this integration by deploying and testing the cuVSLAM-based VO stack on an NVIDIA Jetson platform.

</details>


### [161] [MG-Grasp: Metric-Scale Geometric 6-DoF Grasping Framework with Sparse RGB Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16270)
*Kangxu Wang,Siang Chen,Chenxing Jiang,Shaojie Shen,Yixiang Dai,Guijin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MG-Grasp: a depth-free RGB-based 6-DoF grasping framework that uses a two-view 3D foundation model to reconstruct metric-scale, multi-view-consistent dense point clouds from sparse RGB images and to generate stable 6-DoF grasps; achieves state-of-the-art performance among RGB-based methods on GraspNet-1Billion and in the real world.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB-only 6-DoF grasp methods suffer from inaccurate geometric representation and lack of reliable physical manipulation; depth sensors are not always available or practical. A depth-free approach with robust geometric reconstruction is needed.

Method: Leverages a two-view 3D foundation model with known camera intrinsics/extrinsics to reconstruct metric-scale, multi-view-consistent dense point clouds from sparse RGB inputs, then generates stable 6-DoF grasps from the reconstructed geometry.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art (SOTA) grasp performance among RGB-based 6-DoF grasping methods on the GraspNet-1Billion dataset and in real-world experiments.

Conclusion: Depth-free RGB-based 6-DoF grasping is feasible and can achieve high reliability by exploiting multi-view RGB-to-geometry reconstruction; MG-Grasp advances the capabilities of RGB-only grasping systems.

Abstract: Single-view RGB-D grasp detection remains a common choice in 6-DoF robotic grasping systems, which typically requires a depth sensor. While RGB-only 6-DoF grasp methods has been studied recently, their inaccurate geometric representation is not directly suitable for physically reliable robotic manipulation, thereby hindering reliable grasp generation. To address these limitations, we propose MG-Grasp, a novel depth-free 6-DoF grasping framework that achieves high-quality object grasping. Leveraging two-view 3D foundation model with camera intrinsic/extrinsic, our method reconstructs metric-scale and multi-view consistent dense point clouds from sparse RGB images and generates stable 6-DoF grasp. Experiments on GraspNet-1Billion dataset and real world demonstrate that MG-Grasp achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) grasp performance among RGB-based 6-DoF grasping methods.

</details>


### [162] [GenZ-LIO: Generalizable LiDAR-Inertial Odometry Beyond Indoor--Outdoor Boundaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16273)
*Daehan Lee,Hyungtae Lim,Seongjun Kim,Soonbin Rho,Changhyeon Lee,Sanghyun Park,Junwoo Hong,Eunseon Choi,Hyunyoung Jo,Soohee Han*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GenZ-LIO adapts voxel size via a PID-inspired feedback loop, uses a hybrid point-to-plane/point-to-point update, and voxel-pruned matching to achieve robust and efficient LiDAR-inertial odometry across indoor, outdoor, and transitional environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate LiDAR degeneracy and robustness/efficiency issues caused by spatial-scale variations in LiDAR scans, especially during transitions between indoor (dense) and outdoor (sparse) scenes.

Method: Three components: (1) adaptive voxel size regulation via feedback control driven by a scale-informed setpoint, akin to a PID controller; (2) a hybrid metric state update combining point-to-plane and point-to-point residuals to leverage complementary geometric constraints; (3) voxel-pruned correspondence search to discard unlikely voxel matches and reduce computation.

Result: Experimentally, GenZ-LIO achieves robust odometry and improved computational efficiency across indoor, outdoor, and transitional environments; code will be released publicly.

Conclusion: GenZ-LIO provides a generalizable LIO framework that maintains accuracy and efficiency across diverse environments, addressing scale-induced variations in LiDAR data.

Abstract: Light detection and ranging (LiDAR)-inertial odometry (LIO) enables accurate localization and mapping for autonomous navigation in various scenes. However, its performance remains sensitive to variations in spatial scale, which refers to the spatial extent of the scene reflected in the distribution of point ranges in a LiDAR scan. Transitions between confined indoor and expansive outdoor spaces induce substantial variations in point density, which may reduce robustness and computational efficiency. To address this issue, we propose GenZ-LIO, a LIO framework generalizable across both indoor and outdoor environments. GenZ-LIO comprises three key components. First, inspired by the principle of the proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controller, it adaptively regulates the voxel size for downsampling via feedback control, driving the voxelized point count toward a scale-informed setpoint while enabling stable and efficient processing across varying scene scales. Second, we formulate a hybrid-metric state update that jointly leverages point-to-plane and point-to-point residuals to mitigate LiDAR degeneracy arising from directionally insufficient geometric constraints. Third, to alleviate the computational burden introduced by point-to-point matching, we introduce a voxel-pruned correspondence search strategy that discards non-promising voxel candidates and reduces unnecessary computations. Experimental results demonstrate that GenZ-LIO achieves robust odometry estimation and improved computational efficiency across confined indoor, open outdoor, and transitional environments. Our code will be made publicly available upon publication.

</details>


### [163] [Agile Interception of a Flying Target using Competitive Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16279)
*Timothée Gavin,Simon Lacroix,Murat Bronz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Multi-agent PPO for intercepting agile drones via net capture in a high-fidelity sim with JAX low-level control; achieves superior interception metrics and indoor real-world demonstration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interception of agile drones is challenging due to high maneuverability; heuristic baselines underperform; reinforcement learning with accurate dynamics and low-level control can improve catch performance and robustness.

Method: Two policies (interceptor and target) trained with PPO in a competitive setting; high-fidelity quadrotor model; low-level control using collective thrust and body rates; JAX-based simulation enabling GPU parallelism; evaluation vs baselines on catch rate, time to catch, crash rate; scaled indoor demonstration.

Result: Higher catch rate, faster capture, and lower crash rate than baselines; successful transfer to scaled indoor arena.

Conclusion: Demonstrates viability of competitive RL with accurate dynamics for agile drone interception; suggests path to real-world deployment and extensions (more complex targets, safety, generalization).

Abstract: This article presents a solution to intercept an agile drone by another agile drone carrying a catching net. We formulate the interception as a Competitive Reinforcement Learning problem, where the interceptor and the target drone are controlled by separate policies trained with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). We introduce a high-fidelity simulation environment that integrates a realistic quadrotor dynamics model and a low-level control architecture implemented in JAX, which allows for fast parallelized execution on GPUs. We train the agents using low-level control, collective thrust and body rates, to achieve agile flights both for the interceptor and the target. We compare the performance of the trained policies in terms of catch rate, time to catch, and crash rate, against common heuristic baselines and show that our solution outperforms these baselines for interception of agile targets. Finally, we demonstrate the performance of the trained policies in a scaled real-world scenario using agile drones inside an indoor flight arena.

</details>


### [164] [OGScene3D: Incremental Open-Vocabulary 3D Gaussian Scene Graph Mapping for Scene Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16301)
*Siting Zhu,Ziyun Lu,Guangming Wang,Chenguang Huang,Yongbo Chen,I-Ming Chen,Wolfram Burgard,Hesheng Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OGScene3D enables open-vocabulary, incremental 3D semantic mapping and scene graph construction for robots by integrating a confidence-based Gaussian semantic representation with hierarchical and long-term optimization to ensure local and global semantic consistency as environments are explored over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots operate in partially observed, evolving environments where relying on pre-built complete 3D semantic maps is impractical. There is a need for open-vocabulary, incrementally updatable scene graphs that maintain semantic consistency over time.

Method: 1) Confidence-based Gaussian semantic representation to capture predictions and reliability. 2) Hierarchical 3D semantic optimization for local correspondence and global refinement, yielding globally consistent semantic maps. 3) Long-term global optimization using temporal memory of past observations to enhance predictions. 4) Integration of 2D-3D consistency via Gaussian rendering contributions. 5) Progressive graph construction that dynamically creates/updates nodes and relationships for continuous scene graphs.

Result: Demonstrates effectiveness on open-vocabulary scene understanding across standard datasets and real-world scenes, with improved semantic mapping quality, consistency, and incremental update capabilities over prior methods that require complete 3D semantic maps.

Conclusion: OGScene3D provides a viable framework for open-vocabulary, incrementally updatable 3D scene understanding and scene graph construction for robotics, leveraging probabilistic semantic representations, hierarchical optimization, temporal memory, and progressive graph building to maintain accurate, globally consistent maps as environments are explored.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary scene understanding is crucial for robotic applications, enabling robots to comprehend complex 3D environmental contexts and supporting various downstream tasks such as navigation and manipulation. However, existing methods require pre-built complete 3D semantic maps to construct scene graphs for scene understanding, which limits their applicability in robotic scenarios where environments are explored incrementally. To address this challenge, we propose OGScene3D, an open-vocabulary scene understanding system that achieves accurate 3D semantic mapping and scene graph construction incrementally. Our system employs a confidence-based Gaussian semantic representation that jointly models semantic predictions and their reliability, enabling robust scene modeling. Building on this representation, we introduce a hierarchical 3D semantic optimization strategy that achieves semantic consistency through local correspondence establishment and global refinement, thereby constructing globally consistent semantic maps. Moreover, we design a long-term global optimization method that leverages temporal memory of historical observations to enhance semantic predictions. By integrating 2D-3D semantic consistency with Gaussian rendering contribution, this method continuously refines the semantic understanding of the entire scene. Furthermore, we develop a progressive graph construction approach that dynamically creates and updates both nodes and semantic relationships, allowing continuous updating of the 3D scene graphs. Extensive experiments on widely used datasets and real-world scenes demonstrate the effectiveness of our OGScene3D on open-vocabulary scene understanding.

</details>


### [165] [Toward Deep Representation Learning for Event-Enhanced Visual Autonomous Perception: the eAP Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16303)
*Jinghang Li,Shichao Li,Qing Lian,Peiliang Li,Xiaozhi Chen,Yi Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces eAP, the largest event-camera dataset for autonomous perception in driving, enabling event-enhanced tasks (3D vehicle detection, object time-to-contact estimation) and showing state-of-the-art results, including 200 FPS TTC estimation; dataset, code, and pretrained models will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the lack of large-scale, real-world event-based data for autonomous driving under challenging illumination, and to study how event data can enhance deep visual perception tasks.

Method: Construct and benchmark on the eAP dataset; integrate event streams with deep networks for 3D vehicle detection under poor illumination; propose a geometry-aware representation learning framework for object TTC estimation; demonstrate high-speed TTC estimation at 200 FPS.

Result: First demonstrations that event data can improve a popular 3D vehicle detection network in challenging illumination; geometry-aware representation yields the best event-based TTC estimation network achieving 200 FPS; dataset/code/pretrained models will be publicly available.

Conclusion: eAP enables research into event-based perception for autonomous driving under challenging illumination, offering a large-scale dataset and baselines; promotes faster, robust perception via event data and geometry-aware representations.

Abstract: Recent visual autonomous perception systems achieve remarkable performances with deep representation learning. However, they fail in scenarios with challenging illumination.While event cameras can mitigate this problem, there is a lack of a large-scale dataset to develop event-enhanced deep visual perception models in autonomous driving scenes. To address the gap, we present the eAP (event-enhanced Autonomous Perception) dataset, the largest dataset with event cameras for autonomous perception. We demonstrate how eAP can facilitate the study of different autonomous perception tasks, including 3D vehicle detection and object time-to-contact (TTC) estimation, through deep representation learning. Based on eAP, we demonstrate the ffrst successful use of events to improve a popular 3D vehicle detection network in challenging illumination scenarios. eAP also enables a devoted study of the representation learning problem of object TTC estimation. We show how a geometryaware representation learning framework leads to the best eventbased object TTC estimation network that operates at 200 FPS. The dataset, code, and pre-trained models will be made publicly available for future research.

</details>


### [166] [ADAPT: Adaptive Dual-projection Architecture for Perceptive Traversal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16328)
*Shuo Shao,Tianchen Huang,Wei Gao,Shiwu Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ADAPT introduces an adaptive dual-projection perception for humanoid locomotion: uses horizontal elevation for terrain geometry and vertical distance for traversable-space constraints; makes sensing range a learnable action to adapt perceptual horizon, reducing observation size and boosting training efficiency; shows strong zero-shot transfer to a Unitree G1 and outperforms fixed-range baselines in diverse 3D environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To balance perceptual fidelity and computational efficiency in agile humanoid locomotion, addressing the limitations of rigid sensing configurations in complex 3D environments.

Method: Represent environment with two projections: a horizontal elevation map for terrain geometry and a vertical distance map for traversable-space constraints. Treat the sensing range as a learnable action that expands during fast motion and contracts in clutter for higher local resolution. Compare against voxel-based baselines.

Result: Significant reduction in observation dimensionality and computational overhead, faster training. Achieves successful zero-shot transfer to a Unitree G1 Humanoid and substantially outperforms fixed-range baselines, with robust traversal across varied 3D environments.

Conclusion: ADAPT enables adaptive perception and more efficient, robust traversal in complex 3D environments by dual-projection sensing and learnable perceptual horizon, outperforming voxel-based methods.

Abstract: Agile humanoid locomotion in complex 3D en- vironments requires balancing perceptual fidelity with com- putational efficiency, yet existing methods typically rely on rigid sensing configurations. We propose ADAPT (Adaptive dual-projection architecture for perceptive traversal), which represents the environment using a horizontal elevation map for terrain geometry and a vertical distance map for traversable- space constraints. ADAPT further treats its spatial sensing range as a learnable action, enabling the policy to expand its perceptual horizon during fast motion and contract it in cluttered scenes for finer local resolution. Compared with voxel-based baselines, ADAPT drastically reduces observation dimensionality and computational overhead while substantially accelerating training. Experimentally, it achieves successful zero-shot transfer to a Unitree G1 Humanoid and signifi- cantly outperforms fixed-range baselines, yielding highly robust traversal across diverse 3D environtmental challenges.

</details>


### [167] [Faulty Coffees: Barriers to Adoption of an In-the-wild Robo-Barista](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16336)
*Bruce W. Wilson,David A. Robb,Mei Yii Lim,Helen Hastie,Matthew Peter Aylett,Theodoros Georgiou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Task-based narratives did not sustain long-term engagement with a service robot in a real-world five-week deployment; repeat interactions were low and affected by curiosity trials, technical breakdowns, accessibility barriers, and social dynamics, highlighting practical long-term HRI challenges and lessons for future longitudinal work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether narrative-driven interaction strategies can produce durable user engagement with social robots in everyday, unsupervised settings, and to reveal real-world challenges that arise in longitudinal Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) deployments.

Method: Longitudinal field deployment of a Furhat-based Robo-Barista for five weeks in an over-50s housing complex in Stockton, England. Residents received a free daily coffee via interactions with a robot assigned to either a narrative or non-narrative dialogue condition; data collected on engagement, interaction frequency, and encountered barriers.

Result: Despite planning for sustained engagement, repeat usage was low; the study observed curiosity trials without retention, along with technical breakdowns, accessibility barriers, and complex social dynamics of the housing setting, prompting the authors to foreground these issues rather than treat them as peripheral.

Conclusion: The work foregrounds the in-the-wild realities of longitudinal HRI research, offering lessons and recommendations for conducting long-term studies when practical unraveling occurs, with emphasis on reliability, accessibility, and social-context considerations.

Abstract: We set out to study whether task-based narratives could influence long-term engagement with a service robot. To do so, we deployed a Robo-Barista for five weeks in an over-50's housing complex in Stockton, England. Residents received a free daily coffee by interacting with a Furhat robot assigned to either a narrative or non-narrative dialogue condition. Despite designing for sustained engagement, repeat interaction was low, and we encountered curiosity trials without retention, technical breakdowns, accessibility barriers, and the social dynamics of a housing complex setting. Rather than treating these as peripheral issues, we foreground them in this paper. We reflect on the in-the-wild realities of our experiment and offer lessons for conducting longitudinal Human-Robot Interaction research when studies unravel in practice.

</details>


### [168] [Encoding Predictability and Legibility for Style-Conditioned Diffusion Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16368)
*Adrien Jacquet Crétides,Mouad Abrini,Hamed Rahimi,Mohamed Chetouani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A post-training conditioning framework (SCDP) steers a pre-trained diffusion policy to balance legibility and efficiency by using scene ambiguity to modulate conditioning, improving legibility in ambiguous tasks without sacrificing efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the tension between expressive, legible robot motion and task efficiency in human-robot collaboration, ensuring safety and trust without incurring unnecessary delays or energy use. Leverages a fixed base policy to avoid retraining.

Method: Freeze the base diffusion model; train a lightweight scene encoder and conditioning predictor within a post-training pipeline; implement an ambiguity detector to switch conditioning between legibility-focused and efficiency-focused modes during inference; evaluate on manipulation and navigation tasks.

Result: SCDP enhances legibility in ambiguous settings while preserving optimal efficiency in unambiguous ones, without retraining the base policy and with a modular design that can be applied to multiple tasks (manipulation and navigation).

Conclusion: A modular, post-training conditioning scheme can effectively balance legibility and efficiency in HRC by adapting the diffusion process to scene ambiguity, improving safety and trust without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Striking a balance between efficiency and transparent motion is a core challenge in human-robot collaboration, as highly expressive movements often incur unnecessary time and energy costs. In collaborative environments, legibility allows a human observer a better understanding of the robot's actions, increasing safety and trust. However, these behaviors result in sub-optimal and exaggerated trajectories that are redundant in low-ambiguity scenarios where the robot's goal is already obvious. To address this trade-off, we propose Style-Conditioned Diffusion Policy (SCDP), a modular framework that constrains the trajectory generation of a pre-trained diffusion model toward either legibility or efficiency based on the environment's configuration. Our method utilizes a post-training pipeline that freezes the base policy and trains a lightweight scene encoder and conditioning predictor to modulate the diffusion process. At inference time, an ambiguity detection module activates the appropriate conditioning, prioritizing expressive motion only for ambiguous goals and reverting to efficient paths otherwise. We evaluate SCDP on manipulation and navigation tasks, and results show that it enhances legibility in ambiguous settings while preserving optimal efficiency when legibility is unnecessary, all without retraining the base policy.

</details>


### [169] [Controlling Fish Schools via Reinforcement Learning of Virtual Fish Movement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16384)
*Yusuke Nishii,Hiroaki Kawashima*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Reinforcement learning with virtual 2D fish can guide real fish schools toward target directions; policies learned in simulation can transfer to real fish; achieves guidance and outperforms baselines, including no stimulus and stay-at-edge strategy; an early demonstration of using RL to influence collective animal behavior via artificial agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address practical constraints of physical robotic agents and the absence of detailed behavioral models for real fish by using screen-displayed virtual fish trained with model-free reinforcement learning to steer real fish.

Method: Train model-free RL policies for 2D virtual fish displayed on a screen; use simulation to develop movement policies; apply learned policy to guide live fish; evaluate against baselines (no stimulus, heuristic stay-at-edge) and perform statistical analysis.

Result: Policy demonstrates effective guidance of fish schools toward specified directions; RL can learn useful movement policies even when real fish often ignore virtual stimuli in simulation; real-world experiments confirm guidance and show significant improvement over baseline conditions.

Conclusion: The work provides an early demonstration that reinforcement learning can influence collective animal behavior via artificial agents, highlighting the potential of RL-controlled virtual agents to modulate swarm dynamics in real animals.

Abstract: This study investigates a method to guide and control fish schools using virtual fish trained with reinforcement learning. We utilize 2D virtual fish displayed on a screen to overcome technical challenges such as durability and movement constraints inherent in physical robotic agents. To address the lack of detailed behavioral models for real fish, we adopt a model-free reinforcement learning approach. First, simulation results show that reinforcement learning can acquire effective movement policies even when simulated real fish frequently ignore the virtual stimulus. Second, real-world experiments with live fish confirm that the learned policy successfully guides fish schools toward specified target directions. Statistical analysis reveals that the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline conditions, including the absence of stimulus and a heuristic "stay-at-edge" strategy. This study provides an early demonstration of how reinforcement learning can be used to influence collective animal behavior through artificial agents.

</details>


### [170] [Onboard MuJoCo-based Model Predictive Control for Shipboard Crane with Double-Pendulum Sway Suppression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16407)
*Oscar Pang,Lisa Coiffard,Paul Templier,Luke Beddow,Kamil Dreczkowski,Antoine Cully*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Real-time, simulation-based MPC for crane sway damping using MuJoCo and cross-entropy planning; deployable on embedded hardware and robust to external perturbations and payload changes, outperforming PID and RL baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate hazardous payload sway in maritime crane operations under wind/wave disturbances; address limitations of analytical models and poor generalization of RL; enable predictive control on resource-constrained systems without extensive offline training.

Method: A real-time pipeline using MuJoCo MPC with a cross-entropy method planner that evaluates candidate action sequences via physics-based rollouts in MuJoCo; no heavy offline training; sampling-based optimization balances tracking and sway damping; validated on embedded hardware and with perturbations such as a second payload.

Result: Controller runs on resource-constrained hardware; outperforms PID and RL baselines in counteracting external base perturbations; robust to unmodeled discrepancies (e.g., second payload).

Conclusion: Demonstrates a complete, real-time, simulation-assisted predictive control framework for underactuated crane systems that avoids complex analytical models and offline training while maintaining robustness to disturbances and model mismatches.

Abstract: Transferring heavy payloads in maritime settings relies on efficient crane operation, limited by hazardous double-pendulum payload sway. This sway motion is further exacerbated in offshore environments by external perturbations from wind and ocean waves. Manual suppression of these oscillations on an underactuated crane system by human operators is challenging. Existing control methods struggle in such settings, often relying on simplified analytical models, while deep reinforcement learning (RL) approaches tend to generalise poorly to unseen conditions. Deploying a predictive controller onto compute-constrained, highly non-linear physical systems without relying on extensive offline training or complex analytical models remains a significant challenge. Here we show a complete real-time control pipeline centered on the MuJoCo MPC framework that leverages a cross-entropy method planner to evaluate candidate action sequences directly within a physics simulator. By using simulated rollouts, this sampling-based approach successfully reconciles the conflicting objectives of dynamic target tracking and sway damping without relying on complex analytical models. We demonstrate that the controller can run effectively on a resource-constrained embedded hardware, while outperforming traditional PID and RL baselines in counteracting external base perturbations. Furthermore, our system demonstrates robustness even when subjected to unmodeled physical discrepancies like the introduction of a second payload.

</details>


### [171] [Early-Terminable Energy-Safe Iterative Coupling for Parallel Simulation of Port-Hamiltonian Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16424)
*Qi Wei,Jianfeng Tao,Hongyu Nie,Wangtao Tan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A Douglas–Rachford splitting approach in scattering coordinates yields an energy-safe, early-terminable iterative coupling for port-Hamiltonian subsystems. It guarantees discrete passivity with any finite inner iteration budget, with an explicit residual, and converges to a monolithic update as iterations grow, enabling energy-consistent real-time parallel simulation under varying compute budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Partitioned time-stepping for large-scale robotic systems can inject spurious energy when coupling is truncated. There is a need for energy-consistent, adaptive methods that maintain passivity regardless of inner-iteration budget, enabling reliable parallel simulation.

Method: Embed a Douglas–Rachford (DR) splitting scheme in wave (scattering) coordinates. Enforce lossless interconnection as an orthogonal constraint in the wave domain. Each subsystem provides a discrete-time scattering port map from its one-step integrator. Impose a discrete passivity condition on the subsystem time steps and a mild impedance-tuning condition to prove a new augmented-storage inequality that ensures discrete passivity for any finite inner budget; residual captures mismatch. As inner budget grows, updates converge to the monolithic discrete-time update.

Result: The authors prove an augmented-storage inequality certifying discrete passivity of the coupled macro-step for any finite inner-iteration budget, with a known residual. Numerical experiments on a coupled-oscillator benchmark show passivity certificates hold up to double-precision roundoff and that RMS state error decreases monotonically with increasing inner iterations, approaching the hard-coupling limit.

Conclusion: Provides a principled, adaptive accuracy–compute trade-off for energy-consistent, real-time parallel simulation of port-Hamiltonian systems under varying compute budgets; supports energy-safe coupling with finite iterations and converges to a centralized update as budgets grow.

Abstract: Parallel simulation and control of large-scale robotic systems often rely on partitioned time stepping, yet finite-iteration coupling can inject spurious energy by violating power consistency--even when each subsystem is passive. This letter proposes a novel energy-safe, early-terminable iterative coupling for port-Hamiltonian subsystems by embedding a Douglas--Rachford (DR) splitting scheme in scattering (wave) coordinates. The lossless interconnection is enforced as an orthogonal constraint in the wave domain, while each subsystem contributes a discrete-time scattering port map induced by its one-step integrator. Under a discrete passivity condition on the subsystem time steps and a mild impedance-tuning condition, we prove an augmented-storage inequality certifying discrete passivity of the coupled macro-step for any finite inner-iteration budget, with the remaining mismatch captured by an explicit residual. As the inner budget increases, the partitioned update converges to the monolithic discrete-time update induced by the same integrators, yielding a principled, adaptive accuracy--compute trade-off, supporting energy-consistent real-time parallel simulation under varying computational budgets. Experiments on a coupled-oscillator benchmark validate the passivity certificates at numerical roundoff (on the order of 10e-14 in double precision) and show that the reported RMS state error decays monotonically with increasing inner-iteration budgets, consistent with the hard-coupling limit.

</details>


### [172] [Coverage First Next Best View for Inspection of Cluttered Pipe Networks Using Mobile Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16471)
*Joshua Raymond Bettles,Jiaxu Wu,Bruno Vilhena Adorno,Joaquin Carrasco,Atsushi Yamashita*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Next-best-view planning for autonomous robotic inspection in unknown, cluttered environments, using information gain to drive exploration and coverage; extends vector-field-inequalities with chance constraints to handle stochastic geometric measurements for obstacle avoidance; validated on a mobile manipulator inspecting a pipe network, achieving autonomous planning, online geometric primitive estimation, and collision avoidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable safe, automated inspection of hazardous, confined environments (e.g., radioactive areas) by removing human operators and addressing unknown/uncertain geometries and clutter. Key goals: reconstruct and fully cover surfaces while avoiding collisions under measurement uncertainty.

Method:  Reformulate coverage path planning as an information-gain-based next-best-view problem to enable simultaneous exploration and exploitation. Extend vector-field-inequalities (VFI) framework to incorporate stochastic measurements via chance constraints within a constrained optimal control law. Validate with experiments on a mobile manipulator inspecting a simplified pipe network; enable online geometric primitive estimation and obstacle avoidance between viewpoints.

Result: The system autonomously plans and executes inspection and coverage paths to reconstruct and fully cover the simplified pipe network. It can estimate geometric primitives online and avoid collisions during motion between viewpoints.

Conclusion: Integrates information-theoretic exploration with stochastic constraint handling in VFI for autonomous inspection in confined environments, enabling safe exploration, full coverage, and online geometric estimation with collision avoidance.

Abstract: Robotic inspection of radioactive areas enables operators to be removed from hazardous environments; however, planning and operating in confined, cluttered environments remain challenging. These systems must autonomously reconstruct the unknown environment and cover its surfaces, whilst estimating and avoiding collisions with objects in the environment. In this paper, we propose a new planning approach based on next-best-view that enables simultaneous exploration and exploitation of the environment by reformulating the coverage path planning problem in terms of information gain. To handle obstacle avoidance under uncertainty, we extend the vector-field-inequalities framework to explicitly account for stochastic measurements of geometric primitives in the environment via chance constraints in a constrained optimal control law. The stochastic constraints were evaluated experimentally alongside the planner on a mobile manipulator in a confined environment to inspect a pipe network. These experiments demonstrate that the system can autonomously plan and execute inspection and coverage paths to reconstruct and fully cover the simplified pipe network. Moreover, the system successfully estimated geometric primitives online and avoided collisions during motion between viewpoints.

</details>


### [173] [When Rolling Gets Weird: A Curved-Link Tensegrity Robot for Non-Intuitive Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16503)
*Lauren Ervin,Harish Bezawada,Vishesh Vikas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Semi-circular curved-link tensegrity robot (TeXploR2) aims to balance efficient rolling with stability for unstructured terrain; uses internal shifting masses on curved links to modulate ground contact; validated via simulations and a tetherless prototype with initial impact tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the speed-stability trade-off of conventional tensegrity robots (straight-link) and spherical robots, enabling reliable, controllable locomotion on uneven terrains typical of space exploration.

Method: Developed an improved TeXploR2 with semi-circular curved links. Internal shifting masses roll along curved links to alter ground-contact points. Built on an existing geometric static modeling framework. Conducted quasistatic, piecewise continuous locomotion simulations. Verified motion with a tetherless prototype and performed a preliminary impact test.

Result: Revealed non-intuitive rolling behaviors and the positional displacement between inertial and body frames. Demonstrated successful dynamic locomotion in experiments. The impact test showed inherent shock absorption and conformability of the tensegrity structure.

Conclusion: Dynamic modeling remains to be finalized and experimentally validated; plan to extend testing to real-world environments and incorporate additional curved-links and contact points to enhance controllability.

Abstract: Conventional mobile tensegrity robots constructed with straight links offer mobility at the cost of locomotion speed. While spherical robots provide highly effective rolling behavior, they often lack the stability required for navigating unstructured terrain common in many space exploration environments. This research presents a solution with a semi-circular, curved-link tensegrity robot that strikes a balance between efficient rolling locomotion and controlled stability, enabled by discontinuities present at the arc endpoints. Building upon an existing geometric static modeling framework [1], this work presents the system design of an improved Tensegrity eXploratory Robot 2 (TeXploR2). Internal shifting masses instantaneously roll along each curved-link, dynamically altering the two points of contact with the ground plane. Simulations of quasistatic, piecewise continuous locomotion sequences reveal new insights into the positional displacement between inertial and body frames. Non-intuitive rolling behaviors are identified and experimentally validated using a tetherless prototype, demonstrating successful dynamic locomotion. A preliminary impact test highlights the tensegrity structure's inherent shock absorption capabilities and conformability. Future work will focus on finalizing a dynamic model that is experimentally validated with extended testing in real-world environments as well as further refinement of the prototype to incorporate additional curved-links and subsequent ground contact points for increased controllability.

</details>


### [174] [LIMBERO: A Limbed Climbing Exploration Robot Toward Traveling on Rocky Cliffs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16531)
*Kentaro Uno,Masazumi Imai,Kazuki Takada,Teruhiro Kataonami,Yudai Matsuura,Antonin Ringeval-Meusnier,Keita Nagaoka,Mikio Eguchi,Ryo Nishibe,Kazuya Yoshida*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A 10 kg quadrupedal climbing robot LIMBERO using spine-type grippers with a single-motor coupled closing and hooking mechanism achieves >150 N grip while weighing 525 g per gripper; it includes a geometry-based graspability index visualization algorithm for rough terrain and demonstrates ascent on steep rocky surfaces at 1 g, marking a milestone for large limbed climbing robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enabling robust locomotion on highly irregular and inclined terrains for planetary/lunar exploration where wheeled robots are insufficient.

Method: Design of spine-type grippers with coupled finger-closing and spine-hooking motions actuated by a single motor; development of a geometry-based graspability index visualization for continuous rough terrain; integration into LIMBERO.

Result: Demonstrated steep-rock ascent in 1 g with a 10 kg-class quadrupedal climbing robot, achieving a capability not previously demonstrated at this scale.

Conclusion: The integrated gripper design and graspability visualization enable stable locomotion and climbing on rugged terrain for large limbed climbing robots, advancing capabilities for planetary exploration.

Abstract: In lunar and planetary exploration, legged robots have attracted significant attention as an alternative to conventional wheeled robots, which struggle to traverse rough and uneven terrain. To enable locomotion over highly irregular and steeply inclined surfaces, limbed climbing robots equipped with grippers on their feet have emerged as a promising solution. In this paper, we present LIMBERO, a 10 kg-class quadrupedal climbing robot that employs spine-type grippers for stable locomotion and climbing on rugged and steep terrain. We first introduce a novel gripper design featuring coupled finger-closing and spine-hooking motions, tightly actuated by a single motor, which achieves exceptional grasping performance (>150 N) despite its lightweight design (525 g). Furthermore, we develop an efficient algorithm to visualize a geometry-based graspability index on continuous rough terrain. Finally, we integrate these components into LIMBERO and demonstrate its ability to ascend steep rocky surfaces under a 1 G gravity condition, a performance not previously achieved yet for limbed climbing robots of this scale.

</details>


### [175] [Kamino: GPU-based Massively Parallel Simulation of Multi-Body Systems with Challenging Topologies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16536)
*Vassilios Tsounis,Guirec Maloisel,Christian Schumacher,Ruben Grandia,Agon Serifi,David Müller,Chris Amevor,Tobias Widmer,Moritz Bächer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GPU-based constrained rigid-body dynamics solver (Kamino) enables massively parallel, accurate simulations of heterogeneous robots with closed-loop kinematic chains for data-driven RL, surpassing topology simplifications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional approaches either simplify robot topology (e.g., tree structures, loop closures, mimic joints) or use approximate models, limiting fidelity for RL. There is a need for high-fidelity, scalable simulation that natively handles loops and heterogeneous robot ensembles to advance data-driven methods and RL in robotics.

Method: Kamino is a Python-implemented, CUDA-accelerated solver built on NVIDIA Warp and integrated with the Newton framework. It solves the constrained multi-body forward dynamics by formulating and solving a nonlinear complementarity problem to compute constraint forces for systems with looped/topologically complex constraints, enabling exact loop-closure handling and mimic joints without topology approximations. It supports batched simulation of heterogeneous robots on a single GPU.

Result: The paper demonstrates RL policy training on DR Legs, a six-loop biped, achieving feasibility in walking and simultaneously simulating 4096 parallel environments on one GPU, indicating high-throughput, high-fidelity parallel simulation.

Conclusion: Kamino provides a high-fidelity, scalable solution for simulating complex, closed-chain robotic systems, enabling data-driven methods and large-scale RL without topology simplifications. This advances the practicality of learning-based control in robotics and supports heterogeneous world simulations on GPU hardware.

Abstract: We present Kamino, a GPU-based physics solver for massively parallel simulations of heterogeneous highly-coupled mechanical systems. Implemented in Python using NVIDIA Warp and integrated into the Newton framework, it enables the application of data-driven methods, such as large-scale reinforcement learning, to complex robotic systems that exhibit strongly coupled kinematic and dynamic constraints such as kinematic loops. The latter are often circumvented by practitioners; approximating the system topology as a kinematic tree and incorporating explicit loop-closure constraints or so-called mimic joints. Kamino aims at alleviating this burden by natively supporting these types of coupling. This capability facilitates high-throughput parallelized simulations that capture the true nature of mechanical systems that exploit closed kinematic chains for mechanical advantage. Moreover, Kamino supports heterogeneous worlds, allowing for batched simulation of structurally diverse robots on a single GPU. At its core lies a state-of-the-art constrained optimization algorithm that computes constraint forces by solving the constrained rigid multi-body forward dynamics transcribed as a nonlinear complementarity problem. This leads to high-fidelity simulations that can resolve contact dynamics without resorting to approximate models that simplify and/or convexify the problem. We demonstrate RL policy training on DR Legs, a biped with six nested kinematic loops, generating a feasible walking policy while simulating 4096 parallel environments on a single GPU.

</details>


### [176] [Conservative Offline Robot Policy Learning via Posterior-Transition Reweighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16542)
*Wanpeng Zhang,Hao Luo,Sipeng Zheng,Yicheng Feng,Haiweng Xu,Ziheng Xi,Chaoyi Xu,Haoqi Yuan,Zongqing Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PTR is a reward-free, conservative offline post-training method that reweights each sample by a posterior over potential post-action targets to improve adaptation on heterogeneous robot data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot datasets are heterogeneous (embodiments, cameras, demonstration quality). Uniform post-training misallocates credit, diluting informative signals and overemphasizing low-quality or conflicting data.

Method: For every sample, encode the observed post-action consequence as a latent target, maintain a pool of mismatched targets, train a transition scorer to estimate a softmax posterior over target indices, compute PTR score as posterior-to-uniform ratio, clip and mix this weight, and apply it to the original action objective through self-normalized weighted regression. The approach does not require tractable policy likelihood and is compatible with diffusion and flow-matching action heads.

Result: The abstract claims improved conservative offline adaptation to heterogeneous robot data by reallocating credit according to post-action consequence attribution under the current representation; no empirical results are provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: PTR reallocates credit to samples based on how attributable their post-action consequences are, enabling more reliable offline adaptation without policy-likelihood assumptions and supporting multiple action head families.

Abstract: Offline post-training adapts a pretrained robot policy to a target dataset by supervised regression on recorded actions. In practice, robot datasets are heterogeneous: they mix embodiments, camera setups, and demonstrations of varying quality, so many trajectories reflect recovery behavior, inconsistent operator skill, or weakly informative supervision. Uniform post-training gives equal credit to all samples and can therefore average over conflicting or low-attribution data. We propose Posterior-Transition Reweighting (PTR), a reward-free and conservative post-training method that decides how much each training sample should influence the supervised update. For each sample, PTR encodes the observed post-action consequence as a latent target, inserts it into a candidate pool of mismatched targets, and uses a separate transition scorer to estimate a softmax identification posterior over target indices. The posterior-to-uniform ratio defines the PTR score, which is converted into a clipped-and-mixed weight and applied to the original action objective through self-normalized weighted regression. This construction requires no tractable policy likelihood and is compatible with both diffusion and flow-matching action heads. Rather than uniformly trusting all recorded supervision, PTR reallocates credit according to how attributable each sample's post-action consequence is under the current representation, improving conservative offline adaptation to heterogeneous robot data.

</details>


### [177] [A Pin-Array Structured Climbing Robot for Stable Locomotion on Steep Rocky Terrain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16543)
*Keita Nagaoka,Kentaro Uno,Kazuya Yoshida*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A mobile climbing robot uses a compliant pin-array gripper with vertically split pins to passively adapt to surface irregularities, enabling robust climbing with minimal sensing/control; statistical modeling points to pin force variability and contact count as main grasping uncertainties; demonstrated on indoor inclined walls (10-30°) and outdoor rocky terrain; highlights passive compliance and mechanical redundancy as a practical, robust solution for real-world climbing robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Climbing in unstructured environments requires reliable attachment to irregular surfaces. Reducing control complexity and sensing by leveraging passive mechanical compliance can enhance robustness and simplicity.

Method: Design of a pin-array gripper with vertically split pins (elastic element plus metal spine) to mechanically interlock with microscale surface features. The system emphasizes passive conformity and redundancy over active sensing/control. Statistical modeling identifies grasping uncertainty sources; experimental validation conducted on indoor inclined walls and outdoor rocky terrain.

Result: The robot achieved robust and stable locomotion on indoor walls with 10-30° inclination and on natural outdoor rocky terrain. Grasping performance is robust, with mechanical redundancy and passive compliance reducing control requirements. Variability in individual pin forces and contact numbers were identified as primary sources of grasping uncertainty.

Conclusion: A design that emphasizes passive compliance and mechanical redundancy provides a practical, robust solution for real-world climbing robots while minimizing control complexity and sensing needs.

Abstract: Climbing robots face significant challenges when navigating unstructured environments, where reliable attachment to irregular surfaces is critical. We present a novel mobile climbing robot equipped with compliant pin-array structured grippers that passively conform to surface irregularities, ensuring stable ground gripping without the need for complicated sensing or control. Each pin features a vertically split design, combining an elastic element with a metal spine to enable mechanical interlocking with microscale surface features. Statistical modeling and experimental validation indicate that variability in individual pin forces and contact numbers are the primary sources of grasping uncertainty. The robot demonstrated robust and stable locomotion in indoor tests on inclined walls (10-30 degrees) and in outdoor tests on natural rocky terrain. This work highlights that a design emphasizing passive compliance and mechanical redundancy provides a practical and robust solution for real-world climbing robots while minimizing control complexity.

</details>


### [178] [ASCENT: Transformer-Based Aircraft Trajectory Prediction in Non-Towered Terminal Airspace](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16550)
*Alexander Prutsch,David Schinagl,Horst Possegger*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ASCENT is a lightweight transformer-based model for multi-modal 3D aircraft trajectory forecasting in non-towered terminal airspace, achieving low-latency, diverse maneuver predictions and state-of-the-art performance on TrajAir and TartanAviation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance safety and efficiency in high-density, non-towered terminal airspace by providing accurate, real-time, multi-hypothesis trajectory predictions that capture 3D motion and structured traffic patterns.

Method: Transformer-based motion encoder with a query-based decoder; domain-aware 3D coordinate normalization; parameterized outputs to generate diverse maneuver hypotheses; multi-modal predictions with low latency; validated on TrajAir and TartanAviation; ablation studies.

Result: Outperforms prior baselines on TrajAir and TartanAviation; encoder captures motion dynamics while decoder aligns with traffic patterns; ablations confirm the importance of decoder design, coordinate-frame modeling, and parameterized outputs; demonstrates real-time applicability.

Conclusion: ASCENT is an effective approach for real-time, multi-modal aircraft trajectory prediction in non-towered terminal airspace, with potential for integration into safety-critical aviation systems.

Abstract: Accurate trajectory prediction can improve General Aviation safety in non-towered terminal airspace, where high traffic density increases accident risk. We present ASCENT, a lightweight transformer-based model for multi-modal 3D aircraft trajectory forecasting, which integrates domain-aware 3D coordinate normalization and parameterized predictions. ASCENT employs a transformer-based motion encoder and a query-based decoder, enabling the generation of diverse maneuver hypotheses with low latency. Experiments on the TrajAir and TartanAviation datasets demonstrate that our model outperforms prior baselines, as the encoder effectively captures motion dynamics and the decoder aligns with structured aircraft traffic patterns. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm the contributions of the decoder design, coordinate-frame modeling, and parameterized outputs. These results establish ASCENT as an effective approach for real-time aircraft trajectory prediction in non-towered terminal airspace.

</details>


### [179] [Scalable Inspection Planning via Flow-based Mixed Integer Linear Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16593)
*Adir Morgan,Kiril Solovey,Oren Salzman*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces scalable MILP formulations for Graph Inspection Planning (GIP) by reformulating core constraints as a network-flow and using a specialized Branch-and-Cut solver; achieves substantially tighter lower bounds and scales to large graphs (up to ~15k vertices, thousands of POIs), outperforming prior methods on both runtime and optimality gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GIP is computationally hard due to the dual demands of POI coverage and path connectivity. Sampling-based discretization simplifies the problem but remains challenging at realistic scales, with existing solvers often failing to provide meaningful optimality guarantees. A scalable optimization framework is needed for practical, large-scale GIP applications.

Method: Reformulate the problem constraints as a network-flow model, enabling robust MILP formulations. Develop a specialized Branch-and-Cut solver that exploits the combinatorial structure of flows. Evaluate on medical and infrastructure benchmarks alongside large-scale synthetic instances.

Result: The approach yields tighter lower bounds than prior formulations, reducing optimality gaps by 30–50% on large instances. It demonstrates unprecedented scalability, delivering non-trivial solutions for problems with up to 15,000 vertices and thousands of POIs, where previous methods either ran out of memory or provided weak guarantees.

Conclusion: Network-flow reformulation combined with a specialized Branch-and-Cut solver significantly advances state-of-the-art in Graph Inspection Planning, offering substantial improvements in both solution quality and scalability, with strong potential for real-world deployment in manufacturing and medical robotics contexts.

Abstract: Inspection planning is concerned with computing the shortest robot path to inspect a given set of points of interest (POIs) using the robot's sensors. This problem arises in a wide range of applications from manufacturing to medical robotics. To alleviate the problem's complexity, recent methods rely on sampling-based methods to obtain a more manageable (discrete) graph inspection planning (GIP) problem. Unfortunately, GIP still remains highly difficult to solve at scale as it requires simultaneously satisfying POI-coverage and path-connectivity constraints, giving rise to a challenging optimization problem, particularly at scales encountered in real-world scenarios. In this work, we present highly scalable Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) solutions for GIP that significantly advance the state-of-the-art in both runtime and solution quality. Our key insight is a reformulation of the problem's core constraints as a network flow, which enables effective MILP models and a specialized Branch-and-Cut solver that exploits the combinatorial structure of flows. We evaluate our approach on medical and infrastructure benchmarks alongside large-scale synthetic instances. Across all scenarios, our method produces substantially tighter lower bounds than existing formulations, reducing optimality gaps by 30-50% on large instances. Furthermore, our solver demonstrates unprecedented scalability: it provides non-trivial solutions for problems with up to 15,000 vertices and thousands of POIs, where prior state-of-the-art methods typically exhaust memory or fail to provide any meaningful optimality guarantees.

</details>


### [180] [Dexterous grasp data augmentation based on grasp synthesis with fingertip workspace cloud and contact-aware sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16609)
*Liqi Wu,Haoyu Jia,Kento Kawaharazuka,Hirokazu Ishida,Kei Okada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A teleoperation-based grasp data collection and augmentation pipeline for arbitrary-hand robots, combining FSG and AutoWS to enable real-time, human-like grasp generation and improved data augmentation for data-driven training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven grasping requires large, diverse datasets, but hand diversity and data collection bottlenecks hinder generalization and training efficiency.

Method: Teleoperation to collect a small set of grasp pose demonstrations; augment with Fingertip-contact-aware Sampling-based Grasp generator (FSG); AutoWS automatically generates structured fingertip workspace clouds embedding hand structure to obviate inverse kinematics.

Result: On YCB objects, the approach achieves higher speed and valid pose generation rate than baselines; enables real-time grasp generation for arbitrary hand structures; produces human-like grasps with demonstrations; efficient data augmentation for training.

Conclusion: The framework provides a general, efficient data augmentation tool for data-driven grasp training across diverse robotic hands, enabling robust and real-time grasp generation.

Abstract: Robotic grasping is a fundamental yet crucial component of robotic applications, as effective grasping often serves as the starting point for various tasks. With the rapid advancement of neural networks, data-driven approaches for robotic grasping have become mainstream. However, efficiently generating grasp datasets for training remains a bottleneck. This is compounded by the diverse structures of robotic hands, making the design of generalizable grasp generation methods even more complex. In this work, we propose a teleoperation-based framework to collect a small set of grasp pose demonstrations, which are augmented using FSG--a Fingertip-contact-aware Sampling-based Grasp generator. Based on the demonstrated grasp poses, we propose AutoWS, which automatically generates structured workspace clouds of robotic fingertips, embedding the hand structure information directly into the clouds to eliminate the need for inverse kinematics calculations. Experiments on grasping the YCB objects show that our method significantly outperforms existing approaches in both speed and valid pose generation rate. Our framework enables real-time grasp generation for hands with arbitrary structures and produces human-like grasps when combined with demonstrations, providing an efficient and robust data augmentation tool for data-driven grasp training.

</details>


### [181] [Routing and Control for Marine Oil-Spill Cleanup with a Boom-Towing Vessel Fleet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16626)
