*Snir Carmeli,Adir Morgan,Kiril Solovey*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a scalable, risk-aware multi-robot framework for coordinating autonomous surface-vehicle (ASV) duos to confine and clean multiple oil spills. Formulates a risk-weighted minimum-latency problem, solved via MILP with a tailored warm-start heuristic to yield near-optimal plans for tens of spills. Evaluates two boom-towing controllers (feedback-linearization and PID) through simulations with coupled dynamics, showing effective path tracking. Provides a holistic solution for large-scale, rapid oil-spill response.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Realistic oil-spill events involve multiple concurrent spills and large fleets; existing work mainly handles single spills or small-scale scenarios, lacking scalable, coordinated, and risk-aware planning suitable for real-world incidents.

Method: Formulate multi-spill response as a risk-weighted minimum-latency problem. Solve with a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) framework augmented by a custom warm-start heuristic for fast, near-optimal routing across tens of spills. Deploy two boom-towing ASV duo tracking controllers: a feedback-linearization controller with proven asymptotic stability and a baseline PID controller. Validate via simulations incorporating coupled vessel-boom dynamics and assess path-tracking accuracy and planning latency.

Result: The approach yields near-optimal routing plans for scenarios with tens of spills within minutes on commodity hardware. Simulation results demonstrate accurate path tracking for both controllers under coupled dynamics, supporting rapid, risk-aware multi-robot response at large scale.

Conclusion: The integrated framework delivers a scalable, holistic solution for rapid, risk-aware multi-robot oil-spill response, enabling coordination of ASV duos across multiple spills and providing practical planning and control constructs for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Marine oil spills damage ecosystems, contaminate coastlines, and disrupt food webs, while imposing substantial economic losses on fisheries and coastal communities. Prior work has demonstrated the feasibility of containing and cleaning individual spills using a duo of autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) equipped with a towed boom and skimmers. However, existing algorithmic approaches primarily address isolated slicks and individual ASV duos, lacking scalable methods for coordinating large robotic fleets across multiple spills representative of realistic oil-spill incidents. In this work, we propose an integrated multi-robot framework for coordinated oil-spill confinement and cleanup using autonomous ASV duos. We formulate multi-spill response as a risk-weighted minimum-latency problem, where spill-specific risk factors and service times jointly determine cumulative environmental damage. To solve this problem, we develop a hybrid optimization approach combining mixed-integer linear programming, and a tailored warm-start heuristic, enabling near-optimal routing plans for scenarios with tens of spills within minutes on commodity hardware. For physical execution, we design and analyze two tracking controllers for boom-towing ASV duos: a feedback-linearization controller with proven asymptotic stability, and a baseline PID controller. Simulation results under coupled vessel-boom dynamics demonstrate accurate path tracking for both controllers. Together, these components provide a scalable, holistic framework for rapid, risk-aware multi-robot response to large-scale oil spill disasters.

</details>


### [182] [Reconciling distributed compliance with high-performance control in continuum soft robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16630)
*Vito Daniele Perfetta,Daniel Feliu Talegon,Ebrahim Shahabi,Cosimo Della Santina*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A fully continuum soft robotic arm with distributed compliance and fast, precise dynamic control is demonstrated, achieving millimetric accuracy and nearly fourfold speed gains without discretizing into rigid segments, surpassing prior soft robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the perceived trade-off between softness and dynamic precision in soft robotics by enabling high-bandwidth, accurate control of a truly continuous compliant arm.

Method: Direct-drive actuation with a tendon routing scheme that couples bending and twisting, combined with a structured nonlinear control framework based on reduced-order strain modeling of underactuated systems; co-design of modeling, actuation, and control to preserve mechanical complexity while enabling high-bandwidth loop closure.

Result: Dynamic Cartesian task execution with fast positioning and interaction, millimetric precision, and the fastest reported task-execution speed among soft robots; approximately fourfold speed increase over prior approaches.

Conclusion: Distributed compliance does not preclude high-performance dynamic control; a truly soft continuum manipulator can achieve both rich morphology and high-speed, precise manipulation, approaching the capabilities of rigid robots.

Abstract: High-performance closed-loop control of truly soft continuum manipulators has remained elusive. Experimental demonstrations have largely relied on sufficiently stiff, piecewise architectures in which each actuated segment behaves as a distributed yet effectively rigid element, while deformation modes beyond simple bending are suppressed. This strategy simplifies modeling and control, but sidesteps the intrinsic complexity of a fully compliant body and makes the system behave as a serial kinematic chain, much like a conventional articulated robot. An implicit conclusion has consequently emerged within the community: distributed softness and dynamic precision are incompatible. Here we show this trade-off is not fundamental. We present a highly compliant, fully continuum robotic arm - without hardware discretization or stiffness-based mode suppression - that achieves fast, precise task-space convergence under dynamic conditions. The platform integrates direct-drive actuation, a tendon routing scheme enabling coupled bending and twisting, and a structured nonlinear control architecture grounded in reduced-order strain modeling of underactuated systems. Modeling, actuation, and control are co-designed to preserve essential mechanical complexity while enabling high-bandwidth loop closure. Experiments demonstrate accurate, repeatable execution of dynamic Cartesian tasks, including fast positioning and interaction. The proposed system achieves the fastest reported task-execution speed among soft robots. At millimetric precision, execution speed increases nearly fourfold compared with prior approaches, while operating on a fully compliant continuum body. These results show that distributed compliance and high-performance dynamic control can coexist, opening a path toward truly soft manipulators approaching the operational capabilities of rigid robots without sacrificing morphological richness.

</details>


### [183] [Kinema4D: Kinematic 4D World Modeling for Spatiotemporal Embodied Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16669)
*Mutian Xu,Tianbao Zhang,Tianqi Liu,Zhaoxi Chen,Xiaoguang Han,Ziwei Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Kinema4D introduces a 4D action-conditioned robotic simulator that separates precise 4D robot control from environment reactions, using URDF kinematics to generate a 4D control trajectory and a 4D visual pointmap to synthesize reactive dynamics; trained on Robo4D-200k dataset; claims 4D-consistent, embodiment-agnostic, and potential zero-shot transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of prior 2D generative simulators and static cues by modeling complete 4D spatiotemporal robot-world interactions with accurate control and reactive environment dynamics.

Method: Two-stage disentangling: (i) precise 4D robot control via URDF-based kinematics producing 4D trajectories; (ii) generative 4D environmental reactions by projecting trajectory into a spatiotemporal pointmap and conditioning a generative model to synthesize RGB/pointmap sequences. Dataset Robo4D-200k with 201,426 episodes.

Result: Demonstrates physically-plausible, geometry-consistent, embodiment-agnostic interactions; high fidelity to real-world dynamics; shows potential zero-shot transfer.

Conclusion: Provides a high-fidelity foundation for next-generation embodied simulation and broader applications in embodied AI; dataset and method enable reliable 4D interactions in simulation.

Abstract: Simulating robot-world interactions is a cornerstone of Embodied AI. Recently, a few works have shown promise in leveraging video generations to transcend the rigid visual/physical constraints of traditional simulators. However, they primarily operate in 2D space or are guided by static environmental cues, ignoring the fundamental reality that robot-world interactions are inherently 4D spatiotemporal events that require precise interactive modeling. To restore this 4D essence while ensuring the precise robot control, we introduce Kinema4D, a new action-conditioned 4D generative robotic simulator that disentangles the robot-world interaction into: i) Precise 4D representation of robot controls: we drive a URDF-based 3D robot via kinematics, producing a precise 4D robot control trajectory. ii) Generative 4D modeling of environmental reactions: we project the 4D robot trajectory into a pointmap as a spatiotemporal visual signal, controlling the generative model to synthesize complex environments' reactive dynamics into synchronized RGB/pointmap sequences. To facilitate training, we curated a large-scale dataset called Robo4D-200k, comprising 201,426 robot interaction episodes with high-quality 4D annotations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method effectively simulates physically-plausible, geometry-consistent, and embodiment-agnostic interactions that faithfully mirror diverse real-world dynamics. For the first time, it shows potential zero-shot transfer capability, providing a high-fidelity foundation for advancing next-generation embodied simulation.

</details>


### [184] [When Should a Robot Think? Resource-Aware Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning for Embodied Robotic Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16673)
*Jun Liu,Pu Zhao,Zhenglun Kong,Xuan Shen,Peiyan Dong,Fan Yang,Lin Cui,Hao Tang,Geng Yuan,Wei Niu,Wenbin Zhang,Xue Lin,Gaowen Liu,Yanzhi Wang,Dong Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive, resource-aware orchestration of LLM-based reasoning in embodied agents (RARRL) that learns when to reason, which reasoning role to use, and how much compute to allocate, improving task success and reducing latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM reasoning introduces substantial latency and resource overhead in embodied agents; fixed or heuristic reasoning policies degrade reliability and responsiveness. A principled mechanism is needed to decide when to reason versus act and how to allocate compute.

Method: Propose RARRL, a hierarchical framework that places a high-level orchestration policy at the agent’s decision-making layer. The policy adaptively decides whether to invoke reasoning, selects the reasoning role, and allocates computational budget based on current observations, history, and remaining resources. Trains via reinforcement learning. Evaluation uses empirical latency profiles derived from ALFRED benchmark to simulate realistic timing.

Result: RARRL achieves higher task success rates, lower execution latency, and greater robustness compared with fixed or heuristic reasoning strategies across tasks and latency scenarios.

Conclusion: Adaptive, resource-aware reasoning control is essential for reliable and efficient embodied robotic agents; RARRL demonstrates the practicality and benefits of coordinating reasoning at the decision-making level rather than through fixed policies.

Abstract: Embodied robotic systems increasingly rely on large language model (LLM)-based agents to support high-level reasoning, planning, and decision-making during interactions with the environment. However, invoking LLM reasoning introduces substantial computational latency and resource overhead, which can interrupt action execution and reduce system reliability. Excessive reasoning may delay actions, while insufficient reasoning often leads to incorrect decisions and task failures. This raises a fundamental question for embodied agents: when should the agent reason, and when should it act? In this work, we propose RARRL (Resource-Aware Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning), a hierarchical framework for resource-aware orchestration of embodied agents. Rather than learning low-level control policies, RARRL learns a high-level orchestration policy that operates at the agent's decision-making layer. This policy enables the agent to adaptively determine whether to invoke reasoning, which reasoning role to employ, and how much computational budget to allocate based on current observations, execution history, and remaining resources. Extensive experiments, including evaluations with empirical latency profiles derived from the ALFRED benchmark, show that RARRL consistently improves task success rates while reducing execution latency and enhancing robustness compared with fixed or heuristic reasoning strategies. These results demonstrate that adaptive reasoning control is essential for building reliable and efficient embodied robotic agents.

</details>


### [185] [Learning Whole-Body Control for a Salamander Robot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16683)
*Mengze Tian,Qiyuan Fu,Chuanfang Ning,Javier Jia Jie Pey,Auke Ijspeert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Reinforcement Learning maps proprioception and commanded velocities to unified joint-level actions for a salamander-inspired amphibious robot, enabling real-world walking on flat and uneven terrain with a system-level real-to-sim and sim-to-real transfer pipeline; simulated transitions between walking and swimming demonstrate cross-modal locomotion insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between traditional CPG/model-based control and learning-based, unified joint-level control for amphibious, highly articulated robots; enable robust real-world transfer of learned policies and explore cross-mode locomotion (land–sea).

Method: Train a Reinforcement Learning policy that maps proprioceptive observations and velocity commands to joint-level actions for a salamander-inspired robot. Use system-level real-to-sim alignment to calibrate simulation with hardware and employ sim-to-real transfer to deploy policies on hardware. Evaluate on real-world walking on flat and uneven terrain; extend evaluation to simulated walking–swimming transitions.

Result: The learned controller achieves stable, coordinated walking on both flat and uneven terrains in real hardware. In simulation, the framework supports transitions between walking and swimming, suggesting cross-modal locomotion dynamics and transferability across physical modes.

Conclusion: RL-based, unified joint-level control for salamander-inspired amphibious robots is viable and can transfer to real hardware with proper system-level matching; further work could extend real-world swimming, refine transfer strategies, and explore broader terrain and mode transitions.

Abstract: Amphibious legged robots inspired by salamanders are promising in applications in complex amphibious environments. However, despite the significant success of training controllers that achieve diverse locomotion behaviors in conventional quadrupedal robots, most salamander robots relied on central-pattern-generator (CPG)-based and model-based coordination strategies for locomotion control. Learning unified joint-level whole-body control that reliably transfers from simulation to highly articulated physical salamander robots remains relatively underexplored. In addition, few legged robots have tried learning-based controllers in amphibious environments. In this work, we employ Reinforcement Learning to map proprioceptive observations and commanded velocities to joint-level actions, allowing coordinated locomotor behaviors to emerge. To deploy these policies on hardware, we adopt a system-level real-to-sim matching and sim-to-real transfer strategy. The learned controller achieves stable and coordinated walking on both flat and uneven terrains in the real world. Beyond terrestrial locomotion, the framework enables transitions between walking and swimming in simulation, highlighting a phenomenon of interest for understanding locomotion across distinct physical modes.

</details>


### [186] [vAccSOL: Efficient and Transparent AI Vision Offloading for Mobile Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16685)
*Adam Zahir,Michele Gucciardom Falk Selker,Anastasios Nanos,Kostis Papazafeiropoulos,Carlos J. Bernardos,Nicolas Weber,Roberto Gonzalez*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: vAccSOL enables efficient, transparent AI vision on robots via SOL (compiler) + vAccel (execution framework) for local or edge offloading, improving energy use and frame rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Onboard vision workloads are limited by compute and energy; proprietary accelerators and constrained companion computers hinder user-defined workloads; a flexible, hardware-aware solution is needed to meet performance and energy targets without modifying robot apps.

Method: Two-part approach: SOL, a neural network compiler that outputs optimized inference libraries with minimal runtime dependencies, and vAccel, a lightweight dispatcher that transparently runs inference locally or offloads to nearby edge infrastructure. Evaluated on a real quadruped robot with 12 DL models across image/video classification and semantic segmentation.

Result: SOL matches or surpasses PyTorch compiler baselines; edge offloading reduces robot-system power by up to 80% and edge power by up to 60% versus PyTorch, while increasing vision pipeline frame rate by up to 24x, boosting robot operating lifetime.

Conclusion: Integration of hardware-optimized inference and flexible execution placement enables efficient, portable AI vision on heterogeneous robotic and edge systems without modifying robot applications.

Abstract: Mobile robots are increasingly deployed for inspection, patrol, and search-and-rescue operations, relying on computer vision for perception, navigation, and autonomous decision-making. However, executing modern vision workloads onboard is challenging due to limited compute resources and strict energy constraints. While some platforms include embedded accelerators, these are typically tied to proprietary software stacks, leaving user-defined workloads to run on resource-constrained companion computers.
  We present vAccSOL, a framework for efficient and transparent execution of AI-based vision workloads across heterogeneous robotic and edge platforms. vAccSOL integrates two components: SOL, a neural network compiler that generates optimized inference libraries with minimal runtime dependencies, and vAccel, a lightweight execution framework that transparently dispatches inference locally on the robot or to nearby edge infrastructure. This combination enables hardware-optimized inference and flexible execution placement without requiring modifications to robot applications.
  We evaluate vAccSOL on a real-world testbed with a commercial quadruped robot and twelve deep learning models covering image classification, video classification, and semantic segmentation. Compared to a PyTorch compiler baseline, SOL achieves comparable or better inference performance. With edge offloading, vAccSOL reduces robot-side power consumption by up to 80% and edge-side power by up to 60% compared to PyTorch, while increasing vision pipeline frame rate by up to 24x, extending the operating lifetime of battery-powered robots.

</details>


### [187] [Beyond Cybathlon: On-demand Quadrupedal Assistance for People with Limited Mobility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16772)
*Carmen Scheidemann,Andrei Cramariuc,Changan Chen,Jia-Ruei Chiu,Marco Hutter*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A mobile, on-demand quadrupedal assistant integrates semi-autonomous task execution with human teleoperation, enabling mobile manipulation for wheelchair users. Validated in a Cybathlon race and home tests; shows reduced operator mental load and comparable subjective performance to state-of-the-art, with greater autonomy and agility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance independence for wheelchair users by providing a mobile, dexterous manipulation capability without sacrificing mobility or increasing wheelchair size.

Method: A shared autonomy framework combines semi-autonomous pick-and-place, semantic collision-aware navigation, and a mouth-level joystick interface for precision end-effector control, deployed on a quadrupedal robot system with on-demand autonomous and teleoperated modes.

Result: Demonstrated in Cybathlon 2024 and home experiments. The system handles diverse tasks, including dexterous manipulation, with user satisfaction high and reduced mental load due to autonomy.

Conclusion: A flexible, mobile manipulation solution for wheelchair users that maintains independence and offers greater manipulation agility; performance on subjective metrics is on par with state-of-the-art while enabling more operator autonomy.

Abstract: Background: Assistance robots have the potential to increase the independence of people who need daily care due to limited mobility or being wheelchair-bound. Current solutions of attaching robotic arms to motorized wheelchairs offer limited additional mobility at the cost of increased size and reduced wheelchair maneuverability.
  Methods: We present an on-demand quadrupedal assistance robot system controlled via a shared autonomy approach, which combines semi-autonomous task execution with human teleoperation. Due to the mobile nature of the system it can assist the operator whenever needed and perform autonomous tasks independently, without otherwise restricting their mobility. We automate pick-and-place tasks, as well as robot movement through the environment with semantic, collision-aware navigation. For teleoperation, we present a mouth-level joystick interface that enables an operator with reduced mobility to control the robot's end effector for precision manipulation.
  Results: We showcase our system in the \textit{Cybathlon 2024 Assistance Robot Race}, and validate it in an at-home experimental setup, where we measure task completion times and user satisfaction. We find our system capable of assisting in a broad variety of tasks, including those that require dexterous manipulation. The user study confirms the intuition that increased robot autonomy alleviates the operator's mental load.
  Conclusions: We present a flexible system that has the potential to help people in wheelchairs maintain independence in everyday life by enabling them to solve mobile manipulation problems without external support. We achieve results comparable to previous state-of-the-art on subjective metrics while allowing for more autonomy of the operator and greater agility for manipulation.

</details>


### [188] [Development of Low-Cost and Bidirectional Syringe Pumps for Soft Robotics Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16803)
*Krishamsu Subedi Chhetri,Aryan Mayor,Elise Corbin,Logan Walker,John Rieffel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Low-cost, modular syringe-pump actuation system for silicone voxel soft robots (Silibots) enabling bidirectional pneumatic and vacuum control using off-the-shelf components and 3D-printed parts, scalable for multi-pump setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional pneumatic actuation for soft robotics is costly, complex, and imprecise, limiting accessibility for research and education. A simple, modular, low-cost alternative could democratize experimentation with Silibots.

Method: Designed and fabricated a syringe-pump platform using standard syringes, off-the-shelf valves, and 3D-printed housings. The system provides air inflation and vacuum pulling, with modular hardware and software that supports multiple pumps, each with customizable parameters. The control architecture enables synchronized operation across pumps.

Result: The syringe-pump system functions as a low-cost, flexible actuation solution that reduces setup complexity and enables scalable experimentation with multiple independently controlled pumps for soft robotics.

Conclusion: The platform lowers barriers to entry for soft robotics research and education; future work should quantify actuation precision, timing, and repeatability, integrate sensing and closed-loop control, and evaluate performance on representative Silibot tasks.

Abstract: Soft robotics leverages deformable materials to develop robots capable of navigating unstructured and dynamic environments. Silicone Voxel-Based Soft Robots (Silibots) are a type of pneumatically actuated soft robots that rely on the inflation and deflation of their voxels for shape-shifting behaviors. However, traditional pneumatic actuation methods (high pressure solenoids, medical diaphragm pumps, micro compressors, compressed fluid) pose significant challenges due to their limited efficacy, cost, complexity, or lack of precision. This work introduces a low cost and modular syringe pump system, constructed with off the shelf and 3D printed parts, designed to overcome these limitations. The syringe pump system also enhances actuation with the unique ability to pull a vacuum as well pump air into the soft robot. Furthermore, the syringe pump features modular hardware and customizable software, allowing for researchers to tailor the syringe pump to their requirements or operate multiple pumps simultaneously with unique pump parameters. This flexibility makes the syringe pump an accessible and scalable tool that paves the way for broader adoption of soft robotic technologies in research and education.

</details>


### [189] [DexGrasp-Zero: A Morphology-Aligned Policy for Zero-Shot Cross-Embodiment Dexterous Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16806)
*Yuliang Wu,Yanhan Lin,WengKit Lao,Yuhao Lin,Yi-Lin Wei,Wei-Shi Zheng,Ancong Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexGrasp-Zero introduces a morphology-aligned graph representation and MAGCN with physical property injection to enable zero-shot cross-embodiment grasping across diverse hands. Trained on multiple hands, it transfers to unseen hardware and objects with high success rates in simulation (85% zero-shot on unseen hardware) and real-world (82% on unseen objects).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve zero-shot cross-embodiment grasping amid heterogeneous hand morphologies and physical constraints, addressing limitations of retargeting intermediate targets which can violate embodiment-specific limits and hinder transfer.

Method: Propose Morphology-Aligned Graph Convolutional Network (MAGCN) using a morphology-aligned graph where keypoints map to nodes with tri-axial orthogonal motion primitives; incorporate a Physical Property Injection mechanism to fuse hand-specific constraints; train on four diverse hands (Allegro, Shadow, Schunk, Ability) and perform zero-shot transfer to unseen hands (LEAP, Inspire); evaluation in simulation on YCB dataset and real-world tests on LEAP, Inspire, Revo2.

Result: Simulation shows 85% zero-shot success on unseen hardware (LEAP, Inspire), outperforms state-of-the-art by 59.5%. Real-world experiments yield 82% average success on unseen objects across three platforms (LEAP, Inspire, Revo2).

Conclusion: Morphology-aligned graph-based policy with physical property injection enables effective zero-shot cross-embodiment grasping, achieving strong generalization to unseen hardware and objects and surpassing prior methods.

Abstract: To meet the demands of increasingly diverse dexterous hand hardware, it is crucial to develop a policy that enables zero-shot cross-embodiment grasping without redundant re-learning. Cross-embodiment alignment is challenging due to heterogeneous hand kinematics and physical constraints. Existing approaches typically predict intermediate motion targets and retarget them to each embodiment, which may introduce errors and violate embodiment-specific limits, hindering transfer across diverse hands. To overcome these limitations, we propose DexGrasp-Zero, a policy that learns universal grasping skills from diverse embodiments, enabling zero-shot transfer to unseen hands. We first introduce a morphology-aligned graph representation that maps each hand's kinematic keypoints to anatomically grounded nodes and equips each node with tri-axial orthogonal motion primitives, enabling structural and semantic alignment across different morphologies. Relying on this graph-based representation, we design a Morphology-Aligned Graph Convolutional Network (MAGCN) to encode the graph for policy learning. MAGCN incorporates a Physical Property Injection mechanism that fuses hand-specific physical constraints into the graph features, enabling adaptive compensation for varying link lengths and actuation limits for precise and stable grasping. Our extensive simulation evaluations on the YCB dataset demonstrate that our policy, jointly trained on four heterogeneous hands (Allegro, Shadow, Schunk, Ability), achieves an 85% zero-shot success rate on unseen hardware (LEAP, Inspire), outperforming the state-of-the-art method by 59.5%. Real-world experiments further evaluate our policy on three robot platforms (LEAP, Inspire, Revo2), achieving an 82% average success rate on unseen objects.

</details>


### [190] [Real-Time Decoding of Movement Onset and Offset for Brain-Controlled Rehabilitation Exoskeleton](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16825)
*Kanishka Mitra,Satyam Kumar,Frigyes Samuel Racz,Deland Liu,Ashish D. Deshpande,José del R. Millán*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EEG-driven online dual-state motor imagery enables start-stop control of an upper-limb exoskeleton for rehabilitation, with bias diagnostics and a fixation-based recentering method; shows significant improvements in task separability and reliable online control in eight participants; indicative of bridging offline decoding to real-time, intention-driven assistance, with implications for neuroplasticity-targeted rehab and clinical translation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitation that most robotic rehabilitation systems operate at the limb level, hindering contingent neuroplasticity-targeted rehabilitation. The study aims to enable direct, intention-driven initiation and termination of assistive movement via non-invasive EEG.

Method: Online, dual-state motor imagery control where EEG signals initiate assistance and volitionally pause the robot mid-trajectory. Eight participants completed two online sessions. Evaluation includes hit rates for onset/offset, class-driven bias analysis using an asymmetric margin, and a class-agnostic fixation-based recentering method to track drift without sampling command classes. Outcomes measured include AUC gains (onset and offset) and bias reduction.

Result: Group-mean hit rates: onset 61.5%, offset 64.5%. AUC gains: onset +56% (p=0.0117); offset +34% (p=0.0251). The recentering method reduces bias within and across days and improves threshold-free separability, enabling more reliable online start-stop control and bridging offline decoding with practical rehabilitation exoskeleton use.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates precisely timed, intention-driven assistance aligned with neuroplasticity goals and supports future clinical translation, while addressing methodological biases in EEG-based decoding through novel recentering techniques.

Abstract: Robot-assisted therapy can deliver high-dose, task-specific training after neurologic injury, but most systems act primarily at the limb level-engaging the impaired neural circuits only indirectly-which remains a key barrier to truly contingent, neuroplasticity-targeted rehabilitation. We address this gap by implementing online, dual-state motor imagery control of an upper-limb exoskeleton, enabling goal-directed reaches to be both initiated and terminated directly from non-invasive EEG. Eight participants used EEG to initiate assistance and then volitionally halt the robot mid-trajectory. Across two online sessions, group-mean hit rates were 61.5% for onset and 64.5% for offset, demonstrating reliable start-stop command delivery despite instrumental noise and passive arm motion. Methodologically, we reveal a systematic, class-driven bias induced by common task-based recentering using an asymmetric margin diagnostic, and we introduce a class-agnostic fixation-based recentering method that tracks drift without sampling command classes while preserving class geometry. This substantially improves threshold-free separability (AUC gains: onset +56%, p = 0.0117; offset +34%, p = 0.0251) and reduces bias within and across days. Together, these results help bridge offline decoding and practical, intention-driven start-stop control of a rehabilitation exoskeleton, enabling precisely timed, contingent assistance aligned with neuroplasticity goals while supporting future clinical translation.

</details>


### [191] [BrickSim: A Physics-Based Simulator for Manipulating Interlocking Brick Assemblies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16853)
*Haowei Wen,Ruixuan Liu,Weiyi Piao,Siyu Li,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Real-time, physics-based simulator for interlocking brick assemblies (BrickSim) with a compact force-based snap-fit model solved via convex QP, enabling accurate static stability and dynamic collapse in real time; open-source and integrated with Isaac Sim.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing rigid-body simulators fail to capture snap-fit mechanics of interlocking bricks, hindering dexterous, long-horizon manipulation research and real-time assembly/disassembly tasks.

Method: A compact force-based model for snap-fit connections is developed and the internal force distribution is solved using a structured convex quadratic program. A hybrid architecture delegates rigid-body dynamics to the base physics engine, while snap-fit mechanics are handled separately in real time, enabling accurate assembly, disassembly, and collapse under real-world conditions.

Result: 100% static stability prediction accuracy on 150 real-world assemblies with an average solve time of 5 ms. Dynamic drop tests reproduce real-world structural collapse, matching breakage occurrences and locations. Built on Isaac Sim; open-source under https://github.com/intelligent-control-lab/BrickSim.

Conclusion: BrickSim delivers high-fidelity, real-time simulation of interlocking brick assemblies, supporting dexterous long-horizon robotic manipulation research and robotic construction tasks; its open-source release facilitates adoption and benchmarking.

Abstract: Interlocking brick assemblies provide a standardized yet challenging testbed for contact-rich and long-horizon robotic manipulation, but existing rigid-body simulators do not faithfully capture snap-fit mechanics. We present BrickSim, the first real-time physics-based simulator for interlocking brick assemblies. BrickSim introduces a compact force-based mechanics model for snap-fit connections and solves the resulting internal force distribution using a structured convex quadratic program. Combined with a hybrid architecture that delegates rigid-body dynamics to the underlying physics engine while handling snap-fit mechanics separately, BrickSim enables real-time, high-fidelity simulation of assembly, disassembly, and structural collapse. On 150 real-world assemblies, BrickSim achieves 100% accuracy in static stability prediction with an average solve time of 5 ms. In dynamic drop tests, it also faithfully reproduces real-world structural collapse, precisely mirroring both the occurrence of breakage and the specific breakage locations. Built on Isaac Sim, BrickSim further supports seamless integration with a wide variety of robots and existing pipelines. We demonstrate robotic construction of brick assemblies using BrickSim, highlighting its potential as a foundation for research in dexterous, long-horizon robotic manipulation. BrickSim is open-source, and the code is available at https://github.com/intelligent-control-lab/BrickSim.

</details>


### [192] [DreamPlan: Efficient Reinforcement Fine-Tuning of Vision-Language Planners via Video World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16860)
*Emily Yue-Ting Jia,Weiduo Yuan,Tianheng Shi,Vitor Guizilini,Jiageng Mao,Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DreamPlan uses zero-shot Vision-Language Model (VLM) planners, a video world model trained on exploratory data to capture real-world physics, and Odds Ratio Policy Optimization (ORPO) to fine-tune the VLM planner in imagination. This yields more reliable robotic manipulation, especially for deformable objects, without costly real-world data collection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the mismatch between semantic reasoning of VLMs and physical grounding required for manipulation, while avoiding expensive, unsafe, and sample-inefficient real-world RL data collection.

Method: (1) Collect exploratory interaction data with a zero-shot VLM planner. (2) Train an action-conditioned video generation (video world) model to implicitly encode real-world physics. (3) Fine-tune the VLM planner entirely within the video world model using ORPO, enabling virtual rollouts to inject task-specific knowledge.

Result: The framework significantly improves manipulation success rates by aligning semantic planning with physical grounding, reducing or eliminating the need for large-scale real-world data.

Conclusion: DreamPlan provides a data-efficient, safe route to adapt VLM-based planners for complex manipulation through learned video world models and imagination-based RL optimization.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation requires sophisticated commonsense reasoning, a capability naturally possessed by large-scale Vision-Language Models (VLMs). While VLMs show promise as zero-shot planners, their lack of grounded physical understanding often leads to compounding errors and low success rates when deployed in complex real-world environments, particularly for challenging tasks like deformable object manipulation. Although Reinforcement Learning (RL) can adapt these planners to specific task dynamics, directly fine-tuning VLMs via real-world interaction is prohibitively expensive, unsafe, and sample-inefficient. To overcome this bottleneck, we introduce DreamPlan, a novel framework for the reinforcement fine-tuning of VLM planners via video world models. Instead of relying on costly physical rollouts, DreamPlan first leverages the zero-shot VLM to collect exploratory interaction data. We demonstrate that this sub-optimal data is sufficient to train an action-conditioned video generation model, which implicitly captures complex real-world physics. Subsequently, the VLM planner is fine-tuned entirely within the "imagination" of this video world model using Odds Ratio Policy Optimization (ORPO). By utilizing these virtual rollouts, physical and task-specific knowledge is efficiently injected into the VLM. Our results indicate that DreamPlan bridges the gap between semantic reasoning and physical grounding, significantly improving manipulation success rates without the need for large-scale real-world data collection. Our project page is https://psi-lab.ai/DreamPlan/.

</details>


### [193] [MolmoB0T: Large-Scale Simulation Enables Zero-Shot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16861)
*Abhay Deshpande,Maya Guru,Rose Hendrix,Snehal Jauhri,Ainaz Eftekhar,Rohun Tripathi,Max Argus,Jordi Salvador,Haoquan Fang,Matthew Wallingford,Wilbert Pumacay,Yejin Kim,Quinn Pfeifer,Ying-Chun Lee,Piper Wolters,Omar Rayyan,Mingtong Zhang,Jiafei Duan,Karen Farley,Winson Han,Eli Vanderbilt,Dieter Fox,Ali Farhadi,Georgia Chalvatzaki,Dhruv Shah,Ranjay Krishna*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for manipulation is achieved using large-scale procedural synthetic data and open-source MolmoBot pipeline, with 1.8M expert trajectories and three policy classes, achieving 79.2% real-world success on tabletop pick-and-place without real-world fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenging the belief that some real-world data or task-specific fine-tuning is necessary for sim-to-real transfer. Demonstrate that diverse, large-scale synthetic data can enable zero-shot generalization to real-world manipulation tasks.

Method: 1) MolmoBot-Engine for procedural data generation across robots, tasks, and environments; 2) MolmoBot-Data with 1.8M expert trajectories; 3) three policy classes: MolmoBot (Molmo2-based with flow-matching head), MolmoBot-Pi0 (π0 architecture for direct comparison), MolmoBot-SPOC (lightweight edge-ready policy with RL-finetuning potential); 4) evaluation on Franka FR3 tabletop tasks and Rainbow RB-Y1 mobile manipulator across door/drawer/cabinet interactions and mobile pick-and-place.

Result: Zero-shot real-world transfer achieved for unseen objects/environments without real-world fine-tuning. On tabletop pick-and-place, MolmoBot attains 79.2% success in real-world settings, outperforming π0.5 at 39.2%. Demonstrates strong generalization to both static and mobile manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: Procedural environment generation combined with a diverse articulated-asset dataset yields robust manipulation policies that generalize to the real world, challenging the assumption that real data is necessary for sim-to-real transfer; the open-source MolmoBot pipeline enables broad replication and extension.

Abstract: A prevailing view in robot learning is that simulation alone is not enough; effective sim-to-real transfer is widely believed to require at least some real-world data collection or task-specific fine-tuning to bridge the gap between simulated and physical environments. We challenge that assumption. With sufficiently large-scale and diverse simulated synthetic training data, we show that zero-shot transfer to the real world is not only possible, but effective for both static and mobile manipulation. We introduce MolmoBot-Engine, a fully open-source pipeline for procedural data generation across robots, tasks, and diverse simulated environments in MolmoSpaces. With it, we release MolmoBot-Data, a dataset of 1.8 million expert trajectories for articulated object manipulation and pick-and-place tasks. We train three policy classes: MolmoBot, a Molmo2-based multi-frame vision-language model with a flow-matching action head; MolmoBot-Pi0, which replicates the $π_0$ architecture to enable direct comparison; and MolmoBot-SPOC, a lightweight policy suitable for edge deployment and amenable to RL fine-tuning. We evaluate on two robotic platforms: the Franka FR3 for tabletop manipulation tasks and the Rainbow Robotics RB-Y1 mobile manipulator for door opening, drawer manipulation, cabinet interaction, and mobile pick-and-place. Without any real-world fine-tuning, our policies achieve zero-shot transfer to unseen objects and environments. On tabletop pick-and-place, MolmoBot achieves a success rate of 79.2% in real world evaluations across 4 settings, outperforming $π_{0.5}$ at 39.2%. Our results demonstrate that procedural environment generation combined with diverse articulated assets can produce robust manipulation policies that generalize broadly to the real world. Technical Blog: https://allenai.org/blog/molmobot-robot-manipulation

</details>


### [194] [ManiTwin: Scaling Data-Generation-Ready Digital Object Dataset to 100K](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16866)
*Kaixuan Wang,Tianxing Chen,Jiawei Liu,Honghao Su,Shaolong Zhu,Minxuan Wang,Zixuan Li,Yue Chen,Huan-ang Gao,Yusen Qin,Jiawei Wang,Qixuan Zhang,Lan Xu,Jingyi Yu,Yao Mu,Ping Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ManiTwin provides an automated pipeline to convert a single image into simulation-ready, semantically annotated 3D assets and builds a 100K-asset dataset for scalable robotic manipulation data generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lack of scalable, data-generation-ready digital assets for robotic manipulation hinders data-driven policy learning. ManiTwin aims to enable large-scale, diverse simulation data through automated asset creation and annotation.

Method: An automated pipeline (ManiTwin) that turns a single image into simulation-ready 3D assets with semantic annotations, producing ManiTwin-100K (100K assets) where each asset includes physical properties, language descriptions, functional annotations, and verified manipulation proposals.

Result: Demonstrates an efficient asset synthesis and annotation workflow; ManiTwin-100K provides high-quality, diverse assets suitable for manipulation data generation, random scene synthesis, and VQA data generation, supporting scalable simulation data synthesis and policy learning.

Conclusion: ManiTwin establishes a solid foundation for scalable simulation data synthesis and policy learning in robotic manipulation by delivering a large, diverse, and well-annotated asset dataset.

Abstract: Learning in simulation provides a useful foundation for scaling robotic manipulation capabilities. However, this paradigm often suffers from a lack of data-generation-ready digital assets, in both scale and diversity. In this work, we present ManiTwin, an automated and efficient pipeline for generating data-generation-ready digital object twins. Our pipeline transforms a single image into simulation-ready and semantically annotated 3D asset, enabling large-scale robotic manipulation data generation. Using this pipeline, we construct ManiTwin-100K, a dataset containing 100K high-quality annotated 3D assets. Each asset is equipped with physical properties, language descriptions, functional annotations, and verified manipulation proposals. Experiments demonstrate that ManiTwin provides an efficient asset synthesis and annotation workflow, and that ManiTwin-100K offers high-quality and diverse assets for manipulation data generation, random scene synthesis, and VQA data generation, establishing a strong foundation for scalable simulation data synthesis and policy learning. Our webpage is available at https://manitwin.github.io/.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [195] [Neural-Symbolic Logic Query Answering in Non-Euclidean Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15633)
*Lihui Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HYQNET is a neural-symbolic model that reasons over first-order logic queries on knowledge graphs by leveraging hyperbolic space to capture hierarchical query structure, combining relation projections and logical operations over fuzzy sets, with a hyperbolic GNN for knowledge graph completion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To combine interpretability of symbolic reasoning with neural generalization while effectively handling the hierarchical structure in logical queries and incomplete graphs; hyperbolic geometry is hypothesized to better represent hierarchical dependencies.

Method: Decompose FOL queries into relation projections and logical operations over fuzzy sets; use a hyperbolic GNN to complete knowledge graphs and to embed the recursive query tree in hyperbolic space, preserving hierarchical dependencies.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three benchmark datasets shows HYQNET achieves strong performance, demonstrating the advantages of hyperbolic-space reasoning over Euclidean approaches.

Conclusion: Hyperbolic representations enable more effective capture of the hierarchical nature of logical projection reasoning, yielding superior performance for neural-symbolic FOL query reasoning on knowledge graphs.

Abstract: Answering complex first-order logic (FOL) queries on knowledge graphs is essential for reasoning. Symbolic methods offer interpretability but struggle with incomplete graphs, while neural approaches generalize better but lack transparency. Neural-symbolic models aim to integrate both strengths but often fail to capture the hierarchical structure of logical queries, limiting their effectiveness. We propose HYQNET, a neural-symbolic model for logic query reasoning that fully leverages hyperbolic space. HYQNET decomposes FOL queries into relation projections and logical operations over fuzzy sets, enhancing interpretability. To address missing links, it employs a hyperbolic GNN-based approach for knowledge graph completion in hyperbolic space, effectively embedding the recursive query tree while preserving structural dependencies. By utilizing hyperbolic representations, HYQNET captures the hierarchical nature of logical projection reasoning more effectively than Euclidean-based approaches. Experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that HYQNET achieves strong performance, highlighting the advantages of reasoning in hyperbolic space.

</details>


### [196] [NextMem: Towards Latent Factual Memory for LLM-based Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15634)
*Zeyu Zhang,Rui Li,Xiaoyan Zhao,Yang Zhang,Wenjie Wang,Xu Chen,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NextMem introduces a latent factual memory for LLM-based agents using an autoregressive autoencoder to build and reconstruct latent memory efficiently, coupled with a two-stage training regime (autoregressive reconstruction alignment and progressive latent substitution) and quantization to reduce storage. It aims to overcome textual and parametric memory limitations, and reports strong retrieval, robustness, and extensibility in experiments; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preserve past observations for decision-making in LLM agents; factual memory is foundational. Textual memory incurs heavy context/indexing; parametric memory suffers from forgetting and high costs. A latent, efficient memory framework could combine accuracy with efficiency.

Method: A latent factual memory framework (NextMem) that uses an autoregressive autoencoder to construct latent memory; two-stage training: (1) autoregressive reconstruction alignment; (2) progressive latent substitution. Quantization is used to reduce storage overhead. Likely evaluation against baselines on retrieval/robustness/extensibility.

Result: Experiments indicate NextMem achieves superior performance, excelling in retrieval, robustness, and extensibility properties compared with baselines.

Conclusion: NextMem provides an effective latent memory framework for LLM-based agents, addressing limitations of textual and parametric memory, with open-source release of code and checkpoints.

Abstract: Memory is critical for LLM-based agents to preserve past observations for future decision-making, where factual memory serves as its foundational part. However, existing approaches to constructing factual memory face several limitations. Textual methods impose heavy context and indexing burdens, while parametric methods suffer from catastrophic forgetting and high costs. To address these challenges, we introduce NextMem, a latent factual memory framework that utilizes an autoregressive autoencoder to efficiently construct latent memory while ensuring accurate reconstruction. For better optimization, we propose a two-stage training process, including autoregressive reconstruction alignment and progressive latent substitution. We also incorporate quantization to reduce storage overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate that NextMem achieves superior performance, and excels in retrieval, robustness, and extensibility properties. We release our code and model checkpoints at https://github.com/nuster1128/NextMem.

</details>


### [197] [AIDABench: AI Data Analytics Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15636)
*Yibo Yang,Fei Lei,Yixuan Sun,Yantao Zeng,Chengguang Lv,Jiancao Hong,Jiaojiao Tian,Tianyu Qiu,Xin Wang,Yanbing Chen,Yanjie Li,Zheng Pan,Xiaochen Zhou,Guanzhou Chen,Haoran Lv,Yuning Xu,Yue Ou,Haodong Liu,Shiqi He,Anya Jia,Yulei Xin,Huan Wu,Liang Liu,Jiaye Ge,Jianxin Dong,Dahua Lin,Wenxiu Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AIDABench is a large, end-to-end benchmark for complex document analytics with 600+ tasks across QA, data visualization, and file generation; evaluates 11 models; best pass-at-1 59.43%; publicly available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To close the gap in end-to-end evaluation of AI systems for real-world data analytics tasks, addressing the limitations of existing benchmarks that focus on isolated capabilities.

Method: Construct end-to-end tasks across heterogeneous data types (spreadsheets, databases, financial reports, operational records) and three capability dimensions (question answering, data visualization, file generation); evaluate 11 models including proprietary (Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3 Pro Preview) and open-source (Qwen3-Max-2026-01-23-Thinking); analyze failure modes; provide benchmarking guidance for procurement and optimization.

Result: Demonstrates that complex, real-world data analytics tasks remain challenging; best model achieves 59.43% pass-at-1; human experts may require 1–2 hours per question even with AI aids; provides detailed failure mode analysis and implications for future research; benchmark is publicly available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: AIDABench provides a principled reference for enterprise tool selection, model optimization, and benchmarking in data analytics, and offers a public resource to spur progress in end-to-end AI capabilities.

Abstract: As AI-driven document understanding and processing tools become increasingly prevalent in real-world applications, the need for rigorous evaluation standards has grown increasingly urgent. Existing benchmarks and evaluations often focus on isolated capabilities or simplified scenarios, failing to capture the end-to-end task effectiveness required in practical settings. To address this gap, we introduce AIDABench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating AI systems on complex data analytics tasks in an end-to-end manner. AIDABench encompasses 600+ diverse document analysis tasks across three core capability dimensions: question answering, data visualization, and file generation. These tasks are grounded in realistic scenarios involving heterogeneous data types, including spreadsheets, databases, financial reports, and operational records, and reflect analytical demands across diverse industries and job functions. Notably, the tasks in AIDABench are sufficiently challenging that even human experts require 1-2 hours per question when assisted by AI tools, underscoring the benchmark's difficulty and real-world complexity. We evaluate 11 state-of-the-art models on AIDABench, spanning both proprietary (e.g., Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3 Pro Preview) and open-source (e.g., Qwen3-Max-2026-01-23-Thinking) families. Our results reveal that complex, real-world data analytics tasks remain a significant challenge for current AI systems, with the best-performing model achieving only 59.43% pass-at-1. We provide a detailed analysis of failure modes across each capability dimension and identify key challenges for future research. AIDABench offers a principled reference for enterprise procurement, tool selection, and model optimization, and is publicly available at https://github.com/MichaelYang-lyx/AIDABench.

</details>


### [198] [The Comprehension-Gated Agent Economy: A Robustness-First Architecture for AI Economic Agency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15639)
*Rahul Baxi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CGAE is a formal architecture that gates AI economic permissions using a verified comprehension function derived from adversarial robustness audits. It monitors three orthogonal robustness dimensions (constraint compliance via CDCT, epistemic integrity via DDFT, behavioral alignment via AGT) with intrinsic hallucination rates as cross-cutting diagnostics. A weakest-link gate maps robustness vectors to discrete economic tiers. It proves bounded economic exposure, incentive-compatible robustness investment, and monotonic safety scaling, while incorporating temporal decay and stochastic re-auditing to prevent drift. This work formalizes a bridge between empirical robustness and economic governance, turning safety into a competitive advantage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a disconnect between capability-based gating and real-world robustness. A formal linkage is needed to bound financial risk and align agent incentives with robustness improvements, turning safety evaluation into economically advantageous governance.

Method: Define three robustness dimensions measured by external audits: constraint compliance (CDCT), epistemic integrity (DDFT), and behavioral alignment (AGT). Use intrinsic hallucination rates as a cross-cutting diagnostic. Implement a weakest-link gate that maps robustness vectors to discrete economic tiers. Prove (1) bounded economic exposure, (2) incentive-compatible robustness investment, and (3) monotonic safety scaling. Include temporal decay and stochastic re-auditing to prevent post-certification drift.

Result: Formal properties established: maximum liability bound by verified robustness, agents optimally invest in robustness rather than scaling capability, and aggregate safety does not decrease as the economy expands. The architecture yields a principled bridge between robustness evaluation and economic governance, with post-certification drift mitigated.

Conclusion: CGAE reframes safety as a competitive advantage by embedding robustness comprehension into economic permissions, enabling a formal, auditable, and scalable governance framework for AI-enabled economics.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly granted economic agency (executing trades, managing budgets, negotiating contracts, and spawning sub-agents), yet current frameworks gate this agency on capability benchmarks that are empirically uncorrelated with operational robustness. We introduce the Comprehension-Gated Agent Economy (CGAE), a formal architecture in which an agent's economic permissions are upper-bounded by a verified comprehension function derived from adversarial robustness audits. The gating mechanism operates over three orthogonal robustness dimensions: constraint compliance (measured by CDCT), epistemic integrity (measured by DDFT), and behavioral alignment (measured by AGT), with intrinsic hallucination rates serving as a cross-cutting diagnostic. We define a weakest-link gate function that maps robustness vectors to discrete economic tiers, and prove three properties of the resulting system: (1) bounded economic exposure, ensuring maximum financial liability is a function of verified robustness; (2) incentive-compatible robustness investment, showing rational agents maximize profit by improving robustness rather than scaling capability alone; and (3) monotonic safety scaling, demonstrating that aggregate system safety does not decrease as the economy grows. The architecture includes temporal decay and stochastic re-auditing mechanisms that prevent post-certification drift. CGAE provides the first formal bridge between empirical AI robustness evaluation and economic governance, transforming safety from a regulatory burden into a competitive advantage.

</details>


### [199] [CraniMem: Cranial Inspired Gated and Bounded Memory for Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15642)
*Pearl Mody,Mihir Panchal,Rishit Kar,Kiran Bhowmick,Ruhina Karani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CraniMem is a neurocognitively motivated, gated, bounded multi-stage memory system for LLM agents, combining a bounded episodic buffer and a structured long-term knowledge graph with a scheduled consolidation loop to improve long-horizon robustness and reduce interference, outperforming Vanilla RAG and Mem0 on noisy benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address unstable retention, limited consolidation, and vulnerability to distractor content in existing agent memory systems that act like ad hoc external databases.

Method: A multi-stage memory design with a bounded episodic buffer for near-term continuity, a structured long-term knowledge graph for durable recall, goal-conditioned gating, and utility tagging. A scheduled consolidation loop replays high-utility traces into the graph while pruning low-utility items to bound memory growth and reduce interference.

Result: On long-horizon benchmarks with clean inputs and injected noise, CraniMem is more robust than Vanilla RAG and Mem0 baselines, with smaller performance drops under distraction.

Conclusion: CraniMem offers a bounded, robust, memory-integrated architecture for agentic LLM systems, with code available at GitHub and PyPI for implementation and reuse.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in long running workflows, where they must preserve user and task state across many turns. Many existing agent memory systems behave like external databases with ad hoc read/write rules, which can yield unstable retention, limited consolidation, and vulnerability to distractor content. We present CraniMem, a neurocognitively motivated, gated and bounded multi-stage memory design for agentic systems. CraniMem couples goal conditioned gating and utility tagging with a bounded episodic buffer for near term continuity and a structured long-term knowledge graph for durable semantic recall. A scheduled consolidation loop replays high utility traces into the graph while pruning low utility items, keeping memory growth in check and reducing interference. On long horizon benchmarks evaluated under both clean inputs and injected noise, CraniMem is more robust than a Vanilla RAG and Mem0 baseline and exhibits smaller performance drops under distraction. Our code is available at https://github.com/PearlMody05/Cranimem and the accompanying PyPI package at https://pypi.org/project/cranimem.

</details>


### [200] [GSI Agent: Domain Knowledge Enhancement for Large Language Models in Green Stormwater Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15643)
*Shaohuang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes GSI Agent, a domain-enhanced LLM for Green Stormwater Infrastructure (GSI) inspection/maintenance, combining supervised fine-tuning on GSI data, retrieval-augmented generation over a municipal-doc-based internal knowledge base, and an agent-based reasoning pipeline to coordinate retrieval, context integration, and structured response generation; introduces a GSI dataset aligned with real-world scenarios; reports domain improvements (BLEU-4 0.090→0.307) with stable general-knowledge performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GSI maintenance knowledge is scattered across manuals, regulations, and forms, making it hard for non-experts and maintenance staff to obtain reliable guidance from field observations. LLMs struggle with domain-specific accuracy, limiting their use in professional infrastructure tasks. The work aims to adapt LLMs to GSI tasks by enriching them with domain knowledge and structured reasoning.

Method: Three-pronged approach: (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a curated GSI instruction dataset; (2) retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over an internal GSI knowledge base constructed from municipal documents; (3) an agent-based reasoning pipeline that coordinates retrieval, context integration, and structured response generation. A new GSI Dataset aligned with real-world inspection and maintenance scenarios is also constructed.

Result: On the GSI dataset, BLEU-4 improves from 0.090 to 0.307. Performance on a common-knowledge dataset remains stable (0.304 vs. 0.305). These results indicate significant gains in domain-specific performance with negligible impact on general knowledge ability.

Conclusion: Systematic domain knowledge enhancement can effectively adapt general-purpose LLMs to professional infrastructure tasks like GSI inspection and maintenance, enabling more reliable, actionable guidance for practitioners while maintaining broad language capabilities; suggests potential for real-world deployment and avenues for broader domain coverage.

Abstract: Green Stormwater Infrastructure (GSI) systems, such as permeable pavement, rain gardens, and bioretention facilities, require continuous inspection and maintenance to ensure long-term performance. However, domain knowledge about GSI is often scattered across municipal manuals, regulatory documents, and inspection forms. As a result, non-expert users and maintenance staff may struggle to obtain reliable and actionable guidance from field observations. Although Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong general reasoning and language generation capabilities, they often lack domain-specific knowledge and may produce inaccurate or hallucinated answers in engineering scenarios. This limitation restricts their direct application to professional infrastructure tasks. In this paper, we propose GSI Agent, a domain-enhanced LLM framework designed to improve performance in GSI-related tasks. Our approach integrates three complementary strategies: (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a curated GSI instruction dataset, (2) retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over an internal GSI knowledge base constructed from municipal documents, and (3) an agent-based reasoning pipeline that coordinates retrieval, context integration, and structured response generation. We also construct a new GSI Dataset aligned with real-world GSI inspection and maintenance scenarios. Experimental results show that our framework significantly improves domain-specific performance while maintaining general knowledge capability. On the GSI dataset, BLEU-4 improves from 0.090 to 0.307, while performance on the common knowledge dataset remains stable (0.304 vs. 0.305). These results demonstrate that systematic domain knowledge enhancement can effectively adapt general-purpose LLMs to professional infrastructure applications.

</details>


### [201] [Did You Check the Right Pocket? Cost-Sensitive Store Routing for Memory-Augmented Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15658)
*Madhava Gaikwad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Selective store routing for memory-augmented agents reduces retrieval cost and improves QA accuracy versus uniform retrieval; formalizes store-routing with cost-sensitive decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To cut retrieval costs and irrelevant context in multi-store memory systems while maintaining or improving question-answering performance, enabling scalable memory augmentation.

Method: Formulate the problem as store-routing; evaluate using coverage, exact match, and token-efficiency metrics; conduct downstream QA with an oracle router for upper-bound performance; compare against uniform retrieval; formalize store selection as a cost-sensitive decision problem to interpret routing policies and motivate learned routing.

Result: Oracle routing achieves higher QA accuracy while using substantially fewer context tokens; selective retrieval improves both efficiency and performance; uniform retrieval underperforms; results support treating routing as a first-class design component and motivate learned routing for scalable multi-store systems.

Conclusion: Routing decisions are essential in memory-augmented agents; should be treated as a first-class component and pursued through learned routing policies and principled, cost-aware strategies for multi-store memory architectures.

Abstract: Memory-augmented agents maintain multiple specialized stores, yet most systems retrieve from all stores for every query, increasing cost and introducing irrelevant context. We formulate memory retrieval as a store-routing problem and evaluate it using coverage, exact match, and token efficiency metrics. On downstream question answering, an oracle router achieves higher accuracy while using substantially fewer context tokens compared to uniform retrieval, demonstrating that selective retrieval improves both efficiency and performance. Our results show that routing decisions are a first-class component of memory-augmented agent design and motivate learned routing mechanisms for scalable multi-store systems. We additionally formalize store selection as a cost-sensitive decision problem that trades answer accuracy against retrieval cost, providing a principled interpretation of routing policies.

</details>


### [202] [DynaTrust: Defending Multi-Agent Systems Against Sleeper Agents via Dynamic Trust Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15661)
*Yu Li,Qiang Hu,Yao Zhang,Lili Quan,Jiongchi Yu,Junjie Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DynaTrust introduces a dynamic trust graph (DTG) to defend LLM-based MAS against sleeper agents, dynamically updating trust and restructuring the graph to isolate compromised agents, achieving high defense success with low false positives and preserving task connectivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sleeper agents gradually escalate malicious behavior, while static defenses fail to adapt and produce high false positives; a dynamic, trust-based, graph-restructuring approach can improve resilience and utility.

Method: Model MAS as a dynamic trust graph; continuously update agent trust using historical behavior and expert confidence; autonomously restructure the graph to isolate compromised agents while preserving connectivity; evaluate on AdvBench/HumanEval-derived benchmarks; compare to AgentShield.

Result: DynaTrust increases defense success rate by 41.7% over AgentShield, achieving >86% defense success under adversarial conditions; significantly lowers false positive rate and maintains system usability through adaptive graph modifications.

Conclusion: Dynamic trust graphs enable robust, adaptive defense against sleeper agents, balancing security with utility by replacing blunt blocking with graph restructuring.

Abstract: Large Language Model-based Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) have demonstrated remarkable collaborative reasoning capabilities but introduce new attack surfaces, such as the sleeper agent, which behave benignly during routine operation and gradually accumulate trust, only revealing malicious behaviors when specific conditions or triggers are met. Existing defense works primarily focus on static graph optimization or hierarchical data management, often failing to adapt to evolving adversarial strategies or suffering from high false-positive rates (FPR) due to rigid blocking policies. To address this, we propose DynaTrust, a novel defense method against sleeper agents. DynaTrust models MAS as a dynamic trust graph~(DTG), and treats trust as a continuous, evolving process rather than a static attribute. It dynamically updates the trust of each agent based on its historical behaviors and the confidence of selected expert agents. Instead of simply blocking, DynaTrust autonomously restructures the graph to isolate compromised agents and restore task connectivity to ensure the usability of MAS. To assess the effectiveness of DynaTrust, we evaluate it on mixed benchmarks derived from AdvBench and HumanEval. The results demonstrate that DynaTrust outperforms the state-of-the-art method AgentShield by increasing the defense success rate by 41.7%, achieving rates exceeding 86% under adversarial conditions. Furthermore, it effectively balances security with utility by significantly reducing FPR, ensuring uninterrupted system operations through graph adaptation.

</details>


### [203] [QV May Be Enough: Toward the Essence of Attention in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15665)
*Zhang Edward*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Linguistically grounded, theory-driven analysis of Transformer QKV attention; introduces QV paradigm and QV-Ka optimization; offers a unified framework for MQA, GQA, MLA with empirical validation and implications for future LLM design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a principled, interpretable understanding of the QKV mechanism, unify contemporary attention variants (MQA, GQA, MLA), and identify trade-offs and optimization directions to guide future LLM architectures.

Method: Derive the QKV mechanism from first principles using POS and syntactic analysis; introduce the QV paradigm; develop the QV-Ka optimization scheme; validate claims with empirical experiments.

Result: Offers an interpretable theoretical framework for QKV; presents evidence supporting the QV paradigm; demonstrates benefits of QV-Ka in experiments; frames trade-offs among attention variants for guiding architecture choices.

Conclusion: Establishes a robust foundation for evolving large language model architectures, enabling principled optimization and interpretable design, with a roadmap extending beyond QKV to future attention-based systems.

Abstract: Starting from first principles and a linguistic perspective centered on part-of-speech (POS) and syntactic analysis, this paper explores and derives the underlying essence of the Query-Key-Value (QKV) mechanism within the Transformer architecture. Based on this theoretical foundation, we provide a unified explanatory framework for the efficacy of contemporary architectures, including MQA, GQA, and MLA, while identifying their inherent trade-offs and potential optimization trajectories. We introduce the QV paradigm and provide empirical evidence for its validity. Building upon this, we propose the QV-Ka optimization scheme, which is further substantiated through experimental validation. The interpretable theoretical analysis of the QKV mechanism presented in this work establishes a robust foundation for the future evolution of large language model architectures.

</details>


### [204] [Compiled Memory: Not More Information, but More Precise Instructions for Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15666)
*James Rhodes,George Kang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Atlas defines a memory utility mechanism that distills experiential knowledge into an agent's instruction structure via prompt rewriting, delivering gains without fine-tuning or explicit memory storage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses memory utility: deciding what experiences are worth keeping and how they should influence agent behavior, complementing existing memory management.

Method: A memory kernel that distills accumulated task experience into the agent's instruction structure as learned sub-bullets. Experiences from failures/successes are verified through a three-step promotion gate and delivered by rewriting the agent's system prompt. No fine-tuning, RAG, or external storage. Evaluation includes cross-model transfer.

Result: CUAD: token-level F1 +8.7 points and precision +12.5 points; HotpotQA multi-hop F1 +3.16 points; ablation shows the constraint—the training signal exactly teaches what it is; Claude Sonnet 4.5 with the same prompt yields joint F1 +2.31 points, strongest where baseline is weaker.

Conclusion: The evolved prompt encodes task-shaped knowledge through distillation, not model-specific tweaks. Memory utility here is effectively instruction rewriting that generalizes across models and tasks without fine-tuning.

Abstract: Existing memory systems for language agents address memory management: how to retrieve and page more information within a context budget. We address a complementary problem -- memory utility: what experience is worth keeping, and how it should change agent behavior. We present Atlas, a memory kernel that compiles accumulated task experience into an agent's instruction structure -- without fine-tuning, RAG, or human intervention. Memory is distillation, not storage; delivery is instruction rewriting, not context injection. Facts extracted from agent failures and successes are verified through a three-step promotion gate and delivered by rewriting the agent's system prompt with learned sub-bullets. On CUAD contract analysis, the evolved prompt improves GPT-4o token-level F1 by $+8.7$pp and precision by $+12.5$pp. On HotpotQA multi-hop QA, joint F1 improves $+3.16$pp. An ablation isolates the mechanism's defining property -- the training signal constraint: the evolved prompt learns exactly what it is taught, and nothing more. Applied to Claude Sonnet~4.5 using the same evolved prompt -- compiled from GPT-4o errors, unchanged -- joint F1 improves $+2.31$pp, with gains concentrating where Claude's stronger baseline leaves the most room -- confirming that the compiled knowledge is task-shaped, not model-shaped.

</details>


### [205] [A Dynamic Survey of Fuzzy, Intuitionistic Fuzzy, Neutrosophic, Plithogenic, and Extensional Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15667)
*Takaaki Fujita,Florentin Smarandache*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A large-scale survey unifying Fuzzy, Intuitionistic Fuzzy, Neutrosophic, and Plithogenic Sets; highlights recurring constructions and cross-family patterns; aims to stimulate new insights and applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically overview extensive developments across four major uncertainty-oriented set theories and to illuminate common concepts, patterns, and potential for conceptual extensions.

Method: Literature synthesis and unified exposition across four families, with cross-cutting analysis to identify recurrent ideas and to present a coherent framework that facilitates cross-disciplinary applications.

Result: A comprehensive survey/book providing a systematic, unified overview of Fuzzy, Intuitionistic Fuzzy, Neutrosophic, and Plithogenic Sets, emphasizing shared constructions and patterns and guiding future extensions and applications.

Conclusion: The work underscores the significance and breadth of these theories and offers a platform for new insights, theoretical developments, and diverse applications.

Abstract: Real-world phenomena often exhibit vagueness, partial truth, and incomplete information. To model such uncertainty in a mathematically rigorous way, many generalized set-theoretic frameworks have been introduced, including Fuzzy Sets [1], Intuitionistic Fuzzy Sets [2], Neutrosophic Sets [3,4], Vague Sets [5], Hesitant Fuzzy Sets [6], Picture Fuzzy Sets [7], Quadripartitioned Neutrosophic Sets [8], Penta-Partitioned Neutrosophic Sets [9], Plithogenic Sets [10], HyperFuzzy Sets [11], and HyperNeutrosophic Sets [12]. Within these frameworks, a wide range of notions has been proposed and studied, particularly in the settings of fuzzy, intuitionistic fuzzy, neutrosophic, and plithogenic set theories. This extensive literature underscores both the significance of these theories and the breadth of their application areas. As a result, many ideas, constructions, and structural patterns recur across these four major families of uncertainty-oriented models. In this book, we provide a comprehensive, large-scale survey of Fuzzy, Intuitionistic Fuzzy, Neutrosophic, and Plithogenic Sets. Our goal is to give readers a systematic overview of existing developments and, through a unified exposition, to stimulate new insights, further conceptual extensions, and additional applications across a wide range of disciplines.

</details>


### [206] [Quantum-Secure-By-Construction (QSC): A Paradigm Shift For Post-Quantum Agentic Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15668)
*Arit Kumar Bishwas,Mousumi Sen,Albert Nieto-Morales,Joel Jacob Varghese*

Main category: cs.AI
