When a significant deviation is detected, C.A.P. initiates a structured clarification protocol to help users and the system recalibrate the conversation. This study presents the architecture and theoretical basis of C.A.P., drawing on cognitive science and Common Ground theory in human-computer interaction. We argue that C.A.P. is not only a technical refinement but also a step toward shifting human-computer dialogue from one-way command-execution patterns to two-way, self-correcting, partnership-based collaboration. Finally, we discuss implementation paths, evaluation methods, and implications for the future design of interactive intelligent systems.

</details>


### [234] [ARISE: Agent Reasoning with Intrinsic Skill Evolution in Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16060)
*Yu Li,Rui Miao,Zhengling Qi,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARISE is a hierarchical RL framework that builds a reusable skill library via a manager-worker architecture. A Skills Manager evolves and retrieves skills from post-execution summaries to condition low-level Worker responses. A hierarchical reward signals co-evolution of reasoning and library quality. Across two base models and seven benchmarks (competition math and Omni-MATH), ARISE outperforms GRPO-family and memory-augmented baselines, especially on out-of-distribution tasks; ablations show each component contributes. Code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL with verifiable rewards treats each problem instance in isolation and does not leverage reusable strategies evolved during training. There is a need for a mechanism to accumulate, organize, and reuse reasoning skills to improve generalization and sample efficiency in mathematical reasoning tasks.

Method: Introduce ARISE: a two-tier architecture with a Skills Manager (high-level) and a Worker (low-level). The Manager maintains a tiered skill library and generates skills through a dedicated skill-generation rollout that summarizes successful solution traces after execution. It retrieves relevant skills to condition future rollouts before execution. A hierarchical reward design guides co-evolution of reasoning ability and library quality. Evaluate on two base models and seven benchmarks (competition mathematics and Omni-MATH) and compare to GRPO-family and memory-augmented baselines. Conduct ablations to verify component contributions.

Result: ARISE consistently outperforms GRPO-family algorithms and memory-augmented baselines, with notable gains on out-of-distribution tasks. Ablation studies confirm that each component contributes to improvements; library quality and reasoning performance improve together during training.

Conclusion: ARISE provides a principled hierarchical RL framework for agent reasoning via intrinsic skill evolution, demonstrating robust improvements in mathematical reasoning benchmarks. The authors release code for replication.

Abstract: The dominant paradigm for improving mathematical reasoning in language models relies on Reinforcement Learning with verifiable rewards. Yet existing methods treat each problem instance in isolation without leveraging the reusable strategies that emerge and accumulate during training. To this end, we introduce ARISE (Agent Reasoning via Intrinsic Skill Evolution), a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework, in which a shared policy operates both to manage skills at high-level and to generate responses at low-level (denoted as a Skills Manager and a Worker, respectively). The Manager maintains a tiered skill library through a dedicated skill generation rollout that performs structured summarization of successful solution traces (after execution), while employing a policy-driven selection mechanism to retrieve relevant skills to condition future rollouts (before execution). A hierarchical reward design guides the co-evolution of reasoning ability and library quality. Experiments on two base models and seven benchmarks spanning both competition mathematics and Omni-MATH show that ARISE consistently outperforms GRPO-family algorithms and memory-augmented baselines, with particularly notable gains on out-of-distribution tasks. Ablation studies confirm that each component contributes to the observed improvements and that library quality and reasoning performance improve in tandem throughout training. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/Skylanding/ARISE}{https://github.com/Skylanding/ARISE}.

</details>


### [235] [VIGIL: Towards Edge-Extended Agentic AI for Enterprise IT Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16110)
*Sarthak Ahuja,Neda Kordjazi,Evren Yortucboylu,Vishaal Kapoor,Mariam Dundua,Yiming Li,Derek Ho,Vaibhavi Padala,Jennifer Whitted,Rebecca Steinert*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VIGIL deploys desktop-resident agents for edge-extended, policy-governed on-device diagnosis and remediation, achieving faster, more autonomous IT support with high user trust.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enterprise IT support is constrained by heterogeneous devices, evolving policies, and long-tail failure modes that are hard to resolve centrally; there is a need for on-device, observable remediation with explicit consent.

Method: Edge-extended agentic AI system (VIGIL) deploying desktop-resident agents to perform situated diagnosis, retrieval over enterprise knowledge, and policy-governed remediation directly on user devices, with explicit consent and end-to-end observability.

Result: In a 10-week pilot on 100 resource-constrained endpoints: 39% fewer interaction rounds, at least 4× faster diagnosis, and 82% self-service resolution in matched cases; users report high usability and trust; transparency is critical for trust; on-device diagnosis provides value even when knowledge base coverage is limited; establishes safety and observability foundations for fleet-wide continuous improvement.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility and safety of on-device, edge-extended agentic AI for enterprise IT support, enabling faster diagnosis, policy-governed remediation, and continuous improvement with transparency and observability as core foundations.

Abstract: Enterprise IT support is constrained by heterogeneous devices, evolving policies, and long-tail failure modes that are difficult to resolve centrally. We present VIGIL, an edge-extended agentic AI system that deploys desktop-resident agents to perform situated diagnosis, retrieval over enterprise knowledge, and policy-governed remediation directly on user devices with explicit consent and end-to-end observability. In a 10-week pilot of VIGIL's operational loop on 100 resource-constrained endpoints, VIGIL reduces interaction rounds by 39%, achieves at least 4 times faster diagnosis, and supports self-service resolution in 82% of matched cases. Users report excellent usability, high trust, and low cognitive workload across four validated instruments, with qualitative feedback highlighting transparency as critical for trust. Notably, users rated the system higher when no historical matches were available, suggesting on-device diagnosis provides value independent of knowledge base coverage. This pilot establishes safety and observability foundations for fleet-wide continuous improvement.

</details>


### [236] [NeuronSpark: A Spiking Neural Network Language Model with Selective State Space Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16148)
*Zhengzheng Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A pure spiking neural network (SNN) language model with 0.9B params (NeuronSpark) trained from random initialization using next-token prediction, achieving 3.6 pretraining loss on ~1.4B tokens with SFT steps; suggests feasibility of end-to-end SNN LM at this scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether pure SNN architectures can perform large-scale language modeling without Transformer distillation, challenging the norm of ANN-based LMs and exploring energy-efficient, neuromorphic approaches.

Method: Hybrid spiking architecture with selective state-space spiking dynamics, leakage-current inter-layer communication, PonderNet adaptive timesteps, fused Triton PLIF kernels, and stabilization techniques (residual centering, lateral-inhibition normalization, natural-gradient compensation). Trained as next-token prediction using surrogate gradients on a 0.9B-parameter model, with a constrained pretraining budget (~1.4B tokens) and ~6.5K supervised fine-tuning steps.

Result: Achieves 3.6 pretraining loss and exhibits early multi-turn dialogue behavior after SFT, indicating end-to-end language modeling feasibility with a pure SNN at this scale.

Conclusion: Preliminary evidence that pure SNNs can scale to sizable language-modeling tasks, though results are early and constrained; substantial further work is needed to assess generalization, efficiency, and performance relative to transformer-based LMs.

Abstract: We ask whether a pure spiking backbone can learn large-scale language modeling from random initialization, without Transformer distillation. We introduce NeuronSpark, a 0.9B-parameter SNN language model trained with next-token prediction and surrogate gradients. The model combines selective state-space spiking dynamics, leakage-current inter-layer communication, PonderNet adaptive timesteps, fused Triton PLIF kernels, and stabilization techniques (residual centering, lateral-inhibition normalization, and natural-gradient compensation). Under a constrained budget (about 1.4B pretraining tokens and 6.5K SFT steps), NeuronSpark-0.9B reaches 3.6 pretraining loss and shows early multi-turn dialogue behavior after SFT. These results support the feasibility of end-to-end language modeling with a pure SNN architecture at this scale.

</details>


### [237] [Are Large Language Models Truly Smarter Than Humans?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16197)
*Eshwar Reddy M,Sourav Karmakar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Three experiments audit six frontier LLMs for training-data contamination on public benchmarks (MMLU). Finding: substantial contamination and memorization signals, with STEM hardest; significant implications for benchmark credibility and claimed capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify and characterize contamination risk in public benchmarks used to evaluate LLMs, and understand its impact on measured performance and claims of superiority.

Method: Three complementary experiments: (1) lexical contamination detection on 513 MMLU questions across 57 subjects; (2) paraphrase/indirect-reference diagnostic on 100 MMLU questions; (3) TS-Guessing memorization probes on all 513 questions for six models (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-R1, DeepSeek-V3, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen3-235B).

Result: Contamination rate 13.8% overall (18.1% STEM; up to 66.7% Philosophy); estimated accuracy gains from contamination +0.030 to +0.054 points by category; indirect-reference reduces accuracy by 7.0 percentage points on average (up to 19.8 pp in Law/Ethics); 72.5% of questions trigger memorization signals; DeepSeek-R1 shows a distributed memorization signature (76.6% partial reconstruction, 0% verbatim) explaining its Experiment 2 profile; ranking: STEM > Professional > Social Sciences > Humanities.

Conclusion: Evidence across methods indicates contamination in public benchmarks affecting LLM evaluation, especially in STEM; underscores need for contamination audits, robust benchmark design, and cautious interpretation of public performance claims.

Abstract: Public leaderboards increasingly suggest that large language models (LLMs) surpass human experts on benchmarks spanning academic knowledge, law, and programming. Yet most benchmarks are fully public, their questions widely mirrored across the internet, creating systematic risk that models were trained on the very data used to evaluate them. This paper presents three complementary experiments forming a rigorous multi-method contamination audit of six frontier LLMs: GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-R1, DeepSeek-V3, Llama-3.3-70B, and Qwen3-235B. Experiment 1 applies a lexical contamination detection pipeline to 513 MMLU questions across all 57 subjects, finding an overall contamination rate of 13.8% (18.1% in STEM, up to 66.7% in Philosophy) and estimated performance gains of +0.030 to +0.054 accuracy points by category. Experiment 2 applies a paraphrase and indirect-reference diagnostic to 100 MMLU questions, finding accuracy drops by an average of 7.0 percentage points under indirect reference, rising to 19.8 pp in both Law and Ethics. Experiment 3 applies TS-Guessing behavioral probes to all 513 questions and all six models, finding that 72.5% trigger memorization signals far above chance, with DeepSeek-R1 displaying a distributed memorization signature (76.6% partial reconstruction, 0% verbatim recall) that explains its anomalous Experiment 2 profile. All three experiments converge on the same contamination ranking: STEM > Professional > Social Sciences > Humanities.

</details>


### [238] [Proactive Rejection and Grounded Execution: A Dual-Stage Intent Analysis Paradigm for Safe and Efficient AIoT Smart Homes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16207)
*Xinxin Jin,Zhengwei Ni,Zhengguo Sheng,Victor C. M. Leung*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DS-IA is a two-stage framework for LLM-driven IoT control that separates intent understanding from physical execution via a semantic firewall and a cascade verifier, addressing entity hallucinations and the Interaction Frequency Dilemma; it achieves higher exact-match rates and reduces invalid-instruction rejection in HomeBench and SAGE, and increases autonomous success while preserving ambiguity handling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs controlling IoT devices risk hallucinations and inefficient interactions; existing iterative systems like SAGE oscillate between over-execution and excessive questioning; need a reliable grounding and verification mechanism.

Method: Stage 1: semantic firewall validating intent and resolving vague commands using current home state; Stage 2: deterministic cascade verifier checking room, device, capability sequentially before execution.

Result: EM 58.56% on HomeBench, >28% improvement vs baselines; invalid-instruction rejection 87.04%; on SAGE benchmark Autonomous Success Rate up from 42.86% to 71.43% with maintained precision on irreducible ambiguities.

Conclusion: DS-IA effectively reduces user disturbance by rigorous environmental grounding and staged verification, addressing reliability and interaction efficiency in LLM-powered IoT control.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) transition from information providers to embodied agents in the Internet of Things (IoT), they face significant challenges regarding reliability and interaction efficiency. Direct execution of LLM-generated commands often leads to entity hallucinations (e.g., trying to control non-existent devices). Meanwhile, existing iterative frameworks (e.g., SAGE) suffer from the Interaction Frequency Dilemma, oscillating between reckless execution and excessive user questioning. To address these issues, we propose a Dual-Stage Intent-Aware (DS-IA) Framework. This framework separates high-level user intent understanding from low-level physical execution. Specifically, Stage 1 serves as a semantic firewall to filter out invalid instructions and resolve vague commands by checking the current state of the home. Stage 2 then employs a deterministic cascade verifier-a strict, step-by-step rule checker that verifies the room, device, and capability in sequence-to ensure the action is actually physically possible before execution. Extensive experiments on the HomeBench and SAGE benchmarks demonstrate that DS-IA achieves an Exact Match (EM) rate of 58.56% (outperforming baselines by over 28%) and improves the rejection rate of invalid instructions to 87.04%. Evaluations on the SAGE benchmark further reveal that DS-IA resolves the Interaction Frequency Dilemma by balancing proactive querying with state-based inference. Specifically, it boosts the Autonomous Success Rate (resolving tasks without unnecessary user intervention) from 42.86% to 71.43%, while maintaining high precision in identifying irreducible ambiguities that truly necessitate human clarification. These results underscore the framework's ability to minimize user disturbance through accurate environmental grounding.

</details>


### [239] [MOSAIC: Composable Safety Alignment with Modular Control Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16210)
*Jingyu Peng,Hongyu Chen,Jiancheng Dong,Maolin Wang,Wenxi Li,Yuchen Li,Kai Zhang,Xiangyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MOSAIC enables modular, compositional safety alignment for LLMs by using learnable control tokens activated at inference on a frozen backbone, achieving context-dependent safety with lower over-refusal and preserved utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety in LLMs is typically a single static policy embedded in parameters; real deployments demand context-sensitive safety rules across users, regions, and applications. Existing parameter-level alignment entangles safety with capabilities; prompt-based methods offer weak enforcement.

Method: Propose MOSAIC: a modular framework with learnable safety-control tokens, each encoding a safety constraint; tokens can be composed/activated at inference. Train tokens efficiently via order-based task sampling and a distribution-level alignment objective on a frozen backbone.

Result: Experiments show MOSAIC achieves strong defense against unsafe prompts with substantially reduced over-refusal while preserving model utility compared to baselines.

Conclusion: MOSAIC enables flexible, context-dependent safety enforcement without retraining the backbone, offering efficient training and compositional control for region/user/application-specific safety.

Abstract: Safety alignment in large language models (LLMs) is commonly implemented as a single static policy embedded in model parameters. However, real-world deployments often require context-dependent safety rules that vary across users, regions, and applications. Existing approaches struggle to provide such conditional control: parameter-level alignment entangles safety behaviors with general capabilities, while prompt-based methods rely on natural language instructions that provide weak enforcement. We propose MOSAIC, a modular framework that enables compositional safety alignment through learnable control tokens optimized over a frozen backbone model. Each token represents a safety constraint and can be flexibly activated and composed at inference time. To train compositional tokens efficiently, we introduce order-based task sampling and a distribution-level alignment objective that mitigates over-refusal. Experiments show that MOSAIC achieves strong defense performance with substantially lower over-refusal while preserving model utility.

</details>


### [240] [Adaptive Theory of Mind for LLM-based Multi-Agent Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16264)
*Chunjiang Mu,Ya Zeng,Qiaosheng Zhang,Kun Shao,Chen Chu,Hao Guo,Danyang Jia,Zhen Wang,Shuyue Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Adaptive Theory of Mind (A-ToM) aligns ToM depth between agents by estimating partners' likely ToM order from past interactions, improving coordination across four multi-agent tasks; demonstrates benefits of ToM alignment and discusses generalizability and limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ToM-based reasoning depth misalignment can cause under- or over-thinking about others, impairing coordination in multiagent systems. Existing approaches with fixed ToM depths may be suboptimal in dynamic partnerships; an adaptive mechanism could improve coordination by matching partner's reasoning level.

Method: Introduce an adaptive ToM agent that infers a partner's likely ToM order from prior interactions and uses this estimate to predict the partner's actions, thereby coordinating behavior. Evaluation on four tasks: a repeated matrix game, two grid navigation tasks, and an Overcooked task; include discussion of generalizability to non-LLM agents and factors that diminish the importance of ToM alignment.

Result: Empirical results validate that ToM alignment improves coordination and that A-ToM effectively matches partner ToM order, leading to better joint performance across tasks. The study also explores how alignment generalizes beyond LLM-based agents and identifies conditions under which ToM alignment is less critical.

Conclusion: Adaptive ToM alignment is beneficial for multi-agent coordination. The approach offers a pathway to dynamically harmonize reasoning depths in partners and suggests broader applicability and limitations worth exploring in future work.

Abstract: Theory of Mind (ToM) refers to the ability to reason about others' mental states, and higher-order ToM involves considering that others also possess their own ToM. Equipping large language model (LLM)-driven agents with ToM has long been considered to improve their coordination in multiagent collaborative tasks. However, we find that misaligned ToM orders-mismatches in the depth of ToM reasoning between agents-can lead to insufficient or excessive reasoning about others, thereby impairing their coordination. To address this issue, we design an adaptive ToM (A-ToM) agent, which can align in ToM orders with its partner. Based on prior interactions, the agent estimates the partner's likely ToM order and leverages this estimation to predict the partner's action, thereby facilitating behavioral coordination. We conduct empirical evaluations on four multi-agent coordination tasks: a repeated matrix game, two grid navigation tasks and an Overcooked task. The results validate our findings on ToM alignment and demonstrate the effectiveness of our A-ToM agent. Furthermore, we discuss the generalizability of our A-ToM to non-LLM-based agents, as well as what would diminish the importance of ToM alignment.

</details>


### [241] [NeSy-Route: A Neuro-Symbolic Benchmark for Constrained Route Planning in Remote Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16307)
*Ming Yang,Zhi Zhou,Shi-Yu Tian,Kun-Yang Yu,Lan-Zhe Guo,Yu-Feng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NeSy-Route is a large-scale neuro-symbolic benchmark for constrained route planning in remote sensing, featuring automated data generation with optimal solutions and a three-level evaluation protocol to assess perception, reasoning, and planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing remote-sensing benchmarks emphasize perception and reasoning but neglect planning, and lack scalable, validated tasks with provable optimal solutions; a planning-focused benchmark is needed to fault-dine MLLMs and guide progress.

Method: An automated data-generation framework combines high-fidelity semantic masks with heuristic search to produce 10,821 diverse route-planning tasks with provable optimal solutions; a three-level hierarchical neuro-symbolic evaluation protocol assesses perception, reasoning, and planning.

Result: Comprehensive evaluations reveal current state-of-the-art MLLMs exhibit notable deficiencies in both perception and planning on NeSy-Route.

Conclusion: NeSy-Route provides a scalable platform for rigorous assessment and will drive development of more capable MLLMs for remote sensing.

Abstract: Remote sensing underpins crucial applications such as disaster relief and ecological field surveys, where systems must understand complex scenes and constraints and make reliable decisions. Current remote-sensing benchmarks mainly focus on evaluating perception and reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs). They fail to assess planning capability, stemming either from the difficulty of curating and validating planning tasks at scale or from evaluation protocols that are inaccurate and inadequate. To address these limitations, we introduce NeSy-Route, a large-scale neuro-symbolic benchmark for constrained route planning in remote sensing. Within this benchmark, we introduce an automated data-generation framework that integrates high-fidelity semantic masks with heuristic search to produce diverse route-planning tasks with provably optimal solutions. This allows NeSy-Route to comprehensively evaluate planning across 10,821 route-planning samples, nearly 10 times larger than the largest prior benchmark. Furthermore, a three-level hierarchical neuro-symbolic evaluation protocol is developed to enable accurate assessment and support fine-grained analysis on perception, reasoning, and planning simultaneously. Our comprehensive evaluation of various state-of-the-art MLLMs demonstrates that existing MLLMs show significant deficiencies in perception and planning capabilities. We hope NeSy-Route can support further research and development of more powerful MLLMs for remote sensing.

</details>


### [242] [Learning to Predict, Discover, and Reason in High-Dimensional Discrete Event Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16313)
*Hugo Math*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A Transformer-driven, high-dimensional event-sequence framework for automated vehicle fault diagnostics; unifies predictive modeling, causal discovery, and Boolean EP-rule synthesis via multi-agent systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To scale diagnostic rule learning beyond manual Boolean grouping of tens of thousands of DTCs, treating DTC sequences as a language to capture complex temporal dependencies in high-cardinality, high-volume automotive data; traditional statistics fail to scale and miss dependencies.

Method: Three-part approach: (1) Transformer-based architectures for predictive maintenance; (2) scalable sample- and population-level causal discovery frameworks; (3) a multi-agent system to automate synthesis of Boolean EP rules; together unifying event sequence modeling, causal discovery, and LLMs for high-dimensional event streams.

Result: Abstract outlines proposed architectures and frameworks with a focus on scalability and unification; no empirical results are reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: A paradigm shift toward language-like modeling of DTC sequences to enable prediction, causal understanding, and reasoning for vehicle diagnostics; the framework aims to scale to thousands of DTCs and automate rule synthesis, with future work including validation and deployment.

Abstract: Electronic control units (ECUs) embedded within modern vehicles generate a large number of asynchronous events known as diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs). These discrete events form complex temporal sequences that reflect the evolving health of the vehicle's subsystems. In the automotive industry, domain experts manually group these codes into higher-level error patterns (EPs) using Boolean rules to characterize system faults and ensure safety. However, as vehicle complexity grows, this manual process becomes increasingly costly, error-prone, and difficult to scale. Notably, the number of unique DTCs in a modern vehicle is on the same order of magnitude as the vocabulary of a natural language, often numbering in the tens of thousands. This observation motivates a paradigm shift: treating diagnostic sequences as a language that can be modeled, predicted, and ultimately explained. Traditional statistical approaches fail to capture the rich dependencies and do not scale to high-dimensional datasets characterized by thousands of nodes, large sample sizes, and long sequence lengths. Specifically, the high cardinality of categorical event spaces in industrial logs poses a significant challenge, necessitating new machine learning architectures tailored to such event-driven systems. This thesis addresses automated fault diagnostics by unifying event sequence modeling, causal discovery, and large language models (LLMs) into a coherent framework for high-dimensional event streams. It is structured in three parts, reflecting a progressive transition from prediction to causal understanding and finally to reasoning for vehicle diagnostics. Consequently, we introduce several Transformer-based architectures for predictive maintenance, scalable sample- and population-level causal discovery frameworks and a multi-agent system that automates the synthesis of Boolean EP rules.

</details>


### [243] [FactorEngine: A Program-level Knowledge-Infused Factor Mining Framework for Quantitative Investment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16365)
*Qinhong Lin,Ruitao Feng,Yinglun Feng,Zhenxin Huang,Yukun Chen,Zhongliang Yang,Linna Zhou,Binjie Fei,Jiaqi Liu,Yu Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FactorEngine (FE) introduces a program-level, Turing-complete factor discovery framework that treats predictive signals as executable code and uses separations (logic revision vs. parameter optimization; LLM-guided directional search vs. Bayesian hyperparameter search; LLM usage vs. local computation) plus a bootstrapping knowledge base to extract factors from financial reports, achieving stronger predictive stability and portfolio performance on OHLCV data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Symbolic approaches are limited by bounded expressiveness; neural forecasters sacrifice interpretability and remain vulnerable to regime shifts and overfitting. There is a need for factors that are directly executable, auditable, scalable, and robust in noisy, non-stationary markets.

Method: FE casts factors as Turing-complete code and employs three separations: (i) logic revision vs. parameter optimization, (ii) LLM-guided directional search vs. Bayesian hyperparameter search, and (iii) LLM usage vs. local computation. It includes a knowledge-infused bootstrapping module that converts unstructured financial reports into executable factor programs via a closed-loop multi-agent extraction-verification-code-generation pipeline, and an experience knowledge base for trajectory-aware refinement (learning from failures). Evaluation uses extensive backtests on real-world OHLCV data, reporting stronger predictive stability and portfolio impact (higher IC/ICIR, Rank IC/ICIR, AR/Sharpe) than baselines, i.e., state-of-the-art performance.

Result: Empirical results show FE yields factors with stronger predictive stability and portfolio impact than baselines, achieving state-of-the-art predictive and portfolio performance in backtests on OHLCV data.

Conclusion: FE provides a practical, interpretable, and scalable factor discovery framework that combines programmable factor signals, LLM-assisted search, and knowledge bootstrapping to produce robust signals with improved out-of-sample performance.

Abstract: We study alpha factor mining, the automated discovery of predictive signals from noisy, non-stationary market data-under a practical requirement that mined factors be directly executable and auditable, and that the discovery process remain computationally tractable at scale. Existing symbolic approaches are limited by bounded expressiveness, while neural forecasters often trade interpretability for performance and remain vulnerable to regime shifts and overfitting. We introduce FactorEngine (FE), a program-level factor discovery framework that casts factors as Turing-complete code and improves both effectiveness and efficiency via three separations: (i) logic revision vs. parameter optimization, (ii) LLM-guided directional search vs. Bayesian hyperparameter search, and (iii) LLM usage vs. local computation. FE further incorporates a knowledge-infused bootstrapping module that transforms unstructured financial reports into executable factor programs through a closed-loop multi-agent extraction-verification-code-generation pipeline, and an experience knowledge base that supports trajectory-aware refinement (including learning from failures). Across extensive backtests on real-world OHLCV data, FE produces factors with substantially stronger predictive stability and portfolio impact-for example, higher IC/ICIR (and Rank IC/ICIR) and improved AR/Sharpe, than baseline methods, achieving state-of-the-art predictive and portfolio performance.

</details>


### [244] [Via Negativa for AI Alignment: Why Negative Constraints Are Structurally Superior to Positive Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16417)
*Quan Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a unifying theory: positive preferences are context-sensitive and sycophantic, while negative constraints are finite, verifiable prohibitions; this asymmetry explains why negative signals can match or exceed RLHF and why preference-based approaches can fail, advocating a shift toward learning what humans reject.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Empirical work shows negative-only feedback can rival or surpass standard RLHF across tasks; there is no unified explanation for why negative signals are so effective. The paper offers a theory to account for these results and guide future alignment work.

Method: A theoretical account grounded in asymmetry between positive preferences (continuous, context-dependent) and negative constraints (discrete, verifiable prohibitions), invoking Popperian falsification and the epistemology of negative knowledge; derives testable predictions and outlines implications for evaluation and research focus.

Result: Unifies disparate negative-signal results under a single explanatory framework; explains sycophancy failures in preference-based RLHF and the effectiveness of negative-signal methods; yields concrete, testable predictions for future experiments and evaluations.

Conclusion: Suggests re-centering alignment research on what humans reject rather than what humans prefer; provides predictions and practical guidance for evaluating and designing alignment methods based on negative signals.

Abstract: Recent empirical results have demonstrated that training large language models (LLMs) with negative-only feedback can match or exceed standard reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). Negative Sample Reinforcement achieves parity with PPO on mathematical reasoning; Distributional Dispreference Optimization trains effectively using only dispreferred samples; and Constitutional AI outperforms pure RLHF on harmlessness benchmarks. Yet no unified theoretical account explains why negative signals are so effective. This paper proposes such an account: positive preferences and negative constraints are structurally asymmetric. Positive preferences ("which is better") encode continuously coupled, context-dependent human values that cannot be exhaustively specified -- leading models to learn surface correlates such as agreement with the user (sycophancy). Negative constraints ("what is wrong") encode discrete, finite, independently verifiable prohibitions that can converge to a stable boundary. This asymmetry -- rooted in Popper's falsification logic and the epistemology of negative knowledge -- explains both the sycophancy failure of preference-based RLHF and the surprising effectiveness of negative-signal methods. We argue that alignment research should shift its center of gravity from "learning what humans prefer" to "learning what humans reject," and offer testable predictions for this framework.

</details>


### [245] [From Natural Language to Executable Option Strategies via Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16434)
*Haochen Luo,Zhengzhao Lai,Junjie Xu,Yifan Li,Tang Pok Hin,Yuan Zhang,Chen Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduce Option Query Language (OQL), a domain-specific intermediate representation that translates natural-language trading intents into executable option strategies via a neuro-symbolic pipeline and a deterministic execution engine; accompanied by a new dataset; shows improved execution accuracy and logical consistency over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle to reliably translate broad, multi-dimensional option-chain data and constraints into correct strategies. A formal, grammar-driven IR can constrain outputs, enabling semantic parsing and verifiable execution for option design.

Method: Define OQL as a domain-specific IR with grammatical rules mapping option markets to high-level primitives. Use LLMs as semantic parsers to convert NL intents into OQL queries. Validate and execute queries deterministically in an engine. Introduce a dataset for the task and evaluate against direct NL-to-strategy baselines using execution accuracy and logical consistency metrics.

Result: The neuro-symbolic pipeline achieves higher execution accuracy and better logical consistency than direct baselines, demonstrating improved reliability in producing executable option strategies.

Conclusion: OQL provides a verifiable, reliable path from natural-language intents to executable option designs by constraining LLM outputs to a structured representation and validating them through a deterministic engine.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at general code generation, yet translating natural-language trading intents into correct option strategies remains challenging. Real-world option design requires reasoning over massive, multi-dimensional option chain data with strict constraints, which often overwhelms direct generation methods. We introduce the Option Query Language (OQL), a domain-specific intermediate representation that abstracts option markets into high-level primitives under grammatical rules, enabling LLMs to function as reliable semantic parsers rather than free-form programmers. OQL queries are then validated and executed deterministically by an engine to instantiate executable strategies. We also present a new dataset for this task and demonstrate that our neuro-symbolic pipeline significantly improves execution accuracy and logical consistency over direct baselines.

</details>


### [246] [Visual Distraction Undermines Moral Reasoning in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16445)
*Xinyi Yang,Chenheng Xu,Weijun Hong,Ce Mo,Qian Wang,Fang Fang,Yixin Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multimodal moral-evaluation benchmark shows that visual information shifts moral judgments in state-of-the-art vision-language models, bypassing text-based safety filters; the proposed Moral Dilemma Simulation enables controlled, mechanistic analysis of visual vs. contextual influences, revealing urgent need for multimodal safety alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensure consistent, safe moral reasoning across modalities as AI systems evolve from text-only to embodied, multimodal agents; current text-focused safety measures may not generalize to vision inputs, risking unsafe behavior.

Method: Introduce Moral Dilemma Simulation (MDS), a multimodal benchmark grounded in Moral Foundation Theory. It orthogonally manipulates visual cues and contextual factors to dissect their separate and joint effects on moral decision-making in VLMs, enabling mechanistic, controlled experimentation.

Result: Findings indicate that the visual modality elicits intuition-like responses that override deliberate, safety-oriented reasoning observed in text-only contexts, revealing fragilities where language-tuned filters do not constrain visual processing.

Conclusion: Multimodal moral safety requires alignment beyond text-based strategies; MDS provides a controlled framework for diagnosing and mitigating cross-modal vulnerabilities, guiding future research in robust multimodal safety.

Abstract: Moral reasoning is fundamental to safe Artificial Intelligence (AI), yet ensuring its consistency across modalities becomes critical as AI systems evolve from text-based assistants to embodied agents. Current safety techniques demonstrate success in textual contexts, but concerns remain about generalization to visual inputs. Existing moral evaluation benchmarks rely on textonly formats and lack systematic control over variables that influence moral decision-making. Here we show that visual inputs fundamentally alter moral decision-making in state-of-the-art (SOTA) Vision-Language Models (VLMs), bypassing text-based safety mechanisms. We introduce Moral Dilemma Simulation (MDS), a multimodal benchmark grounded in Moral Foundation Theory (MFT) that enables mechanistic analysis through orthogonal manipulation of visual and contextual variables. The evaluation reveals that the vision modality activates intuition-like pathways that override the more deliberate and safer reasoning patterns observed in text-only contexts. These findings expose critical fragilities where language-tuned safety filters fail to constrain visual processing, demonstrating the urgent need for multimodal safety alignment.

</details>


### [247] [TRUST-SQL: Tool-Integrated Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning for Text-to-SQL over Unknown Schemas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16448)
*Ai Jian,Xiaoyun Zhang,Wanrou Du,Jingqing Ruan,Jiangbo Pei,Weipeng Zhang,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes TRUST-SQL for Unknown Schema in Text-to-SQL using a four-phase POMDP-guided protocol and Dual-Track GRPO with token-level masked advantages; achieves large gains without preloaded schema.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Full-schema assumption is impractical in real-world enterprises where databases are large with noisy metadata. An autonomous agent must identify and verify only a relevant subset, addressing credit assignment in GRPO and enabling effective reasoning without pre-loaded metadata.

Method: Formulates the task as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) and introduces a four-phase grounding protocol to align reasoning with verified metadata. Develops a Dual-Track GRPO strategy that uses token-level masked advantages to separate exploration rewards from execution outcomes, mitigating credit assignment issues.

Result: Achieves 9.9% relative improvement over standard GRPO. Across five benchmarks, TRUST-SQL yields average absolute gains of 30.6% (4B) and 16.6% (8B) over base models, and matches or surpasses strong schema-prefilling baselines despite operating without pre-loaded metadata.

Conclusion: Trust-SQL demonstrates robust effectiveness in Unknown Schema settings, validating active, metadata-grounded reasoning with a structured protocol and reward-shaping approach. It reduces reliance on pre-loaded schema while delivering substantial performance gains across benchmarks.

Abstract: Text-to-SQL parsing has achieved remarkable progress under the Full Schema Assumption. However, this premise fails in real-world enterprise environments where databases contain hundreds of tables with massive noisy metadata. Rather than injecting the full schema upfront, an agent must actively identify and verify only the relevant subset, giving rise to the Unknown Schema scenario we study in this work. To address this, we propose TRUST-SQL (Truthful Reasoning with Unknown Schema via Tools). We formulate the task as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process where our autonomous agent employs a structured four-phase protocol to ground reasoning in verified metadata. Crucially, this protocol provides a structural boundary for our novel Dual-Track GRPO strategy. By applying token-level masked advantages, this strategy isolates exploration rewards from execution outcomes to resolve credit assignment, yielding a 9.9% relative improvement over standard GRPO. Extensive experiments across five benchmarks demonstrate that TRUST-SQL achieves an average absolute improvement of 30.6% and 16.6% for the 4B and 8B variants respectively over their base models. Remarkably, despite operating entirely without pre-loaded metadata, our framework consistently matches or surpasses strong baselines that rely on schema prefilling.

</details>


### [248] [RetailBench: Evaluating Long-Horizon Autonomous Decision-Making and Strategy Stability of LLM Agents in Realistic Retail Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16453)
*Linghua Zhang,Jun Wang,Jingtong Wu,Zhisong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RetailBench provides a high-fidelity benchmark for long-horizon, autonomous decision-making in dynamic retail-like environments, and the Evolving Strategy & Execution framework separates high-level strategy from low-level action execution to enable adaptation over time. Experiments across eight LLMs show improved stability/efficiency but substantial degradation with increasing task complexity, highlighting fundamental limits of current LLMs for long-horizon multi-factor decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs excel in short-horizon, structured tasks but struggle with coherent, long-horizon decision-making in non-stationary, real-world environments. A benchmark and a scalable framework are needed to evaluate and drive improvements in long-horizon autonomy.

Method: 1) Propose RetailBench: a high-fidelity benchmark simulating realistic, stochastic, and evolving commercial scenarios. 2) Propose Evolving Strategy & Execution (ESE) framework that decouples high-level strategic reasoning from low-level action execution, allowing strategy evolution at a different temporal scale. 3) Empirically evaluate eight state-of-the-art LLMs across progressively challenging environments, comparing against baselines.

Result: ESE framework improves operational stability and efficiency over baselines. However, performance deteriorates as task complexity increases, exposing fundamental limitations of current LLMs for long-horizon, multi-factor decision-making.

Conclusion: Long-horizon autonomous decision-making in dynamic environments remains challenging for current LLMs. The proposed RetailBench and ESE framework offer a path to more robust, interpretable long-horizon autonomy, but further advances are needed to sustain performance as task complexity grows.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents have achieved notable success on short-horizon and highly structured tasks. However, their ability to maintain coherent decision-making over long horizons in realistic and dynamic environments remains an open challenge.
  We introduce RetailBench, a high-fidelity benchmark designed to evaluate long-horizon autonomous decision-making in realistic commercial scenarios, where agents must operate under stochastic demand and evolving external conditions.
  We further propose the Evolving Strategy & Execution framework, which separates high-level strategic reasoning from low-level action execution. This design enables adaptive and interpretable strategy evolution over time. It is particularly important for long-horizon tasks, where non-stationary environments and error accumulation require strategies to be revised at a different temporal scale than action execution.
  Experiments on eight state-of-the-art LLMs across progressively challenging environments show that our framework improves operational stability and efficiency compared to other baselines. However, performance degrades substantially as task complexity increases, revealing fundamental limitations in current LLMs for long-horizon, multi-factor decision-making.

</details>


### [249] [Follow the Clues, Frame the Truth: Hybrid-evidential Deductive Reasoning in Open-Vocabulary Multimodal Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16463)
*Yu Liu,Lei Zhang,Haoxun Li,Hanlei Shi,Yuxuan Ding,Leyuan Qu,Taihao Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HyDRA proposes a Hybrid-evidential Deductive Reasoning Architecture for open-vocabulary multimodal emotion recognition, using a Propose-Verify-Decide protocol and reinforcement learning with hierarchical reward shaping to align reasoning with task performance; achieves robust and interpretable results, especially in ambiguous scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses ambiguity in equivocal multimodal cues and premature priors in Multimodal Large Language Models, advocating abductive reconstruction of emotional states via multiple evidence-grounded rationales from diverse latent perspectives.

Method: Formalizes inference as Propose-Verify-Decide. HyDRA consists of a hybrid evidential reasoning pipeline with reinforcement learning, hierarchical reward shaping, and interpretable evidence traces to reconcile multimodal cues from multiple latent viewpoints.

Result: HyDRA consistently outperforms strong baselines, particularly in ambiguous or conflicting scenarios, and provides interpretable, diagnostic evidence traces.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective open-vocabulary MER with abductive, evidence-grounded reasoning and interpretable diagnostics, showing promise for robust affective inference under uncertainty.

Abstract: Open-Vocabulary Multimodal Emotion Recognition (OV-MER) is inherently challenging due to the ambiguity of equivocal multimodal cues, which often stem from distinct unobserved situational dynamics. While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) offer extensive semantic coverage, their performance is often bottlenecked by premature commitment to dominant data priors, resulting in suboptimal heuristics that overlook crucial, complementary affective cues across modalities. We argue that effective affective reasoning requires more than surface-level association; it necessitates reconstructing nuanced emotional states by synthesizing multiple evidence-grounded rationales that reconcile these observations from diverse latent perspectives. We introduce HyDRA, a Hybrid-evidential Deductive Reasoning Architecture that formalizes inference as a Propose-Verify-Decide protocol. To internalize this abductive process, we employ reinforcement learning with hierarchical reward shaping, aligning the reasoning trajectories with final task performance to ensure they best reconcile the observed multimodal cues. Systematic evaluations validate our design choices, with HyDRA consistently outperforming strong baselines--especially in ambiguous or conflicting scenarios--while providing interpretable, diagnostic evidence traces.

</details>


### [250] [Breaking the Chain: A Causal Analysis of LLM Faithfulness to Intermediate Structures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16475)
*Oleg Somov,Mikhail Chaichuk,Mikhail Seleznyov,Alexander Panchenko,Elena Tutubalina*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Intermediate structures guide reasoning but do not robustly determine outputs; they behave as influential context rather than true causal mediators. A causal protocol reveals fragility: many edits to the structure fail to update decisions, though using an external tool to derive outcomes from the structure reduces fragility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To rigorously test whether schema-guided intermediate artifacts (rubrics, checklists, verification queries) causally mediate LLM decisions or merely accompany them, and to quantify faithfulness under controlled interventions.

Method: Propose a causal evaluation protocol: identify tasks where a deterministic function maps intermediate structures to final decisions; perform controlled edits to the structure and check if the output changes uniquely as dictated by the mapping; evaluate across eight models and three benchmarks; compare scenarios where the final decision is derived from the structure by an external tool and scenarios with prompts that emphasize structure over input.

Result: Models show self-consistency with their own intermediate structures but fail to update predictions after interventions in up to about 60% of cases, indicating fragile faithfulness when structures change; using an external tool to derive the final decision largely eliminates this fragility; prompts that prioritize structure over input do not close the gap.

Conclusion: Intermediate structures act as influential context rather than stable causal mediators in schema-guided pipelines; true causal mediation appears limited unless the derivation is delegated to external tools, and relying solely on structure-focused prompts is insufficient.

Abstract: Schema-guided reasoning pipelines ask LLMs to produce explicit intermediate structures -- rubrics, checklists, verification queries -- before committing to a final decision. But do these structures causally determine the output, or merely accompany it? We introduce a causal evaluation protocol that makes this directly measurable: by selecting tasks where a deterministic function maps intermediate structures to decisions, every controlled edit implies a unique correct output. Across eight models and three benchmarks, models appear self-consistent with their own intermediate structures but fail to update predictions after intervention in up to 60% of cases -- revealing that apparent faithfulness is fragile once the intermediate structure changes. When derivation of the final decision from the structure is delegated to an external tool, this fragility largely disappears; however, prompts which ask to prioritize the intermediate structure over the original input do not materially close the gap. Overall, intermediate structures in schema-guided pipelines function as influential context rather than stable causal mediators.

</details>


### [251] [ExpressMind: A Multimodal Pretrained Large Language Model for Expressway Operation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16495)
*Zihe Wang,Yihuan Wang,Haiyang Yu. Zhiyong Cui,Xiaojian Liao,Chengcheng Wang,Yonglin Tian,Yongxin Tong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multimodal expressway LLM (ExpressMind) with a full-stack dataset, Graph-Augmented RAG, RL-CoT, and cross-modal encoder, achieving state-of-the-art on expressway tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rule-based and isolated models limit cross-system knowledge integration; general LLMs struggle with expressway regulations and causal reasoning in unconventional scenarios; a data-scarce domain needs a cognitive, multimodal model.

Method: Develop ExpressMind as a pre-trained multimodal LLM for expressways; release a full-stack expressway dataset (texts, emergency reasoning chains, annotated video); propose a dual-layer self-supervised/unsupervised pre-training; introduce Graph-Augmented RAG for dynamic knowledge indexing; implement RL-aligned Chain-of-Thought (RL-CoT) for aligning reasoning with expert heuristics; integrate a cross-modal encoder to align visual/textual features; evaluate on a new multi-modal expressway benchmark.

Result: ExpressMind outperforms existing baselines in event detection, safety response generation, and complex traffic analysis on the benchmark; code and data released at the project site.

Conclusion: ExpressMind provides a cognitive core for intelligent expressway operations, advancing expressway-specific multimodal AI through new dataset, training paradigm, and reasoning mechanisms, with demonstrated effectiveness.

Abstract: The current expressway operation relies on rule-based and isolated models, which limits the ability to jointly analyze knowledge across different systems. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in intelligent transportation, advancing traffic models from algorithmic to cognitive intelligence. However, general LLMs are unable to effectively understand the regulations and causal relationships of events in unconventional scenarios in the expressway field. Therefore, this paper constructs a pre-trained multimodal large language model (MLLM) for expressways, ExpressMind, which serves as the cognitive core for intelligent expressway operations. This paper constructs the industry's first full-stack expressway dataset, encompassing traffic knowledge texts, emergency reasoning chains, and annotated video events to overcome data scarcity. This paper proposes a dual-layer LLM pre-training paradigm based on self-supervised training and unsupervised learning. Additionally, this study introduces a Graph-Augmented RAG framework to dynamically index the expressway knowledge base. To enhance reasoning for expressway incident response strategies, we develop a RL-aligned Chain-of-Thought (RL-CoT) mechanism that enforces consistency between model reasoning and expert problem-solving heuristics for incident handling. Finally, ExpressMind integrates a cross-modal encoder to align the dynamic feature sequences under the visual and textual channels, enabling it to understand traffic scenes in both video and image modalities. Extensive experiments on our newly released multi-modal expressway benchmark demonstrate that ExpressMind comprehensively outperforms existing baselines in event detection, safety response generation, and complex traffic analysis. The code and data are available at: https://wanderhee.github.io/ExpressMind/.

</details>


### [252] [Exploring different approaches to customize language models for domain-specific text-to-code generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16526)
*Luís Freire,Fernanda A. Andaló,Nicki Skafte Detlefsen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Smaller open-source LLMs can be adapted for domain-specific code generation via synthetic Python datasets; prompting and retrieval help domain relevance but not accuracy, while LoRA-based fine-tuning yields higher accuracy and domain alignment across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable cost-effective, domain-aware code generation without relying on large proprietary models, by adapting small LLMs using synthetic data and various customization strategies.

Method: Create synthetic datasets covering general Python, Scikit-learn workflows, and OpenCV tasks. Compare three customization strategies—few-shot prompting, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning—evaluated with benchmark metrics and domain-alignment (similarity) metrics.

Result: Prompting approaches improve domain relevance cost-effectively; their impact on standard benchmarks is limited. LoRA fine-tuning consistently yields higher accuracy and stronger domain alignment across most tasks.

Conclusion: Trade-offs exist between flexibility, compute cost, and performance when adapting smaller models to specialized programming. LoRA-based fine-tuning is generally the most effective for domain-specific code generation, while prompting/RAG offer cheaper, more adaptable options with modest accuracy gains.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in generating executable code from natural language descriptions. However, general-purpose models often struggle in specialized programming contexts where domain-specific libraries, APIs, or conventions must be used. Customizing smaller open-source models offers a cost-effective alternative to relying on large proprietary systems. In this work, we investigate how smaller language models can be adapted for domain-specific code generation using synthetic datasets. We construct datasets of programming exercises across three domains within the Python ecosystem: general Python programming, Scikit-learn machine learning workflows, and OpenCV-based computer vision tasks. Using these datasets, we evaluate three customization strategies: few-shot prompting, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and parameter-efficient fine-tuning using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). Performance is evaluated using both benchmark-based metrics and similarity-based metrics that measure alignment with domain-specific code. Our results show that prompting-based approaches such as few-shot learning and RAG can improve domain relevance in a cost-effective manner, although their impact on benchmark accuracy is limited. In contrast, LoRA-based fine-tuning consistently achieves higher accuracy and stronger domain alignment across most tasks. These findings highlight practical trade-offs between flexibility, computational cost, and performance when adapting smaller language models for specialized programming tasks.

</details>


### [253] [Designing for Disagreement: Front-End Guardrails for Assistance Allocation in LLM-Enabled Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16537)
*Carmen Ng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes bounded calibration with contestability for real-time multi-user LLM-enabled robot assistance, constraining choice to a governance-approved menu, maintaining legibility at deferral, and enabling outcome-specific contestation without changing the global rule. Illustrated with a public-concourse robot vignette; outlines an evaluation agenda focused on legibility, procedural legitimacy, and actionability, and on risks like automation bias and uneven contest usability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address how pluralistic human values and LLM behavioral variability affect real-time assistance in multi-user social settings, where guardrails are under-specified and outcomes depend on prompts, contexts, and user groups.

Method: Introduce a procedural front-end pattern named bounded calibration with contestability: (i) governance-approved menu of admissible modes; (ii) active mode remains legible at deferral; (iii) a contest pathway for outcomes without renegotiating the global rule. The approach is illustrated via a public-concourse robot vignette and complemented by an evaluation agenda.

Result: Conceptual design proposal with an illustrative vignette; no empirical results reported. Provides a framework and planned evaluation rather than deployment data.

Conclusion: The pattern mitigates silent defaults and unchecked user-configurable value settings under time pressure by treating pluralism and LLM uncertainty as persistent conditions, emphasizing legibility, legitimacy, and actionability, while noting risks such as automation bias and uneven usability of contest channels and calling for empirical evaluation.

Abstract: LLM-enabled robots prioritizing scarce assistance in social settings face pluralistic values and LLM behavioral variability: reasonable people can disagree about who is helped first, while LLM-mediated interaction policies vary across prompts, contexts, and groups in ways that are difficult to anticipate or verify at contact point. Yet user-facing guardrails for real-time, multi-user assistance allocation remain under-specified. We propose bounded calibration with contestability, a procedural front-end pattern that (i) constrains prioritization to a governance-approved menu of admissible modes, (ii) keeps the active mode legible in interaction-relevant terms at the point of deferral, and (iii) provides an outcome-specific contest pathway without renegotiating the global rule. Treating pluralism and LLM uncertainty as standing conditions, the pattern avoids both silent defaults that hide implicit value skews and wide-open user-configurable "value settings" that shift burden under time pressure. We illustrate the pattern with a public-concourse robot vignette and outline an evaluation agenda centered on legibility, procedural legitimacy, and actionability, including risks of automation bias and uneven usability of contest channels.

</details>


### [254] [BenchPreS: A Benchmark for Context-Aware Personalized Preference Selectivity of Persistent-Memory LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16557)
*Sangyeon Yoon,Sunkyoung Kim,Hyesoo Hong,Wonje Jeung,Yongil Kim,Wooseok Seo,Heuiyeen Yeen,Albert No*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BenchPreS evaluates how well memory-based user preferences are applied or suppressed across communication contexts. Findings: frontier LLMs struggle with context-sensitive application; stronger preference adherence correlates with over-application; reasoning or prompts do not fully fix the issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As personalization via persistent memory grows, there is a risk that stored user preferences are inappropriately applied in socially or institutionally constrained communications. The work seeks to determine when to apply or suppress memory-based preferences.

Method: Two metrics—Misapplication Rate (MR) and Appropriate Application Rate (AAR)—are used to assess context sensitivity of memory-based preferences across multiple models and scenarios.

Result: Frontier LLMs frequently misapply preferences; models with higher adherence exhibit more over-application; improvements in reasoning or prompt defenses do not fully resolve the problem.

Conclusion: Current LLMs tend to treat personalized preferences as globally enforceable rules rather than context-sensitive normative signals; better alignment is needed to ensure normative, context-aware memory usage.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly store user preferences in persistent memory to support personalization across interactions. However, in third-party communication settings governed by social and institutional norms, some user preferences may be inappropriate to apply. We introduce BenchPreS, which evaluates whether memory-based user preferences are appropriately applied or suppressed across communication contexts. Using two complementary metrics, Misapplication Rate (MR) and Appropriate Application Rate (AAR), we find even frontier LLMs struggle to apply preferences in a context-sensitive manner. Models with stronger preference adherence exhibit higher rates of over-application, and neither reasoning capability nor prompt-based defenses fully resolve this issue. These results suggest current LLMs treat personalized preferences as globally enforceable rules rather than as context-dependent normative signals.

</details>


### [255] [V-DyKnow: A Dynamic Benchmark for Time-Sensitive Knowledge in Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16581)
*Seyed Mahed Mousavi,Christian Moiola,Massimo Rizzoli,Simone Alghisi,Giuseppe Riccardi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: V-DyKnow benchmarks time-sensitive factual knowledge in Vision-Language Models across modalities and finds widespread outdated predictions, highlighting limitations of current update methods; benchmark and data are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world facts are time-sensitive and often change, while VLM training data and benchmarks are static. There is a need to evaluate how VLMs acquire and maintain time-aware knowledge across modalities.

Method: Introduce V-DyKnow benchmark to assess time-sensitive factual knowledge in VLMs. Benchmark both closed- and open-source VLMs; analyze reliability across modalities and input perturbations; evaluate knowledge editing and multi-modal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) methods for updates; perform data and mechanistic analysis to identify sources of outdated predictions.

Result: VLMs frequently output outdated facts; factual reliability degrades from textual to visual stimuli even when entities are correctly recognized; existing alignment approaches fail to consistently update knowledge across modalities.

Conclusion: V-DyKnow reveals fundamental limitations in how current VLMs acquire and update time-sensitive knowledge across modalities; benchmark, code, and evaluation data are released.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are trained on data snapshots of documents, including images and texts. Their training data and evaluation benchmarks are typically static, implicitly treating factual knowledge as time-invariant. However, real-world facts are intrinsically time-sensitive and subject to erratic and periodic changes, causing model predictions to become outdated. We present V-DyKnow, a Visual Dynamic Knowledge benchmark for evaluating time-sensitive factual knowledge in VLMs. Using V-DyKnow, we benchmark closed- and open-source VLMs and analyze a) the reliability (correctness and consistency) of model responses across modalities and input perturbations; b) the efficacy of knowledge editing and multi-modal RAG methods for knowledge updates across modalities; and c) the sources of outdated predictions, through data and mechanistic analysis. Our results show that VLMs frequently output outdated facts, reflecting outdated snapshots used in the (pre-)training phase. Factual reliability degrades from textual to visual stimuli, even when entities are correctly recognized. Besides, existing alignment approaches fail to consistently update the models' knowledge across modalities. Together, these findings highlight fundamental limitations in how current VLMs acquire and update time-sensitive knowledge across modalities. We release the benchmark, code, and evaluation data.

</details>


### [256] [Runtime Governance for AI Agents: Policies on Paths](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16586)
*Maurits Kaptein,Vassilis-Javed Khan,Andriy Podstavnychy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A runtime governance framework for AI agents that handles path-dependent, non-deterministic behavior by modeling compliance as a deterministic function of identity, partial path, next action, and organizational state; shows prompt-level instructions and static access control are special cases; presents a formal theory, policy examples, a reference implementation, and open problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address governance challenges posed by path-dependent agent behavior and balance task success with compliance costs, enabling runtime evaluation of policies.

Method: Formalize compliance policies as deterministic probabilistic mappings from identity, partial path, proposed action, and organizational state to violation probability; demonstrate that prompt-level instructions and static access control are subsumed; develop a formal framework; provide concrete policy examples; discuss a reference implementation; identify open questions.

Result: A general runtime governance framework for path-dependent AI agents; unifies design-time prompts and access-control as special cases; offers concrete policy examples and a reference implementation; highlights need for risk calibration and exploration of enforcement limits.

Conclusion: Runtime evaluation over the execution path is necessary for comprehensive path-dependent policy enforcement and offers a clear direction for practical governance research, including calibrating risk and understanding enforcement limits.

Abstract: AI agents -- systems that plan, reason, and act using large language models -- produce non-deterministic, path-dependent behavior that cannot be fully governed at design time, where with governed we mean striking the right balance between as high as possible successful task completion rate and the legal, data-breach, reputational and other costs associated with running agents. We argue that the execution path is the central object for effective runtime governance and formalize compliance policies as deterministic functions mapping agent identity, partial path, proposed next action, and organizational state to a policy violation probability. We show that prompt-level instructions (and "system prompts"), and static access control are special cases of this framework: the former shape the distribution over paths without actually evaluating them; the latter evaluates deterministic policies that ignore the path (i.e., these can only account for a specific subset of all possible paths). In our view, runtime evaluation is the general case, and it is necessary for any path-dependent policy. We develop the formal framework for analyzing AI agent governance, present concrete policy examples (inspired by the AI act), discuss a reference implementation, and identify open problems including risk calibration and the limits of enforced compliance.

</details>


### [257] [When AI Navigates the Fog of War](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16642)
*Ming Li,Xirui Li,Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Temporally grounded evaluation of LLM reasoning about an unfolding geopolitical crisis (2026 Middle East conflict) using 11 temporal nodes and verifiable questions to mitigate training-data leakage; finds strategic realism, domain-dependent reliability, and evolving narratives; provides archival snapshot for future studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether current large language models can reason about ongoing geopolitical crises without hindsight bias or training-data leakage, and to establish a rigorous temporally constrained evaluation framework for model reasoning.

Method: Construct 11 time-point nodes, 42 node-specific verifiable questions, and 5 general exploratory questions. Restrict reasoning to information publicly available at each moment to mitigate data leakage. Apply frontier LLMs to evaluate reasoning across domains (economic/logistical versus political multi-actor settings) and track narrative evolution over time.

Result: Models exhibit strategic realism beyond surface rhetoric; reliability is higher in economically/logistically structured domains than in politically ambiguous multi-actor settings; narratives evolve from expectations of rapid containment to accounts of regional entrenchment and attritional de-escalation.

Conclusion: First temporally grounded analysis of LLM reasoning in an unfolding geopolitical crisis; mitigates hindsight bias and data leakage concerns; provides an archival snapshot and a transferable framework for future studies of model reasoning under real-world crises.

Abstract: Can AI reason about a war before its trajectory becomes historically obvious? Analyzing this capability is difficult because retrospective geopolitical prediction is heavily confounded by training-data leakage. We address this challenge through a temporally grounded case study of the early stages of the 2026 Middle East conflict, which unfolded after the training cutoff of current frontier models. We construct 11 critical temporal nodes, 42 node-specific verifiable questions, and 5 general exploratory questions, requiring models to reason only from information that would have been publicly available at each moment. This design substantially mitigates training-data leakage concerns, creating a setting well-suited for studying how models analyze an unfolding crisis under the fog of war, and provides, to our knowledge, the first temporally grounded analysis of LLM reasoning in an ongoing geopolitical conflict. Our analysis reveals three main findings. First, current state-of-the-art large language models often display a striking degree of strategic realism, reasoning beyond surface rhetoric toward deeper structural incentives. Second, this capability is uneven across domains: models are more reliable in economically and logistically structured settings than in politically ambiguous multi-actor environments. Finally, model narratives evolve over time, shifting from early expectations of rapid containment toward more systemic accounts of regional entrenchment and attritional de-escalation. Since the conflict remains ongoing at the time of writing, this work can serve as an archival snapshot of model reasoning during an unfolding geopolitical crisis, enabling future studies without the hindsight bias of retrospective analysis.

</details>


### [258] [Domain-Independent Dynamic Programming with Constraint Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16648)
*Imko Marijnissen,J. Christopher Beck,Emir Demirović,Ryo Kuroiwa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Integrating constraint propagation into DP via a general CP solver within a Domain-Independent DP framework reduces state-space and improves solvability on three combinatorial problems, with favorable runtime trade-offs for constrained instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between DP (state-based) and CP (constraint-based) paradigms by enabling constraint propagation to prune DP search, aiming to leverage the strengths of both approaches.

Method: Embed a general-purpose CP solver inside Domain-Independent Dynamic Programming (DIDP); propagate constraints to prune DP states and transitions; evaluate with heuristic search on Single Machine Scheduling with Time Windows, RCPSP, and TSPTW; compare against a DP baseline in terms of state expansions, solved instances, and runtime.

Result: Constraint propagation significantly reduces state expansions; more instances solved for Single Machine Scheduling with Time Windows and RCPSP, with similar improvements for tightly constrained TSPTW; runtime benefits outweigh overhead for constrained instances; indicates value of CP propagation within DP.

Conclusion: Shows that constraint propagation can be effectively integrated into DP solvers, providing a model-based pathway to combine DP and CP, and highlighting potential performance gains on constrained combinatorial problems.

Abstract: There are two prevalent model-based paradigms for combinatorial problems: 1) state-based representations, such as heuristic search, dynamic programming (DP), and decision diagrams, and 2) constraint and domain-based representations, such as constraint programming (CP), (mixed-)integer programming, and Boolean satisfiability. In this paper, we bridge the gap between the DP and CP paradigms by integrating constraint propagation into DP, enabling a DP solver to prune states and transitions using constraint propagation. To this end, we implement constraint propagation using a general-purpose CP solver in the Domain-Independent Dynamic Programming framework and evaluate using heuristic search on three combinatorial optimisation problems: Single Machine Scheduling with Time Windows, the Resource Constrained Project Scheduling Problem (RCPSP), and the Travelling Salesperson Problem with Time Windows (TSPTW). Our evaluation shows that constraint propagation significantly reduces the number of state expansions, causing our approach to solve more instances than a DP solver for Single Machine Scheduling and RCPSP, and showing similar improvements for tightly constrained TSPTW instances. The runtime performance indicates that the benefits of propagation outweigh the overhead for constrained instances, but that further work into reducing propagation overhead could improve performance further. Our work is a key step in understanding the value of constraint propagation in DP solvers, providing a model-based approach to integrating DP and CP.

</details>


### [259] [What if Pinocchio Were a Reinforcement Learning Agent: A Normative End-to-End Pipeline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16651)
*Benoît Alcaraz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Pino, a hybrid RL+argumentation-based normative advisory system for norm-compliant, context-aware agents, with automatic argument extraction and norm-avoidance mitigation; empirically evaluated across pipeline components; discusses limitations and future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Societal rules and norms must be respected by AI to ensure safe, acceptable integration into daily life. The Pinocchio-inspired framework aims to provide explainable, normative guidance for agents.

Method: A hybrid model where reinforcement learning agents are supervised by argumentation-based normative advisors. It builds on AJAR, Jiminy, and NGRL architectures. Introduces a novel algorithm to automatically extract the arguments and relationships underlying the advisors’ decisions.

Result: Empirical evaluation of each component of the pipeline. Demonstrates normative compliance and context-awareness, and proposes mitigation strategies for norm avoidance.

Conclusion: Discussion of related work, current limitations, and avenues for future research.

Abstract: In the past decade, artificial intelligence (AI) has developed quickly. With this rapid progression came the need for systems capable of complying with the rules and norms of our society so that they can be successfully and safely integrated into our daily lives.
