Inspired by the story of Pinocchio in ``Le avventure di Pinocchio - Storia di un burattino'', this thesis proposes a pipeline that addresses the problem of developing norm compliant and context-aware agents. Building on the AJAR, Jiminy, and NGRL architectures, the work introduces \pino, a hybrid model in which reinforcement learning agents are supervised by argumentation-based normative advisors. In order to make this pipeline operational, this thesis also presents a novel algorithm for automatically extracting the arguments and relationships that underlie the advisors' decisions. Finally, this thesis investigates the phenomenon of \textit{norm avoidance}, providing a definition and a mitigation strategy within the context of reinforcement learning agents. Each component of the pipeline is empirically evaluated. The thesis concludes with a discussion of related work, current limitations, and directions for future research.

</details>


### [260] [Machines acquire scientific taste from institutional traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16659)
*Ziqin Gong,Ning Li,Huaikang Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Fine-tuning language models on journal publication decisions yields superior evaluative judgment for research pitches, outperforming frontier models and human editors on a held-out task; suggests AI can capture 'scientific taste' from institutional records and scale triage of research quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap that AI excels at verifiable problems but struggles with evaluative judgment. Investigate whether editorial records encode a tacit 'taste' for quality and if language models can learn and apply it to triage novel ideas.

Method: Train and evaluate multiple language models: 11 frontier models and human panels (editors and editorial boards) against a held-out benchmark of management research pitches spanning four quality tiers. Fine-tune models on years of publication decisions. Compare accuracy (and calibration) on held-out data. Assess transfer to untrained pairwise comparisons and one-sentence summaries, and test cross-domain transfer with economics publication records.

Result: Frontier models average ~31% accuracy on the pitch benchmark, barely above chance. Human editors/panels reach ~42% accuracy by majority vote. Fine-tuned models trained on publication decisions outperform frontier models and expert panels, with the best single model achieving ~59% accuracy and calibrated confidence (100% accuracy at the highest confidence). The models also transfer the evaluative signal to untrained tasks and to economics publication records, achieving ~70% accuracy in the transfer scenario.

Conclusion: Scientific taste is embedded in institutional editorial records and can be extracted by fine-tuning language models, enabling scalable triage of scientific production across disciplines where quality is difficult to verify algorithmically.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence matches or exceeds human performance on tasks with verifiable answers, from protein folding to Olympiad mathematics. Yet the capacity that most governs scientific advance is not reasoning but taste: the ability to judge which untested ideas deserve pursuit, exercised daily by editors and funders but never successfully articulated, taught, or automated. Here we show that fine-tuning language models on journal publication decisions recovers evaluative judgment inaccessible to both frontier models and human expertise. Using a held-out benchmark of research pitches in management spanning four quality tiers, we find that eleven frontier models, spanning major proprietary and open architectures, barely exceed chance, averaging 31% accuracy. Panels of journal editors and editorial board members reach 42% by majority vote. Fine-tuned models trained on years of publication records each surpass every frontier model and expert panel, with the best single model achieving 59%. These models exhibit calibrated confidence, reaching 100% accuracy on their highest-confidence predictions, and transfer this evaluative signal to untrained pairwise comparisons and one-sentence summaries. The mechanism generalizes: models trained on economics publication records achieve 70% accuracy. Scientific taste was not missing from AI's reach; it was deposited in the institutional record, waiting to be extracted. These results provide a scalable mechanism to triage the expanding volume of scientific production across disciplines where quality resists formal verification.

</details>


### [261] [CritiSense: Critical Digital Literacy and Resilience Against Misinformation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16672)
*Firoj Alam,Fatema Ahmad,Ali Ezzat Shahroor,Mohamed Bayan Kmainasi,Elisa Sartori,Giovanni Da San Martino,Abul Hasnat,Raian Ali*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CritiSense is a multilingual modular prebunking mobile app that trains users to recognize misinformation through microlearning challenges; usability results from a 93-user study are favorable; 300+ active users over 3+ months; free on App Store and Google Play; serves as a testbed for measuring microlearning impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Misinformation undermines informed decision-making; prebunking provides a proactive defense by training recognition of manipulation tactics; a multilingual, modular platform enables rapid topic updates across domains.

Method: Developed CritiSense with short interactive challenges and instant feedback; conducted a usability study with 93 users; measured satisfaction (83.9%) and ease of use (90.1%); collected qualitative feedback; documented reach (300+ active users) and availability on major app stores; included a demo video.

Result: High levels of user satisfaction and perceived ease of use; qualitative feedback suggests improved digital literacy; broad multilingual reach and modular architecture; evidence of real-world use with 300+ active users and free availability.

Conclusion: CritiSense provides a multilingual prebunking platform and a testbed for measuring the impact of microlearning on misinformation resilience; demonstrates feasibility, usability, and potential for rapid topic updates across domains.

Abstract: Misinformation on social media undermines informed decision-making and public trust. Prebunking offers a proactive complement by helping users recognize manipulation tactics before they encounter them in the wild. We present CritiSense, a mobile media-literacy app that builds these skills through short, interactive challenges with instant feedback. It is the first multilingual (supporting nine languages) and modular platform, designed for rapid updates across topics and domains. We report a usability study with 93 users: 83.9% expressed overall satisfaction and 90.1% rated the app as easy to use. Qualitative feedback indicates that CritiSense helps improve digital literacy skills. Overall, it provides a multilingual prebunking platform and a testbed for measuring the impact of microlearning on misinformation resilience. Over 3+ months, we have reached 300+ active users. It is freely available to all users on the Apple App Store (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/critisense/id6749675792) and Google Play Store (https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.critisense&hl=en). Demo Video: https://shorturl.at/CDcdc

</details>


### [262] [IQuest-Coder-V1 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16733)
*Jian Yang,Wei Zhang,Shawn Guo,Zhengmao Ye,Lin Jing,Shark Liu,Yizhi Li,Jiajun Wu,Cening Liu,X. Ma,Yuyang Song,Siwei Wu,Yuwen Li,L. Liao,T. Zheng,Ziling Huang,Zelong Huang,Che Liu,Yan Xing,Renyuan Li,Qingsong Cai,Hanxu Yan,Siyue Wang,Shikai Li,Jason Klein Liu,An Huang,Yongsheng Kang,Jinxing Zhang,Chuan Hao,Haowen Wang,Weicheng Gu,Ran Tao,Mingjie Tang,Peihao Wu,Jianzhou Wang,Xianglong Liu,Weifeng Lv,Bryan Dai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IQuest-Coder-V1 is a family of code LLMs with a code-flow multi-stage training paradigm, large context capabilities (32k/128k), and two post-training specialization paths (thinking via reasoning RL, instruct for general tasks). A Loop variant adds recurrence to trade capacity and deployment footprint. Reported to achieve state-of-the-art in agentic software engineering, competitive programming, and tool use; release includes full white-box checkpoints to spur autonomous code intelligence research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of static code representations and deployment constraints by enabling dynamic, agentic code reasoning and tool use; accelerate progress in autonomous coding systems through scalable, transparent training and release.

Method: Multi-stage training: (1) pre-training on code facts, repositories, and completion data; (2) mid-training integrating reasoning and agentic trajectories with 32k-context and repository-scale 128k-context to establish deep logical foundations; (3) post-training with two specialized paths—the thinking path using reasoning-driven RL and the instruct path optimized for general assistance. Loop variant introduces a recurrent mechanism to balance model capacity with deployment footprint.

Result: Claims state-of-the-art performance across critical dimensions of code intelligence: agentic software engineering, competitive programming, and complex tool use.

Conclusion: The IQuest-Coder-V1 series, with transparent white-box checkpoints from pre-training to final thinking and instruction models, aims to advance research in autonomous code intelligence and real-world agentic systems, while the Loop variant offers an efficiency-enabled deployment path.

Abstract: In this report, we introduce the IQuest-Coder-V1 series-(7B/14B/40B/40B-Loop), a new family of code large language models (LLMs). Moving beyond static code representations, we propose the code-flow multi-stage training paradigm, which captures the dynamic evolution of software logic through different phases of the pipeline. Our models are developed through the evolutionary pipeline, starting with the initial pre-training consisting of code facts, repository, and completion data. Following that, we implement a specialized mid-training stage that integrates reasoning and agentic trajectories in 32k-context and repository-scale in 128k-context to forge deep logical foundations. The models are then finalized with post-training of specialized coding capabilities, which is bifurcated into two specialized paths: the thinking path (utilizing reasoning-driven RL) and the instruct path (optimized for general assistance). IQuest-Coder-V1 achieves state-of-the-art performance among competitive models across critical dimensions of code intelligence: agentic software engineering, competitive programming, and complex tool use. To address deployment constraints, the IQuest-Coder-V1-Loop variant introduces a recurrent mechanism designed to optimize the trade-off between model capacity and deployment footprint, offering an architecturally enhanced path for efficacy-efficiency trade-off. We believe the release of the IQuest-Coder-V1 series, including the complete white-box chain of checkpoints from pre-training bases to the final thinking and instruction models, will advance research in autonomous code intelligence and real-world agentic systems.

</details>


### [263] [Differential Harm Propensity in Personalized LLM Agents: The Curious Case of Mental Health Disclosure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16734)
*Caglar Yildirim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Personalization (bio or mental health disclosure) offers limited protective effect against harmful agentic behavior; effect is model-dependent, fragile under jailbreak, and can cause over-refusal on benign tasks, indicating a safety-utility trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess how sensitive user-context signals (e.g., mental health disclosures) influence harmful task completion by LLM-based agents and whether personalization serves as a safety guardrail.

Method: Empirical evaluation of frontier lab LLMs (GPT 5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3-Pro) and open-model DeepSeek 3.2 on multi-step malicious and benign tasks from the AgentHarm benchmark, under prompt conditions varying personalization (no bio, bio-only, bio+mental health disclosure) and with a lightweight jailbreak injection; measure task completion and refusals.

Result: Harmful task completion remains non-trivial across models: frontier models complete some harmful tasks; DeepSeek 3.2 shows higher harmful completion. Bio-only context reduces harm scores and increases refusals; explicit mental health disclosure often shifts outcomes further toward safety but effects are modest and not uniformly reliable after multiple-testing correction. Refusal increases also occur on benign tasks, indicating a safety-utility trade-off via over-refusal. Jailbreak prompts sharply elevate harm and can weaken or override personalization's protective effect.

Conclusion: Personalization can act as a weak protective factor for agentic misuse, but its robustness is limited under adversarial pressure; personalization-aware evaluations and safeguards that remain effective across user-context conditions are needed.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as tool-using agents, shifting safety concerns from harmful text generation to harmful task completion. Deployed systems often condition on user profiles or persistent memory, yet agent safety evaluations typically ignore personalization signals. To address this gap, we investigated how mental health disclosure, a sensitive and realistic user-context cue, affects harmful behavior in agentic settings. Building on the AgentHarm benchmark, we evaluated frontier and open-source LLMs on multi-step malicious tasks (and their benign counterparts) under controlled prompt conditions that vary user-context personalization (no bio, bio-only, bio+mental health disclosure) and include a lightweight jailbreak injection. Our results reveal that harmful task completion is non-trivial across models: frontier lab models (e.g., GPT 5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3-Pro) still complete a measurable fraction of harmful tasks, while an open model (DeepSeek 3.2) exhibits substantially higher harmful completion. Adding a bio-only context generally reduces harm scores and increases refusals. Adding an explicit mental health disclosure often shifts outcomes further in the same direction, though effects are modest and not uniformly reliable after multiple-testing correction. Importantly, the refusal increase also appears on benign tasks, indicating a safety--utility trade-off via over-refusal. Finally, jailbreak prompting sharply elevates harm relative to benign conditions and can weaken or override the protective shift induced by personalization. Taken together, our results indicate that personalization can act as a weak protective factor in agentic misuse settings, but it is fragile under minimal adversarial pressure, highlighting the need for personalization-aware evaluations and safeguards that remain robust across user-context conditions.

</details>


### [264] [MedCL-Bench: Benchmarking stability-efficiency trade-offs and scaling in biomedical continual learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16738)
*Min Zeng,Shuang Zhou,Zaifu Zhan,Rui Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MedCL-Bench is a unified, task-diverse benchmark for continual learning in biomedical NLP that reveals sequential fine-tuning causes forgetting and CL methods balance retention and compute differently across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biomedical NLP evolves with new evidence and terminology; there is no standardized, task-diverse benchmark to evaluate continual learning under consistent protocols, robustness to task order, and compute-aware reporting.

Method: MedCL-Bench streams ten biomedical NLP datasets across five task families and evaluates eleven continual learning strategies across eight task orders, reporting retention, transfer, and GPU-hour cost; experiments span multiple backbones.

Result: Sequential fine-tuning on incoming tasks causes catastrophic forgetting and update-induced regressions on prior tasks. Retention–compute frontiers vary by method: parameter-isolation best retains accuracy per GPU-hour; replay offers strong protection at higher compute cost; regularization yields limited benefits. Forgetting is task-dependent, with multi-label topic classification most vulnerable and constrained-output tasks more robust.

Conclusion: MedCL-Bench provides a reproducible framework for auditing model updates before deployment and enables standardized evaluation of continual learning strategies in biomedical NLP.

Abstract: Medical language models must be updated as evidence and terminology evolve, yet sequential updating can trigger catastrophic forgetting. Although biomedical NLP has many static benchmarks, no unified, task-diverse benchmark exists for evaluating continual learning under standardized protocols, robustness to task order and compute-aware reporting. We introduce MedCL-Bench, which streams ten biomedical NLP datasets spanning five task families and evaluates eleven continual learning strategies across eight task orders, reporting retention, transfer, and GPU-hour cost. Across backbones and task orders, direct sequential fine-tuning on incoming tasks induces catastrophic forgetting, causing update-induced performance regressions on prior tasks. Continual learning methods occupy distinct retention-compute frontiers: parameter-isolation provides the best retention per GPU-hour, replay offers strong protection at higher cost, and regularization yields limited benefit. Forgetting is task-dependent, with multi-label topic classification most vulnerable and constrained-output tasks more robust. MedCL-Bench provides a reproducible framework for auditing model updates before deployment.

</details>


### [265] [Nonstandard Errors in AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16744)
*Ruijiang Gao,Steven Chong Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: 150 autonomous Claude Code agents testing six NYSE TAQ hypotheses on SPY (2015–2024) reveal sizable nonstandard errors (NSEs) across agents, with stable empirical styles by model family; exemplar papers sharply reduce dispersion (80–99% IQR shrink) within converging measure families; convergence driven by imitation, not understanding; raises concerns and guidance for AI in automated policy evaluation and empirical research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess reproducibility and consistency of AI-driven empirical analyses, specifically whether autonomous agents yield the same empirical results given the same data and question; understand how methodological choices and model families affect inference and whether AI-driven policy evaluation can be trusted.

Method: Deploy 150 autonomous Claude Code agents to independently test six hypotheses about market-quality trends in NYSE TAQ data for SPY (2015–2024). Compare measure choices (e.g., autocorrelation vs. variance ratio; dollar vs. share volume) and model families (Sonnet 4.6 vs. Opus 4.6). Use a three-stage feedback protocol: AI peer review (written critiques) and exposure to top-rated exemplar papers to study effects on dispersion of estimates.

Result: Agents exhibit substantial nonstandard errors (NSEs): agent-to-agent variation in analytical choices and results. Divergence in measure choice and methodological preferences across model families (stable “empirical styles”). In a three-stage protocol, AI peer review has minimal effect on dispersion, while exposure to exemplar papers reduces the within-family interquartile range of estimates by 80–99% within converging measure families. Convergence occurs via within-family tightening and occasional switching of measure families, driven by imitation rather than genuine understanding.

Conclusion: Findings imply that AI-driven automated policy evaluation and empirical research may yield non-neplicable variation unless mitigated by targeted training and exemplar-based standardization. While exemplars can induce convergence, the mechanism is imitation, raising concerns about genuine understanding and generalizability. Policy-relevant AI research pipelines should account for NSEs and leverage exemplar-guided standardization to improve consistency.

Abstract: We study whether state-of-the-art AI coding agents, given the same data and research question, produce the same empirical results. Deploying 150 autonomous Claude Code agents to independently test six hypotheses about market quality trends in NYSE TAQ data for SPY (2015--2024), we find that AI agents exhibit sizable \textit{nonstandard errors} (NSEs), that is, uncertainty from agent-to-agent variation in analytical choices, analogous to those documented among human researchers. AI agents diverge substantially on measure choice (e.g., autocorrelation vs.\ variance ratio, dollar vs.\ share volume). Different model families (Sonnet 4.6 vs.\ Opus 4.6) exhibit stable ``empirical styles,'' reflecting systematic differences in methodological preferences. In a three-stage feedback protocol, AI peer review (written critiques) has minimal effect on dispersion, whereas exposure to top-rated exemplar papers reduces the interquartile range of estimates by 80--99\% within \textit{converging} measure families. Convergence occurs both through within-family estimation tightening and through agents switching measure families entirely, but convergence reflects imitation rather than understanding. These findings have implications for the growing use of AI in automated policy evaluation and empirical research.

</details>


### [266] [Anticipatory Planning for Multimodal AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16777)
*Yongyuan Liang,Shijie Zhou,Yu Gu,Hao Tan,Gang Wu,Franck Dernoncourt,Jihyung Kil,Ryan A. Rossi,Ruiyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TraceR1 introduces anticipatory trajectory reasoning via a two-stage RL framework for multimodal agents, forecasting short-horizon trajectories before execution and refining with grounded fine-tuning to improve long-horizon planning and action executability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal agents are largely reactive and optimize actions in isolation, lacking forward-looking planning for long-horizon tasks; this harms planning coherence and task solvability.

Method: Stage 1 performs trajectory-level reinforcement learning with rewards enforcing global consistency across predicted action sequences. Stage 2 applies grounded reinforcement fine-tuning using execution feedback from frozen tool agents to refine step-level accuracy and executability.

Result: Evaluated on seven benchmarks spanning online/offline computer-use and multimodal tool-use reasoning tasks; TraceR1 achieves substantial improvements in planning stability, execution robustness, and generalization over reactive and single-stage baselines.

Conclusion:  anticipatory trajectory reasoning is a key principle for building multimodal agents capable of reasoning, planning, and acting effectively in complex real-world environments.

Abstract: Recent advances in multimodal agents have improved computer-use interaction and tool-usage, yet most existing systems remain reactive, optimizing actions in isolation without reasoning about future states or long-term goals. This limits planning coherence and prevents agents from reliably solving high-level, multi-step tasks. We introduce TraceR1, a two-stage reinforcement learning framework that explicitly trains anticipatory reasoning by forecasting short-horizon trajectories before execution. The first stage performs trajectory-level reinforcement learning with rewards that enforce global consistency across predicted action sequences. The second stage applies grounded reinforcement fine-tuning, using execution feedback from frozen tool agents to refine step-level accuracy and executability. TraceR1 is evaluated across seven benchmarks, covering online computer-use, offline computer-use benchmarks, and multimodal tool-use reasoning tasks, where it achieves substantial improvements in planning stability, execution robustness, and generalization over reactive and single-stage baselines. These results show that anticipatory trajectory reasoning is a key principle for building multimodal agents that can reason, plan, and act effectively in complex real-world environments.

</details>


### [267] [Beyond Accuracy: Evaluating Forecasting Models by Multi-Echelon Inventory Cost](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16815)
*Swata Marik,Swayamjit Saha,Garga Chatterjee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified forecasting-inventory pipeline integrating multiple forecast models within an inventory simulation framework improves cost and fill rate in single- and two-echelon newsvendor settings on the M5 dataset; Temporal CNN and LSTM perform best, with robustness across sensitivity and multi-echelon tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To close the gap between forecasting accuracy and inventory decision-making by embedding diverse forecasting models into a unified, data-driven optimization framework that supports decision-making in modern supply chains.

Method: Build a digitalized pipeline combining traditional forecasting models, machine learning regressors, and deep sequence models inside a single inventory simulation framework; evaluate seven forecasting approaches using the M5 Walmart dataset; test under one- and two-echelon newsvendor structures; perform sensitivity and multi-echelon analyses.

Result: Temporal CNN and LSTM reduce inventory costs and improve fill rates relative to statistical baselines; the approach demonstrates robustness and scalability across sensitivity and multi-echelon tests.

Conclusion: The integrated forecasting-inventory pipeline offers a data-driven decision-support tool for modern supply chains, with strong performance gains for inventory control and potential applicability across various network configurations.

Abstract: This study develops a digitalized forecasting-inventory optimization pipeline integrating traditional forecasting models, machine learning regressors, and deep sequence models within a unified inventory simulation framework. Using the M5 Walmart dataset, we evaluate seven forecasting approaches and assess their operational impact under single- and two-echelon newsvendor systems. Results indicate that Temporal CNN and LSTM models significantly reduce inventory costs and improve fill rates compared to statistical baselines. Sensitivity and multi-echelon analyses demonstrate robustness and scalability, offering a data-driven decision-support tool for modern supply chains.

</details>


### [268] [Is Conformal Factuality for RAG-based LLMs Robust? Novel Metrics and Systematic Insights](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16817)
*Yi Chen,Daiwei Chen,Sukrut Madhav Chikodikar,Caitlyn Heqi Yin,Ramya Korlakai Vinayak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Conformal factuality filtering for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) offers distribution-free reliability but struggles with usefulness and robustness under real-world shifts; lightweight entailment-based verifiers can be far more efficient and competitive with LLM-based scorers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs frequently hallucinate, especially in knowledge-intensive tasks. RAG grounds responses in retrieved evidence but lacks statistical guarantees of correctness. Conformal factuality provides a distribution-free reliability guarantee via calibrated thresholds, yet its impact on task usefulness and resilience under distribution shifts remains unclear.

Method: Systematic analysis of conformal factuality in RAG-based LLMs across generation, scoring, calibration, robustness, and efficiency. Proposes informativeness-aware metrics. Evaluates across three benchmarks and multiple model families. Compares conformal filtering and LLM confidence scorers. Tests lightweight entailment-based verifiers and reports FLOPs.

Result: Conformal filtering shows low usefulness at high factuality due to vacuous outputs. Its guarantees fail to be robust under distribution shifts and distractors, requiring calibration data matching deployment conditions. Lightweight entailment-based verifiers match or outperform LLM-based scorers while using over 100x fewer FLOPs.

Conclusion: There is a factuality–informativeness trade-off; conformal filtering is fragile under shifts and distractors. New approaches are needed that balance reliability with robustness and usefulness, along with practical guidance for building efficient, reliable RAG pipelines.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) frequently hallucinate, limiting their reliability in knowledge-intensive applications. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and conformal factuality have emerged as potential ways to address this limitation. While RAG aims to ground responses in retrieved evidence, it provides no statistical guarantee that the final output is correct. Conformal factuality filtering offers distribution-free statistical reliability by scoring and filtering atomic claims using a threshold calibrated on held-out data, however, the informativeness of the final output is not guaranteed. We systematically analyze the reliability and usefulness of conformal factuality for RAG-based LLMs across generation, scoring, calibration, robustness, and efficiency. We propose novel informativeness-aware metrics that better reflect task utility under conformal filtering. Across three benchmarks and multiple model families, we find that (i) conformal filtering suffers from low usefulness at high factuality levels due to vacuous outputs, (ii) conformal factuality guarantee is not robust to distribution shifts and distractors, highlighting the limitation that requires calibration data to closely match deployment conditions, and (iii) lightweight entailment-based verifiers match or outperform LLM-based model confidence scorers while requiring over $100\times$ fewer FLOPs. Overall, our results expose factuality-informativeness trade-offs and fragility of conformal filtering framework under distribution shifts and distractors, highlighting the need for new approaches for reliability with robustness and usefulness as key metrics, and provide actionable guidance for building RAG pipelines that are both reliable and computationally efficient.

</details>


### [269] [Surg$Σ$: A Spectrum of Large-Scale Multimodal Data and Foundation Models for Surgical Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16822)
*Zhitao Zeng,Mengya Xu,Jian Jiang,Pengfei Guo,Yunqiu Xu,Zhu Zhuo,Chang Han Low,Yufan He,Dong Yang,Chenxi Lin,Yiming Gu,Jiaxin Guo,Yutong Ban,Daguang Xu,Qi Dou,Yueming Jin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework and data foundation (SurgΣ-DB) for surgical intelligence: large-scale multimodal data across 6 specialties and 18 tasks, with hierarchical reasoning annotations, enabling cross-task generalization and interpretability via SurgΣ foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the generalization gap in surgical AI and the lack of large-scale, standardized multimodal data; consolidate heterogeneous datasets into a unified schema to support diverse surgical tasks.

Method: Build SurgΣ-DB by aggregating open-source, in-house, and web data into a unified schema; annotate image- and video-level data across 18 tasks (understanding, reasoning, planning, generation); span 6 specialties; incorporate hierarchical reasoning annotations; validate with SurgΣ foundation models trained on SurgΣ-DB.

Result: Empirical evidence that large-scale multimodal annotations, unified semantics, and structured reasoning annotations improve cross-task generalization and interpretability of surgical foundation models.

Conclusion: SurgΣ-DB and SurgΣ models enable more generalizable and interpretable surgical AI across procedures and institutions; standardized, semantically rich data with structured reasoning is essential for progress.

Abstract: Surgical intelligence has the potential to improve the safety and consistency of surgical care, yet most existing surgical AI frameworks remain task-specific and struggle to generalize across procedures and institutions. Although multimodal foundation models, particularly multimodal large language models, have demonstrated strong cross-task capabilities across various medical domains, their advancement in surgery remains constrained by the lack of large-scale, systematically curated multimodal data. To address this challenge, we introduce Surg$Σ$, a spectrum of large-scale multimodal data and foundation models for surgical intelligence. At the core of this framework lies Surg$Σ$-DB, a large-scale multimodal data foundation designed to support diverse surgical tasks. Surg$Σ$-DB consolidates heterogeneous surgical data sources (including open-source datasets, curated in-house clinical collections and web-source data) into a unified schema, aiming to improve label consistency and data standardization across heterogeneous datasets. Surg$Σ$-DB spans 6 clinical specialties and diverse surgical types, providing rich image- and video-level annotations across 18 practical surgical tasks covering understanding, reasoning, planning, and generation, at an unprecedented scale (over 5.98M conversations). Beyond conventional multimodal conversations, Surg$Σ$-DB incorporates hierarchical reasoning annotations, providing richer semantic cues to support deeper contextual understanding in complex surgical scenarios. We further provide empirical evidence through recently developed surgical foundation models built upon Surg$Σ$-DB, illustrating the practical benefits of large-scale multimodal annotations, unified semantic design, and structured reasoning annotations for improving cross-task generalization and interpretability.

</details>


### [270] [Prompt Programming for Cultural Bias and Alignment of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16827)
*Maksim Eren,Eric Michalak,Brian Cook,Johnny Seales*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reproduces a survey-based cultural alignment framework on open-weight LLMs and introduces DSPy-based prompt programming to optimize cultural conditioning, finding that prompt optimization often outperforms hand-crafted prompts and yields more transferable cultural alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve cultural alignment of LLMs for strategic decision-making and document tasks, extending prior framework to open-weight models and enabling reproducible, modular prompting.

Method: Reproduce survey-based projection and distance metrics on open-weight LLMs; compare culture prompts vs DSPy-based prompt optimization; treat prompts as modular programs; optimize against cultural-distance objectives.

Result: Prompt optimization frequently surpasses culture prompting in aligning outputs with target cultures; DSPy prompting yields more stable, transferable culturally aligned responses across open-weight models.

Conclusion: Prompt-programming with DSPy offers a scalable, transferable path to cultural alignment in LLMs, enabling better reflection of target-population value profiles and extending cultural alignment beyond proprietary systems.

Abstract: Culture shapes reasoning, values, prioritization, and strategic decision-making, yet large language models (LLMs) often exhibit cultural biases that misalign with target populations. As LLMs are increasingly used for strategic decision-making, policy support, and document engineering tasks such as summarization, categorization, and compliance-oriented auditing, improving cultural alignment is important for ensuring that downstream analyses and recommendations reflect target-population value profiles rather than default model priors. Previous work introduced a survey-grounded cultural alignment framework and showed that culture-specific prompting can reduce misalignment, but it primarily evaluated proprietary models and relied on manual prompt engineering. In this paper, we validate and extend that framework by reproducing its social sciences survey based projection and distance metrics on open-weight LLMs, testing whether the same cultural skew and benefits of culture conditioning persist outside closed LLM systems. Building on this foundation, we introduce use of prompt programming with DSPy for this problem-treating prompts as modular, optimizable programs-to systematically tune cultural conditioning by optimizing against cultural-distance objectives. In our experiments, we show that prompt optimization often improves upon cultural prompt engineering, suggesting prompt compilation with DSPy can provide a more stable and transferable route to culturally aligned LLM responses.

</details>


### [271] [Learning to Present: Inverse Specification Rewards for Agentic Slide Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16839)
*Karthik Ragunath Ananda Kumar,Subrahmanyam Arunachalam*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OpenEnv-compatible RL framework for automated slide generation (SlideRL) where LLM agents research topics, plan content, and generate HTML slides via tool use, optimized with a multi-component reward including an inverse specification reward; a 7B model fine-tuned with GRPO achieves 91.2% of Claude Opus 4.6's quality, 33.1% better than the base, with six-model evidence that instruction adherence and tool-use matter more than parameter count; dataset and code released (SlideRL: 288 trajectories).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address automated presentation generation's core challenges—coherent content creation, visual design, and audience-aware communication—by enabling LLM agents to operate in an OpenEnv RL setting, using tool use and holistic evaluation signals (including inverse task reward) to align generated slides with intended purposes.

Method: An OpenEnv-compatible RL environment where LLM agents perform research, planning, and HTML slide generation via tool use. The approach uses a multi-component reward: structural validation, render quality assessment, LLM-based aesthetic scoring, content quality metrics, and an inverse specification reward that tests whether slides convey the intended purpose. The Qwen2.5-Coder-7B model is fine-tuned via GRPO with 0.5% of its parameters, using prompts derived from expert demonstrations collected with Claude Opus 4.6. Experiments span 48 business briefs across six models; SlideRL dataset comprises 288 multi-turn rollout trajectories, released with code and data links.

Result: The 7B model attains 91.2% of Claude Opus 4.6’s quality and shows a 33.1% improvement over the base model. Across six models, instruction adherence and tool-use compliance drive task performance more than raw parameter count. A public dataset (SlideRL) and code repository are provided for reproducibility.

Conclusion: High-performance automated slide generation can be achieved with modest parameter updates when guided by comprehensive, composite rewards; controlling for instruction adherence and tool use is crucial for agentic capabilities. The work contributes SlideRL (288 trajectories) and open-source code/dataset to foster further research.

Abstract: Automated presentation generation remains a challenging task requiring coherent content creation, visual design, and audience-aware communication. This work proposes an OpenEnv-compatible reinforcement learning environment where LLM agents learn to research topics, plan content, and generate professional HTML slide presentations through tool use. We introduce a multi-component reward system combining structural validation, render quality assessment, LLM-based aesthetic scoring, content quality metrics, and an inverse specification reward that measures how faithfully generated slides convey their intended purpose. The inverse specification reward, an "inverse task" where an LLM attempts to recover the original specification from generated slides, provides a holistic quality signal. Our approach fine-tunes Qwen2.5-Coder-7B via GRPO, training only 0.5% of parameters on prompts derived from expert demonstrations collected using Claude Opus 4.6. Experiments on 48 diverse business briefs across six models demonstrate that our fine-tuned 7B model achieves 91.2% of Claude Opus 4.6's quality while improving 33.1% over the base model. The six-model comparison reveals that instruction adherence and tool-use compliance, rather than raw parameter count, determine agentic task performance. We contribute SlideRL, an open-source dataset of 288 multi-turn rollout trajectories across all six models: https://huggingface.co/datasets/KarthikRagunathAnandaKumar/sliderl-multi-turn-rollouts Code: https://github.com/pushing-the-frontier/slide-forge-llm

</details>


### [272] [Internalizing Agency from Reflective Experience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16843)
*Rui Ge,Yichao Fu,Yuyang Qian,Junda Su,Yiming Zhao,Peng Zhao,Hao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LEAFE learns from reflective experience to improve long-horizon agent agency by backtracking and distilling corrective feedback into supervised fine-tuning, yielding improved Pass@k metrics over baseline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outcome-driven post-training methods optimize final success but underutilize rich environment feedback, causing distribution sharpening and limited long-horizon problem solving.

Method: During exploration, summarize environment feedback into actionable experience, backtrack to earlier decisions, explore alternative branches with revised actions, and distill corrections via supervised fine-tuning to enhance recovery.

Result: On interactive coding and agentic tasks under fixed budgets, LEAFE improves Pass@1 over the base model and achieves higher Pass@k than GRPO and Early Experience, with gains up to 14% on Pass@128.

Conclusion: LEAFE enables feedback-grounded learning from reflective experience, mitigating distributional collapse and enhancing long-horizon problem-solving capabilities in LLM agents.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents that must plan, act, and recover from mistakes through long-horizon interaction with environments that provide rich feedback. However, prevailing outcome-driven post-training methods (e.g., RL with verifiable rewards) primarily optimize final success signals, leaving rich environment feedback underutilized. Consequently, they often lead to distribution sharpening: the policy becomes better at reproducing a narrow set of already-successful behaviors, while failing to improve the feedback-grounded agency needed to expand problem-solving capacity (e.g., Pass@k) in long-horizon settings.
  To address this, we propose LEAFE (Learning Feedback-Grounded Agency from Reflective Experience), a framework that internalizes recovery agency from reflective experience. Specifically, during exploration, the agent summarizes environment feedback into actionable experience, backtracks to earlier decision points, and explores alternative branches with revised actions. We then distill these experience-guided corrections into the model through supervised fine-tuning, enabling the policy to recover more effectively in future interactions. Across a diverse set of interactive coding and agentic tasks under fixed interaction budgets, LEAFE consistently improves Pass@1 over the base model and achieves higher Pass@k than outcome-driven baselines (GRPO) and experience-based methods such as Early Experience, with gains of up to 14% on Pass@128.

</details>


### [273] [SocialOmni: Benchmarking Audio-Visual Social Interactivity in Omni Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16859)
*Tianyu Xie,Jinfa Huang,Yuexiao Ma,Rongfang Luo,Yan Yang,Wang Chen,Yuhui Zeng,Ruize Fang,Yixuan Zou,Xiawu Zheng,Jiebo Luo,Rongrong Ji*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SocialOmni is a benchmark to evaluate social-interactive abilities of omni-modal LLMs, focusing on speaker separation, interruption timing, and interruption generation; includes perception and interaction-generation sets and tests robustness; reveals gap between perception and interaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses lack of evaluation for conversational interactivity in OLMs, beyond static accuracy tasks; aims to quantify dynamic social cues in dialogue.

Method: Construct SocialOmni with 2,000 perception samples and 209 interaction-generation instances with strict temporal/context constraints; add audio-visual inconsistency tests; benchmark 12 leading OLMs; analyze correlation between perception accuracy and interruption capability.

Result: Notable variance across models; poor correlation between perceptual accuracy and interruption quality; suggests current metrics are insufficient; provides actionable diagnostics to bridge perception-interaction gap.

Conclusion: SocialOmni offers a tool to diagnose and guide improvements in social interactivity for OLMs and emphasizes the need to integrate perception and interaction in benchmarks.

Abstract: Omni-modal large language models (OLMs) redefine human-machine interaction by natively integrating audio, vision, and text. However, existing OLM benchmarks remain anchored to static, accuracy-centric tasks, leaving a critical gap in assessing social interactivity, the fundamental capacity to navigate dynamic cues in natural dialogues. To this end, we propose SocialOmni, a comprehensive benchmark that operationalizes the evaluation of this conversational interactivity across three core dimensions: (i) speaker separation and identification (who is speaking), (ii) interruption timing control (when to interject), and (iii) natural interruption generation (how to phrase the interruption). SocialOmni features 2,000 perception samples and a quality-controlled diagnostic set of 209 interaction-generation instances with strict temporal and contextual constraints, complemented by controlled audio-visual inconsistency scenarios to test model robustness. We benchmarked 12 leading OLMs, which uncovers significant variance in their social-interaction capabilities across models. Furthermore, our analysis reveals a pronounced decoupling between a model's perceptual accuracy and its ability to generate contextually appropriate interruptions, indicating that understanding-centric metrics alone are insufficient to characterize conversational social competence. More encouragingly, these diagnostics from SocialOmni yield actionable signals for bridging the perception-interaction divide in future OLMs.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [274] [Tokenization Tradeoffs in Structured EHR Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15644)
*Lin Lawrence Guo,Santiago Eduardo Arciniegas,Joseph Jihyung Lee,Adam Paul Yan,George Tomlinson,Jason Fries,Lillian Sung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Joint event encoding and positional time encoding in EHR transformers yield higher predictive performance across 74 tasks and reduce pretraining compute; improvements attributed to local binding efficiency; generalizes to an adult ICU setting despite vocabulary mismatch; tokenization is a tunable lever for EHR foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically assess how tokenization design choices (event encoding, time encoding, and workflow annotation) affect downstream performance and computational efficiency in EHR foundation models, a largely unexplored area.

Method: Pretrain a transformer on pediatric EHR data under a factorial design that varies event encoding, time encoding, and workflow annotation. Evaluate AUC across 74 clinical prediction tasks. Compare pretraining compute (FLOPs) across configurations. Conduct targeted ablations to identify binding mechanisms. Validate generalizability with an external adult ICU cohort, examining vocabulary mismatch and institution-specific temporal/workflow effects.

Result: Joint event encoding and positional time encoding outperformed alternatives on most tasks (73/74 and 71/74). They also required 39.5% and 9.6% fewer pretraining FLOPs, respectively. Ablations linked the advantage to improved local binding, where code-attribute pairs are merged into single tokens. External adult ICU evaluation showed generalization despite vocabulary mismatch; temporal and workflow effects were more institution-specific.

Conclusion: Tokenization design is a practical lever to enhance both performance and computational efficiency of EHR foundation models, with local binding as a key mechanism driving gains.

Abstract: Foundation models for structured electronic health records (EHRs) are pretrained on longitudinal sequences of timestamped clinical events to learn adaptable patient representations. Tokenization -- how these timelines are converted into discrete model inputs -- determines what information is preserved, how efficiently it is encoded, and which relationships must be learned versus precomputed. Yet the impact of tokenization design choices on downstream performance and computational efficiency remains largely unexplored. Here, we pretrained a transformer on pediatric EHR data under a factorial design, varying tokenization along event encoding, time encoding, and workflow annotation. We evaluated area-under-the-receiver-operating-characteristic curve across 74 clinical prediction tasks. Joint event encoding and positional time encoding outperformed their alternatives (73/74 and 71/74 tasks) while requiring 39.5% and 9.6% fewer pretraining floating-point operations, respectively. Targeted ablations traced the joint encoding advantage to local binding efficiency, that is, code-attribute pairs are combined into single tokens, rather than split across tokens that the model must learn to associate during pretraining. External evaluation on an adult intensive care unit cohort demonstrated that this advantage generalizes despite substantial vocabulary mismatch, while temporal and workflow effects remain institution-specific. These results establish tokenization as a tractable lever for improving both the performance and efficiency of EHR foundation models.

</details>


### [275] [XLinear: Frequency-Enhanced MLP with CrossFilter for Robust Long-Range Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15645)
*Xiang Ao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: XLinear is an MLP-based long-range forecaster that decomposes time series into trend and seasonal parts, applying Enhanced Frequency Attention for the trend and CrossFilter Block for the seasonal component to achieve state-of-the-art performance while preserving robustness and a lightweight architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLP-based forecasters are robust to noise but struggle with modeling long-range dependencies; there is a need to enhance long-range modeling without sacrificing efficiency or robustness.

Method: Decompose the series into trend and seasonal components. For the trend, apply Enhanced Frequency Attention to capture long-range dependencies via frequency-domain processing. For the seasonal component, use a CrossFilter Block to maintain robustness to noise and avoid robustness issues linked to standard attention mechanisms.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on test datasets. XLinear outperforms other MLP-based forecasters in capturing long-range dependencies while maintaining a lightweight and robust architecture.

Conclusion: XLinear effectively balances robustness, efficiency, and long-range modeling in MLP-based time series forecasting, offering broad applicability and improved performance over existing MLP-based approaches.

Abstract: Time series forecasters are widely used across various domains. Among them, MLP (multi-layer perceptron)-based forecasters have been proven to be more robust to noise compared to Transformer-based forecasters. However, MLP struggles to capture complex features, resulting in limitations on capturing long-range dependencies. To address this challenge, we propose XLinear, an MLP-based forecaster for long-range forecasting. Firstly, we decompose the time series into trend and seasonal components. For the trend component which contains long-range characteristics, we design Enhanced Frequency Attention (EFA) to capture long-term dependencies by leveraging frequency-domain operations. Additionally, a CrossFilter Block is proposed for the seasonal component to maintain the model's robustness to noise, avoiding the problems of low robustness often caused by attention mechanisms. Experimental results demonstrate that XLinear achieves state-of-the-art performance on test datasets. While keeping the lightweight architecture and high robustness of MLP-based models, our forecaster outperforms other MLP-based forecasters in capturing long-range dependencies.

</details>


### [276] [Alternating Reinforcement Learning with Contextual Rubric Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15646)
*Guangchen Lan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ARL-RR eliminates fixed scalarization by alternating rubric meta-classes, improving performance and training efficiency across model scales on HealthBench; reward aggregation induces variance contraction, and a lightweight search-based meta-class selector guides emphasis on critical objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLRR methods rely on scalarized, fixed-weight rewards that compress multi-dimensional rubric signals, failing to capture inter-dimensional correlations and robustly guiding learning. There is a need to preserve multi-dimensional rubric structure and dynamically emphasize objectives during training.

Method: Propose Alternating Reinforcement Learning with Rubric Rewards (ARL-RR) that optimizes one semantic rubric meta-class at a time. Introduce a lightweight, search-based adaptation procedure to dynamically select the next meta-class based on task performance. Provide theoretical insight that reward aggregation induces a variance contraction effect. Validate empirically on HealthBench with expert annotations across multiple model scales (1.7B–14B).

Result: ARL-RR uniformly outperforms scalarized methods in both model performance and training efficiency on HealthBench across model scales (1.7B, 4B, 8B, 14B). The approach demonstrates robustness to rubric design and provides a practical mechanism to focus learning on critical objectives via dynamic meta-class selection.

Conclusion: Alternating rubric-based reinforcement learning offers a principled solution to the limitations of fixed scalar rewards by preserving multi-dimensional rubric information and adaptively weighting objectives, yielding improved performance and efficiency with potential applicability beyond HealthBench.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Rubric Rewards (RLRR) is a framework that extends conventional reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and verifiable rewards (RLVR) by replacing scalar preference signals with structured, multi-dimensional, contextual rubric-based evaluations. However, existing approaches in RLRR are limited to linearly compressing vector rewards into a scalar reward with a fixed weightings, which is sensitive to artificial score design and fails to capture correlations among reward dimensions. To overcome the limitations of reward aggregation, this work proposes Alternating Reinforcement Learning with Rubric Rewards (ARL-RR), a framework that eliminates the need for a fixed scalarization by optimizing one semantic rubric meta-class at a time. Theoretically, we show that reward aggregation induces a variance contraction effect, which helps explain the performance gains. We further introduce a lightweight, search-based adaptation procedure that selects the next meta-class dynamically based on task performance, enabling the policy to emphasize critical objectives and thereby improve the model performance. Empirically, our experiments on the HealthBench dataset with experts annotations demonstrate that ARL-RR uniformly outperforms scalarized methods in both model performance and training efficiency across different model scales (1.7B, 4B, 8B, and 14B).

</details>


### [277] [Steering Frozen LLMs: Adaptive Social Alignment via Online Prompt Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15647)
*Zeyu Zhang,Xiangxiang Dai,Ziyi Han,Xutong Liu,John C. S. Lui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CCLUB: an inference-time governance framework for adaptive social alignment in LLMs, using a consensus-clustering LinUCB bandit with system-prompt routing; provides sublinear regret and empirical gains (~11% cumulative reward, ~14% suboptimality gap reduction).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static post-training alignment yields brittle safety under evolving jailbreaks and time-varying norms; real-world safety requires adaptive, inference-time controls without retraining.

Method: Introduce CCLUB, a unified framework combining consensus clustering with a LinUCB bandit to route system prompts. Data pooling is restricted to the intersection of utility and safety similarity graphs to prevent unsafe generalization.

Result: Theoretical: sublinear regret guarantee, near-optimal performance. Empirical: CCLUB beats baselines, 10.98% cumulative reward improvement, 14.42% reduction in average suboptimality gap.

Conclusion: Inference-time governance via CCLUB is effective for adaptive social alignment, balancing safety and utility with principled data sharing constraints and provable regret bounds.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are typically governed by post-training alignment (e.g., RLHF or DPO), which yields a largely static policy during deployment and inference. However, real-world safety is a full-lifecycle problem: static defenses degrade against evolving jailbreak behaviors, and fixed weights cannot adapt to pluralistic, time-varying safety norms. This motivates inference-time governance that steers behavior without costly retraining. To address this, we introduce the Consensus Clustering LinUCB Bandit (CCLUB), a unified framework for adaptive social alignment via system-prompt routing. CCLUB employs a conservative consensus clustering mechanism: it pools data only within the intersection of utility and safety similarity graphs, effectively preventing unsafe generalization across semantically proximal but risk-divergent contexts. Our theoretical analysis yields a sublinear regret guarantee, demonstrating near-optimal performance of CCLUB. Extensive experiments validate that CCLUB outperforms strong baselines, achieving a 10.98% improvement in cumulative reward and a 14.42% reduction in the average suboptimality gap.

</details>


### [278] [How to Achieve Prototypical Birth and Death for OOD Detection?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15650)
*Ningkang Peng,Qianfeng Yu,Xiaoqian Peng,Linjing Qian,Yafei Liu,Canran Xiao,Xinyu Lu,Tingyu Lu,Zhichao Zheng,Yanhui Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic prototype-based learning (PID) adjusts prototype counts during training through birth and death mechanisms, enabling adaptive capacity to capture intra-class substructures and improve OOD detection, achieving state-of-the-art results on CIFAR-100 (notably FPR95).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed, pre-defined prototype counts limit expressivity and fail to accommodate varying data complexity across classes and regions; adaptive prototype management can better represent intra-class substructures and tighten decision boundaries for OOD detection.

Method: Introduce two dynamic processes during training: (1) prototype birth adds new prototypes in underrepresented regions by measuring prototype overload and data complexity, (2) prototype death prunes prototypes with ambiguous or weak discriminability to tighten decision boundaries; these mechanisms yield a variable, data-driven number of prototypes and more compact, well-separated in-distribution embeddings.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows the PID method outperforms existing prototype-based and OOD detection methods, achieving state-of-the-art results on CIFAR-100, with strong improvements on the FPR95 metric indicating better false-positive rates at high true positive rates.

Conclusion: Adaptive prototype birth and death effectively tailor representation capacity to data complexity, improving ID embedding quality and OOD detection performance; the approach offers a general mechanism potentially extensible to other proto-type-based learning tasks.

Abstract: Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection is crucial for the secure deployment of machine learning models, and prototype-based learning methods are among the mainstream strategies for achieving OOD detection. Existing prototype-based learning methods generally rely on a fixed number of prototypes. This static assumption fails to adapt to the inherent complexity differences across various categories. Currently, there is still a lack of a mechanism that can adaptively adjust the number of prototypes based on data complexity. Inspired by the processes of cell birth and death in biology, we propose a novel method named PID (Prototype bIrth and Death) to adaptively adjust the prototype count based on data complexity. This method relies on two dynamic mechanisms during the training process: prototype birth and prototype death. The birth mechanism instantiates new prototypes in data regions with insufficient representation by identifying the overload level of existing prototypes, thereby meticulously capturing intra-class substructures. Conversely, the death mechanism reinforces the decision boundary by pruning prototypes with ambiguous class boundaries through evaluating their discriminability. Through birth and death, the number of prototypes can be dynamically adjusted according to the data complexity, leading to the learning of more compact and better-separated In-Distribution (ID) embeddings, which significantly enhances the capability to detect OOD samples. Experiments demonstrate that our dynamic method, PID, significantly outperforms existing methods on benchmarks such as CIFAR-100, achieving State-of-the-Art (SOTA) performance, especially on the FPR95 metric.

</details>


### [279] [A federated learning framework with knowledge graph and temporal transformer for early sepsis prediction in multi-center ICUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15651)
*Yue Chang,Guangsen Lin,Jyun Jie Chuang,Shunqi Liu,Xinkui Li,Yaozheng Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated learning with a medical knowledge graph and a temporal transformer, augmented by meta-learning, for privacy-preserving early sepsis prediction across hospitals; achieves AUC 0.956 on MIMIC-IV and eICU, outperforming centralized and standard FL baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data fragmentation across healthcare institutions and strict privacy constraints hinder accurate, generalizable sepsis prediction; need privacy-preserving, multi-center models that leverage domain knowledge and temporal dynamics.

Method: A federated learning framework integrated with a medical knowledge graph to encode structured clinical relationships; a temporal transformer to capture long-range temporal dependencies in ICU time-series; model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) for rapid local adaptation; evaluated on MIMIC-IV and eICU datasets.

Result: AUC of 0.956, representing a 22.4% improvement over centralized models and a 12.7% improvement over standard federated learning.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers a privacy-preserving, high-performing approach for multi-center early sepsis warning and demonstrates the value of combining FL, knowledge graphs, temporal transformers, and meta-learning in healthcare predictive modelling.

Abstract: The early prediction of sepsis in intensive care unit (ICU) patients is crucial for improving survival rates. However, the development of accurate predictive models is hampered by data fragmentation across healthcare institutions and the complex, temporal nature of medical data, all under stringent privacy constraints. To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework that uniquely integrates federated learning (FL) with a medical knowledge graph and a temporal transformer model, enhanced by meta-learning capabilities. Our approach enables collaborative model training across multiple hospitals without sharing raw patient data, thereby preserving privacy. The model leverages a knowledge graph to incorporate structured medical relationships and employs a temporal transformer to capture long-range dependencies in clinical time-series data. A model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) strategy is further incorporated to facilitate rapid adaptation of the global model to local data distributions. Evaluated on the MIMIC-IV and eICU datasets, our method achieves an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.956, which represents a 22.4% improvement over conventional centralized models and a 12.7% improvement over standard federated learning, demonstrating strong predictive capability for sepsis. This work presents a reliable and privacy-preserving solution for multi-center collaborative early warning of sepsis.

</details>


### [280] [Discovering the Hidden Role of Gini Index In Prompt-based Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15654)
*Ruixi Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gini index used as a diagnostic and optimization tool to detect and reduce class-accuracy disparities in prompt-based and multimodal classification; proposes a post-hoc, model-agnostic debiasing method that improves minority class performance across few-shot, zero-shot, and various domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-tailed class distributions yield high accuracy for dominant classes but poor performance for minority classes. Understanding and leveraging the Gini index can reveal and mitigate disparities in accuracy, enabling fairer, more balanced predictions in prompt-based and cross-modal classification tasks.

Method: Introduce and benchmark the Gini metric for real-world LLMs and vision models; conduct case analyses to show prevalence of relative accuracy imbalance across tasks; propose a post-hoc, model-agnostic bias mitigation method that optimizes according to Gini; evaluate on few-shot news, biomedical, and zero-shot image classification.

Result: Gini reveals persistent relative accuracy imbalance across both text and image classification tasks, regardless of dimensionality. The proposed mitigation method reduces both relative and absolute accuracy imbalances, lowers top-class dominance, and improves performance for the weakest classes.

Conclusion: Gini can serve as both a diagnostic measure of equity and an optimization objective for debiasing in prompt-based and multimodal classification; the proposed post-hoc, model-agnostic method demonstrates effective, cross-domain improvements.

Abstract: In classification tasks, the long-tailed minority classes usually offer the predictions that are most important. Yet these classes consistently exhibit low accuracies, whereas a few high-performing classes dominate the game. We pursue a foundational understanding of the hidden role of Gini Index as a tool for detecting and optimizing (debiasing) disparities in class accuracy, focusing on the case of prompt-based classification. We introduce the intuitions, benchmark Gini scores in real-world LLMs and vision models, and thoroughly discuss the insights of Gini not only as a measure of relative accuracy dominance but also as a direct optimization metric. Through rigorous case analyses, we first show that weak to strong relative accuracy imbalance exists in both prompt-based, text and image classification results and regardless of whether the classification is high-dimensional or low-dimensional. Then, we harness the Gini metric to propose a post-hoc model-agnostic bias mitigation method. Experimental results across few-shot news, biomedical, and zero-shot image classification show that our method significantly reduces both relative and absolute accuracy imbalances, minimizing top class relative dominance while elevating weakest classes.

</details>


### [281] [Beyond Reward Suppression: Reshaping Steganographic Communication Protocols in MARL via Dynamic Representational Circuit Breaking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15655)
*Liu Hung Ming*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DRCB is a novel optimization-substrate defense that detects and mitigates latent steganographic coordination in decentralized MARL by converting messages through a VQ-VAE into auditable statistics and computing an EMA Collusion Score to trigger escalating interventions, improving detection and stability without harming mean rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the AI-safety risk of steganographic collusion in MARL where coordination hidden in latent channels evades traditional behavioral or reward-based defenses; provide a pre-deployment auditing mechanism.

Method: DRCB, built on the AIM framework, uses a VQ-VAE bottleneck to map unobservable messages to auditable statistics. It monitors Jensen-Shannon Divergence drift, L2-norm codebook displacement, and Randomized Observer Pool accuracy to compute an EMA-based Collusion Score. Threshold breaches trigger four escalating interventions: (1) dynamic adaptation, (2) gradient-space penalty injected into the Advantage function A^pi, (3) temporal reward suppression, and (4) full substrate circuit breaking via codebook shuffling and optimizer state reset.

Result: On a Contextual Prisoner's Dilemma with MNIST labels, static monitoring fails (p = 0.3517). DRCB raises observer mean accuracy from 0.858 to 0.938 (+9.3%), reduces volatility by 43%, and preserves mean joint reward (p = 0.854). Analysis of 214,298 symbol samples reveals Semantic Degradation where high-frequency sequences collapse to zero entropy. Identifies a Transparency Paradox (surface determinism with residual tail capacity) in line with Goodhart’s Law. The work presents a task-agnostic path toward MICA-compliant pre-deployment auditing for autonomous systems.

Conclusion: DRCB provides a practical, task-agnostic methodology for auditing latent communications in MARL and enforcing staged interventions to curb covert coordination, advancing pre-deployment safety auditing (MICA) for autonomous systems.

Abstract: In decentralized Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL), steganographic collusion -- where agents develop private protocols to evade monitoring -- presents a critical AI safety threat. Existing defenses, limited to behavioral or reward layers, fail to detect coordination in latent communication channels. We introduce the Dynamic Representational Circuit Breaker (DRCB), an architectural defense operating at the optimization substrate.
  Building on the AI Mother Tongue (AIM) framework, DRCB utilizes a Vector Quantized Variational Autoencoder (VQ-VAE) bottleneck to convert unobservable messages into auditable statistical objects. DRCB monitors signals including Jensen-Shannon Divergence drift, L2-norm codebook displacement, and Randomized Observer Pool accuracy to compute an EMA-based Collusion Score. Threshold breaches trigger four escalating interventions: dynamic adaptation, gradient-space penalty injection into the Advantage function A^pi, temporal reward suppression, and full substrate circuit breaking via codebook shuffling and optimizer state reset.
  Experiments on a Contextual Prisoner's Dilemma with MNIST labels show that while static monitoring fails (p = 0.3517), DRCB improves observer mean accuracy from 0.858 to 0.938 (+9.3 percent) and reduces volatility by 43 percent, while preserving mean joint reward (p = 0.854). Analysis of 214,298 symbol samples confirms "Semantic Degradation," where high-frequency sequences converge to zero entropy, foreclosing complex steganographic encodings. We identify a "Transparency Paradox" where agents achieve surface-level determinism while preserving residual capacity in long-tail distributions, reflecting Goodhart's Law. This task-agnostic methodology provides a technical path toward MICA-compliant (Multi-Agent Internal Coupling Audit) pre-deployment auditing for autonomous systems.

</details>


### [282] [Attribution-Guided Model Rectification of Unreliable Neural Network Behaviors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15656)
*Peiyu Yang,Naveed Akhtar,Jiantong Jiang,Ajmal Mian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces attribution-guided rank-one model editing to rectify unreliable model behavior with minimal cleansed data, using layer-localization to identify and edit the most editable layer; applicable to neural Trojans, spurious correlations, and feature leakage, achieving effective edits with as little as one cleansed sample.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural networks exhibit unreliable behavior on non-robust features, especially under corruption. Traditional editing requires extensive data cleaning and retraining, incurring high computational and manual costs. A data-efficient, targeted rectification method is needed to preserve performance while correcting misbehaviors.

Method: Proposes a rank-one model editing framework guided by attributions to locate and rectify unreliable behavior. Introduces attribution-guided layer localization to quantify layer-wise editability and identify the layer most responsible for unreliabilities, enabling targeted edits that preserve overall performance with minimal cleansed data.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate effective correction of unreliabilities for neural Trojans, spurious correlations, and feature leakage. The method achieves the editing objective with as few as a single cleansed sample, reducing data and manual overhead.

Conclusion: Attribution-guided rank-one editing provides a data-efficient, targeted rectification of unreliable model behavior by localizing edits to the most editable layer, addressing a bottleneck in existing rectification approaches and offering practical benefits.

Abstract: The performance of neural network models deteriorates due to their unreliable behavior on non-robust features of corrupted samples. Owing to their opaque nature, rectifying models to address this problem often necessitates arduous data cleaning and model retraining, resulting in huge computational and manual overhead. In this work, we leverage rank-one model editing to establish an attribution-guided model rectification framework that effectively locates and corrects model unreliable behaviors. We first distinguish our rectification setting from existing model editing, yielding a formulation that corrects unreliable behavior while preserving model performance and reducing reliance on large budgets of cleansed samples. We further reveal a bottleneck of model rectifying arising from heterogeneous editability across layers. To target the primary source of misbehavior, we introduce an attribution-guided layer localization method that quantifies layer-wise editability and identifies the layer most responsible for unreliabilities. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in correcting unreliabilities observed for neural Trojans, spurious correlations and feature leakage. Our method shows remarkable performance by achieving its editing objective with as few as a single cleansed sample, which makes it appealing for practice.

</details>


### [283] [Spectral Edge Dynamics of Training Trajectories: Signal--Noise Geometry Across Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15678)
*Yongzhong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectral Edge Dynamics (SED) uses rolling-window SVD of parameter updates to locate a spectral edge between coherent optimization directions and noise. It reveals a universal three-phase pattern across models, with an adaptive effective rank and robustness to Johnson-Lindenstrauss projection; also linked to early signs of grokking in companion work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the intrinsic low-dimensional geometry of transformer training trajectories, identify when optimization dynamics concentrate on a small set of directions, and provide a scalable diagnostic across model sizes and tasks.

Method: Compute rolling-window singular value decomposition (SVD) of parameter update vectors during training. Define the spectral edge as the index k maximizing the ratio σ_k/σ_{k+1}, i.e., the boundary between coherent optimization directions and stochastic noise. Track the spectral gap and the effective rank over time. Apply to a 51M-parameter TinyStories model (4 seeds) and GPT-2 124M under distribution shift. Use Johnson–Lindenstrauss projection to reduce dimensionality (e.g., d = 10W) and check preservation of the spectral gap. Explore how the spectral geometry correlates with validation loss under different window sizes (lag flip). Reference companion work assessing grokking signals across modular arithmetic, Dyck languages, and SCAN.

Result: A universal three-phase pattern in the spectral edge emerges: rise, plateau, collapse. The effective rank k* is task/size dependent (k* = 2 for 51M; k* = 3 for 124M). The spectral gap persists under JL projection with small error (~5.7%). Lag between spectral directions and validation loss depends on window size, revealing a timescale of trajectory integration. The same spectral geometry provides early-warning signals of grokking, predicting generalization 600–1,700 steps before it occurs across multiple tasks.

Conclusion: SED offers a scalable, geometry-based diagnostic of training dynamics in transformers, linking low-rank spectral structure to generalization behavior and enabling early detection of grokking. The approach is compatible with large models via dimensionality reduction and holds promise for cross-task universality, though sensitivity to window size and interpretation of the spectral edge warrants further study.

Abstract: Despite hundreds of millions of parameters, transformer training trajectories evolve within only a few coherent directions. We introduce \emph{Spectral Edge Dynamics} (SED) to measure this structure: rolling-window SVD of parameter updates reveals a sharp boundary -- the \emph{spectral edge} -- between coherent optimization directions and stochastic noise, identified by the maximum consecutive singular value ratio $σ_k/σ_{k+1}$. Across a 51M-parameter TinyStories model (4~seeds) and GPT-2 124M under a distribution shift, the spectral edge exhibits a universal three-phase pattern (rise, plateau, collapse), signal rank adjusts with task complexity ($k^* = 2$ at 51M, $k^* = 3$ at 124M), and the directional coupling between spectral geometry and validation loss reverses with window size -- a \emph{lag flip} reflecting the timescale of trajectory integration. Johnson--Lindenstrauss projection to $d = 10W$ dimensions (e.g., $d = 100$ for $W = 10$) preserves the spectral gap within 5.7\%, making the framework applicable to models of arbitrary size. In companion work, the same spectral geometry provides early-warning signals of grokking -- predicting generalization 600--1{,}700 steps before it occurs across modular arithmetic, Dyck languages, and the SCAN benchmark.

</details>


### [284] [Flood Risk Follows Valleys, Not Grids: Graph Neural Networks for Flash Flood Susceptibility Mapping in Himachal Pradesh with Conformal Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15681)
*Paras Sharma,Swastika Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG
