TL;DR: A graph neural network leveraging watershed connectivity (GraphSAGE) outperforms pixel-based models for HP flood susceptibility mapping, with 0.978 AUC vs ~0.88 baselines; conformal prediction provides 90% coverage intervals; high-risk zones align with critical infrastructure; SAR label noise limits coverage in high-risk areas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-level risk maps ignore upstream-downstream flood propagation. Incorporating hydrological connectivity should improve predictive accuracy and spatial validity for flood susceptibility in Himachal Pradesh.

Method: Constructed a watershed connectivity graph (460 sub-watersheds, 1,700 directed edges) and trained GraphSAGE on 6-year Sentinel-1 SAR flood inventory (2018-2023; 3,000 events) plus 12 environmental variables at 30 m. Compared against four pixel-based models (RF, XGBoost, LightGBM, stacking). Evaluated with leave-one-basin-out spatial CV; used conformal prediction to produce 90% coverage intervals.

Result: GNN achieved AUC 0.978 ± 0.017, outperforming best baseline AUC 0.881 and HP benchmark 0.88. Connectivity adds predictive signal (ΔAUC = 0.097). High-susceptibility zones span 1,457 km of highways, 2,759 bridges, and 4 hydroelectric installations. Conformal intervals achieved 82.9% empirical coverage on held-out 2023 data; coverage lower in high-risk zones (45-59%) due to SAR label noise.

Conclusion: Accounting for river connectivity via GraphSAGE yields superior predictive performance and provides uncertainty-guided risk maps; remaining SAR label noise—especially in high-risk zones—limits coverage, suggesting focus on label quality and further integration of connectivity.

Abstract: Flash floods are the most destructive natural hazard in Himachal Pradesh (HP), India, causing over 400 fatalities and $1.2 billion in losses in the 2023 monsoon season alone. Existing risk maps treat every pixel independently, ignoring the basic fact that flooding upstream raises risk downstream. We address this with a Graph Neural Network (GraphSAGE) trained on a watershed connectivity graph (460 sub-watersheds, 1,700 directed edges), built from a six-year Sentinel-1 SAR flood inventory (2018-2023, 3,000 events) and 12 environmental variables at 30 m resolution. Four pixel-based ML models (RF, XGBoost, LightGBM, stacking ensemble) serve as baselines. All models are evaluated with leave-one-basin-out spatial cross-validation to avoid the 5-15% AUC inflation of random splits. Conformal prediction produces the first HP susceptibility maps with statistically guaranteed 90% coverage intervals.
  The GNN achieved AUC = 0.978 +/- 0.017, outperforming the best baseline (AUC = 0.881) and the published HP benchmark (AUC = 0.88). The +0.097 gain confirms that river connectivity carries predictive signal that pixel-based models miss. High-susceptibility zones overlap 1,457 km of highways (including 217 km of the Manali-Leh corridor), 2,759 bridges, and 4 major hydroelectric installations. Conformal intervals achieved 82.9% empirical coverage on the held-out 2023 test set; lower coverage in high-risk zones (45-59%) points to SAR label noise as a target for future work.

</details>


### [285] [Evidential Domain Adaptation for Remaining Useful Life Prediction with Incomplete Degradation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15687)
*Yubo Hou,Mohamed Ragab,Yucheng Wang,Min Wu,Abdulla Alseiari,Chee-Keong Kwoh,Xiaoli Li,Zhenghua Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EviAdapt proposes an evidential adaptation framework for RUL with incomplete target trajectories, enabling stage-wise degradation alignment and uncertainty-based cross-stage alignment to improve transfer without labeled target data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Incomplete degradation trajectories in the target domain create extrapolation challenges and misalignment when using global DA; varying operating conditions can alter degradation patterns even within the same stage.

Method: Segment source/target data into degradation stages by degradation rate; perform stage-wise alignment between corresponding stages; apply evidential learning to estimate uncertainty and align uncertainties across matched stages to handle diverse degradation patterns.

Result: The abstract does not report empirical results; it asserts that stage-wise alignment and uncertainty alignment address the two main limitations, implying improved DA performance for incomplete trajectories.

Conclusion: EviAdapt introduces stage-wise degradation segmentation and evidential uncertainty alignment to improve DA for RUL with incomplete trajectories; empirical validation and comparison to baselines are necessary to establish effectiveness.

Abstract: Accurate Remaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction without labeled target domain data is a critical challenge, and domain adaptation (DA) has been widely adopted to address it by transferring knowledge from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain. Despite its success, existing DA methods struggle significantly when faced with incomplete degradation trajectories in the target domain, particularly due to the absence of late degradation stages. This missing data introduces a key extrapolation challenge. When applied to such incomplete RUL prediction tasks, current DA methods encounter two primary limitations. First, most DA approaches primarily focus on global alignment, which can misaligns late degradation stage in the source domain with early degradation stage in the target domain. Second, due to varying operating conditions in RUL prediction, degradation patterns may differ even within the same degradation stage, resulting in different learned features. As a result, even if degradation stages are partially aligned, simple feature matching cannot fully align two domains. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel evidential adaptation approach called EviAdapt, which leverages evidential learning to enhance domain adaptation. The method first segments the source and target domain data into distinct degradation stages based on degradation rate, enabling stage-wise alignment that ensures samples from corresponding stages are accurately matched. To address the second limitation, we introduce an evidential uncertainty alignment technique that estimates uncertainty using evidential learning and aligns the uncertainty across matched stages.

</details>


### [286] [Transition Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15689)
*Chenrui Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new paradigm for flow modeling shifts from learning local velocity fields (requiring multi-step integration) to directly learning a global transition flow, enabling single-step or arbitrary-time generation and unifying with Mean Velocity Flow through a grounded theoretical link; experiments validate effectiveness and claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional flow matching methods rely on integrating local velocity fields over time, which is computationally costly and inflexible for variable-time generation. A transition-flow perspective promises a single-step or time-point–agnostic generation and a unified theory with Mean Velocity Flow.

Method: Introduce a global quantity—the transition flow—that encapsulates the evolution of the flow. Develop a mathematically grounded formulation connecting the local velocity field to the global mean velocity and show how the transition flow relates to Mean Velocity Flow. Implement models to learn the transition flow directly, enabling generation at a single step or at arbitrary future times.

Result: The approach demonstrates effectiveness through extensive experiments, confirming both practical performance gains and the proposed theoretical connections to Mean Velocity Flow.

Conclusion: The work offers a unified theoretical framework bridging Mean Velocity Flow and direct transition-flow learning, enabling efficient, flexible generation across time and enabling future work to explore broader applicability and deeper theoretical analysis.

Abstract: Mainstream flow matching methods typically focus on learning the local velocity field, which inherently requires multiple integration steps during generation. In contrast, Mean Velocity Flow models establish a relationship between the local velocity field and the global mean velocity, enabling the latter to be learned through a mathematically grounded formulation and allowing generation to be transferred to arbitrary future time points. In this work, we propose a new paradigm that directly learns the transition flow. As a global quantity, the transition flow naturally supports generation in a single step or at arbitrary time points. Furthermore, we demonstrate the connection between our approach and Mean Velocity Flow, establishing a unified theoretical perspective. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of our method and support our theoretical claims.

</details>


### [287] [Tackling Over-smoothing on Hypergraphs: A Ricci Flow-guided Neural Diffusion Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15696)
*Mengyao Zhou,Zhiheng Zhou,Xiao Han,Xingqin Qi,Guanghui Wang,Guiying Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A discrete Ricci-flow-guided hypergraph diffusion framework (RFHND) that regulates node-feature diffusion to mitigate over-smoothing, achieving state-of-the-art performance and robustness on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address over-smoothing and uncontrolled message passing in deep hypergraph neural networks. Discrete Ricci flow provides a principled geometric mechanism to regulate feature evolution on hypergraphs.

Method: Introduce RFHND, a PDE-based diffusion paradigm on hypergraphs guided by discrete Ricci flow. The method models continuous node-feature evolution and adaptively modulates diffusion rate using curvature to prevent feature homogenization, forming a new message-passing scheme for hypergraphs.

Result: RFHND significantly outperforms existing methods across multiple benchmark datasets and exhibits strong robustness, while effectively mitigating over-smoothing.

Conclusion: Integrating discrete Ricci flow into hypergraph neural diffusion offers principled control over information propagation, yielding higher-quality node representations and broad applicability to higher-order relational learning.

Abstract: Hypergraph neural networks (HGNNs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in modeling complex higher-order relationships. However, existing HGNNs often suffer from over-smoothing as the number of layers increases and lack effective control over message passing among nodes. Inspired by the theory of Ricci flow in differential geometry, we theoretically establish that introducing discrete Ricci flow into hypergraph structures can effectively regulate node feature evolution and thereby alleviate over-smoothing. Building on this insight, we propose Ricci Flow-guided Hypergraph Neural Diffusion(RFHND), a novel message passing paradigm for hypergraphs guided by discrete Ricci flow. Specifically, RFHND is based on a PDE system that describes the continuous evolution of node features on hypergraphs and adaptively regulates the rate of information diffusion at the geometric level, preventing feature homogenization and producing high-quality node representations. Experimental results show that RFHND significantly outperforms existing methods across multiple benchmark datasets and demonstrates strong robustness, while also effectively mitigating over-smoothing.

</details>


### [288] [Mastering the Minority: An Uncertainty-guided Multi-Expert Framework for Challenging-tailed Sequence Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15708)
*Ye Wang,Zixuan Wu,Lifeng Shen,Jiang Xie,Xiaoling Wang,Hong Yu,Guoyin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UME tackles imbalanced sequential learning via three innovations: Ensemble LoRA for parameter efficiency, DST-guided Sequential Specialization for tail classes, and uncertainty-guided fusion using DST to resolve expert conflicts, achieving state-of-the-art results on four hierarchical text classification datasets with notable parameter reductions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imbalanced distributions in sequential learning cause minority classes to be underrepresented. Traditional Mixture-of-Experts suffers from parameter inefficiency, poor expert specialization, and unresolved conflicts. A scalable, uncertainty-aware approach is needed to improve minority-class performance while controlling model size.

Method: 1) Ensemble LoRA for parameter-efficient modeling; 2) Sequential Specialization guided by Dempster-Shafer Theory to focus on challenging-tailed classes; 3) Uncertainty-Guided Fusion using DST certainties to weight expert opinions and select the most confident expert for the final prediction. Evaluated on four public hierarchical text classification datasets.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across four public hierarchical text classification datasets. Reports up to 17.97% gain over the best baseline on individual categories and up to 10.32% reduction in trainable parameters.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-guided coordination among multiple experts is a principled and effective strategy for addressing challenging-tailed sequence learning, enabling strong minority-class performance with parameter efficiency.

Abstract: Imbalanced data distribution remains a critical challenge in sequential learning, leading models to easily recognize frequent categories while failing to detect minority classes adequately. The Mixture-of-Experts model offers a scalable solution, yet its application is often hindered by parameter inefficiency, poor expert specialization, and difficulty in resolving prediction conflicts. To Master the Minority classes effectively, we propose the Uncertainty-based Multi-Expert fusion network (UME) framework. UME is designed with three core innovations: First, we employ Ensemble LoRA for parameter-efficient modeling, significantly reducing the trainable parameter count. Second, we introduce Sequential Specialization guided by Dempster-Shafer Theory (DST), which ensures effective specialization on the challenging-tailed classes. Finally, an Uncertainty-Guided Fusion mechanism uses DST's certainty measures to dynamically weigh expert opinions, resolving conflicts by prioritizing the most confident expert for reliable final predictions. Extensive experiments across four public hierarchical text classification datasets demonstrate that UME achieves state-of-the-art performance. We achieve a performance gain of up to 17.97\% over the best baseline on individual categories, while reducing trainable parameters by up to 10.32\%. The findings highlight that uncertainty-guided expert coordination is a principled strategy for addressing challenging-tailed sequence learning. Our code is available at https://github.com/CQUPTWZX/Multi-experts.

</details>


### [289] [Embedding-Aware Feature Discovery: Bridging Latent Representations and Interpretable Features in Event Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15713)
*Artem Sakhno,Ivan Sergeev,Alexey Shestov,Omar Zoloev,Elizaveta Kovtun,Gleb Gusev,Andrey Savchenko,Maksim Makarenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Embedding-Aware Feature Discovery (EAFD) unifies pretrained event-sequence embeddings with a self-reflective LLM-driven feature-generation agent to iteratively discover and refine features from raw event sequences using alignment and complementarity criteria; it outperforms embedding-only and feature-based baselines on open-source and industrial benchmarks, achieving up to +5.8% gains and establishing new state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a persistent disconnect between learned embeddings and feature-based pipelines in industrial temporal event data. Embeddings offer powerful representations but often lack interpretability, robustness under limited supervision, and strict latency guarantees needed in production. A unified framework to discover features that align with and complement embeddings can improve performance while preserving practical advantages of feature-based pipelines.

Method: EAFD couples pretrained event-sequence embeddings with a self-reflective LLM-driven feature generation agent. It iteratively discovers, evaluates, and refines features directly from raw event sequences based on two complementary criteria: alignment (explain information captured by embeddings) and complementarity (identify predictive signals missing from embeddings). The process alternates between proposing candidate features, evaluating them against both criteria, and refining the feature set.

Result: Empirical evaluation on both open-source and industrial transaction benchmarks shows that EAFD consistently outperforms embedding-only and feature-based baselines. It achieves relative gains up to 5.8 percentage points over state-of-the-art pretrained embeddings and achieves new state-of-the-art performance across event-sequence datasets.

Conclusion: EAFD provides a practical and effective bridge between learned embeddings and traditional feature pipelines for industrial temporal event data, delivering improved predictive performance while leveraging the strengths of both approaches. The two-criteria evaluation (alignment and complementarity) guides the discovery of features that are both explainable in terms of existing embeddings and capable of capturing additional predictive signals.

Abstract: Industrial financial systems operate on temporal event sequences such as transactions, user actions, and system logs. While recent research emphasizes representation learning and large language models, production systems continue to rely heavily on handcrafted statistical features due to their interpretability, robustness under limited supervision, and strict latency constraints. This creates a persistent disconnect between learned embeddings and feature-based pipelines. We introduce Embedding-Aware Feature Discovery (EAFD), a unified framework that bridges this gap by coupling pretrained event-sequence embeddings with a self-reflective LLM-driven feature generation agent. EAFD iteratively discovers, evaluates, and refines features directly from raw event sequences using two complementary criteria: \emph{alignment}, which explains information already encoded in embeddings, and \emph{complementarity}, which identifies predictive signals missing from them. Across both open-source and industrial transaction benchmarks, EAFD consistently outperforms embedding-only and feature-based baselines, achieving relative gains of up to $+5.8\%$ over state-of-the-art pretrained embeddings, resulting in new state-of-the-art performance across event-sequence datasets.

</details>


### [290] [Meta-TTRL: A Metacognitive Framework for Self-Improving Test-Time Reinforcement Learning in Unified Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15724)
*Lit Sin Tan,Junzhe Chen,Xiaolong Fu,Lichen Ma,Junshi Huang,Jianzhong Shi,Yan Li,Lijie Wen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Meta-TTRL: a metacognitive test-time reinforcement learning framework for unified multimodal models (UMMs) in text-to-image generation that optimizes test-time parameters using intrinsic monitoring signals to achieve self-improvement and capability-level gains across prompts, demonstrated on Janus-Pro-7B, BAGEL, and Qwen-Image with improvements on compositional reasoning and T2I benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-time scaling (TTS) methods rely on search or sampling and typically yield instance-level gains, failing to learn from prior inferences or accumulate knowledge across similar prompts, which limits capability-level improvements at test time.

Method: Meta-TTRL conducts test-time parameter optimization guided by model-intrinsic monitoring signals derived from the meta-knowledge of unified multimodal models, enabling metacognitive self-improvement by aligning monitoring signals with the optimization regime.

Result: Extensive experiments show that Meta-TTRL generalizes across three representative UMMs (Janus-Pro-7B, BAGEL, Qwen-Image) and achieves significant gains on compositional reasoning tasks and multiple T2I benchmarks with limited data.

Conclusion: This work provides the first comprehensive analysis of test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) for text-to-image generation in UMMs; a key insight is metacognitive synergy, where monitoring signals align with the model’s optimization dynamics to enable self-improvement at test time.

Abstract: Existing test-time scaling (TTS) methods for unified multimodal models (UMMs) in text-to-image (T2I) generation primarily rely on search or sampling strategies that produce only instance-level improvements, limiting the ability to learn from prior inferences and accumulate knowledge across similar prompts. To overcome these limitations, we propose Meta-TTRL, a metacognitive test-time reinforcement learning framework. Meta-TTRL performs test-time parameter optimization guided by model-intrinsic monitoring signals derived from the meta-knowledge of UMMs, achieving self-improvement and capability-level improvement at test time. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Meta-TTRL generalizes well across three representative UMMs, including Janus-Pro-7B, BAGEL, and Qwen-Image, achieving significant gains on compositional reasoning tasks and multiple T2I benchmarks with limited data. We provide the first comprehensive analysis to investigate the potential of test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) for T2I generation in UMMs. Our analysis further reveals a key insight underlying effective TTRL: metacognitive synergy, where monitoring signals align with the model's optimization regime to enable self-improvement.

</details>


### [291] [OMNIFLOW: A Physics-Grounded Multimodal Agent for Generalized Scientific Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15797)
*Hao Wu,Yongheng Zhang,Yuan Gao,Fan Xu,Fan Zhang,Ruobing Xie,Ruijian Gou,Yuxuan Liang,Xiaomeng Huang,Xian Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OMNIFLOW introduces a neuro-symbolic, physics-grounded framework for LLMs to handle PDE-driven spatiotemporal dynamics without domain-specific fine-tuning. It uses Semantic-Symbolic Alignment to map flow tensors to topological linguistic descriptors and a Physics-Guided Chain-of-Thought with dynamic constraint injection for interpretable, physically consistent reasoning. It shows strong zero-shot and few-shot generalization on turbulence, Navier-Stokes, and weather benchmarks, with transparent reasoning reports.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with continuous spatiotemporal PDE dynamics and often generate non-physical hallucinations; fine-tuning is costly and harms cross-domain generalization and interpretability. A cross-domain, interpretable approach that grounds frozen LLMs in fundamental physical laws is needed.

Method: OMNIFLOW combines a Semantic-Symbolic Alignment mechanism that projects high-dimensional flow tensors into topological linguistic descriptors, enabling perception of physical structures rather than raw pixels. It also employs a Physics-Guided Chain-of-Thought workflow that injects dynamic constraints (e.g., mass conservation) and performs iterative reflexive verification to guide reasoning.

Result: Empirical evaluation across microscopic turbulence, theoretical Navier-Stokes equations, and macroscopic weather forecasting shows OMNIFLOW outperforms traditional deep learning baselines in zero-shot generalization and few-shot adaptation. It provides transparent, physically consistent reasoning reports, enabling interpretable scientific reasoning rather than opaque fitting.

Conclusion: OMNIFLOW demonstrates that grounding frozen multimodal LLMs with physics-based semantics and constraint-guided CoT yields strong generalization and interpretable, physically consistent reasoning across diverse PDE-based domains.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional logical reasoning capabilities but frequently struggle with the continuous spatiotemporal dynamics governed by Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), often resulting in non-physical hallucinations. Existing approaches typically resort to costly, domain-specific fine-tuning, which severely limits cross-domain generalization and interpretability. To bridge this gap, we propose OMNIFLOW, a neuro-symbolic architecture designed to ground frozen multimodal LLMs in fundamental physical laws without requiring domain-specific parameter updates. OMNIFLOW introduces a novel \textit{Semantic-Symbolic Alignment} mechanism that projects high-dimensional flow tensors into topological linguistic descriptors, enabling the model to perceive physical structures rather than raw pixel values. Furthermore, we construct a Physics-Guided Chain-of-Thought (PG-CoT) workflow that orchestrates reasoning through dynamic constraint injection (e.g., mass conservation) and iterative reflexive verification. We evaluate OMNIFLOW on a comprehensive benchmark spanning microscopic turbulence, theoretical Navier-Stokes equations, and macroscopic global weather forecasting. Empirical results demonstrate that OMNIFLOW significantly outperforms traditional deep learning baselines in zero-shot generalization and few-shot adaptation tasks. Crucially, it offers transparent, physically consistent reasoning reports, marking a paradigm shift from black-box fitting to interpretable scientific reasoning.

</details>


### [292] [Time-Aware Prior Fitted Networks for Zero-Shot Forecasting with Exogenous Variables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15802)
*Andres Potapczynski,Ravi Kiran Selvam,Tatiana Konstantinova,Shankar Ramasubramanian,Malcolm Wolff,Kin G. Olivares,Ruijun Ma,Mengfei Cao,Michael W. Mahoney,Andrew Gordon Wilson,Boris N. Oreshkin,Dmitry Efimov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Time-aware prior-data fitted network (ApolloPFN) that natively uses exogenous covariates for time series forecasting; uses synthetic data generation and time-aware architecture to outperform univariate PFNs and current TS foundation models on benchmarks with exogenous information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exogenous signals and temporal context drive many real-world time series; ignoring them degrades forecasting performance. Existing PFNs are tabular/univariate or time-agnostic, limiting effectiveness on time series with exogenous drivers; a time-aware PFN that leverages exogenous covariates is needed.

Method: Two-pronged approach: (i) synthetic data generation tailored to correct failure modes when applying tabular PFNs to time series; (ii) time-aware architectural modifications that embed inductive biases for temporal context and allow native incorporation of exogenous covariates. Demonstrates on benchmarks (e.g., M5, electric price forecasting) with exogenous information.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on benchmarks containing exogenous information, outperforming existing time series foundation models by leveraging exogenous covariates and temporal inductive biases.

Conclusion: Shows the importance and effectiveness of a time-aware PFN with exogenous covariates for forecasting; suggests broader applicability and potential for improved performance by combining synthetic data strategies with tailored architectures.

Abstract: In many time series forecasting settings, the target time series is accompanied by exogenous covariates, such as promotions and prices in retail demand; temperature in energy load; calendar and holiday indicators for traffic or sales; and grid load or fuel costs in electricity pricing. Ignoring these exogenous signals can substantially degrade forecasting accuracy, particularly when they drive spikes, discontinuities, or regime and phase changes in the target series. Most current time series foundation models (e.g., Chronos, Sundial, TimesFM, TimeMoE, TimeLLM, and LagLlama) ignore exogenous covariates and make forecasts solely from the numerical time series history, thereby limiting their performance. In this paper, we develop ApolloPFN, a prior-data fitted network (PFN) that is time-aware (unlike prior PFNs) and that natively incorporates exogenous covariates (unlike prior univariate forecasters). Our design introduces two major advances: (i) a synthetic data generation procedure tailored to resolve the failure modes that arise when tabular (non-temporal) PFNs are applied to time series; and (ii) time-aware architectural modifications that embed inductive biases needed to exploit the time series context. We demonstrate that ApolloPFN achieves state-of-the-art results across benchmarks, such as M5 and electric price forecasting, that contain exogenous information.

</details>


### [293] [Mask Is What DLLM Needs: A Masked Data Training Paradigm for Diffusion LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15803)
*Linrui Ma,Yufei Cui,Kai Han,Yunhe Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces an information-density aware, noise-scheduler for discrete diffusion LMs, using information-dense hubs and complementary priority masking to separate reasoning and syntax, yielding ~4% average gains on Code/Math tasks and mitigating contextual collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard uniform noise schedulers ignore the non-uniform information density in real-world sequences, wasting optimization on low-density regions and under-optimizing high-density pivot points.

Method: Identify information-dense hubs in sequences and apply Complementary Priority Masking to split a training instance into reasoning and syntax samples, enabling a decoupled, mutually reinforcing learning process during block diffusion training.

Result: Achieves about 4% average accuracy improvement across four Code/Math reasoning benchmarks, outperforming uniform baselines; mechanistic analyses show probabilistic priority masking mitigates contextual collapse; dataset provided for replication.

Conclusion: Density-aware, masked data training for Diffusion LMs is a promising, low-cost paradigm for unlocking reasoning potential and could generalize to broader diffusion-based NLP tasks; dataset available at HuggingFace for replication.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models offer global context awareness and flexible parallel generation. However, uniform random noise schedulers in standard DLLM training overlook the highly non-uniform information density inherent in real-world sequences. This wastes optimization resources on low-density structural glues while leaving high-density logical pivot points severely under-optimized. To address this, we propose an Information Density Driven Smart Noise Scheduler. By extracting information-dense hubs and applying Complementary Priority Masking, our method decouples a single training instance into mutually reinforcing reasoning and syntax samples, forcing the model to master both logical deduction and foundational sequence structure. Experiments demonstrate that our approach improves average accuracy by ~4\% across four Code and Math reasoning benchmarks, significantly outperforming uniform baselines. Mechanistic analyses further reveal that probabilistic priority masking effectively mitigates contextual collapse during block diffusion training. Overall, this density-aware strategy efficiently unlocks the reasoning potential of diffusion language models at minimal annotation cost, emerging as a promising new masked data training paradigm for Diffusion LLMs. Our processed dataset can be found at https://huggingface.co/datasets/malr07/opc-sft-stage2-dense-extracted.

</details>


### [294] [Longitudinal Risk Prediction in Mammography with Privileged History Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15814)
*Banafsheh Karimian,Alexis Guichemerre,Soufiane Belharbi,Natacha Gillet,Luke McCaffrey,Mohammadhadi Shateri,Eric Granger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Privileged History Distillation trains horizon-specific teachers on full longitudinal mammography history and distills their prognostic signals into a student model that uses only the current exam at inference, enabling long-horizon risk prediction without prior exams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world longitudinal risk models suffer when prior exams are missing; enabling effective deployment without complete histories.

Method: A privileged multi-teacher distillation framework: horizon-specific teachers are trained on full history; the student learns from reconstructed histories derived from the current exam; distillation aligns horizon-specific risk cues to the student.

Result: Time-dependent AUC across horizons shows the proposed method substantially improves no-history baselines and matches full-history models for long horizons on CSAW-CC.

Conclusion: Privileged history distillation offers a practical approach to leverage rich longitudinal data during training to produce deployable models that perform well with only a single exam at inference.

Abstract: Breast cancer remains a leading cause of cancer-related mortality worldwide. Longitudinal mammography risk prediction models improve multi-year breast cancer risk prediction based on prior screening exams. However, in real-world clinical practice, longitudinal histories are often incomplete, irregular, or unavailable due to missed screenings, first-time examinations, heterogeneous acquisition schedules, or archival constraints. The absence of prior exams degrades the performance of longitudinal risk models and limits their practical applicability. While substantial longitudinal history is available during training, prior exams are commonly absent at test time. In this paper, we address missing history at inference time and propose a longitudinal risk prediction method that uses mammography history as privileged information during training and distills its prognostic value into a student model that only requires the current exam at inference time. The key idea is a privileged multi-teacher distillation scheme with horizon-specific teachers: each teacher is trained on the full longitudinal history to specialize in one prediction horizon, while the student receives only a reconstructed history derived from the current exam. This allows the student to inherit horizon-dependent longitudinal risk cues without requiring prior screening exams at deployment. Our new Privileged History Distillation (PHD) method is validated on a large longitudinal mammography dataset with multi-year cancer outcomes, CSAW-CC, comparing full-history and no-history baselines to their distilled counterparts. Using time-dependent AUC across horizons, our privileged history distillation method markedly improves the performance of long-horizon prediction over no-history models and is comparable to that of full-history models, while using only the current exam at inference time.

</details>


### [295] [Hypothesis Class Determines Explanation: Why Accurate Models Disagree on Feature Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15821)
*Thackshanaramana B*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prediction-equivalent models can yield substantially different explanations; the agreement on feature attributions depends on the hypothesis class, forming an 'Explanation Lottery' where cross-class pairs show low agreement despite identical predictions. A post-hoc diagnostic, the Explanation Reliability Score R(x), is proposed to predict explanation stability across architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the common assumption that identical predictive behavior guarantees equivalent explanations, and to understand how model class affects attribution, with implications for auditing and regulation.

Method: Large-scale empirical study across 24 datasets and multiple model classes. Measure agreement of feature attributions for identical data splits, analyze within-class versus cross-class agreement, and assess structural drivers. Provide a theoretical result showing the Agreement Gap persists with data-generating process interaction structures. Propose a post-hoc diagnostic, the Explanation Reliability Score R(x).

Result: Found strong within-class agreement and substantially reduced cross-class agreement, often near or below lottery thresholds. Identified hypothesis class as the structural driver of the phenomenon (Explanation Lottery). The Agreement Gap persists under interaction structures. The Explanation Reliability Score R(x) can predict stability of explanations without retraining. Demonstrated that deployment choices (model class) affect which features are deemed responsible.

Conclusion: Model selection is not explanation-neutral; the chosen hypothesis class determines attributional explanations. This motivates diagnostic tools and cautions in auditing/regulation, as identical predictions do not guarantee stable or transferable explanations across model families.

Abstract: The assumption that prediction-equivalent models produce equivalent explanations underlies many practices in explainable AI, including model selection, auditing, and regulatory evaluation. In this work, we show that this assumption does not hold. Through a large-scale empirical study across 24 datasets and multiple model classes, we find that models with identical predictive behavior can produce substantially different feature attributions. This disagreement is highly structured: models within the same hypothesis class exhibit strong agreement, while cross-class pairs (e.g., tree-based vs. linear) trained on identical data splits show substantially reduced agreement, consistently near or below the lottery threshold. We identify hypothesis class as the structural driver of this phenomenon, which we term the Explanation Lottery. We theoretically show that the resulting Agreement Gap persists under interaction structure in the data-generating process. This structural finding motivates a post-hoc diagnostic, the Explanation Reliability Score R(x), which predicts when explanations are stable across architectures without additional training. Our results demonstrate that model selection is not explanation-neutral: the hypothesis class chosen for deployment can determine which features are attributed responsibility for a decision.

</details>


### [296] [When Stability Fails: Hidden Failure Modes Of LLMS in Data-Constrained Scientific Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15840)
*Nazia Riasat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a controlled behavioral evaluation framework for LLMs in data-constrained scientific tasks, separating stability, correctness, prompt sensitivity, and output validity, and shows stability alone does not ensure alignment with statistical ground truth; emphasizes ground-truth validation and output validity checks in automated scientific workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation often prioritizes stability/reproducibility, which can mask systematic deviations from ground truth. In scientific decision tasks with limited data, ensuring correctness and valid outputs is crucial.

Method: Propose a four-dimension evaluation framework (stability, correctness, prompt sensitivity, output validity) and apply it to a statistical gene prioritization task derived from differential expression analysis. The study varies prompt regimes (strict/relaxed significance thresholds, borderline rankings, minor wording changes) and tests multiple LLMs using fixed statistical inputs; assesses run-to-run stability, correctness to ground truth, prompt sensitivity, and presence of invalid outputs.

Result: LLMs can exhibit near-perfect run-to-run stability yet diverge from statistical ground truth, over-select under relaxed thresholds, react sharply to minor prompt wording changes, and generate syntactically plausible gene identifiers absent from the input. Stability records robustness but does not guarantee agreement with ground truth in structured scientific tasks.

Conclusion: Explicit ground-truth validation and output validity checks are essential when deploying LLMs in automated or semi-automated scientific workflows.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as decision-support tools in data-constrained scientific workflows, where correctness and validity are critical. However, evaluation practices often emphasize stability or reproducibility across repeated runs. While these properties are desirable, stability alone does not guar- antee agreement with statistical ground truth when such references are available. We introduce a controlled behavioral evaluation framework that explicitly sep- arates four dimensions of LLM decision-making: stability, correctness, prompt sensitivity, and output validity under fixed statistical inputs. We evaluate multi- ple LLMs using a statistical gene prioritization task derived from differential ex- pression analysis across prompt regimes involving strict and relaxed significance thresholds, borderline ranking scenarios, and minor wording variations. Our ex- periments show that LLMs can exhibit near-perfect run-to-run stability while sys- tematically diverging from statistical ground truth, over-selecting under relaxed thresholds, responding sharply to minor prompt wording changes, or producing syntactically plausible gene identifiers absent from the input table. Although sta- bility reflects robustness across repeated runs, it does not guarantee agreement with statistical ground truth in structured scientific decision tasks. These findings highlight the importance of explicit ground-truth validation and output validity checks when deploying LLMs in automated or semi-automated scientific work- flows.

</details>


### [297] [Informationally Compressive Anonymization: Non-Degrading Sensitive Input Protection for Privacy-Preserving Supervised Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15842)
*Jeremy J Samuelson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Informationally Compressive Anonymization (ICA) and the VEIL framework, a privacy-preserving ML approach that achieves strong non-invertibility via architectural and information-theoretic design, enabling high-performance ML without DP/HE overhead and with post-quantum security.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy, security, and regulatory risks in modern ML with sensitive data. DP and Homomorphic Encryption incur performance loss, complexity, or overhead; need architecture-level privacy guarantees that preserve utility.

Method: Introduce ICA by embedding a supervised, multi-objective encoder in a trusted Source Environment to transform raw inputs into low-dimensional, task-aligned latent representations. Export only irreversibly anonymized vectors to untrusted Training and Inference Environments. Provide topological and information-theoretic proofs of non-invertibility; show divergence of attacker conditional entropy and vanishing reconstruction probability in realistic deployments. VEIL enforces strict trust boundaries, supports scalable multi-region deployment, and avoids gradient clipping, noise budgets, or encryption at inference.

Result: The encodings are structurally non-invertible with theoretically grounded guarantees. Inversion is logically impossible; conditional entropy over original data diverges, driving reconstruction probability to zero. Predictive utility is preserved, enabling low-latency, high-performance ML without typical privacy-induced trade-offs.

Conclusion: VEIL and ICA establish a privacy-by-design, secure-by-construction foundation for enterprise ML that remains performant and robust against post-quantum threats, with strong regulatory alignment and scalable deployment across regions.

Abstract: Modern machine learning systems increasingly rely on sensitive data, creating significant privacy, security, and regulatory risks that existing privacy-preserving machine learning (ppML) techniques, such as Differential Privacy (DP) and Homomorphic Encryption (HE), address only at the cost of degraded performance, increased complexity, or prohibitive computational overhead. This paper introduces Informationally Compressive Anonymization (ICA) and the VEIL architecture, a privacy-preserving ML framework that achieves strong privacy guarantees through architectural and mathematical design rather than noise injection or cryptography. ICA embeds a supervised, multi-objective encoder within a trusted Source Environment to transform raw inputs into low-dimensional, task-aligned latent representations, ensuring that only irreversibly anonymized vectors are exported to untrusted Training and Inference Environments. The paper rigorously proves that these encodings are structurally non-invertible using topological and information-theoretic arguments, showing that inversion is logically impossible, even under idealized attacker assumptions, and that, in realistic deployments, the attackers conditional entropy over the original data diverges, driving reconstruction probability to zero. Unlike prior autoencoder-based ppML approaches, ICA preserves predictive utility by aligning representation learning with downstream supervised objectives, enabling low-latency, high-performance ML without gradient clipping, noise budgets, or encryption at inference time. The VEIL architecture enforces strict trust boundaries, supports scalable multi-region deployment, and naturally aligns with privacy-by-design regulatory frameworks, establishing a new foundation for enterprise ML that is secure, performant, and safe by construction, even in the face of post-quantum threats.

</details>


### [298] [FlashSampling: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15854)
*Tomas Ruiz,Zhen Qin,Yifan Zhang,Xuyang Shen,Yiran Zhong,Mengdi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes FlashSampling, an exact sampling primitive fused into the LM-head matmul to avoid materializing logits, achieving speedups up to ~19% in end-to-end vLLM workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-vocabulary sampling is bandwidth- and memory-bound due to logits materialization and postprocessing; there is a need for an exact, memory-efficient solution that integrates into the compute path.

Method: Tile-based on-chip computation of logits with per-tile Gumbel noise, per-row per-tile argmax, followed by a small reduction across tiles. The logits tensor is never materialized in memory. Grouped/hierarchical variants support online and tensor-parallel settings and preserve exactness via partitioned argmax and hierarchical factorization of the categorical distribution.

Result: Kernel-level decode workloads see speedups on diverse GPUs (H100, H200, B200, B300). End-to-end vLLM experiments report up to 19% reduction in time per output token for the tested models, while maintaining exact sampling.

Conclusion: Exact sampling can be embedded into the matmul epilogue, turning a bandwidth-bound postprocessing step into a lightweight fused kernel. The approach is portable across hardware and parallelism strategies and achieves meaningful latency reductions.

Abstract: Sampling from a categorical distribution is mathematically simple, but in large-vocabulary decoding, it often triggers extra memory traffic and extra kernels after the LM head. We present FlashSampling, an exact sampling primitive that fuses sampling into the LM-head matmul and never materializes the logits tensor in HBM. The method is simple: compute logits tile-by-tile on chip, add Gumbel noise, keep only one maximizer per row and per vocabulary tile, and finish with a small reduction over tiles. The fused tiled kernel is exact because $\argmax$ decomposes over a partition; grouped variants for online and tensor-parallel settings are exact by hierarchical factorization of the categorical distribution. Across H100, H200, B200, and B300 GPUs, FlashSampling speeds up kernel-level decode workloads, and in end-to-end vLLM experiments, it reduces time per output token by up to $19%$ on the models we test. These results show that exact sampling, with no approximation, can be integrated into the matmul itself, turning a bandwidth-bound postprocessing step into a lightweight epilogue. Project Page: https://github.com/FlashSampling/FlashSampling.

</details>


### [299] [Evaluating Black-Box Vulnerabilities with Wasserstein-Constrained Data Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15867)
*Adriana Laurindo Monteiro,Jean-Michel Loubes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a Wasserstein-based projection of input distributions to study ML model sensitivity and explainability under distributional shifts; provides convergence guarantees and demonstrates it on regression and classification datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of explainable models and robustness in ML by analyzing how ML responses change when input distributions vary; leverage Optimal Transport to obtain the closest distribution satisfying a constraint and assess its effect on model behavior.

Method: Formulate the problem as finding the Wasserstein-closest distribution to the empirical input distribution that satisfies a given constraint; analyze the effect of this projection on model outputs; prove convergence properties of the projected distribution; validate with real-world regression and classification datasets.

Result: Identification of the Wasserstein-projected distribution satisfying the constraint and its impact on model behavior; established convergence results for the projected distribution; empirical demonstrations on real-world datasets in both regression and classification tasks.

Conclusion: Optimal Transport-based projection offers a principled, theoretically-grounded tool to study model explainability and robustness under input distribution shifts, with applicability to both regression and classification and accompanying convergence guarantees.

Abstract: The massive use of Machine Learning (ML) tools in industry comes with critical challenges, such as the lack of explainable models and the use of black-box algorithms. We address this issue by applying Optimal Transport theory in the analysis of responses of ML models to variations in the distribution of input variables. We find the closest distribution, in the Wasserstein sense, that satisfies a given constraintt and examine its impact on model behavior. Furthermore, we establish convergence results for this projected distribution and demonstrate our approach using examples and real-world datasets in both regression and classification settings.

</details>


### [300] [Counteractive RL: Rethinking Core Principles for Efficient and Scalable Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15871)
*Ezgi Korkmaz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretically-grounded learning paradigm using counteractive actions to accelerate RL in high-dimensional MDPs, achieving faster, more sample-efficient training with no extra computation, validated on Arcade Learning Environment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional MDPs cause exponential growth in state spaces, creating a trade-off between computational cost and policy performance. There is a need for efficient, scalable, and accelerated learning methods that leverage interactions with the environment.

Method: Introduce a novel paradigm based on experiences obtained through counteractive actions, with a theoretically-founded basis; aim for zero additional computational complexity while accelerating training.

Result: Empirical results on the Arcade Learning Environment show significant performance gains and substantial sample efficiency in high-dimensional state representations, consistent with theoretical analysis.

Conclusion: The proposed counteractive-action-based learning paradigm provides theoretically-grounded, scalable acceleration for RL in high-dimensional MDPs without increasing computational burden.

Abstract: Following the pivotal success of learning strategies to win at tasks, solely by interacting with an environment without any supervision, agents have gained the ability to make sequential decisions in complex MDPs. Yet, reinforcement learning policies face exponentially growing state spaces in high dimensional MDPs resulting in a dichotomy between computational complexity and policy success. In our paper we focus on the agent's interaction with the environment in a high-dimensional MDP during the learning phase and we introduce a theoretically-founded novel paradigm based on experiences obtained through counteractive actions. Our analysis and method provide a theoretical basis for efficient, effective, scalable and accelerated learning, and further comes with zero additional computational complexity while leading to significant acceleration in training. We conduct extensive experiments in the Arcade Learning Environment with high-dimensional state representation MDPs. The experimental results further verify our theoretical analysis, and our method achieves significant performance increase with substantial sample-efficiency in high-dimensional environments.

</details>


### [301] [Electrodermal Activity as a Unimodal Signal for Aerobic Exercise Detection in Wearable Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15880)
*Rena Mira Krishna,Ramya Sankar,Shadi Ghiasi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EDA alone can moderately distinguish rest from sustained aerobic exercise in a subject-independent LOSO setup; phasic dynamics aid discrimination but multimodal sensing remains advantageous.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify the standalone discriminative power of EDA for activity-state inference and delineate its role as a unimodal input.

Method: Use a public dataset (30 healthy individuals); extract EDA features; apply benchmark ML models with leave-one-subject-out validation; analyze contributions of phasic/temporal dynamics and event timing to class separation.

Result: EDA-only classifiers achieve moderate subject-independent performance; phasic temporal dynamics and event timing contribute to class separation; findings provide a conservative benchmark for EDA's unimodal discriminative power in rest vs sustained exercise.

Conclusion: EDA can serve as unimodal input for wearable activity-state inference; not a replacement for multimodal sensing; establishes a conservative benchmark of EDA's discriminative power and clarifies its role in unimodal inference.

Abstract: Electrodermal Activity (EDA) is a non-invasive physiological signal widely available in wearable devices and reflects sympathetic nervous system (SNS) activation. Prior multi-modal studies have demonstrated robust performance in distinguishing stress and exercise states when EDA is combined with complementary signals such as heart rate and accelerometry. However, the ability of EDA to independently distinguish sustained aerobic exercise from low-arousal states under subject-independent evaluation remains insufficiently characterized.
  This study investigates whether features derived exclusively from EDA can reliably differentiate rest from sustained aerobic exercise. Using a publicly available dataset collected from thirty healthy individuals, EDA features were evaluated using benchmark machine learning models with leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) validation. Across models, EDA-only classifiers achieved moderate subject-independent performance, with phasic temporal dynamics and event timing contributing to class separation. Rather than proposing EDA as a replacement for multimodal sensing, this work provides a conservative benchmark of the discriminative power of EDA alone and clarifies its role as a unimodal input for wearable activity-state inference.

</details>


### [302] [PhasorFlow: A Python Library for Unit Circle Based Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15886)
*Dibakar Sigdel,Namuna Panday*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PhasorFlow introduces a unit-circle computation paradigm with phasor-based circuits, a 22-gate library, a variational phasor circuit, and a DFT-based Phasor Transformer; it is validated on diverse tasks and proposed as a lightweight, deterministic alternative to classical neural networks and quantum circuits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Develop a computation framework operating on the S^1 unit circle where inputs are encoded as complex phasors and global norm is preserved via unitary wave interference, enabling continuous geometric gradients for learning on classical hardware.

Method: Define the Phasor Circuit model (N unit-circle threads, M gates) with a 22-gate library spanning Standard Unitary, Non-Linear, Neuromorphic, and Encoding operations. Introduce the Variational Phasor Circuit (VPC) for optimizing continuous phase parameters. Propose the Phasor Transformer, a parameter-free, DFT-based token mixer that replaces attention. All operations are simulated with full matrix algebra and validated on multiple tasks.

Result: Empirical validation across non-linear spatial classification, time-series prediction, financial volatility detection, and neuromorphic tasks (neural binding, oscillatory associative memory), suggesting unit-circle computing as a deterministic, lightweight alternative on classical hardware to traditional neural networks and quantum circuits.

Conclusion: Unit-circle computing provides a principled, unitarity-preserving framework that operates on classical hardware and can serve as an efficient alternative to classical NNs and quantum circuits; the PhasorFlow library is open-source.

Abstract: We present PhasorFlow, an open-source Python library introducing a computational paradigm operating on the $S^1$ unit circle. Inputs are encoded as complex phasors $z = e^{iθ}$ on the $N$-Torus ($\mathbb{T}^N$). As computation proceeds via unitary wave interference gates, global norm is preserved while individual components drift into $\mathbb{C}^N$, allowing algorithms to natively leverage continuous geometric gradients for predictive learning. PhasorFlow provides three core contributions. First, we formalize the Phasor Circuit model ($N$ unit circle threads, $M$ gates) and introduce a 22-gate library covering Standard Unitary, Non-Linear, Neuromorphic, and Encoding operations with full matrix algebra simulation. Second, we present the Variational Phasor Circuit (VPC), analogous to Variational Quantum Circuits (VQC), enabling optimization of continuous phase parameters for classical machine learning tasks. Third, we introduce the Phasor Transformer, replacing expensive $QK^TV$ attention with a parameter-free, DFT-based token mixing layer inspired by FNet. We validate PhasorFlow on non-linear spatial classification, time-series prediction, financial volatility detection, and neuromorphic tasks including neural binding and oscillatory associative memory. Our results establish unit circle computing as a deterministic, lightweight, and mathematically principled alternative to classical neural networks and quantum circuits. It operates on classical hardware while sharing quantum mechanics' unitary foundations. PhasorFlow is available at https://github.com/mindverse-computing/phasorflow.

</details>


### [303] [Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving Medical AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15901)
*Tin Hoang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Site-aware data partitioning combined with Adaptive Local Differential Privacy (ALDP) in federated learning for 3D MRI ADNI classification; demonstrates FedProx achieving competitive accuracy with improved privacy-utility trade-offs and robust benchmarking across client federations and privacy budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable privacy-preserving collaborative medical imaging AI under realistic data-sharing constraints, addressing unrealistic partitions, weak privacy guarantees, and insufficient benchmarking that hinder practical deployment in healthcare.

Method: Introduce site-aware data partitioning that preserves institutional boundaries; develop ALDP that dynamically tunes privacy parameters during training based on progression and parameter characteristics; evaluate with FedProx in multi-client federations on ADNI 3D MRI data; perform ablations and benchmarking across privacy budgets.

Result: ALDP outperforms fixed-noise Local DP, improving privacy-utility trade-off; up to 80.4% accuracy in a two-client setup; FedProx can match or exceed centralized training performance; shows greater training stability and comprehensive ablations establish quantitative standards for privacy-preserving medical AI.

Conclusion: Advances privacy-preserving federated learning for medical imaging, providing both methodological foundations (site-aware partitioning, adaptive DP) and empirical evidence essential for real-world, privacy-compliant healthcare AI deployment.

Abstract: This dissertation investigates privacy-preserving federated learning for Alzheimer's disease classification using three-dimensional MRI data from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI). Existing methodologies often suffer from unrealistic data partitioning, inadequate privacy guarantees, and insufficient benchmarking, limiting their practical deployment in healthcare. To address these gaps, this research proposes a novel site-aware data partitioning strategy that preserves institutional boundaries, reflecting real-world multi-institutional collaborations and data heterogeneity. Furthermore, an Adaptive Local Differential Privacy (ALDP) mechanism is introduced, dynamically adjusting privacy parameters based on training progression and parameter characteristics, thereby significantly improving the privacy-utility trade-off over traditional fixed-noise approaches. Systematic empirical evaluation across multiple client federations and privacy budgets demonstrated that advanced federated optimisation algorithms, particularly FedProx, could equal or surpass centralised training performance while ensuring rigorous privacy protection. Notably, ALDP achieved up to 80.4% accuracy in a two-client configuration, surpassing fixed-noise Local DP by 5-7 percentage points and demonstrating substantially greater training stability. The comprehensive ablation studies and benchmarking establish quantitative standards for privacy-preserving collaborative medical AI, providing practical guidelines for real-world deployment. This work thereby advances the state-of-the-art in federated learning for medical imaging, establishing both methodological foundations and empirical evidence necessary for future privacy-compliant AI adoption in healthcare.

</details>


### [304] [Game-Theory-Assisted Reinforcement Learning for Border Defense: Early Termination based on Analytical Solutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15907)
*Goutam Das,Michael Dorothy,Kyle Volle,Daigo Shishika*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid game theory + reinforcement learning (RL) approach using the Apollonius Circle to compute post-detection equilibrium in a border defense game with limited perceptual range; enables early termination of RL episodes after detection, allowing RL to focus on learning search strategies. Results show 10-20% higher rewards, faster convergence, and more efficient search trajectories in single- and multi-defender settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address brittleness of perfect-information game-theoretic guarantees and sample inefficiency of RL in large, complex domains. Leverage game-theoretic structure to improve RL training efficiency for pursuit-evasion with imperfect information.

Method: Hybrid framework where the Apollonius Circle computes the equilibrium in the post-detection phase; RL handles search strategy learning; early termination of episodes upon detection fixes the continuation phase, enabling RL to concentrate on search while guaranteeing optimal continuation after detection; evaluated in single- and multi-defender border defense scenarios.

Result: Across single- and multi-defender settings, the method yields 10-20% higher rewards, faster convergence, and more efficient search trajectories; extensive experiments validate the approach.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that integrating a game-theoretic equilibrium computation (via Apollonius Circle) into RL training can improve sample efficiency and performance in imperfect-information adversarial tasks, with potential applicability to similar hybrid control/search problems.

Abstract: Game theory provides the gold standard for analyzing adversarial engagements, offering strong optimality guarantees. However, these guarantees often become brittle when assumptions such as perfect information are violated. Reinforcement learning (RL), by contrast, is adaptive but can be sample-inefficient in large, complex domains. This paper introduces a hybrid approach that leverages game-theoretic insights to improve RL training efficiency. We study a border defense game with limited perceptual range, where defender performance depends on both search and pursuit strategies, making classical differential game solutions inapplicable. Our method employs the Apollonius Circle (AC) to compute equilibrium in the post-detection phase, enabling early termination of RL episodes without learning pursuit dynamics. This allows RL to concentrate on learning search strategies while guaranteeing optimal continuation after detection. Across single- and multi-defender settings, this early termination method yields 10-20% higher rewards, faster convergence, and more efficient search trajectories. Extensive experiments validate these findings and demonstrate the overall effectiveness of our approach.

</details>


### [305] [The Agentic Researcher: A Practical Guide to AI-Assisted Research in Mathematics and Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15914)
*Max Zimmer,Nico Pelleriti,Christophe Roux,Sebastian Pokutta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A practical guide to AI-assisted research in mathematics and machine learning, presenting a five-level AI-integration taxonomy, an open-source sandboxed framework to turn CLI coding agents into autonomous research assistants, and case studies; designed to augment researchers rather than replace them; code available publicly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between powerful AI tools and everyday research practice by providing actionable workflows, guardrails, and an integrative framework to harness AI for mathematical and ML research.

Method: Proposes (I) a five-level taxonomy of AI integration into research workflows; (II) an open-source framework using agent prompts to convert CLI coding agents into autonomous research assistants within a sandboxed container; (III) case studies in deep learning and mathematics; the framework is containerized, CLI-agnostic, scalable from laptop to multi-node clusters, and interoperates with frontier LLMs via existing CLI agents.

Result: The framework runs inside a sandboxed container, works with any frontier LLM through CLI agents, is quick to install, scales from personal laptops to clusters, and supported an autonomous session exceeding 20 hours that dispatched experiments across multiple nodes without human input.

Conclusion: The framework is intended to augment researchers, not replace the human in the loop; it offers a practical, scalable, and accessible approach to AI-assisted research in math and ML with open-source code available for adoption and adaptation.

Abstract: AI tools and agents are reshaping how researchers work, from proving theorems to training neural networks. Yet for many, it remains unclear how these tools fit into everyday research practice. This paper is a practical guide to AI-assisted research in mathematics and machine learning: We discuss how researchers can use modern AI systems productively, where these systems help most, and what kinds of guardrails are needed to use them responsibly. It is organized into three parts: (I) a five-level taxonomy of AI integration, (II) an open-source framework that, through a set of methodological rules formulated as agent prompts, turns CLI coding agents (e.g., Claude Code, Codex CLI, OpenCode) into autonomous research assistants, and (III) case studies from deep learning and mathematics. The framework runs inside a sandboxed container, works with any frontier LLM through existing CLI agents, is simple enough to install and use within minutes, and scales from personal-laptop prototyping to multi-node, multi-GPU experimentation across compute clusters. In practice, our longest autonomous session ran for over 20 hours, dispatching independent experiments across multiple nodes without human intervention. We stress that our framework is not intended to replace the researcher in the loop, but to augment them. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/ZIB-IOL/The-Agentic-Researcher.

</details>


### [306] [Auto Researching, not hyperparameter tuning: Convergence Analysis of 10,000 Experiments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15916)
*Xiaoyi Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In a large-scale study, architectural choices explain 94% of performance variance in LLM-guided ML experiment design; hyperparameters within an architecture explain 6%. Cross-task validation confirms architecture-driven discovery with a different winning backbone. LLM-guided search concentrates on productive architectural regions, discovering a novel backbone (V-JEPA2 with Zipformer) achieving 0.9245 AP; at N=50, LLM-guided search reaches 0.985 AP vs 0.965 with random search. Post-bugfix convergence follows a power law; entropy and Jensen–Shannon metrics illustrate multi-agent search dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether LLM agents perform genuine architectural search or primarily hyperparameter tuning within a limited region, and to establish an empirical framework for evaluating LLM-guided combinatorial ML experiment design.

Method: ANOVA decomposition over 10,469 experiments across a combinatorial space of 108,000 discrete cells for dashcam collision detection, using two LLM agents (Claude Opus and Gemini 2.5 Pro); performance measured by AP; cross-task validation on a second collision dataset with a different winning backbone; analysis of architecture vs hyperparameter contributions and search dynamics using entropy cycles and Jensen–Shannon specialization.

Result: Architectural choices explain 94% of performance variance (F=1324, η^2=0.94); hyperparameter variation within fixed architecture explains 6%. Cross-task validation shows ~75% architecture-explained variance with a different winning backbone. The agents identified that V-JEPA2 video features with Zipformer temporal encoders achieve 0.9245 AP (novel by human). LLM-guided search at N=50 achieves AP=0.985, higher than from-scratch random search at 0.965. Post-bugfix convergence follows a power law (c=0.11, R^2=0.93). Multi-agent search dynamics characterized by entropy cycles and Jensen–Shannon specialization.

Conclusion: Provides the first large-scale empirical framework for LLM-guided combinatorial ML experiment design, demonstrating genuine architecture discovery, productive architectural regions where search concentrates, and quantifiable search dynamics; supports broad applicability to automated ML configuration search.

Abstract: When LLM agents autonomously design ML experiments, do they perform genuine architecture search -- or do they default to hyperparameter tuning within a narrow region of the design space? We answer this question by analyzing 10,469 experiments executed by two LLM agents (Claude Opus and Gemini 2.5 Pro) across a combinatorial configuration space of 108,000 discrete cells for dashcam collision detection over 27 days. Through ANOVA decomposition, we find that \textbf{architectural choices explain 94\% of performance variance} ($F = 1324$, $η^2 = 0.94$), while hyperparameter variation within a fixed architecture explains only 6\%. Cross-task validation on a second collision dataset confirms this finding (75\% architecture-explained variance) with a \emph{different} winning backbone, confirming genuine architecture discovery. The agents' key contribution is discovering that V-JEPA\,2 video features with Zipformer temporal encoders achieve 0.9245 AP -- a configuration no human proposed -- and concentrating search on productive architectural regions: at $N = 50$, LLM-guided search reaches AP $= 0.985$ versus $0.965$ for from-scratch random search. Post-bugfix convergence follows a power law ($c = 0.11$, $R^2 = 0.93$); the low exponent reflects the cost of broad exploration, not inefficiency, since the LLM discovers qualitatively better regions than random or Bayesian baselines. We characterize multi-agent search dynamics via entropy cycles and Jensen--Shannon specialization, providing the first large-scale empirical framework for LLM-guided combinatorial ML experiment design.

</details>


### [307] [Generative Inverse Design with Abstention via Diagonal Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15925)
*Miguel de Campos,Werner Krebs,Hanno Gottschalk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diag-CFM introduces a zero-anchoring scheme for diagonal flow matching to achieve permutation-invariant inverse design, delivering an order-of-magnitude gains in round-trip accuracy and providing two architecture-intrinsic uncertainty metrics for candidate selection, abstention, and out-of-distribution detection across high-dimensional designs (up to P=100).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inverse design with bidirectional generative mappings can produce diverse solutions, but standard conditional flow matching (CFM) is sensitive to arbitrary ordering and scaling of design coordinates and labels, causing unstable training. A permutation- and scale-invariant, scalable method is needed.

Method: Present Diagonal Flow Matching (Diag-CFM) with a zero-anchoring strategy that pairs design coordinates with noise and labels with zero, making the learning objective invariant to coordinate permutations. Introduce two architecture-intrinsic uncertainty metrics (Zero-Deviation and Self-Consistency) to enable candidate ranking, abstention, and out-of-distribution detection.

Result: Empirically, Diag-CFM achieves an order-of-magnitude improvement in round-trip accuracy over CFM and invertible neural networks across design dimensions up to P=100. The two uncertainty metrics enable selecting the best candidate among multiple generations, abstaining from unreliable predictions, and detecting OOD targets, consistently outperforming ensemble and general-purpose baselines. Validated on airfoil, gas turbine combustor, and an analytical scalable-design benchmark.

Conclusion: Diag-CFM with zero-anchoring yields stable, scalable inverse design with intrinsic uncertainty estimation, enabling practical capabilities for selection, abstention, and OOD detection. Demonstrated effectiveness across diverse engineering domains and high-dimensional design spaces.

Abstract: Inverse design aims to find design parameters $x$ achieving target performance $y^*$. Generative approaches learn bidirectional mappings between designs and labels, enabling diverse solution sampling. However, standard conditional flow matching (CFM), when adapted to inverse problems by pairing labels with design parameters, exhibits strong sensitivity to their arbitrary ordering and scaling, leading to unstable training. We introduce Diagonal Flow Matching (Diag-CFM), which resolves this through a zero-anchoring strategy that pairs design coordinates with noise and labels with zero, making the learning problem provably invariant to coordinate permutations. This yields order-of-magnitude improvements in round-trip accuracy over CFM and invertible neural network baselines across design dimensions up to $P{=}100$. We develop two architecture-intrinsic uncertainty metrics, Zero-Deviation and Self-Consistency, that enable three practical capabilities: selecting the best candidate among multiple generations, abstaining from unreliable predictions, and detecting out-of-distribution targets; consistently outperforming ensemble and general-purpose alternatives across all tasks. We validate on airfoil, gas turbine combustor, and an analytical benchmark with scalable design dimension.

</details>


### [308] [Evaluating Causal Discovery Algorithms for Path-Specific Fairness and Utility in Healthcare](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15926)
*Nitish Nagesh,Elahe Khatibi,Thomas Hughes,Mahdi Bagheri,Pratik Gajane,Amir M. Rahmani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proxy-ground-truth benchmarking for causal discovery in health data; PC excels in structural recovery on synthetic data; FCI excels in heart-failure data utility; path-specific effects (e.g., ejection fraction) reveal indirect causality and affect fairness-utility trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground-truth causal graphs in clinical data are rarely known. The study constructs expert-validated proxy graphs to benchmark causal-discovery methods on synthetic Alzheimer's disease (AD) and heart-failure clinical records, enabling evaluation of structural recovery and path-specific fairness.

Method: Compare three causal-discovery algorithms—Peter-Clark (PC), Greedy Equivalence Search (GES), and Fast Causal Inference (FCI)—on tasks of (i) structural recovery and (ii) path-specific fairness decomposition. Use proxy ground-truth graphs created with clinical expert collaboration, evaluated on synthetic AD data and real heart-failure records. Assess not only composite fairness but also path-specific effects, measuring utility and fairness, and analyze how specific paths (e.g., ejection fraction) contribute to indirect effects.

Result: On synthetic data, PC achieved the best structural recovery. On heart-failure data, FCI achieved the highest utility. For path-specific effects, ejection fraction contributed 3.37 percentage points to the indirect effect in the ground-truth graph, influencing the fairness-utility ratio across algorithms.

Conclusion: Graph-aware fairness evaluation and fine-grained path-specific analysis are essential for reliable causal-discovery deployment in clinical contexts, given variability in algorithm performance across data types and the impact of specific physiological paths on fairness and utility.

Abstract: Causal discovery in health data faces evaluation challenges when ground truth is unknown. We address this by collaborating with experts to construct proxy ground-truth graphs, establishing benchmarks for synthetic Alzheimer's disease and heart failure clinical records data. We evaluate the Peter-Clark, Greedy Equivalence Search, and Fast Causal Inference algorithms on structural recovery and path-specific fairness decomposition, going beyond composite fairness scores. On synthetic data, Peter-Clark achieved the best structural recovery. On heart failure data, Fast Causal Inference achieved the highest utility. For path-specific effects, ejection fraction contributed 3.37 percentage points to the indirect effect in the ground truth. These differences drove variations in the fairness-utility ratio across algorithms. Our results highlight the need for graph-aware fairness evaluation and fine-grained path-specific analysis when deploying causal discovery in clinical applications.

</details>


### [309] [Discovery of interaction and diffusion kernels in particle-to-mean-field multi-agent systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15927)
*Giacomo Albi,Alessandro Alla,Elisa Calzola*

Main category: cs.LG
