TL;DR: Nine sparse autoencoders on the residual stream of Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B enable fine-grained, non-retraining steering of five agentic traits by decoding linear probe weights through the SAE; autonomy is dominated by a single axis (independence vs defer to user) with trait-specific effects being secondary; steering is effective when applied during prefill, not autoregressive decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether latent-space probes on SAEs can identify and controllably steer agentic behaviors in a large LLM with a hybrid architecture, enabling inference-time interventions without retraining and examining cross-trait interactions and causality.

Method: Train nine sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on the model's residual stream; train linear probes on SAE latent activations; project probe weights back through the SAE decoder to obtain continuous steering vectors in the model’s activation space (bypassing top-k discretization); apply these vectors at inference across 1,800 rollouts (50 scenarios × 36 conditions) to steer five agentic traits; evaluate impact on behavior (e.g., tool use, risk calibration), and perform causal timing analysis by comparing steering during prefill vs autoregressive decoding; report effect sizes (Cohen’s d) and p-values.

Result: Autonomy steering at multiplier 2 yields Cohen’s d = 1.01 (p < 0.0001), moving from user-asking 78% of the time to proactive code execution and web search; all five traits primarily modulate a single dominant axis: independence vs deference; tool-use vector has d=0.39; risk-calibration vector yields suppression; steering during autoregressive decoding has no effect (p > 0.35), indicating commitments are computed during prefill in GatedDeltaNet architectures.

Conclusion: Inference-time, non-retraining steering of agentic behavior is feasible via latent-space interventions on SAEs, driven predominantly by a single agency axis. Trait-specific effects are secondary and manifest in tool-type composition and dose-response; causal timing evidence points to prefill computations as the locus of behavioral commitments, with implications for controllability and safety in hybrid MoE/attention models.

Abstract: We train nine sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on the residual stream of Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B, a 35-billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model with a hybrid GatedDeltaNet/attention architecture, and use them to identify and steer five agentic behavioral traits. Our method trains linear probes on SAE latent activations, then projects the probe weights back through the SAE decoder to obtain continuous steering vectors in the model's native activation space. This bypasses the SAE's top-k discretization, enabling fine-grained behavioral intervention at inference time with no retraining. Across 1,800 agent rollouts (50 scenarios times 36 conditions), we find that autonomy steering at multiplier 2 achieves Cohen's d = 1.01 (p < 0.0001), shifting the model from asking the user for help 78% of the time to proactively executing code and searching the web. Cross-trait analysis, however, reveals that all five steering vectors primarily modulate a single dominant agency axis (the disposition to act independently versus defer to the user), with trait specific effects appearing only as secondary modulations in tool-type composition and dose-response shape. The tool-use vector steers behavior (d = 0.39); the risk-calibration vector produces only suppression. We additionally show that steering only during autoregressive decoding has zero effect (p > 0.35), providing causal evidence that behavioral commitments are computed during prefill in GatedDeltaNet architectures.

</details>


### [335] [DynamicGate MLP Conditional Computation via Learned Structural Dropout and Input Dependent Gating for Functional Plasticity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16367)
*Yong Il Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DynamicGate-MLP learns input-dependent gates to selectively activate units/blocks, merging dropout-like regularization with conditional computation for efficient MLPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconcile the regularization benefits of dropout with the efficiency of conditional computation, enabling a unified, input-adaptive framework for MLPs.

Method: Introduce continuous gate probabilities, infer a discrete execution mask at test time, and train with a penalty on expected gate usage. Use Straight-Through Estimator to optimize the discrete mask. Compare against MLP baselines and MoE-style variants on MNIST, CIFAR-10, Tiny-ImageNet, Speech Commands, and PBMC3k. Evaluate compute efficiency with gate activation ratios and a layer-weighted relative MAC metric rather than wall-clock latency.

Result: Demonstrates that the model can concentrate computation on input-relevant parts via learned gates, achieving sample-dependent execution and improved compute efficiency while maintaining accuracy across multiple datasets; provides practical metrics for compute without relying on hardware-specific latency.

Conclusion: DynamicGate-MLP offers a unified framework that combines regularization and conditional computation in MLPs through learnable gates and STE-based optimization, enabling efficient, input-dependent inference.

Abstract: Dropout is a representative regularization technique that stochastically deactivates hidden units during training to mitigate overfitting. In contrast, standard inference executes the full network with dense computation, so its goal and mechanism differ from conditional computation, where the executed operations depend on the input. This paper organizes DynamicGate-MLP into a single framework that simultaneously satisfies both the regularization view and the conditional-computation view. Instead of a random mask, the proposed model learns gates that decide whether to use each unit (or block), suppressing unnecessary computation while implementing sample-dependent execution that concentrates computation on the parts needed for each input. To this end, we define continuous gate probabilities and, at inference time, generate a discrete execution mask from them to select an execution path. Training controls the compute budget via a penalty on expected gate usage and uses a Straight-Through Estimator (STE) to optimize the discrete mask. We evaluate DynamicGate-MLP on MNIST, CIFAR-10, Tiny-ImageNet, Speech Commands, and PBMC3k, and compare it with various MLP baselines and MoE-style variants. Compute efficiency is compared under a consistent criterion using gate activation ratios and a layerweighted relative MAC metric, rather than wall-clock latency that depends on hardware and backend kernels.

</details>


### [336] [FederatedFactory: Generative One-Shot Learning for Extremely Non-IID Distributed Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16370)
*Andrea Moleri,Christian Internò,Ali Raza,Markus Olhofer,David Klindt,Fabio Stella,Barbara Hammer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FederatedFactory flips FL from aggregating discriminative parameters to exchanging generative priors, enabling zero-dependency, modular unlearning, and near-centralized performance under label-heterogeneous conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-iid local label distributions cause gradient conflicts in standard FL; reliance on pretrained foundation models introduces external biases and unrealistic assumptions; a zero-dependency, priors-based federation could preserve data sovereignty while enabling robust, unbiased learning and exact unlearning.

Method: Introduce FederatedFactory, in which the federation unit is the generative priors rather than discriminative parameters. Exchanges occur via a single communication round of generative modules. The framework supports ex nihilo synthesis of universally class-balanced datasets, eliminating gradient conflicts and external prior bias. It is zero-dependency and enables deterministic modular unlearning by deleting specific generative modules.

Result: Empirical evaluation on MedMNIST and ISIC2019 shows recovery of centralized upper-bound performance. Under severe data heterogeneity, CIFAR-10 accuracy improves from 11.36% to 90.57%, and ISIC2019 AUROC reaches 90.57%. The framework also enables exact modular unlearning through deterministic removal of modules.

Conclusion: FederatedFactory effectively mitigates gradient conflicts and external biases in federated learning with heterogeneous labels, removes the need for pretrained priors, and enables precise, modular unlearning, with strong empirical performance on medical imaging benchmarks and competitive gains on standard datasets.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables distributed optimization without compromising data sovereignty. Yet, where local label distributions are mutually exclusive, standard weight aggregation fails due to conflicting optimization trajectories. Often, FL methods rely on pretrained foundation models, introducing unrealistic assumptions. We introduce FederatedFactory, a zero-dependency framework that inverts the unit of federation from discriminative parameters to generative priors. By exchanging generative modules in a single communication round, our architecture supports ex nihilo synthesis of universally class balanced datasets, eliminating gradient conflict and external prior bias entirely. Evaluations across diverse medical imagery benchmarks, including MedMNIST and ISIC2019, demonstrate that our approach recovers centralized upper-bound performance. Under pathological heterogeneity, it lifts baseline accuracy from a collapsed 11.36% to 90.57% on CIFAR-10 and restores ISIC2019 AUROC to 90.57%. Additionally, this framework facilitates exact modular unlearning through the deterministic deletion of specific generative modules.

</details>


### [337] [Prior-Informed Neural Network Initialization: A Spectral Approach for Function Parameterizing Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16376)
*David Orlando Salazar Torres,Diyar Altinses,Andreas Schwung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prior-informed initialization and architecture for function-parameterized neural nets using FFT-derived seasonal priors and residual trend parameterization, yielding more compact, interpretable models with faster convergence and improved efficiency without changing the training procedure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome sensitivity to data-agnostic initializations in BoF-like architectures by leveraging intrinsic spectral-temporal structure of signals, aiming for better convergence, robustness, and interpretability.

Method: Extract dominant seasonal priors via Fast Fourier Transform to guide model depth and initial states; use a residual-based regression module to parameterize trend components; reduce encoder dimensionality through structural alignment while keeping the same training procedure.

Result: Theoretical analysis on trend estimation under finite-sample regimes; extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show accelerated convergence, reduced trial variability, and improved computational efficiency; outperforms standard initialization baselines while maintaining reconstruction fidelity.

Conclusion: Embedding data-driven priors enables more compact and interpretable architectures that achieve superior performance with no changes to the core training loop.

Abstract: Neural network architectures designed for function parameterization, such as the Bag-of-Functions (BoF) framework, bridge the gap between the expressivity of deep learning and the interpretability of classical signal processing. However, these models are inherently sensitive to parameter initialization, as traditional data-agnostic schemes fail to capture the structural properties of the target signals, often leading to suboptimal convergence. In this work, we propose a prior-informed design strategy that leverages the intrinsic spectral and temporal structure of the data to guide both network initialization and architectural configuration. A principled methodology is introduced that uses the Fast Fourier Transform to extract dominant seasonal priors, informing model depth and initial states, and a residual-based regression approach to parameterize trend components. Crucially, this structural alignment enables a substantial reduction in encoder dimensionality without compromising reconstruction fidelity. A supporting theoretical analysis provides guidance on trend estimation under finite-sample regimes. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that embedding data-driven priors significantly accelerates convergence, reduces performance variability across trials, and improves computational efficiency. Overall, the proposed framework enables more compact and interpretable architectures while outperforming standard initialization baselines, without altering the core training procedure.

</details>


### [338] [Age Predictors Through the Lens of Generalization, Bias Mitigation, and Interpretability: Reflections on Causal Implications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16377)
*Debdas Paul,Elisa Ferrari,Irene Gravili,Alessandro Cellerino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates learning invariant representations via adversarial learning to improve out-of-distribution age prediction by removing biases from attributes like race, gender, or tissue. It provides theoretical framing across bias mitigation, confounding, and fairness, and presents an interpretable neural network model with adversarial representation learning, illustrated on mouse transcriptomic data, and aligns with a published Elamipretide study; it also discusses limitations in causal interpretation from predictive models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exogenous attributes (race, gender, tissue, etc.) bias chronological age predictors and hinder OOD generalization. The work motivates invariant representations to improve generalization, enable bias mitigation, and facilitate cautious causal interpretation.

Method: Propose an interpretable neural network using adversarial representation learning to learn invariant representations that are predictive of age while invariant to protected attributes. The approach is theoretically framed and empirically demonstrated on publicly available mouse transcriptomic datasets, comparing against conventional ML models and discussing interpretability.

Result: The invariant-representation model yields outcomes consistent with predictive results from a published study on the effects of Elamipretide on mouse skeletal and cardiac muscle. The experiments illustrate how invariance affects predictive behavior relative to standard models.

Conclusion: Invariant representation learning is valuable for bias mitigation and fairness in predictive tasks and for cleaner causal interpretation, but purely predictive models have limitations in causal inference; the study highlights these limitations and the need for careful interpretation when drawing causal claims from such models.

Abstract: Chronological age predictors often fail to achieve out-of-distribution (OOD) gen- eralization due to exogenous attributes such as race, gender, or tissue. Learning an invariant representation with respect to those attributes is therefore essential to improve OOD generalization and prevent overly optimistic results. In predic- tive settings, these attributes motivate bias mitigation; in causal analyses, they appear as confounders; and when protected, their suppression leads to fairness. We coherently explore these concepts with theoretical rigor and discuss the scope of an interpretable neural network model based on adversarial representation learning. Using publicly available mouse transcriptomic datasets, we illustrate the behavior of this model relative to conventional machine learning models. We observe that the outcome of this model is consistent with the predictive results of a published study demonstrating the effects of Elamipretide on mouse skeletal and cardiac muscle. We conclude by discussing the limitations of deriving causal interpretation from such purely predictive models.

</details>


### [339] [Trained Persistent Memory for Frozen Encoder--Decoder LLMs: Six Architectural Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16413)
*Hong Jeong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A pilot study shows persistent, differentiable memory in the latent space of a frozen LLM using adapters, enabling conversational learning; memory capacity critically affects performance, with scalable memory independent of the backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation:  address the stateless nature of encoder–decoder LMs by exploring persistent memory in a continuous latent space, aiming to enable conversational learning without full backpropagation.

Method:  Six architectural methods across three injection points and four write mechanisms were explored. All memory operations are differentiable on dense vectors. Only adapters were trained; the memory bank accumulates at inference without gradients. Evaluations used LoCoMo at 1× and 10× capacity.

Result: At 1× capacity baseline, memory recall is zero. At 10× capacity, all six adapters yield positive memory-recall curves. At 1×, three methods collapse, indicating capacity is a critical design parameter. The memory bank is compact and scalable without altering the backbone.

Conclusion: The work establishes feasibility and a design-space taxonomy for persistent latent-space memory in frozen LMs. It argues that larger models, more data, and larger memory can yield substantially stronger results with end-to-end training in future work.

Abstract: Frozen encoder--decoder language models are stateless: the latent representation is discarded after every forward pass, so no information persists across sessions. This paper presents a \textbf{proof-of-concept pilot study} showing that persistent memory in the \emph{continuous latent space} of a frozen LLM is feasible -- even under severe resource constraints (a single frozen Flan-T5-XL backbone, small trainable adapters, a single dataset). We implement six architectural methods spanning three injection points and four write mechanisms; unlike text-level memory systems, every write and read is a differentiable operation on dense vectors. After training only the adapter, the memory bank continues to accumulate at inference time without gradients, enabling \emph{conversational learning}. Under a forgetting-curve evaluation on LoCoMo at two capacity scales (1$\times$ and 10$\times$), the stateless baseline scores exactly zero; at 10$\times$ all six trained adapters produce positive memory-recall curves; at 1$\times$ three methods collapse, revealing capacity as a critical design parameter. Because the memory bank is a compact numerical array, it can be scaled to arbitrarily large capacity without altering the backbone. We argue that full end-to-end training with larger models, larger data, and orders-of-magnitude larger memory will yield substantially stronger results; this pilot study establishes the feasibility baseline and design-space taxonomy that such efforts require.

</details>


### [340] [DISCOVER: A Solver for Distributional Counterfactual Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16436)
*Yikai Gu,Lele Cao,Bo Zhao,Lei Lei,Lei You*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A gradient-free, model-agnostic solver for distributional counterfactual explanations (DISCOVER) that preserves the DCE objective and chance-constrained certification, replacing gradient descent with a sparse propose-and-select search guided by OT geometry; demonstrates effective alignment of input and output distributions on tabular, possibly non-differentiable pipelines; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DCE achieves distributional alignment with certification but relies on gradient-based optimization, which is unsuitable for many real-world tabular pipelines that are non-differentiable. A model-agnostic solver is needed to extend distributional counterfactual reasoning to black-box models while maintaining theoretical guarantees.

Method: Replace gradient-based optimization with a sparse propose-and-select search. Perform sample-wise decomposition of the transport objective to compute per-row impact scores and enforce a top-k intervention budget, editing the most influential samples. Use OT-guided cone sampling to generate candidates without predictor gradients, guided by input-side transport geometry.

Result: Empirical evaluation on multiple tabular datasets shows strong joint alignment of input and output distributions, extending distributional counterfactual reasoning to black-box learning pipelines.

Conclusion: DISCOVER enables model-agnostic distributional counterfactual explanations while preserving the original objective and certification, making distributional CF explanations feasible for non-differentiable/tabular pipelines; code is available.

Abstract: Counterfactual explanations (CE) explain model decisions by identifying input modifications that lead to different predictions. Most existing methods operate at the instance level. Distributional Counterfactual Explanations (DCE) extend this setting by optimizing an optimal transport objective that balances proximity to a factual input distribution and alignment to a target output distribution, with statistical certification via chance constrained bounds. However, DCE relies on gradient based optimization, while many real-world tabular pipelines are dominated by non-differentiable models. We propose DISCOVER, a model-agnostic solver for distributional counterfactual explanations. DISCOVER preserves the original DCE objective and certification while replacing gradient descent with a sparse propose-and-select search paradigm. It exploits a sample-wise decomposition of the transport objective to compute per-row impact scores and enforce a top-$k$ intervention budget, focusing edits on the most influential samples. To guide candidate generation without predictor gradients, DISCOVER introduces an OT-guided cone sampling primitive driven by input-side transport geometry. Experiments on multiple tabular datasets demonstrate strong joint alignment of input and output distributions, extending distributional counterfactual reasoning to modern black box learning pipelines. A code repository is available at https://github.com/understanding-ml/DCE.

</details>


### [341] [Capability-Guided Compression: Toward Interpretability-Aware Budget Allocation for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16440)
*Rishaank Gupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Capability-Guided Compression (CGC) that uses Sparse Autoencoder (SAE)-derived capability density to allocate differential compression budgets across transformer components, linking higher density to lower redundancy and earlier phase transitions; shows independence from Wanda head-importance scores and reports mixed empirical results, laying groundwork for capability-aware compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome capability-blind compression budgets that ignore the functional role of model components, which can mask reasoning capability loss and cause abrupt performance phase transitions.

Method: Define capability density as a scalar combining feature breadth, activation entropy, and cross-input consistency from SAE feature activations. The density guides per-component compression budgets. Provide theoretical results proving that high-density components have lower redundancy and reach phase transitions at lower compression ratios. Validate with experiments on GPT-2 Medium, comparing with Wanda head-importance scores.

Result: Capability density is statistically independent of Wanda importance scores (Spearman rho = -0.054, n = 384 heads). Negative result on PPL-based compression comparison; GPT-2 Medium may be an insufficient test bed for the full CGC hypothesis. The work establishes a novel, orthogonal compression signal and a formal density framework, enabling component-level phase-transition prediction.

Conclusion: CGC provides a foundational, density-based framework for capability-aware compression, introducing an orthogonal signal to existing metrics and outlining directions for broader validation and refinement across more capable testbeds and components.

Abstract: Large language model compression has made substantial progress through pruning, quantization, and low-rank decomposition, yet a fundamental limitation persists across all existing methods: compression budgets are allocated without any representation of what individual model components functionally encode. We term this the capability-blind compression problem and argue it is a root cause of two well-documented failures -- the insensitivity of perplexity-based evaluation to reasoning capability loss, and the abrupt phase transitions in model performance recently characterized by Ma et al. (2026). We propose Capability-Guided Compression (CGC), a framework that addresses this by using Sparse Autoencoder (SAE)-derived capability density maps to allocate differential compression budgets across transformer components. Capability density is a formally defined scalar measure combining the feature breadth, activation entropy, and cross-input consistency of a component's SAE feature activation distribution. We prove theoretically that components with higher capability density exhibit lower structural redundancy and reach their individual phase transition points at lower compression ratios, providing the first pre-compression mechanism for component-level phase transition prediction. Experiments on GPT-2 Medium confirm that capability density is statistically independent of Wanda importance scores (Spearman rho = -0.054, n = 384 heads), establishing it as a genuinely novel compression signal orthogonal to all existing importance metrics. We report a negative result on PPL-based compression comparison and provide a principled diagnosis identifying GPT-2 Medium as an insufficient test bed for the full CGC hypothesis. The theoretical framework, density formalism, and orthogonality finding constitute a foundation for capability-aware compression research.

</details>


### [342] [Optimal uncertainty bounds for multivariate kernel regression under bounded noise: A Gaussian process-based dual function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16481)
*Amon Lahr,Anna Scampicchio,Johannes Köhler,Melanie N. Zeilinger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A tight, distribution-free uncertainty bound is derived for multi-output kernel-based estimates using an unconstrained duality-based formulation, yielding GP-like confidence bounds that can be integrated into downstream optimization; demonstrated on a quadrotor dynamics learning example.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-conservative uncertainty bounds are essential for reliable, safe learning-based control with latent functions from noisy data. Existing bounds are often distribution-dependent, overly conservative, scale poorly to multi-output settings, or are hard to plug into optimization pipelines.

Method: An unconstrained duality-based formulation produces a distribution-free bound for multi-output kernel regression, preserving the familiar Gaussian-process confidence-bound structure and enabling straightforward integration into optimization tasks.

Result: The bound is tight and generalizes many existing results; it is applicable in practice and validated via an example inspired by quadrotor dynamics learning.

Conclusion: The proposed bound improves reliability of uncertainty quantification in multi-output kernel learning and can be readily incorporated into downstream control/optimization pipelines; it generalizes existing results and sets a foundation for broader applicability and future extensions.

Abstract: Non-conservative uncertainty bounds are essential for making reliable predictions about latent functions from noisy data--and thus, a key enabler for safe learning-based control. In this domain, kernel methods such as Gaussian process regression are established techniques, thanks to their inherent uncertainty quantification mechanism. Still, existing bounds either pose strong assumptions on the underlying noise distribution, are conservative, do not scale well in the multi-output case, or are difficult to integrate into downstream tasks. This paper addresses these limitations by presenting a tight, distribution-free bound for multi-output kernel-based estimates. It is obtained through an unconstrained, duality-based formulation, which shares the same structure of classic Gaussian process confidence bounds and can thus be straightforwardly integrated into downstream optimization pipelines. We show that the proposed bound generalizes many existing results and illustrate its application using an example inspired by quadrotor dynamics learning.

</details>


### [343] [Bridging the High-Frequency Data Gap: A Millisecond-Resolution Network Dataset for Advancing Time Series Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16497)
*Subina Khanal,Seshu Tirupathi,Merim Dzaferagic,Marco Ruffini,Torben Bach Pedersen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A millisecond-resolution 5G wireless dataset is proposed to extend time series foundation models (TSFMs) to high-frequency data; initial benchmarks show TSFMs underperform on this domain, emphasizing the need for high-frequency pretraining and improved fine-tuning strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-frequency (millisecond-scale) time series are underrepresented in existing TSFM datasets, limiting generalization across domains (now introducing wireless networks) and temporal resolutions. The work argues for pretraining on high-frequency data to improve TSFM robustness and applicability.

Method: Create and publicize a millisecond-resolution dataset from an operational 5G deployment, covering wireless and traffic conditions; define short-term forecasting tasks (100 ms to 9.6 s); benchmark traditional ML models and TSFMs in zero-shot and fine-tuning settings.

Result: Most TSFM configurations perform poorly on this high-frequency data distribution in both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings, indicating a mismatch between current pretraining regimes and high-frequency domain characteristics.

Conclusion: High-frequency datasets are crucial for enhancing TSFM architectures, pretraining strategies, and generalization/robustness for real-world forecasting.

Abstract: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) require diverse, real-world datasets to adapt across varying domains and temporal frequencies. However, current large-scale datasets predominantly focus on low-frequency time series with sampling intervals, i.e., time resolution, in the range of seconds to years, hindering their ability to capture the nuances of high-frequency time series data. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel dataset that captures millisecond-resolution wireless and traffic conditions from an operational 5G wireless deployment, expanding the scope of TSFMs to incorporate high-frequency data for pre-training. Further, the dataset introduces a new domain, wireless networks, thus complementing existing more general domains like energy and finance. The dataset also provides use cases for short-term forecasting, with prediction horizons spanning from 100 milliseconds (1 step) to 9.6 seconds (96 steps). By benchmarking traditional machine learning models and TSFMs on predictive tasks using this dataset, we demonstrate that most TSFM model configurations perform poorly on this new data distribution in both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings. Our work underscores the importance of incorporating high-frequency datasets during pre-training and forecasting to enhance architectures, fine-tuning strategies, generalization, and robustness of TSFMs in real-world applications.

</details>


### [344] [From the Inside Out: Progressive Distribution Refinement for Confidence Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16500)
*Xizhong Yang,Yinan Xia,Huiming Wang,Mofei Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DistriTTRL uses the model’s confidence distribution as a progressive self-reward signal in reinforcement learning to complement internal signals, addressing test-train information gaps and reward hacking in voting-based test-time strategies through diversity-aware penalties, yielding strong, cross-model benchmark gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL with self-reward signals is appealing due to label-free learning, but test-time scaling (TTS) introduces distributional shifts between training and testing. Voting-based TTS can suffer from reward hacking. There is a need to align internal signals with test-time information and reduce incentive for adversarial rewards.

Method: Introduce a distribution-prior–based self-reward mechanism (DistriTTRL) that progressively optimizes the reward signal using the model’s confidence distribution instead of relying solely on single-query rollouts. Apply diversity-targeted penalties to discourage homogeneous voting and mitigate reward hacking in TTS.

Result: The approach yields significant performance improvements across multiple models and benchmarks, demonstrating stronger robustness to reward hacking and better alignment between training and test-time signals.

Conclusion: Leveraging confidence-distribution priors and diversity-based penalties enables more reliable self-reward optimization in RL, improving performance and reducing vulnerabilities associated with voting-based TTS.

Abstract: Leveraging the model's internal information as the self-reward signal in Reinforcement Learning (RL) has received extensive attention due to its label-free nature. While prior works have made significant progress in applying the Test-Time Scaling (TTS) strategies to RL, the discrepancy in internal information between test and training remains inadequately addressed. Moreover, Test-Time Training based on voting-based TTS strategies often suffers from reward hacking problems. To address these issues, we propose DistriTTRL, which leverages the distribution prior of the model's confidence during RL to progressively optimize the reward signal, rather than relying solely on single-query rollouts. Additionally, we mitigate the phenomenon of consistent reward hacking caused by the voting-based TTS strategies through diversity-targeted penalties. Benefiting from this training mechanism where model capability and self-reward signals complement each other, and the mitigation of reward hacking, DistriTTRL has achieved significant performance improvements across multiple models and benchmarks.

</details>


### [345] [FEAT: A Linear-Complexity Foundation Model for Extremely Large Structured Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16513)
*Zhenghang Song,Tang Qian,Lu Chen,Yushuai Li,Zhengke Hu,Bingbing Fang,Yumeng Song,Junbo Zhao,Sheng Zhang,Tianyi Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FEAT is a linear-complexity foundation model for extremely large structured data, replacing quadratic self-attention with a hybrid linear encoding (local AF-BiMamba-2 + global Conv-GLA). It achieves linear scaling, better zero-shot performance, and up to 40x faster inference across 11 real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome three key limitations of existing large structured-data models: quadratic complexity of sample-wise self-attention, hidden-state compression and artificial causal bias in linear sequence models, and mismatch between synthetic pre-training and real-world distributions.

Method: FEAT employs a multi-layer dual-axis architecture that uses adaptive-fusion bi-Mamba-2 (AFBM) for local sample dependencies and convolutional gated linear attention (Conv-GLA) for global memory, enabling linear-complexity cross-sample modeling. It also integrates a hybrid structural causal model pipeline and a stable reconstruction objective to improve robustness.

Result: Across 11 real-world datasets, FEAT consistently outperforms baselines in zero-shot tasks, scales linearly with data, and achieves up to 40x faster inference.

Conclusion: FEAT demonstrates that a linear-complexity, dual-axis architecture with hybrid linear encodings can effectively model extremely large structured data, delivering strong zero-shot performance and significant speedups over quadratic-attention baselines.

Abstract: Structured data is foundational to healthcare, finance, e-commerce, and scientific data management. Large structured-data models (LDMs) extend the foundation model paradigm to unify heterogeneous datasets for tasks such as classification, regression, and decision support. However, existing LDMs face major limitations. First, most rely on sample-wise self-attention, whose O(N^2) complexity limits the sample count. Second, linear sequence models often degrade representations due to hidden-state compression and artificial causal bias. Third, synthetic-only pre-training often fails to match real-world distributions. We propose FEAT, a linear-complexity foundation model for extremely large structured data. FEAT introduces a multi-layer dual-axis architecture that replaces quadratic attention with hybrid linear encoding. The architecture combines adaptive-fusion bi-Mamba-2 (AFBM) for local sample dependencies and convolutional gated linear attention (Conv-GLA) for global memory. This design enables linear-complexity cross-sample modeling while preserving expressive representations. To improve robustness, FEAT adopts a hybrid structural causal model pipeline and a stable reconstruction objective. Experiments on 11 real-world datasets show that FEAT consistently outperforms baselines in zero-shot performance, while scaling linearly and achieving up to 40x faster inference.

</details>


### [346] [SympFormer: Accelerated attention blocks via Inertial Dynamics on Density Manifolds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16535)
*Viktor Stein,Wuchen Li,Gabriele Steidl*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends transformer attention with accelerated, inertial dynamics by treating tokens as particles with position and velocity; derives Hamiltonian momentum attention blocks via density-space discretization; links linear self-attention to Stein variational gradient flow with a bilinear kernel; shows preservation of elliptically contoured distributions; provides implementable particle-based algorithms with faster convergence without increasing oracle calls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To go beyond standard mean-field gradient-flow interpretations of attention by incorporating acceleration (Nesterov-like dynamics) in density spaces, potentially yielding faster convergence and richer dynamics while preserving statistical structure.

Method: Introduce tokens carrying spatial and velocity variables; derive accelerated attention blocks as inertial (Nesterov-type) dynamics on density spaces; discretize time to obtain Hamiltonian momentum attention blocks; for linear self-attention, show it approximates a Stein variational gradient flow with a bilinear kernel; prove preservation of elliptically contoured distributions; develop particle-based algorithms.

Result: Accelerated attention blocks converge faster than classical attention blocks while preserving the number of oracle calls; provide implementable particle-based algorithms; in the linear case, characterize the exact link to Stein variational gradient flow with a bilinear kernel and preserve EC distributions.

Conclusion: The framework extends attention models with accelerated, Hamiltonian dynamics on density spaces, yielding faster convergence without extra oracle cost and maintaining important distributional invariances; it connects transformer attention to well-developed PDE/gradient-flow tools (Stein variational flow) and offers practical particle-based implementations.

Abstract: Transformers owe much of their empirical success in natural language processing to the self-attention blocks. Recent perspectives interpret attention blocks as interacting particle systems, whose mean-field limits correspond to gradient flows of interaction energy functionals on probability density spaces equipped with Wasserstein-$2$-type metrics. We extend this viewpoint by introducing accelerated attention blocks derived from inertial Nesterov-type dynamics on density spaces. In our proposed architecture, tokens carry both spatial (feature) and velocity variables. The time discretization and the approximation of accelerated density dynamics yield Hamiltonian momentum attention blocks, which constitute the proposed accelerated attention architectures. In particular, for linear self-attention, we show that the attention blocks approximate a Stein variational gradient flow, using a bilinear kernel, of a potential energy. In this setting, we prove that elliptically contoured probability distributions are preserved by the accelerated attention blocks. We present implementable particle-based algorithms and demonstrate that the proposed accelerated attention blocks converge faster than the classical attention blocks while preserving the number of oracle calls.

</details>


### [347] [Manifold-Matching Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16568)
*Laurent Cheret,Vincent Létourneau,Isar Nejadgholi,Chris Drummond,Hussein Al Osman,Maia Fraser*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unsupervised regularization for autoencoders via Manifold-Matching (MMAE): align latent-space pairwise distances with input-space distances, enabling flexible low-dimensional manifolds and improving manifold-preservation metrics, while approximating MDS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preserve the geometric (manifold) structure of data in latent representations without supervision, improving downstream tasks and quality of learned representations.

Method: Compute pairwise distances for a minibatch in input space and the corresponding latent representations; minimize the mean squared error between these two pairwise-distance matrices; can extend to a lower-dimensional latent space to gain flexibility; integrated as a regularizer with the autoencoder objective.

Result: MMAE outperforms similar unsupervised regularizers on metrics based on nearest-neighbor preservation and persistent homology; provides a scalable approximation to Multi-Dimensional Scaling (MDS).

Conclusion: MMAE provides a simple, scalable unsupervised regularization that aligns latent geometry with data geometry, yielding better manifold-preserving representations and offering a practical link to MDS, with applicability to lower-dimensional latent spaces.

Abstract: We study a simple unsupervised regularization scheme for autoencoders called Manifold-Matching (MMAE): we align the pairwise distances in the latent space to those of the input data space by minimizing mean squared error. Because alignment occurs on pairwise distances rather than coordinates, it can also be extended to a lower-dimensional representation of the data, adding flexibility to the method. We find that this regularization outperforms similar methods on metrics based on preservation of nearest-neighbor distances and persistent homology-based measures. We also observe that MMAE provides a scalable approximation of Multi-Dimensional Scaling (MDS).

</details>


### [348] [Deep Tabular Representation Corrector](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16569)
*Hangting Ye,Peng Wang,Wei Fan,Xiaozhuang Song,He Zhao,Dandan Gun,Yi Chang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A model-agnostic post-hoc module (TRC) improves deep tabular models by correcting representations without changing parameters, via two tasks: re-estimating representation shifts and mapping to a light embedding space, yielding improved performance on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep tabular models suffer from representation shift and redundancy; traditional in-learning or pre-learning approaches either require retraining or extra pretext tasks. A parameter-free, model-agnostic corrective module can enhance representations without modifying the base model, improving efficiency and flexibility.

Method: Introduce Tabular Representation Corrector (TRC) with two tasks: (i) Tabular Representation Re-estimation, training a shift estimator to quantify and mitigate the inherent shift in tabular representations; (ii) Tabular Space Mapping, transforming the re-estimated representations into a compact light-embedding space via a coordinate estimator while preserving predictive information.

Result: Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art deep tabular models with TRC across various tabular benchmarks show consistent performance improvements, validating the effectiveness and efficiency of the approach.

Conclusion: TRC provides a model-agnostic, efficient enhancement to deep tabular representations by addressing shift and redundancy without altering the original model parameters, achieving superior results on diverse benchmarks.

Abstract: Tabular data have been playing a mostly important role in diverse real-world fields, such as healthcare, engineering, finance, etc. The recent success of deep learning has fostered many deep networks (e.g., Transformer, ResNet) based tabular learning methods. Generally, existing deep tabular machine learning methods are along with the two paradigms, i.e., in-learning and pre-learning. In-learning methods need to train networks from scratch or impose extra constraints to regulate the representations which nonetheless train multiple tasks simultaneously and make learning more difficult, while pre-learning methods design several pretext tasks for pre-training and then conduct task-specific fine-tuning, which however need much extra training effort with prior knowledge. In this paper, we introduce a novel deep Tabular Representation Corrector, TRC, to enhance any trained deep tabular model's representations without altering its parameters in a model-agnostic manner. Specifically, targeting the representation shift and representation redundancy that hinder prediction, we propose two tasks, i.e., (i) Tabular Representation Re-estimation, that involves training a shift estimator to calculate the inherent shift of tabular representations to subsequently mitigate it, thereby re-estimating the representations and (ii) Tabular Space Mapping, that transforms the above re-estimated representations into a light-embedding vector space via a coordinate estimator while preserves crucial predictive information to minimize redundancy. The two tasks jointly enhance the representations of deep tabular models without touching on the original models thus enjoying high efficiency. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments on state-of-the-art deep tabular machine learning models coupled with TRC on various tabular benchmarks which have shown consistent superiority.

</details>


### [349] [When and Why Does Unsupervised RL Succeed in Mathematical Reasoning? A Manifold Envelopment Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16578)
*Zelin Zhang,Fei Cheng,Chenhui Chu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unsupervised RL with intrinsic rewards can boost LLM mathematical reasoning, but stability is conditional and fragile; the authors design rewards to enforce concise and certain output, test across models with varying intrinsic reasoning priors, and introduce a geometric diagnostic showing successful cases cluster on latent manifolds, revealing when and why the approach breaks down.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outcome-based RL requires costly ground-truth annotations, limiting scalability. An intrinsic-reward, unsupervised alternative promises scalable improvement but risks unstable training and reward hacking; understanding where and why it works is essential.

Method: Develop and evaluate a suite of intrinsic rewards that explicitly promote concise and certain generation. Assess performance across base models with different intrinsic reasoning capabilities. Introduce a geometric diagnostic lens demonstrating that effective regimes are organized as manifolds in the training dynamics.

Result: Intrinsic rewards can improve mathematical reasoning under certain configurations, but failures and instabilities occur in other regimes. The model’s foundational logical priors significantly influence success. The geometric analysis reveals successful cases are enveloped by manifolds, helping diagnose stability and transitions between regimes.

Conclusion: Enforcing concise and certain responses via intrinsic rewards can boost reasoning but is not universally reliable. The unsupervised approach has well-defined boundaries, which can be diagnosed geometrically. This work clarifies when such methods work and why, informing design choices and future research on scalable, stable NL(Property) reasoning enhancements.

Abstract: Although outcome-based reinforcement learning (RL) significantly advances the mathematical reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), its reliance on computationally expensive ground-truth annotations imposes a severe scalability bottleneck. Unsupervised RL guided by intrinsic rewards offers a scalable alternative, yet it suffers from opaque training dynamics and catastrophic instability, such as policy collapse and reward hacking. In this paper, we first design and evaluate a suite of intrinsic rewards that explicitly enforce concise and certain generation. Second, to discover the boundaries of this approach, we test base models across a spectrum of intrinsic reasoning capabilities, revealing how a model's foundational logical prior dictates its success or failure. Finally, to demystify why certain configurations stabilize while others collapse, we introduce a novel geometric diagnostic lens, showing that successful cases are enveloped by manifolds. Ultimately, our work goes beyond merely demonstrating that enforcing concise and certain responses successfully boosts mathematical reasoning; we reveal when this unsupervised approach breaks down and geometrically diagnose why.

</details>


### [350] [Trajectory-Optimized Time Reparameterization for Learning-Compatible Reduced-Order Modeling of Stiff Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16583)
*Joe Standridge,Daniel Livescu,Paul Cizmas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces TOTR, an optimization-based time reparameterization for neural ODE ROMs to mitigate stiffness by learning a smooth arc-length time map, enabling stable explicit integration and improved learnability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stiff dynamical systems destabilize explicit integrators in ML-ROMs; existing time-reparameterization (TR) methods are not clearly beneficial for learnability; a principled TR that improves training dynamics is needed.

Method: Formulate TR as a trajectory-optimization problem in arc-length coordinates. Optimize a traversal-speed profile that penalizes acceleration in stretched time to produce smooth trajectories. Integrate this TR with neural-ODE-based ROMs and evaluate on stiff benchmarks.

Result: TOTR yields smoother, better-conditioned reparameterized trajectories, leading to more accurate physical-time predictions under the same training regime. Quantitative gains include loss reductions of 1–2 orders of magnitude compared with benchmark TR methods across three stiff problems (parametrized stiff linear system, van der Pol oscillator, HIRES chemical kinetics).

Conclusion: Optimization-based time reparameterization provides a robust framework for explicit reduced-order modeling of multiscale dynamical systems by enhancing regularity and learnability of the time map, outperforming existing TR approaches.

Abstract: Stiff dynamical systems present a challenge for machine-learning reduced-order models (ML-ROMs), as explicit time integration becomes unstable in stiff regimes while implicit integration within learning loops is computationally expensive and often degrades training efficiency. Time reparameterization (TR) offers an alternative by transforming the independent variable so that rapid physical-time transients are spread over a stretched-time coordinate, enabling stable explicit integration on uniformly sampled grids. Although several TR strategies have been proposed, their effect on learnability in ML-ROMs remains incompletely understood. This work investigates time reparameterization as a stiffness-mitigation mechanism for neural ODE reduced-order modeling and introduces a trajectory-optimized TR (TOTR) formulation. The proposed approach casts time reparameterization as an optimization problem in arc-length coordinates, in which a traversal-speed profile is selected to penalize acceleration in stretched time. By targeting the smoothness of the training dynamics, this formulation produces reparameterized trajectories that are better conditioned and easier to learn than existing TR methods. TOTR is evaluated on three stiff problems: a parameterized stiff linear system, the van der Pol oscillator, and the HIRES chemical kinetics model. Across all cases, the proposed approach yields smoother reparameterizations and improved physical-time predictions under identical training regimens than other TR approaches. Quantitative results demonstrate loss reductions of one to two orders of magnitude compared to benchmark algorithms. These results highlight that effective stiffness mitigation in ML-ROMs depends critically on the regularity and learnability of the time map itself, and that optimization-based TR provides a robust framework for explicit reduced-order modeling of multiscale dynamical systems.

</details>


### [351] [Simplex-to-Euclidean Bijection for Conjugate and Calibrated Multiclass Gaussian Process](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16621)
*Bernardo Williams,Harsha Vardhan Tetali,Arto Klami,Marcelo Hartmann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conjugate, calibrated GP for multi-class classification via Aitchison geometry on the probability simplex, enabling regression-style GP with fewer latent dims and scalable, well-calibrated predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for well-calibrated probabilistic predictions in multi-class GP classifiers and scalable inference; current methods rely on approximate likelihoods and can be miscalibrated.

Method: Map simplex-probabilities to Euclidean space using Aitchison geometry; perform GP regression in latent space with conjugate inference; use sparse GP for scalability; predictions yield calibrated class probabilities without distributional approximations; works with standard sparse GP machinery.

Result: Empirically shows well-calibrated predictive probabilities and competitive accuracy on synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: This framework offers a principled, scalable approach to multi-class GP classification by exploiting simplex geometry, achieving calibration and efficiency advantages over conventional approaches.

Abstract: We propose a conjugate and calibrated Gaussian process (GP) model for multi-class classification by exploiting the geometry of the probability simplex. Our approach uses Aitchison geometry to map simplex-valued class probabilities to an unconstrained Euclidean representation, turning classification into a GP regression problem with fewer latent dimensions than standard multi-class GP classifiers. This yields conjugate inference and reliable predictive probabilities without relying on distributional approximations in the model construction. The method is compatible with standard sparse GP regression techniques, enabling scalable inference on larger datasets. Empirical results show well-calibrated and competitive performance across synthetic and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [352] [Self-Aware Markov Models for Discrete Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16661)
*Gregor Kornhardt,Jannis Chemseddine,Christian Wald,Gabriele Steidl*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A diffusion-based model with a learnable Markov kernel and self-training enables remasking and adaptive stopping, improving reasoning tasks by matching computation to problem difficulty and reusing pretrained models; shows strong results on Sudoku-Extreme (95% validity) and Countdown-4 (≈96% accuracy with ~10 steps on average).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the rigidity of standard masked diffusion models that cannot correct mistakes during the masking path and use a fixed number of denoising steps, limiting their ability to handle varying problem complexity in reasoning tasks.

Method: Learn a Markov transition kernel trained on the model's own outputs. Allow remasking of tokens to enable self-correction. Replace fixed schedules with a trained stopping criterion to adapt the number of function evaluations to problem difficulty. Add two lightweight prediction heads to enable reuse and fine-tuning of existing pretrained models.

Result: Outperforms flow-based methods on Sudoku-Extreme with 95% validity. On Countdown-4, solves ~96% with an average of 10 steps, and many problems solvable in 2 steps.

Conclusion: An adaptive, self-correcting diffusion framework with remasking and a learned stopping condition improves reasoning capabilities, reduces fixed-computation constraints, and is compatible with existing pretrained models, achieving strong empirical results on selected reasoning benchmarks.

Abstract: Standard masked discrete diffusion models face limitations in reasoning tasks due to their inability to correct their own mistakes on the masking path. Since they rely on a fixed number of denoising steps, they are unable to adjust their computation to the complexity of a given problem. To address these limitations, we introduce a method based on learning a Markov transition kernel that is trained on its own outputs. This design enables tokens to be remasked, allowing the model to correct its previous mistakes. Furthermore, we do not need a fixed time schedule but use a trained stopping criterion. This allows for adaptation of the number of function evaluations to the difficulty of the reasoning problem. Our adaptation adds two lightweight prediction heads, enabling reuse and fine-tuning of existing pretrained models. On the Sudoku-Extreme dataset we clearly outperform other flow based methods with a validity of 95%. For the Countdown-4 we only need in average of 10 steps to solve almost 96% of them correctly, while many problems can be solved already in 2 steps.

</details>


### [353] [Grid-World Representations in Transformers Reflect Predictive Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16689)
*Sasha Brenner,Thomas R. Knösche,Nico Scherf*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformers trained on optimal prefixes of constrained random-walks learn low-dimensional representations that align with analytically derived predictive vectors, linking world geometry to predictive distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify how the geometry of the world shapes internal representations in next-token predictors and connect probabilistic structure to learned representations.

Method: Study constrained random walks on a 2D lattice with a fixed endpoint after a set number of steps. Compute the exact predictive distribution and the sufficient predictive vector (based on position and remaining horizon). Train decoder-only transformers on prefixes sampled from this exact distribution. Compare hidden activations to the analytic vectors across models and layers.

Result: Hidden activations align strongly with the ground-truth predictive vectors and are often low-dimensional, across models and layers.

Conclusion: This toy setting demonstrates that world-model–like representations can arise directly from the predictive geometry of data, offering a lens to study how neural networks internalize structural constraints (e.g., grammar) via optimal prediction.

Abstract: Next-token predictors often appear to develop internal representations of the latent world and its rules. The probabilistic nature of these models suggests a deep connection between the structure of the world and the geometry of probability distributions. In order to understand this link more precisely, we use a minimal stochastic process as a controlled setting: constrained random walks on a two-dimensional lattice that must reach a fixed endpoint after a predetermined number of steps. Optimal prediction of this process solely depends on a sufficient vector determined by the walker's position relative to the target and the remaining time horizon; in other words, the probability distributions are parametrized by the world's geometry. We train decoder-only transformers on prefixes sampled from the exact distribution of these walks and compare their hidden activations to the analytically derived sufficient vectors. Across models and layers, the learned representations align strongly with the ground-truth predictive vectors and are often low-dimensional. This provides a concrete example in which world-model-like representations can be directly traced back to the predictive geometry of the data itself. Although demonstrated in a simplified toy system, the analysis suggests that geometric representations supporting optimal prediction may provide a useful lens for studying how neural networks internalize grammatical and other structural constraints.

</details>


### [354] [One Operator to Rule Them All? On Boundary-Indexed Operator Families in Neural PDE Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01406)
*Lennon J. Shikhman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural PDE solvers that vary boundary conditions effectively learn boundary-conditioned operators, not a single boundary-agnostic operator, leading to non-identifiability and poor generalization across boundary shifts; boundary information is essential for robust PDE foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Boundary conditions are a core data component in PDEs, and treating them as fixed or ignoring them can mischaracterize the learning problem. The abstract argues that existing neural operators implicitly encode boundary-condition distributions, which limits generalization.

Method: Formulate operator learning as conditional risk minimization over boundary conditions, derive non-identifiability outside the training boundary-support, and validate with controlled Poisson equation experiments that measure degradation under boundary shifts, cross-distribution failures, and convergence to conditional expectations when boundary data is removed.

Result: Theoretical result: non-identifiability of the learned operator outside the boundary-distribution support. Empirical result: degradation under boundary-condition shifts, cross-distribution failures between distinct boundary ensembles, and convergence to conditional expectations when boundary information is removed.

Conclusion: Current neural PDE solvers have a fundamental boundary-condition sensitivity; achieving robust PDE foundation models requires explicit boundary-aware modeling and possibly new training paradigms to handle varying boundary data.

Abstract: Neural PDE solvers are often described as learning solution operators that map problem data to PDE solutions. In this work, we argue that this interpretation is generally incorrect when boundary conditions vary. We show that standard neural operator training implicitly learns a boundary-indexed family of operators, rather than a single boundary-agnostic operator, with the learned mapping fundamentally conditioned on the boundary-condition distribution seen during training. We formalize this perspective by framing operator learning as conditional risk minimization over boundary conditions, which leads to a non-identifiability result outside the support of the training boundary distribution. As a consequence, generalization in forcing terms or resolution does not imply generalization across boundary conditions. We support our theoretical analysis with controlled experiments on the Poisson equation, demonstrating sharp degradation under boundary-condition shifts, cross-distribution failures between distinct boundary ensembles, and convergence to conditional expectations when boundary information is removed. Our results clarify a core limitation of current neural PDE solvers and highlight the need for explicit boundary-aware modeling in the pursuit of foundation models for PDEs.

</details>


### [355] [Learning Lineage-guided Geodesics with Finsler Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16708)
*Aaron Zweig,Mingxuan Zhang,David A. Knowles,Elham Azizi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a Finsler metric that unifies geometry with discrete, directed prior knowledge to improve trajectory interpolation between timepoints, outperforming baselines on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete, directed priors (e.g., lineage trees) are common in dynamical systems but are poorly integrated in geometric trajectory methods that rely on Riemannian metrics; combining both priors can yield more accurate interpolations.

Method: Introduce a Finsler metric on the state space that blends geometry-based costs with a classification/admissibility component to encode discrete priors. Compute constrained geodesics respecting both priors to interpolate trajectories between observed timepoints.

Result: The approach yields improved interpolation performance on synthetic and real-world datasets compared to methods relying solely on geometric priors.

Conclusion: A unified framework that fuses continuous geometric priors with discrete transition priors via a Finsler metric enhances trajectory inference and can be extended to other domains with mixed priors.

Abstract: Trajectory inference investigates how to interpolate paths between observed timepoints of dynamical systems, such as temporally resolved population distributions, with the goal of inferring trajectories at unseen times and better understanding system dynamics. Previous work has focused on continuous geometric priors, utilizing data-dependent spatial features to define a Riemannian metric. In many applications, there exists discrete, directed prior knowledge over admissible transitions (e.g. lineage trees in developmental biology). We introduce a Finsler metric that combines geometry with classification and incorporate both types of priors in trajectory inference, yielding improved performance on interpolation tasks in synthetic and real-world data.

</details>


### [356] [Novelty-Driven Target-Space Discovery in Automated Electron and Scanning Probe Microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16715)
*Utkarsh Pratiush,Kamyar Barakati,Boris N. Slautin,Catherine C. Bodinger,Christopher D. Lowe,Brandi M. Cossairt,Sergei V. Kalinin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A deep-kernel-learning BEACON framework for discovery in the target-space of automated microscopy, validated offline and on STEM, with benchmarking and open notebooks for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In automated microscopy, crucial information often lies in the target space (sequences of spectra or responses) rather than the immediately visible image features, necessitating active discovery strategies over simple objective optimization.

Method: Develop BEACON to learn structure-property relationships during experiments and actively seek diverse target-space regimes. Demonstrate via offline workflows on pre-acquired ground-truth data, benchmark against classical acquisition strategies using monitoring functions for exploration, target-space coverage, and surrogate-model behavior, then deploy on STEM. Provide notebooks to enable reproduction and adaptation.

Result: Benchmarking framework enables evaluation of discovery-driven algorithms beyond optimization; demonstration of offline validation transitioning to real experimental deployment; notebooks facilitate community adoption and extension across instruments and datasets.

Conclusion: BEACON enables discovery in target space, with a practical benchmarking platform and open workflows that support adoption and adaptation by the research community.

Abstract: Modern automated microscopy faces a fundamental discovery challenge: in many systems, the most important scientific information does not reside in the immediately visible image features, but in the target space of sequentially acquired spectra or functional responses, making it essential to develop strategies that can actively search for new behaviors rather than simply optimize known objectives. Here, we developed a deep-kernel-learning BEACON framework that is explicitly designed to guide discovery in the target space by learning structure-property relationships during the experiment and using that evolving model to seek diverse response regimes. We first established the method through demonstration workflows built on pre-acquired ground-truth datasets, which enabled direct benchmarking against classical acquisition strategies and allowed us to define a set of monitoring functions for comparing exploration quality, target-space coverage, and surrogate-model behavior in a transparent and reproducible manner. This benchmarking framework provides a practical basis for evaluating discovery-driven algorithms, not just optimization performance. We then operationalized and deployed the workflow on STEM, showing that the approach can transition from offline validation to real experimental implementation. To support adoption and extension by the broader community, the associated notebooks are available, allowing users to reproduce the workflows, test the benchmarks, and adapt the method to their own instruments and datasets.

</details>


### [357] [Federated Learning with Multi-Partner OneFlorida+ Consortium Data for Predicting Major Postoperative Complications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16723)
*Yuanfang Ren,Varun Sai Vemuri,Zhenhong Hu,Benjamin Shickel,Ziyuan Guan,Tyler J. Loftus,Parisa Rashidi,Tezcan Ozrazgat-Baslanti,Azra Bihorac*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated learning across five centers on predicting postoperative complications and mortality; achieves comparable/superior AUROC/AUPRC vs local models, preserving privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve generalizability of surgical risk prediction while preserving patient privacy by leveraging multicenter data with federated learning.

Method: Retrospective, multicenter cohort study (2012-2023) including 358,644 adults and 494,163 inpatient major surgical procedures across five institutions. Developed and validated federated learning models to predict ICU admission, MV therapy, AKI, and in-hospital mortality; compared with local single-center models and central pooled models; evaluated with AUROC and AUPRC; internal and external validation.

Result: Federated models showed strong predictive performance with AUROC and AUPRC comparable or superior to local models across outcomes and sites; demonstrated good generalizability; privacy-preserving; feasible for clinical decision support.

Conclusion: Federated learning using multicenter data yields robust, generalizable, privacy-preserving predictive models for major postoperative complications and mortality; supports integration into clinical decision support systems.

Abstract: Background: This study aims to develop and validate federated learning models for predicting major postoperative complications and mortality using a large multicenter dataset from the OneFlorida Data Trust. We hypothesize that federated learning models will offer robust generalizability while preserving data privacy and security. Methods: This retrospective, longitudinal, multicenter cohort study included 358,644 adult patients admitted to five healthcare institutions, who underwent 494,163 inpatient major surgical procedures from 2012-2023. We developed and internally and externally validated federated learning models to predict the postoperative risk of intensive care unit (ICU) admission, mechanical ventilation (MV) therapy, acute kidney injury (AKI), and in-hospital mortality. These models were compared with local models trained on data from a single center and central models trained on a pooled dataset from all centers. Performance was primarily evaluated using area under the receiver operating characteristics curve (AUROC) and the area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) values. Results: Our federated learning models demonstrated strong predictive performance, with AUROC scores consistently comparable or superior performance in terms of AUROC and AUPRC across all outcomes and sites. Our federated learning models also demonstrated strong generalizability, with comparable or superior performance in terms of both AUROC and AUPRC compared to the best local learning model at each site. Conclusions: By leveraging multicenter data, we developed robust, generalizable, and privacy-preserving predictive models for major postoperative complications and mortality. These findings support the feasibility of federated learning in clinical decision support systems.

</details>


### [358] [The Cost of Reasoning: Chain-of-Thought Induces Overconfidence in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16728)
*Robert Welch,Emir Konuk,Kevin Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chain-of-thought reasoning in vision-language models undermines uncertainty estimates by overfitting token probabilities to the model's internal reasoning traces; agreement-based consistency remains more reliable for uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In high-stakes applications, reliable UQ is as important as accuracy. Although reasoning traces (CoT) can boost task performance, it is unclear how they affect the reliability of uncertainty estimates in VLMs.

Method: Empirical analysis of VLMs with and without reasoning prompts, examining how token-level probabilities and model-consistency signals correlate with true uncertainty. Identification of the mechanism by which reasoning traces bias probabilities (implicit answer conditioning) and comparison with agreement-based consistency as an uncertainty estimator.

Result: Reasoning traces converge on a conclusion before the final answer, causing token probabilities to align with the model's own reasoning rather than true uncertainty. This leads to overconfidence and degraded UQ. In contrast, agreement-based consistency remains robust and often improves under reasoning, making it a more reliable estimator for uncertainty in reasoning-enabled VLMs.

Conclusion: For UQ in reasoning-enabled VLMs, prefer agreement-based consistency over token-level probabilities derived from chain-of-thought prompts. CoT can improve accuracy but risks overconfident, miscalibrated uncertainty estimates; design UQ strategies around robust consensus signals.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings where reliable uncertainty quantification (UQ) is as important as predictive accuracy. Extended reasoning via chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting or reasoning-trained models has become ubiquitous in modern VLM pipelines, yet its effect on UQ reliability remains poorly understood. We show that reasoning consistently degrades the quality of most uncertainty estimates, even when it improves task accuracy. We identify implicit answer conditioning as the primary mechanism: as reasoning traces converge on a conclusion before the final answer is generated, token probabilities increasingly reflect consistency with the model's own reasoning trace rather than uncertainty about correctness. In effect, the model becomes overconfident in its answer. In contrast, agreement-based consistency remains robust and often improves under reasoning, making it a practical choice for uncertainty estimation in reasoning-enabled VLMs.

</details>


### [359] [GeMA: Learning Latent Manifold Frontiers for Benchmarking Complex Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16729)
*Jia Ming Li,Anupriya,Daniel J. Graham*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GeMA replaces frontier estimation in observed input-output space with a latent-manifold frontier learned via a productivity-manifold VAE, enabling scale-invariant benchmarking and endogenous peer grouping, with local robustness quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical frontier methods like DEA/SFA assume restrictive production sets and struggle to handle heterogeneity, non-convex frontiers, and scale effects across diverse technologies and sizes.

Method: GeMA models the production set as the boundary of a low-dimensional manifold in joint input-output space. It uses a split-head encoder to learn latent technology- and inefficiency-related variables, defines efficiency relative to the learned manifold, derives endogenous peer groups in latent space, employs a quotient construction for scale-invariance, and computes a local certification radius from the decoder Jacobian and a Lipschitz bound to assess robustness.

Result: On synthetic data with non-convex fronts, heterogeneity, and scale bias, GeMA performs well; on four real-world datasets (global urban rail COMET, British rail ORR, Penn World Table economies, high-frequency wind farms) it matches traditional methods under classical assumptions and offers richer insights in heterogeneous, non-convex, or size-biased settings.

Conclusion: GeMA extends frontier analysis beyond convex, homogeneous settings by leveraging a latent-manifold frontier, enabling endogenous peer clustering, scale-invariant benchmarking, and robustness quantification, thereby yielding comparable results to classical methods while providing added interpretability in challenging contexts.

Abstract: Benchmarking the performance of complex systems such as rail networks, renewable generation assets and national economies is central to transport planning, regulation and macroeconomic analysis. Classical frontier methods, notably Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA) and Stochastic Frontier Analysis (SFA), estimate an efficient frontier in the observed input-output space and define efficiency as distance to this frontier, but rely on restrictive assumptions on the production set and only indirectly address heterogeneity and scale effects.
  We propose Geometric Manifold Analysis (GeMA), a latent manifold frontier framework implemented via a productivity-manifold variational autoencoder (ProMan-VAE). Instead of specifying a frontier function in the observed space, GeMA represents the production set as the boundary of a low-dimensional manifold embedded in the joint input-output space. A split-head encoder learns latent variables that capture technological structure and operational inefficiency. Efficiency is evaluated with respect to the learned manifold, endogenous peer groups arise as clusters in latent technology space, a quotient construction supports scale-invariant benchmarking, and a local certification radius, derived from the decoder Jacobian and a Lipschitz bound, quantifies the geometric robustness of efficiency scores.
  We validate GeMA on synthetic data with non-convex frontiers, heterogeneous technologies and scale bias, and on four real-world case studies: global urban rail systems (COMET), British rail operators (ORR), national economies (Penn World Table) and a high-frequency wind-farm dataset. Across these domains GeMA behaves comparably to established methods when classical assumptions hold, and provides additional insight in settings with pronounced heterogeneity, non-convexity or size-related bias.

</details>
