### [360] [Understanding Quantization of Optimizer States in LLM Pre-training: Dynamics of State Staleness and Effectiveness of State Resets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16731)
*Kristi Topollai,Anna Choromanska*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Low-precision EMA states can stall updates due to quantization; a theory-guided reset schedule recovers performance while reducing memory in pre-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory-efficient large-scale pre-training requires storing optimizer states in low precision; understanding quantization-induced stalling explains why resets help and guides reset timing.

Method: Develop a predictive model of one-step stalling probabilities and how stalling accumulates after initialization; derive a reset-period rule from theory; validate via controlled simulations and LLM pre-training.

Result: Quantization causes many nominal updates to map to the same stored EMA value, making the state effectively stale; resets temporarily restore responsiveness. A simple, theory-guided reset schedule recovers performance lost to low-precision storage and substantially reduces optimizer-state memory.

Conclusion: Theory-guided resets offer a practical solution to mitigate low-precision EMA stalling, enabling memory-efficient pre-training without substantial performance loss.

Abstract: Quantizing optimizer states is becoming an important ingredient of memory-efficient large-scale pre-training, but the resulting optimizer dynamics remain only partially understood. We study low-precision exponential moving average (EMA) optimizer states and show how quantization can cause many nominal updates to round back to the same stored value, making the state effectively stale and slowing adaptation beyond what the nominal decay would suggest. We then develop a simple predictive model of stalling that estimates one-step stalling probabilities and characterizes how stalling builds up over time after the initialization. This perspective provides a mechanistic explanation for why optimizer-state resets help in low precision: once a quantized EMA becomes effectively stale, resetting it can temporarily restore responsiveness. Motivated by this picture, we derive a simple theory-guided method for choosing useful reset periods, showing that in low precision the key question is not only whether resets help, but when they should be applied. Experiments in controlled simulations and LLM pre-training show that suitable reset schedules recover the performance lost to low-precision state storage while substantially reducing optimizer-state memory.

</details>


### [361] [SpecMoE: Spectral Mixture-of-Experts Foundation Model for Cross-Species EEG Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16739)
*D. Darankoum,C. Habermacher,J. Volle,S. Grudinin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gaussian-smoothed masking on STFT maps with SpecHi-Net and SpecMoE yields state-of-the-art EEG decoding and cross-species/generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome bias toward high-frequency components and enable robust, scalable EEG decoding across tasks and species; leverage joint time-frequency masking and scalable pretraining.

Method: Apply time, frequency, and time-frequency Gaussian masks to STFT maps for self-supervised pretraining; train SpecHi-Net (a U-shaped hierarchical encoder-decoder) to reconstruct masked signals; partition data into three subsets to train independent expert models; fuse with SpecMoE (mixture of experts) guided by a learned spectral gating mechanism.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across sleep staging, emotion recognition, motor imagery classification, abnormal signal detection, and drug effect prediction; strong cross-subject and cross-species generalization (human and murine EEG).

Conclusion: The masking strategy and the SpecHi-Net/SpecMoE architecture effectively capture intricate neural patterns across frequencies, reduce frequency bias, and enable scalable, generalizable EEG decoding.

Abstract: Decoding the orchestration of neural activity in electroencephalography (EEG) signals is a central challenge in bridging neuroscience with artificial intelligence. Foundation models have made strides in generalized EEG decoding, yet many existing frameworks primarily relying on separate temporal and spectral masking of raw signals during self-supervised pretraining. Such strategies often tend to bias learning toward high-frequency oscillations, as low-frequency rhythmic patterns can be easily inferred from the unmasked signal. We introduce a foundation model that utilizes a novel Gaussian-smoothed masking scheme applied to short-time Fourier transform (STFT) maps. By jointly applying time, frequency, and time-frequency Gaussian masks, we make the reconstruction task much more challenging, forcing the model to learn intricate neural patterns across both high- and low-frequency domains. To effectively recover signals under this aggressive masking strategy, we design SpecHi-Net, a U-shaped hierarchical architecture with multiple encoding and decoding stages. To accelerate large-scale pretraining, we partition the data into three subsets, each used to train an independent expert model. We then combine these models through SpecMoE, a mixture of experts framework guided by a learned spectral gating mechanism. SpecMoE achieves state-of-the-art performance across a diverse set of EEG decoding tasks, including sleep staging, emotion recognition, motor imagery classification, abnormal signal detection, and drug effect prediction. Importantly, the model demonstrates strong cross-species and cross-subject generalization, maintaining high accuracy on both human and murine EEG datasets.

</details>


### [362] [Bayesian Inference of Psychometric Variables From Brain and Behavior in Implicit Association Tests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16741)
*Christian A. Kothe,Sean Mullen,Michael V. Bronstein,Grant Hanada,Marcelo Cicconet,Aaron N. McInnes,Tim Mullen,Marc Aafjes,Scott R. Sponheim,Alik S. Widge*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A sparse hierarchical Bayesian multivariate extension of the D-score for IAT data to predict mental health-related experiences from multi-modal data, achieving AUCs around 0.73–0.76 in two small-sample IAT studies; shows cross-task consistency and performance on par with reference methods but requires larger validation due to wide CIs and marginal significance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited predictive performance of the traditional D-score (based only on RTs) by leveraging multi-modal data and a trainable, parameter-efficient Bayesian framework suitable for small cohorts typical of IAT studies.

Method: Propose a sparse hierarchical Bayesian model that generalizes the D-score to a multivariate setting with trainable parameters to enable parameter efficiency. Analyzed data from two IAT variants: suicidality-related E-IAT (n=39) and psychosis-related PSY-IAT (n=34).

Result: Best configurations yielded AUCs of 0.73 (E-IAT) and 0.76 (PSY-IAT); 95% CIs are wide (±0.18) and results are marginal after FDR (q=0.10). Restricting E-IAT to MDD participants raised AUC to 0.79 [0.62,0.97] (significant at q=0.05). Performance comparable to shrinkage LDA and EEGNet; accuracy far above near-chance D-scores (0.50–0.53); cross-task performance more consistent than any single reference method.

Conclusion: The framework shows promise for improving IAT-based assessment of experiences related to entrapment and psychosis, with potential applicability to other mental health conditions, but requires validation on larger, independent cohorts to establish clinical utility.

Abstract: Objective. We establish a principled method for inferring mental health related psychometric variables from neural and behavioral data using the Implicit Association Test (IAT) as the data generation engine, aiming to overcome the limited predictive performance (typically under 0.7 AUC) of the gold-standard D-score method, which relies solely on reaction times.
  Approach. We propose a sparse hierarchical Bayesian model that leverages multi-modal data to predict experiences related to mental illness symptoms in new participants. The model is a multivariate generalization of the D-score with trainable parameters, engineered for parameter efficiency in the small-cohort regime typical of IAT studies. Data from two IAT variants were analyzed: a suicidality-related E-IAT ($n=39$) and a psychosis-related PSY-IAT ($n=34$).
  Main Results. Our approach overcomes a high inter-individual variability and low within-session effect size in the dataset, reaching AUCs of 0.73 (E-IAT) and 0.76 (PSY-IAT) in the best modality configurations, though corrected 95% confidence intervals are wide ($\pm 0.18$) and results are marginally significant after FDR correction ($q=0.10$). Restricting the E-IAT to MDD participants improves AUC to 0.79 $[0.62, 0.97]$ (significant at $q=0.05$). Performance is on par with the best reference methods (shrinkage LDA and EEGNet) for each task, even when the latter were adapted to the task, while the proposed method was not. Accuracy was substantially above near-chance D-scores (0.50-0.53 AUC) in both tasks, with more consistent cross-task performance than any single reference method.
  Significance. Our framework shows promise for enhancing IAT-based assessment of experiences related to entrapment and psychosis, and potentially other mental health conditions, though further validation on larger and independent cohorts will be needed to establish clinical utility.

</details>


### [363] [A Practical Algorithm for Feature-Rich, Non-Stationary Bandit Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16755)
*Wei Min Loh,Sajib Kumer Sinha,Ankur Agarwal,Pascal Poupart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces conditionally coupled contextual C3 Thompson sampling for Bernoulli bandits to handle dense features, nonlinear rewards, and time-varying yet correlated reward distributions; combines a nonparametric estimator with Thompson sampling for online learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address realistic contextual bandits where contexts are dense, rewards are nonlinear, and reward distributions drift over time while preserving correlation; motivated by recommendation systems.

Method: Develops conditionally coupled contextual C3 Thompson sampling (C3-TS) using an improved Nadaraya-Watson estimator on an embedding space together with Thompson sampling to enable online learning without retraining, accommodating non-stationarity.

Result: Empirical results show 5.7% lower average cumulative regret on four OpenML tabular datasets and a 12.4% click lift on the Microsoft News Dataset (MIND) relative to competing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed C3-TS method improves performance in complex contextual bandit settings with dense features and nonlinear rewards, validating online applicability without retraining and suggesting broader applicability.

Abstract: Contextual bandits are incredibly useful in many practical problems. We go one step further by devising a more realistic problem that combines: (1) contextual bandits with dense arm features, (2) non-linear reward functions, and (3) a generalization of correlated bandits where reward distributions change over time but the degree of correlation maintains. This formulation lends itself to a wider set of applications such as recommendation tasks. To solve this problem, we introduce conditionally coupled contextual C3 Thompson sampling for Bernoulli bandits. It combines an improved Nadaraya-Watson estimator on an embedding space with Thompson sampling that allows online learning without retraining. Empirical results show that C3 outperforms the next best algorithm by 5.7% lower average cumulative regret on four OpenML tabular datasets as well as demonstrating a 12.4% click lift on Microsoft News Dataset (MIND) compared to other algorithms.

</details>


### [364] [pADAM: A Plug-and-Play All-in-One Diffusion Architecture for Multi-Physics Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16757)
*Amirhossein Mollaali,Bongseok Kim,Christian Moya,Guang Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: pADAM is a unified probabilistic generative framework that learns a shared prior across heterogeneous PDE families to enable forward prediction, inverse inference, and probabilistic model selection from sparse data, with uncertainty quantification via conformal prediction, without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalize learning across disparate physical laws and PDE regimes to enable transfer, multi-task solving, and uncertainty-aware inference within a single architecture.

Method: Train a single generative model that captures a joint distribution over system states (and physical parameters where available) across PDE families, enabling forward prediction, inverse inference, and model selection. Use conformal prediction for calibrated uncertainty guarantees and perform probabilistic model selection from as few as two sparse snapshots.

Result: Benchmarks from scalar diffusion to nonlinear Navier–Stokes show accurate inference under sparse observations; uncertainty quantification with coverage guarantees via conformal prediction; capable of identifying governing equations through learned generative representation using minimal data.

Conclusion: Generative multi-physics modeling can provide a unified, uncertainty-aware framework for scientific inference across diverse physical laws, enabling transferability, robust uncertainty quantification, and data-efficient model selection.

Abstract: Generalizing across disparate physical laws remains a fundamental challenge for artificial intelligence in science. Existing deep-learning solvers are largely confined to single-equation settings, limiting transfer across physical regimes and inference tasks. Here we introduce pADAM, a unified generative framework that learns a shared probabilistic prior across heterogeneous partial differential equation families. Through a learned joint distribution of system states and, where applicable, physical parameters, pADAM supports forward prediction and inverse inference within a single architecture without retraining. Across benchmarks ranging from scalar diffusion to nonlinear Navier--Stokes equations, pADAM achieves accurate inference even under sparse observations. Combined with conformal prediction, it also provides reliable uncertainty quantification with coverage guarantees. In addition, pADAM performs probabilistic model selection from only two sparse snapshots, identifying governing laws through its learned generative representation. These results highlight the potential of generative multi-physics modeling for unified and uncertainty-aware scientific inference.

</details>


### [365] [SOMP: Scalable Gradient Inversion for Large Language Models via Subspace-Guided Orthogonal Matching Pursuit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16761)
*Yibo Li,Qiongxiu Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOMP is a scalable gradient inversion method that recovers private training text from aggregated transformer gradients by exploiting head-wise structure and sample sparsity, outperforming baselines in large-batch and long-sequence regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address privacy risks in gradient-based leakage for LLMs, particularly when batching is large and sequences long, where prior attacks struggle due to signal mixing and high cost.

Method: Subspace-Guided Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (SOMP): cast gradient inversion as sparse signal recovery; leverage geometry of transformer heads and sample-level sparsity to progressively narrow the search space and disentangle mixed signals without exhaustive search.

Result: SOMP achieves higher reconstruction fidelity than strong baselines across multiple LLM families, scales, languages; notably improves long-sequence reconstruction at B=16 and remains meaningful up to B=128 with competitive compute.

Conclusion: Aggregated gradients retain exploitable structure enabling successful text recovery; while SOMP improves scalability and fidelity, privacy leakage persists under aggregation, underscoring the need for defenses in gradient-sharing workflows.

Abstract: Gradient inversion attacks reveal that private training text can be reconstructed from shared gradients, posing a privacy risk to large language models (LLMs). While prior methods perform well in small-batch settings, scaling to larger batch sizes and longer sequences remains challenging due to severe signal mixing, high computational cost, and degraded fidelity. We present SOMP (Subspace-Guided Orthogonal Matching Pursuit), a scalable gradient inversion framework that casts text recovery from aggregated gradients as a sparse signal recovery problem. Our key insight is that aggregated transformer gradients retain exploitable head-wise geometric structure together with sample-level sparsity. SOMP leverages these properties to progressively narrow the search space and disentangle mixed signals without exhaustive search. Experiments across multiple LLM families, model scales, and five languages show that SOMP consistently outperforms prior methods in the aggregated-gradient regime.For long sequences at batch size B=16, SOMP achieves substantially higher reconstruction fidelity than strong baselines, while remaining computationally competitive. Even under extreme aggregation (up to B=128), SOMP still recovers meaningful text, suggesting that privacy leakage can persist in regimes where prior attacks become much less effective.

</details>


### [366] [Conservative Continuous-Time Treatment Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16789)
*Nora Schneider,Georg Manten,Niki Kilbertus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A conservative continuous-time stochastic control framework for treatment optimization from irregularly sampled patient trajectories using a signature-MMD regularizer to constrain policy/exploration; provides an upper bound on true cost; shows robustness gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Healthcare control problems suffer from model misspecification and out-of-support policies when optimizing estimated dynamics on irregularly sampled data; a robust, conservative approach is needed to limit extrapolation and improve safety.

Method: Model patient dynamics as a controlled stochastic differential equation with treatment as a continuous-time control. Introduce a consistent signature-based MMD regularizer on path space to penalize treatment plans whose induced trajectory distribution deviates from observed trajectories, yielding an optimization objective that minimizes a computable upper bound on the true cost.

Result: Empirical experiments on benchmark datasets show improved robustness and performance compared with non-conservative baselines.

Conclusion: A conservative, path-distribution regularized control framework reduces extrapolation risk and improves robustness in treatment optimization under irregular sampling and model uncertainty.

Abstract: We develop a conservative continuous-time stochastic control framework for treatment optimization from irregularly sampled patient trajectories. The unknown patient dynamics are modeled as a controlled stochastic differential equation with treatment as a continuous-time control. Naive model-based optimization can exploit model errors and propose out-of-support controls, so optimizing the estimated dynamics may not optimize the true dynamics. To limit extrapolation, we add a consistent signature-based MMD regularizer on path space that penalizes treatment plans whose induced trajectory distribution deviates from observed trajectories. The resulting objective minimizes a computable upper bound on the true cost. Experiments on benchmark datasets show improved robustness and performance compared to non-conservative baselines.

</details>


### [367] [Adaptive Moments are Surprisingly Effective for Plug-and-Play Diffusion Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16797)
*Christian Belardi,Justin Lovelace,Kilian Q. Weinberger,Carla P. Gomes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive moment estimation stabilizes noisy likelihood scores in guided diffusion sampling, delivering state-of-the-art results in image restoration and class-conditional generation with lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gradient/noise from intractable likelihood scores during guided diffusion harms sampling quality; a simple stabilizer is needed.

Method: Integrate adaptive moment estimation (Adam-like first and second moment estimates) to denoise and stabilize the gradients (likelihood scores) used in sampling.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on image restoration and class-conditional generation; outperforms more complex, computationally heavier methods; empirical analysis on synthetic and real data shows improved alignment via reduced gradient noise.

Conclusion: A simple, adaptive-moment-based stabilization suffices to improve guided diffusion sampling quality and efficiency, with broad empirical support across data types.

Abstract: Guided diffusion sampling relies on approximating often intractable likelihood scores, which introduces significant noise into the sampling dynamics. We propose using adaptive moment estimation to stabilize these noisy likelihood scores during sampling. Despite its simplicity, our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on image restoration and class-conditional generation tasks, outperforming more complicated methods, which are often computationally more expensive. We provide empirical analysis of our method on both synthetic and real data, demonstrating that mitigating gradient noise through adaptive moments offers an effective way to improve alignment.

</details>


### [368] [High-Dimensional Gaussian Mean Estimation under Realizable Contamination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16798)
*Ilias Diakonikolas,Daniel M. Kane,Thanasis Pittas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Shows an information-computation gap for Gaussian mean estimation under ε-realizable contamination: statistically optimal sample complexity but computationally hard in SQ, with a near-matching algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the computational limits of mean estimation under a realistic, intermediate missing-data model that sits between MCAR and MNAR, and to determine whether efficient algorithms can exist in high dimensions.

Method: Information-theoretic SQ lower bounds are derived for the ε-realizable contamination model; corollaries apply to Low-Degree Polynomials and PTF tests. The authors also present an algorithm with a near-optimal tradeoff between sample complexity and runtime, achieving a nearly tight match to the lower bound.

Result: There is an information-computation gap: any algorithm in the SQ framework (and related models) must either use substantially more samples than information-theoretically necessary or incur exponential runtime. The authors provide a constructive algorithm whose sample-time performance nearly matches this lower bound.

Conclusion: Gaussian mean estimation under ε-realizable contamination exhibits a clear information-computation gap in high dimensions: computationally efficient solutions may require more samples, while statistically optimal methods can be computationally prohibitive; the proposed algorithm comes close to the fundamental tradeoff.

Abstract: We study mean estimation for a Gaussian distribution with identity covariance in $\mathbb{R}^d$ under a missing data scheme termed realizable $ε$-contamination model. In this model an adversary can choose a function $r(x)$ between 0 and $ε$ and each sample $x$ goes missing with probability $r(x)$. Recent work Ma et al., 2024 proposed this model as an intermediate-strength setting between Missing Completely At Random (MCAR) -- where missingness is independent of the data -- and Missing Not At Random (MNAR) -- where missingness may depend arbitrarily on the sample values and can lead to non-identifiability issues. That work established information-theoretic upper and lower bounds for mean estimation in the realizable contamination model. Their proposed estimators incur runtime exponential in the dimension, leaving open the possibility of computationally efficient algorithms in high dimensions. In this work, we establish an information-computation gap in the Statistical Query model (and, as a corollary, for Low-Degree Polynomials and PTF tests), showing that algorithms must either use substantially more samples than information-theoretically necessary or incur exponential runtime. We complement our SQ lower bound with an algorithm whose sample-time tradeoff nearly matches our lower bound. Together, these results qualitatively characterize the complexity of Gaussian mean estimation under $ε$-realizable contamination.

</details>


### [369] [RaDAR: Relation-aware Diffusion-Asymmetric Graph Contrastive Learning for Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16800)
*Yixuan Huang,Jiawei Chen,Shengfan Zhang,Zongsheng Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RaDAR is a relation-aware, diffusion-guided graph contrastive learning framework for recommendations that uses a graph generative model for global structure and a denoising module for noisy edges, featuring asymmetric, globally negative sampled contrastive learning, diffusion-based augmentation, and dynamic edge refinement; it shows robustness to noise and sparsity and outperforms baselines on three benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address two key problems in GNN-based collaborative filtering with graph contrastive learning: (i) random edge perturbations can distort structural signals and undermine semantic consistency across augmented views, and (ii) data sparsity hampers propagation of collaborative signals, limiting generalization.

Method: RaDAR integrates two complementary view-generation mechanisms: (a) a graph generative model to capture global structure, and (b) a relation-aware denoising model to refine noisy edges. It introduces three innovations: (1) asymmetric contrastive learning with global negative sampling to preserve semantic alignment while suppressing noise; (2) diffusion-guided augmentation that applies progressive noise injection and denoising for robustness; (3) relation-aware edge refinement that dynamically adjusts edge weights based on latent node semantics.

Result: Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks show RaDAR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, particularly under noisy and sparse conditions.

Conclusion: RaDAR provides a robust, semantically aligned graph-contrastive learning framework for recommendation systems, effectively mitigating perturbation effects and data sparsity, leading to improved generalization across benchmarks.

Abstract: Collaborative filtering (CF) recommendation has been significantly advanced by integrating Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL). However, (i) random edge perturbations often distort critical structural signals and degrade semantic consistency across augmented views, and (ii) data sparsity hampers the propagation of collaborative signals, limiting generalization.
  To tackle these challenges, we propose RaDAR (Relation-aware Diffusion-Asymmetric Graph Contrastive Learning Framework for Recommendation Systems), a novel framework that combines two complementary view generation mechanisms: a graph generative model to capture global structure and a relation-aware denoising model to refine noisy edges.
  RaDAR introduces three key innovations: (1) asymmetric contrastive learning with global negative sampling to maintain semantic alignment while suppressing noise; (2) diffusion-guided augmentation, which employs progressive noise injection and denoising for enhanced robustness; and (3) relation-aware edge refinement, dynamically adjusting edge weights based on latent node semantics.
  Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks demonstrate that RaDAR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, particularly under noisy and sparse conditions.

</details>


### [370] [Stochastic Resetting Accelerates Policy Convergence in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16842)
*Jello Zhou,Vudtiwat Ngampruetikorn,David J. Schwab*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Stochastic resetting accelerates reinforcement learning by truncating long uninformative trajectories, speeding up policy convergence in both tabular and deep RL without sacrificing optimality, and offering a novel mechanism beyond traditional first-passage optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how a canonical stochastic-resetting mechanism interacts with reinforcement learning, particularly when the underlying policy and value estimates adapt through experience.

Method: Empirical studies in two settings: (i) tabular grid environments to assess policy convergence under resetting vs diffusive search; (ii) continuous control with neural-network value approximation to evaluate learning performance under random resets, focusing on exploration, sparse rewards, and value propagation.

Result: Resetting speeds up policy convergence even when it does not shorten search time for a purely diffusive agent; in deep RL with sparse rewards, random resets improve learning; resetting preserves the optimal policy while accelerating convergence by truncating long, uninformative trajectories to enhance value propagation.

Conclusion: Stochastic resetting is a simple, tunable mechanism to accelerate learning in RL, translating a statistical mechanics concept into an optimization principle that improves exploration and value propagation without altering the final policy.

Abstract: Stochastic resetting, where a dynamical process is intermittently returned to a fixed reference state, has emerged as a powerful mechanism for optimizing first-passage properties. Existing theory largely treats static, non-learning processes. Here we ask how stochastic resetting interacts with reinforcement learning, where the underlying dynamics adapt through experience. In tabular grid environments, we find that resetting accelerates policy convergence even when it does not reduce the search time of a purely diffusive agent, indicating a novel mechanism beyond classical first-passage optimization. In a continuous control task with neural-network-based value approximation, we show that random resetting improves deep reinforcement learning when exploration is difficult and rewards are sparse. Unlike temporal discounting, resetting preserves the optimal policy while accelerating convergence by truncating long, uninformative trajectories to enhance value propagation. Our results establish stochastic resetting as a simple, tunable mechanism for accelerating learning, translating a canonical phenomenon of statistical mechanics into an optimization principle for reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [371] [Dynamic Meta-Layer Aggregation for Byzantine-Robust Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16846)
*Reek Das,Biplab Kanti Sen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedAOT uses a metalearning-inspired adaptive aggregation to down-weight unreliable client updates in federated learning, defending against multi-label flipping and untargeted poisoning without predefined attack assumptions. It generalizes across datasets and attacks with efficient computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Byzantine faults in federated learning and the limitations of attack-specific defenses. Untargeted and composite attacks can degrade global model performance; a generalizable, threshold-free defense is needed.

Method: Adaptive client weighting based on reliability signals derived from a meta-learning inspired framework, enabling dynamic aggregation without fixed thresholds or attack-specific assumptions.

Result: Significant improvements in accuracy and robustness across diverse datasets and unseen attacks, while maintaining computational efficiency.

Conclusion: FedAOT offers a scalable, practical defense for secure federated learning with strong generalization to various attack types.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) is increasingly applied in sectors like healthcare, finance, and IoT, enabling collaborative model training while safeguarding user privacy. However, FL systems are susceptible to Byzantine adversaries that inject malicious updates, which can severely compromise global model performance. Existing defenses tend to focus on specific attack types and fail against untargeted strategies, such as multi-label flipping or combinations of noise and backdoor patterns. To overcome these limitations, we propose FedAOT-a novel defense mechanism that counters multi-label flipping and untargeted poisoning attacks using a metalearning-inspired adaptive aggregation framework. FedAOT dynamically weights client updates based on their reliability, suppressing adversarial influence without relying on predefined thresholds or restrictive attack assumptions. Notably, FedAOT generalizes effectively across diverse datasets and a wide range of attack types, maintaining robust performance even in previously unseen scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that FedAOT substantially improves model accuracy and resilience while maintaining computational efficiency, offering a scalable and practical solution for secure federated learning.

</details>


### [372] [GIST: Gauge-Invariant Spectral Transformers for Scalable Graph Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16849)
*Mattia Rigotti,Nicholas Thumiger,Thomas Frick*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GIST is a gauge-invariant, scalable graph transformer for mesh/graph data that achieves end-to-end O(N) complexity via random projections and inner-product attention, enabling parameter transfer across mesh resolutions and delivering state-of-the-art performance on graph and neural-operator benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spectral positional encodings on graphs/meshes face two failure modes: exact spectral methods are cubic and can break gauge invariance due to solver artifacts; approximate methods are faster but destroy gauge symmetry. This leads to poor generalization when spectral decompositions or discretizations vary. A gauge-invariant, scalable spectral transformer is needed for robust neural operators.

Method: Propose GIST (Gauge-Invariant Spectral Transformers). It uses random projections to achieve O(N) complexity and preserves gauge invariance by employing inner-product-based attention on projected embeddings. The method ensures discretization-invariant learning with bounded mismatch error, enabling parameter transfer across arbitrary mesh resolutions.

Result: Empirically competitive: matches state-of-the-art on standard graph benchmarks (e.g., 99.50% micro-F1 on PPI) and scales to mesh-based neural-operator benchmarks with up to 750K nodes, achieving state-of-the-art aerodynamic prediction on DrivAerNet and DrivAerNet++ datasets.

Conclusion: GIST provides an end-to-end, scalable, gauge-invariant spectral transformer for graphs and meshes. It mitigates generalization failures due to numerical spectral choices, enabling cross-resolution parameter transfer for neural operators and delivering strong results on both graph benchmarks and large mesh datasets.

Abstract: Adapting transformer positional encoding to meshes and graph-structured data presents significant computational challenges: exact spectral methods require cubic-complexity eigendecomposition and can inadvertently break gauge invariance through numerical solver artifacts, while efficient approximate methods sacrifice gauge symmetry by design. Both failure modes cause catastrophic generalization in inductive learning, where models trained with one set of numerical choices fail when encountering different spectral decompositions of similar graphs or discretizations of the same mesh. We propose GIST (Gauge-Invariant Spectral Transformers), a new graph transformer architecture that resolves this challenge by achieving end-to-end $\mathcal{O}(N)$ complexity through random projections while algorithmically preserving gauge invariance via inner-product-based attention on the projected embeddings. We prove GIST achieves discretization-invariant learning with bounded mismatch error, enabling parameter transfer across arbitrary mesh resolutions for neural operator applications. Empirically, GIST matches state-of-the-art on standard graph benchmarks (e.g., achieving 99.50% micro-F1 on PPI) while uniquely scaling to mesh-based Neural Operator benchmarks with up to 750K nodes, achieving state-of-the-art aerodynamic prediction on the challenging DrivAerNet and DrivAerNet++ datasets.

</details>


### [373] [Long-Horizon Traffic Forecasting via Incident-Aware Conformal Spatio-Temporal Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16857)
*Mayur Patil,Qadeer Ahmed,Shawn Midlam-Mohler,Stephanie Marik,Allen Sheldon,Rajeev Chhajer,Nithin Santhanam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Spatio-Temporal Transformer with Adaptive Conformal Prediction leverages dynamic, incident-informed graphs and a log-normal CV-based uncertainty model to deliver calibrated, long-horizon traffic forecasts validated on ODOT data via SUMO and Monte Carlo simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable long-horizon traffic forecasting is challenged by stochastic network conditions, intermittent incidents, and time-varying spatial dependencies. Calibrated uncertainty is essential for decision-making.

Method: 1) Spatio-Temporal Transformer (STT) with Adaptive Conformal Prediction (ACP) for multi-horizon forecasts. 2) Piecewise Coefficient of Variation (CV) modeling of hour-to-hour travel-time variability using a log-normal distribution to build a per-hour dynamic adjacency matrix. 3) Dynamic edge-weight perturbations using incident severity signals (clearance time, weather, speed violations, work zones, functional class) to capture disruptions. 4) Replaces fixed-CV with dynamic graph construction; validates with extended SUMO trips and Monte Carlo travel-time distributions for a Vehicle Under Test (VUT).

Result: Improved long-horizon forecast accuracy and well-calibrated prediction intervals compared with baseline methods on realistic traffic networks.

Conclusion: Dynamic, incident-aware graph construction combined with ACP and a probabilistic CV model enhances multi-horizon traffic forecasting accuracy and interval calibration under varying conditions; validated through realistic simulations and data.

Abstract: Reliable multi-horizon traffic forecasting is challenging because network conditions are stochastic, incident disruptions are intermittent, and effective spatial dependencies vary across time-of-day patterns. This study is conducted on the Ohio Department of Transportation (ODOT) traffic count data and corresponding ODOT crash records. This work utilizes a Spatio-Temporal Transformer (STT) model with Adaptive Conformal Prediction (ACP) to produce multi-horizon forecasts with calibrated uncertainty. We propose a piecewise Coefficient of Variation (CV) strategy that models hour-to-hour traveltime variability using a log-normal distribution, enabling the construction of a per-hour dynamic adjacency matrix. We further perturb edge weights using incident-related severity signals derived from the ODOT crash dataset that comprises incident clearance time, weather conditions, speed violations, work zones, and roadway functional class, to capture localized disruptions and peak/off-peak transitions. This dynamic graph construction replaces a fixed-CV assumption and better represents changing traffic conditions within the forecast window. For validation, we generate extended trips via multi-hour loop runs on the Columbus, Ohio, network in SUMO simulations and apply a Monte Carlo simulation to obtain travel-time distributions for a Vehicle Under Test (VUT). Experiments demonstrate improved long-horizon accuracy and well-calibrated prediction intervals compared to other baseline methods.

</details>


### [374] [Efficient Reasoning on the Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.16867)
*Yelysei Bondarenko,Thomas Hehn,Rob Hesselink,Romain Lepert,Fabio Valerio Massoli,Evgeny Mironov,Leyla Mirvakhabova,Tribhuvanesh Orekondy,Spyridon Stasis,Andrey Kuzmin,Anna Kuzina,Markus Nagel,Ankita Nayak,Corrado Rainone,Ork de Rooij,Paul N Whatmough,Arash Behboodi,Babak Ehteshami Bejnordi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a lightweight, on-device reasoning framework for small LLMs using LoRA adapters, supervised fine-tuning, and RL-based budget control, complemented by memory- and latency-focused optimizations (parallel test-time scaling, dynamic adapter switching, KV-cache sharing) to enable accurate reasoning under strict resource constraints on mobile hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the practical barriers of deploying chain-of-thought reasoning in edge devices: verbose reasoning traces, high token costs, large KV caches, and inefficiencies in distilling reasoning to smaller models.

Method:  combines LoRA adapters with supervised fine-tuning for lightweight reasoning; introduces budget forcing via reinforcement learning to trim response length; leverages parallel test-time scaling to address memory-bound decoding; implements dynamic adapter switching to enable reasoning only when necessary; applies KV-cache sharing during prompt encoding to reduce time-to-first-token; evaluates on Qwen2.5-7B.

Result: Demonstrates efficient, accurate reasoning under stringent resource constraints on a 7B-class model, with reduced latency and memory footprints; project page includes videos of on-device demonstrations.

Conclusion: The approach makes on-device reasoning for mobile scenarios feasible by integrating lightweight adapter-based fine-tuning with RL-guided budget control and practical decoding-time optimizations; results suggest useful trade-offs between accuracy and resource usage.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) with chain-of-thought reasoning achieve state-of-the-art performance across complex problem-solving tasks, but their verbose reasoning traces and large context requirements make them impractical for edge deployment. These challenges include high token generation costs, large KV-cache footprints, and inefficiencies when distilling reasoning capabilities into smaller models for mobile devices. Existing approaches often rely on distilling reasoning traces from larger models into smaller models, which are verbose and stylistically redundant, undesirable for on-device inference. In this work, we propose a lightweight approach to enable reasoning in small LLMs using LoRA adapters combined with supervised fine-tuning. We further introduce budget forcing via reinforcement learning on these adapters, significantly reducing response length with minimal accuracy loss. To address memory-bound decoding, we exploit parallel test-time scaling, improving accuracy at minor latency increase. Finally, we present a dynamic adapter-switching mechanism that activates reasoning only when needed and a KV-cache sharing strategy during prompt encoding, reducing time-to-first-token for on-device inference. Experiments on Qwen2.5-7B demonstrate that our method achieves efficient, accurate reasoning under strict resource constraints, making LLM reasoning practical for mobile scenarios. Videos demonstrating our solution running on mobile devices are available on our project page.

</details>
